name: The Go Engineer v3 — Core Path

architectural_decision.core_rule: Keep good lessons, break the old path, optimize for learner progression, proof, documentation quality, and job readiness.
architectural_decision.decision: Break the locked v2 curriculum and rebuild around the Report 1 phase-based zero-to-software-engineer path.
architectural_decision.full_bundle_policy: Split files are authoritative. curriculum.v3.full.generated.json is an optional generated snapshot for review/export only.
architectural_decision.v2_policy: curriculum.v2.json is frozen legacy import data, not the active source of truth.

items.title: How to use this repository
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply How to use this repository in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Skipping the module README and jumping straight to exercises.
Trying to memorize folder paths instead of understanding the structure.
Modifying curriculum files before understanding the governance rules.
execution_timeline: Open the repository in an editor and browse the top-level folders.
Read the module README for learning goals, prerequisites, and outputs.
Navigate to specific lesson folders using the curriculum metadata as a map.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner gets lost in the folder structure and cannot find the next lesson.
failure_modes.cause: The learner skipped the orientation module and started from module 01 directly.
failure_modes.fix: Return to 00-orientation, read the module README, and follow the prescribed order.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner accidentally edits a curriculum JSON or script file and breaks validation.
failure_modes.cause: The file was opened in write mode without understanding the governance contract.
failure_modes.fix: Revert the change via git restore and use the README-based workflow instead.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must locate a specific lesson folder given only the lesson ID from the curriculum metadata.
Learner must explain which file to read first when entering a new module.
Learner must identify the correct README contract for a given lesson type.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's standard library uses a consistent package layout convention (std vs internal, cmd vs pkg) that mirrors this repository's intentional folder structure. Understanding one helps navigate the other.
zero_magic.mental_model: This repository is a structured Go curriculum that builds from first principles to production deployment. Each module depends on previous ones — the graph ensures you have prerequisite knowledge before tackling harder topics.
performance_implications: Reading orientation materials takes 30-60 minutes but saves hours of confusion later.
Following the prescribed order reduces context-switching overhead across lessons.
Using curriculum metadata to navigate is faster than manual folder browsing.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Learners need a clear, repeatable way to navigate a large multi-module curriculum without getting lost or missing prerequisite content.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must find three lessons by their IDs in the curriculum JSON, locate their README files on disk, and explain the relationship between the JSON entry and the folder structure.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Professional developers navigate large monorepos daily knowing how to find code, tests, docs, and config by convention. This skill transfers from this curriculum to any Go codebase.
step_by_step_execution: Open the repository root and read the top-level directory listing.
Read the module README to understand the module's learning goal and prerequisites.
Check next_item_ids in the curriculum JSON to determine the progression.
Navigate to the correct lesson folder and open its README.md.
Follow the lesson instructions to complete the exercises or review content.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The repository uses a convention-over-configuration folder layout where module, lesson, and asset paths are derivable from IDs in the JSON metadata. The curriculum validator script enforces this relationship.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without a standard navigation contract, learners waste time searching for materials, miss prerequisites, and lose confidence. This lesson establishes the map before the journey begins.

proof.expected_artifact: Runnable code, tests where appropriate, and a short explanation.
mastery_checks: Can explain the concept without hand-waving.
Can identify common mistakes.
Can apply the concept in a small working program.
proof.practice_task: Complete a focused exercise that applies How to use this repository.

verification.expected_output: Learner can demonstrate the concept and explain it.

items.title: What zero magic means
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply What zero magic means in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Assuming the instructor will always fill in hidden gaps instead of the materials.
Treating code examples as copy-paste templates rather than studying why they work.
Skipping explanations because 'it just works' feels sufficient in the moment.
execution_timeline: Read a concept explanation and identify the core invariant the code relies on.
Examine the runnable example and trace each line's purpose.
Explain the behavior out loud before running the code to verify.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner uses a pattern from a lesson without understanding the underlying contract.
failure_modes.cause: The learner treated the example as a recipe instead of reasoning about invariants.
failure_modes.fix: Go back to the lesson, identify what invariant the code assumes, and verify it holds in the new context.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner cannot debug code because they assumed a library handles edge cases it does not.
failure_modes.cause: The library documentation was not read fully, and the assumption was treated as truth.
failure_modes.fix: Read the library source or docs explicitly; never assume behavior you have not verified.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify one hidden assumption in a provided code snippet and explain why it could fail.
Learner must rewrite a 'magic' one-liner into explicit step-by-step code without changing behavior.
Learner must explain what invariant a Go interface implicitly guarantees about its implementations.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's design philosophy explicitly rejects hidden magic: imports are explicit, errors are values, goroutines share nothing by communicating, and the zero value is defined for every type. Zero magic is Go's default.
zero_magic.mental_model: A computer program is a sequence of instructions the CPU fetches, decodes, and executes. Every language produces these instructions. Understanding this pipeline removes the magic from compilation and execution.
performance_implications: Reasoning explicitly about code takes 2x longer initially but reduces debugging time by 10x.
Zero-magic code is easier to review, test, and refactor because assumptions are documented.
The upfront cost of explicitness pays down technical debt before it accumulates.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Beginner programmers hit walls when code 'magically' works one day and breaks the next. Zero magic eliminates hidden assumptions so every behavior has a traceable cause.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must take a one-line Go expression that uses implicit behavior, expand it into 5+ explicit lines, and explain what assumption was hidden in the original.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Senior engineers at Google, Docker, and HashiCorp review code by asking 'what invariant does this assume?' Zero magic makes those assumptions visible so teams can reason about correctness together.
step_by_step_execution: Encounter a concept or code pattern that seems to 'just work'.
Ask: what must be true for this to be correct?
Trace the dependency: does the language, library, or runtime guarantee that?
Write down the invariant as a comment or assertion.
Test that the invariant actually holds in edge cases.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Magic in programming is usually a framework or runtime behavior that the developer did not explicitly invoke. Go minimizes magic by requiring explicit imports, explicit error handling, and explicit goroutine communication. When something 'just works' in Go, it is because the language spec guarantees it, not because a framework guessed your intent.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Every hidden assumption is a future bug. Zero magic is a discipline for making assumptions visible so you can reason about, test, and trust your code.

items.title: How lessons, exercises, projects, and checkpoints work
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply How lessons, exercises, projects, and checkpoints work in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Treating every lesson as a reading exercise and skipping the practical project.
Rushing through exercises without understanding the feedback from test output.
Confusing the checkpoint review process with formal grading.
execution_timeline: Read the lesson README and study the concept explanation.
Open the starter folder and attempt the exercise before looking at the solution.
Run tests or verification commands to check your work.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner reads the solution before attempting the exercise and does not build debugging skills.
failure_modes.cause: The learner treated exercises as examples to read rather than problems to solve.
failure_modes.fix: Reset the exercise by deleting the working file, retry from the starter, and check the solution only after a genuine attempt.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner skips the project because it looks intimidating and falls behind on portfolio output.
failure_modes.cause: The learner did not understand that projects are the primary proof of job readiness.
failure_modes.fix: Start the project incrementally: complete one milestone, get checkpoint feedback, then proceed.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain the difference between a lesson, an exercise, a project, and a checkpoint.
Learner must describe the correct workflow for starting and completing an exercise.
Learner must identify which artifacts are portfolio-proof and which are skill-building.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go testing conventions (Test functions, table-driven tests, test coverage, benchmarks) mirror the exercise-checkpoint cycle: write code, verify it, measure it, iterate.
zero_magic.mental_model: Source code becomes an executable through the compiler, assembler, and linker. Go's toolchain handles all three with a single build command. The result is a native binary with no virtual machine.
performance_implications: Exercises with immediate test feedback accelerate learning by 40 percent compared to passive reading.
Projects take 3-5x longer than exercises but produce portfolio artifacts that demonstrate capability.
Checkpoints prevent wasted effort by catching misunderstandings early.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Learners need structured progression from understanding a concept to proving they can apply it in a realistic context, with feedback at each stage.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must complete one lesson cycle (read, exercise, pass tests), then describe which artifact they would add to their portfolio and why.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: The lesson-exercise-project-checkpoint structure mirrors industry onboarding: read docs, complete a ticket, ship a feature, get a code review. Companies like Google and Stripe use similar progression in their onboarding programs.
step_by_step_execution: Start with the lesson README to learn the concept and see examples.
Open the _starter directory and implement the exercise without looking at _solution.
Run the test or verify command to check correctness.
If stuck for more than 15 minutes, review the concept section again before peeking at the solution.
For checkpoints, submit your work-in-progress for feedback before moving to the next milestone.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The starter/solution pattern works because Go's module system treats each lesson directory as its own package. The _starter directory contains intentionally incomplete code with marker comments (TODO or FIXME) that learners replace with their implementation.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Reading code builds familiarity; writing code builds skill; submitting code builds proof. Separating these into distinct artifact types gives learners clear goals and employers clear evidence.

items.title: How to run code in this repository
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply How to run code in this repository in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Running go run from the wrong directory and getting 'package is not in GOROOT' errors.
Forgetting to run go mod tidy after adding dependencies.
Running go test without the -v flag and missing detailed failure output.
execution_timeline: Open a terminal and navigate to the lesson folder.
Run go run . to execute a single-file program.
Run go test ./... to run all tests in the lesson package.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner runs go run main.go but the file is in a subdirectory and the import path is wrong.
failure_modes.cause: go run main.go only works for single-file main packages in the current directory; multi-file packages need go run .
failure_modes.fix: Use go run . or go run .\path	o\package for multi-file packages.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner runs go build but expects a binary to appear when the package is not main.
failure_modes.cause: Only main packages produce executables; library packages compile but produce no binary.
failure_modes.fix: Use go build -o output.exe . for main packages, or go install for module binaries.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain the difference between go run, go build, and go install.
Learner must run the correct command to execute tests in a specific subdirectory.
Learner must diagnose a 'package not found' error from a missing go.mod.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's toolchain is designed around running code with zero configuration: go run, go test, go fmt, go vet, and go build are the five commands that cover 90 percent of daily development workflow.
zero_magic.mental_model: Memory is a linear byte array addressed by the CPU. The stack is fast and scoped; the heap is flexible but requires garbage collection. Virtual memory gives each process its own private address space.
performance_implications: go run rebuilds from scratch every time; use go test -count=1 to avoid caching issues during development.
go build uses cached compilations for fast iteration; use go clean -cache to force a full rebuild.
The Go compiler is fast enough that rebuild times rarely exceed 2 seconds for lesson-sized projects.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers waste time on toolchain confusion instead of learning the language. Knowing the five essential commands removes the configuration barrier.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a small Go program, run it with go run, write a test for it, run the test with go test -v, and run go vet without errors.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go services are built and tested with the same commands. CI pipelines run go test ./... and go vet ./... on every pull request. Mastery of these commands is table stakes for professional Go development.
step_by_step_execution: Navigate to the lesson directory containing the go.mod and .go files.
Run go run . to compile and execute the main package.
Run go test . to run unit tests and verify correctness.
Run go vet . to check for common mistakes like unreachable code or misused strings.
Run go build . to compile without executing, then run the resulting binary from the command line.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: go run compiles the package into a temporary directory, executes the binary, and cleans up. go test compiles test files alongside the package, runs each Test function in a subprocess, and reports results via the testing framework's protocol buffer output.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Reading about Go is not the same as running Go. The fastest way to internalize a concept is to write, run, break, and fix code. This lesson removes the friction of getting started.

items.title: How starter folders work
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply How starter folders work in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Writing solution code directly in the starter folder and mixing working and broken copies.
Deleting the starter folder and having no clean state to retry the exercise.
Modifying the starter template markers (TODO comments) instead of replacing them with implementation.
execution_timeline: Open the _starter folder and inspect all files to understand the expected inputs and outputs.
Identify the TODO or FIXME markers that indicate where implementation is needed.
Write your solution, keeping the existing function signatures and types unchanged.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner writes code in the _starter folder that does not compile because they changed a function signature.
failure_modes.cause: The starter intentionally provides correct type signatures; changing them breaks the test harness.
failure_modes.fix: Restore the original signature from the test file or revert to the starter template.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner copies the solution into the starter without understanding why it works.
failure_modes.cause: The solution is treated as a shortcut rather than a reference for self-assessment.
failure_modes.fix: Delete the copied solution, retry from the starter, and use the solution only to check your reasoning after your code passes.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify which files in the starter are part of the test harness and which are for implementation.
Learner must explain why modifying the test file defeats the purpose of the exercise.
Learner must describe when it is appropriate to look at the solution folder.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's convention of separating public API (exported identifiers) from implementation (unexported) mirrors the starter/solution pattern: the starter defines the contract (function signatures), and the learner fills in the implementation.
zero_magic.mental_model: The terminal is a text interface to the OS for navigating filesystems, running programs, and chaining commands. Terminal confidence means you can diagnose issues and automate workflows without leaving the keyboard.
performance_implications: Starting from a well-designed starter template reduces setup time by 5-10 minutes per exercise.
Comparing your solution to the provided one after passing tests reveals alternative techniques and deeper understanding.
Having a clean starter to retry from is essential for spaced repetition learning.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Blank-page paralysis prevents learners from starting. The starter folder removes the 'where do I begin?' barrier by providing compilable scaffolding with clearly marked gaps.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must complete an exercise from the starter, pass all tests, then explain one design choice where their solution differs from the provided solution and why both are valid.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Professional Go codebases use similar patterns: interface definitions (starter) with multiple implementations (solutions), and test doubles that verify behavior against a contract. The starter/skill pattern teaches this real-world workflow.
step_by_step_execution: Copy or navigate to the _starter directory for the current exercise.
Read all files in the starter to understand the problem, test expectations, and TODO markers.
Implement the missing logic in the designated files without changing exported signatures.
Run go test -v from the starter directory and fix any failures.
Once all tests pass, optionally open the _solution directory to compare approaches.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Starter and solution directories are both valid Go packages with identical public interfaces but different internal implementations. Tests compile against the public API, so they work identically against both the starter (incomplete) and solution (complete) versions.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Learning programming requires writing code, not just reading it. Starter folders reduce the activation energy for writing code by providing structure, so learners spend time on the concept rather than on boilerplate.

items.title: How assessments work
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply How assessments work in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Treating assessment questions as yes/no memorization instead of reasoning exercises.
Skipping the self-assessment and moving on without verifying understanding.
Assuming passing multiple-choice questions equals mastery of the topic.
execution_timeline: Complete the lesson or module content and all exercises.
Open the assessment for the module and read each question carefully.
Answer each question with explanation not just the final answer.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner guesses an answer without reasoning and fails to identify the knowledge gap.
failure_modes.cause: The assessment was treated as a test to pass rather than a diagnostic tool.
failure_modes.fix: For every question, write down the reasoning even if you are unsure; the reasoning reveals what you actually understand.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner passes the module assessment but cannot apply the concept in a project.
failure_modes.cause: The assessment measures recognition, not application; true mastery requires project-level proof.
failure_modes.fix: After passing the assessment, immediately apply the concept in the module project to bridge recognition to application.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must complete a module assessment and correctly explain why each wrong answer is wrong.
Learner must describe the difference between recognition-level and application-level mastery.
Learner must create a personal retry plan after identifying a knowledge gap from an assessment.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's testing framework (go test) functions as a continuous assessment system for code. Table-driven tests in particular let you assess multiple input/output pairs against a single function, mirroring how module assessments test multiple facets of a concept.
zero_magic.mental_model: An OS runs many processes by multiplexing CPU time. Each process has its own address space, file descriptors, and state. The kernel schedules, manages memory, and mediates hardware access. Isolation means one crash does not bring down the whole machine.
performance_implications: Taking a 15-minute assessment after each lesson improves long-term retention by 50 percent compared to no assessment.
Spaced repetition of assessment topics requires 30 minutes per week and cuts relearning time by 70 percent.
Writing explanations for answers doubles learning per minute compared to multiple-choice-only responses.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Learners often mistake familiarity for understanding. Assessments reveal the gap between 'I have seen this before' and 'I can explain this correctly.'
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must take a module assessment, identify the question they were least confident about, return to the relevant lesson, and write a one-paragraph explanation that fills the gap.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Technical interviews at companies like Google and Stripe use the same pattern: a question tests a concept, your answer reveals your depth, and the follow-up discussion diagnoses your reasoning process.
step_by_step_execution: Finish the module content and all exercises.
Open the assessment in the module's assets or curriculum assessment file.
Read each question and write down your answer with reasoning.
After answering all questions, check against the answer key.
For each incorrect or uncertain answer, revisit the relevant lesson and note what you missed.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Assessments in this curriculum follow a multi-facet model: each question targets a specific combination of concept, context, and cognitive level (recall, apply, evaluate). This structure makes gaps diagnosable at the facet level.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without assessment, learners inflate their self-assessment. Objective, structured questions expose knowledge gaps before they cause failure in projects or interviews.

items.title: How to ask good debugging questions
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply How to ask good debugging questions in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Asking 'why does my code not work?' without providing error messages or expected output.
Dumping 200 lines of code and asking someone to 'fix it' without isolating the problem area.
Describing what the code should do instead of what it actually does.
execution_timeline: Observe the bug: what did you expect to happen and what actually happened?
Isolate the smallest reproducing example by removing unrelated code.
Read the error message carefully it usually tells you the exact line and type of failure.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner spends 2 hours debugging a bug that a 30-second rubber-duck explanation would have solved.
failure_modes.cause: The learner did not verbalize the problem step by step before diving into code changes.
failure_modes.fix: Before changing any code, explain the bug out loud or in writing as if teaching someone else.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner's forum question gets ignored because it lacks error messages and minimal reproduction steps.
failure_modes.cause: The question did not provide enough information for anyone to help without asking follow-ups.
failure_modes.fix: Always include: the exact error message, the code that produced it, what you expected, and what you tried already.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must take a vague bug report and rewrite it as a well-structured debugging question.
Learner must identify the key diagnostic information missing from a given question.
Learner must reproduce a bug, isolate the root cause, and explain the fix in one sentence.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's error handling philosophy (errors are values, handle them explicitly) mirrors good debugging practice: do not ignore signals, inspect them, isolate them, and respond to them explicitly.
zero_magic.mental_model: Debugging is hypothesis testing. The error message is your first data point. Form a hypothesis about the cause, design an experiment (add a print, check a value), and let the evidence confirm or refute it.
performance_implications: Writing a clear bug report takes 5 minutes but can save the responder 30 minutes of back-and-forth.
Isolating a minimal reproduction case narrows the root cause by 90 percent before anyone reads your question.
Good debugging questions get answered in minutes; vague ones may never get answered.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Vague bug reports waste everyone's time. A structured debugging methodology turns a frustrating blocker into a solvable puzzle and builds a reputation as a thoughtful engineer.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must take a provided buggy code snippet, write a well-structured debugging question for it, then debug and fix it using the hypothesis-testing methodology.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Senior engineers at top tech companies are evaluated partly by how they ask for help. A well-structured question signals competence and respect for the responder's time, which is why sites like Stack Overflow enforce question quality standards.
step_by_step_execution: Identify what you expect to happen vs what actually happens.
Read the full error message and locate the exact line and column.
Remove code until the bug still reproduces with the smallest possible example.
Check the most common Go-specific pitfalls: missed error returns, nil interface values, range variable capture, and goroutine races.
Write your question with: expected behavior, actual behavior, minimal code, full error output, and what you already investigated.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The Go runtime produces detailed stack traces on panic, including file, line, goroutine ID, and the chain of calls that led to the failure. go vet and staticcheck catch many bugs at compile time. A good debugger uses all three layers: compiler warnings, runtime traces, and logical deduction.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Debugging is the skill that separates junior from senior engineers. Knowing how to ask for help efficiently is force multiplier: it unblocks you faster and respects the time of the people who help you.

items.title: What job-ready means
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply What job-ready means in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Believing job-ready means knowing every Go feature and standard library package.
Focusing on syntax trivia rather than engineering judgment and problem-solving.
Skipping portfolio projects because 'I understand the concept so I do not need to build it.'
execution_timeline: Complete all module projects and ensure they are in a portfolio-ready state.
Practice explaining your design decisions for each project out loud.
Review the job-readiness rubric: code quality, testing, documentation, architecture, delivery.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner has strong coding skills but cannot explain their choices in an interview setting.
failure_modes.cause: All practice was writing code; none was verbalizing design reasoning.
failure_modes.fix: After each project, write and record a 3-minute explanation of your architecture decisions, tradeoffs, and what you would improve.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner has a GitHub with cloned tutorials but no original projects demonstrating engineering judgment.
failure_modes.cause: The learner focused on consuming content instead of producing portfolio artifacts.
failure_modes.fix: Delete cloned tutorial repos and replace them with original projects from this curriculum's portfolio milestones.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must evaluate a sample portfolio against the job-readiness rubric and identify gaps.
Learner must explain which three curriculum projects best demonstrate job readiness and why.
Learner must describe the difference between 'can write Go' and 'can engineer Go systems.'
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's cultural emphasis on simplicity, readability, and maintainability directly maps to what employers look for: not clever code, but code that a team can understand, deploy, and operate confidently.
zero_magic.mental_model: Job-ready means you can take a vague requirement, design a solution, implement it testably, document your decisions, and deliver it through CI to production — independently.
performance_implications: A portfolio with 3 well-documented projects is more impressive than 10 unfinished tutorials.
Employers spend an average of 7 seconds per resume scan; your portfolio link and project descriptions must communicate competence instantly.
Interview performance is 70 percent communication and 30 percent coding; practice verbalizing your reasoning.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Learners cannot tell when they are actually ready for a job vs when they are stuck in a learning loop. Job readiness is defined by demonstrable outputs, not time spent or content consumed.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must audit their own portfolio against a provided rubric, identify their weakest dimension, and write an improvement plan with specific next steps.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Hiring managers at Go shops evaluate candidates on four axes: code quality, system design, collaboration, and delivery. This curriculum maps each module to one of those axes so learners build a balanced skill profile.
step_by_step_execution: Complete a project from the curriculum with all milestones.
Review your project against the job-readiness rubric: is the code clean, tested, documented, and deployable?
Write a README that explains the architecture, tradeoffs, and how to run the project.
Practice explaining the project out loud as if in an interview.
Iterate: improve the weakest area and repeat the self-review until all rubric criteria are met.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The Go engineering hiring rubric at most companies evaluates candidates on a 1-4 scale across coding, design, communication, and testing. The curriculum's proof system (exercises, projects, checkpoints, assessments) maps to this rubric so every completed module produces measurable progress on the hiring scale.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without a clear definition of job-ready, learners stay in tutorial purgatory indefinitely — never feeling ready enough to apply. A concrete rubric makes readiness measurable and the path to it clear.

items.title: How to use the roadmap
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply How to use the roadmap in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Treating the roadmap as a checklist to complete linearly without revisiting earlier modules.
Hopping between modules based on interest rather than prerequisite order.
Ignoring portfolio milestones and focusing only on theory modules.
execution_timeline: Open the curriculum roadmap (path.core.json) and find your current module.
Check the modules prerequisites to ensure you have completed them.
Identify the modules learning goal, portfolio output, and estimated time.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner starts module 08 (HTTP APIs) without completing modules 01-07 and gets overwhelmed.
failure_modes.cause: The learner skipped foundational modules that teach prerequisites like functions, types, and testing.
failure_modes.fix: Return to module 01 and follow the prescribed order. The roadmap is designed to build skills incrementally.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner completes every module but has no portfolio projects to show employers.
failure_modes.cause: The learner skipped modules flagged with portfolio_output: true.
failure_modes.fix: Identify all portfolio_output modules in the roadmap and complete at least three projects before applying for jobs.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify the next three modules they should study given their current module ID.
Learner must explain the difference between required and elective modules using the roadmap.
Learner must locate the portfolio_output flag in the JSON and list all portfolio-producing modules.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's module system (go.mod, semantic import versioning) uses a dependency graph similar to this curriculum's prerequisite chain. Each module declares what it needs (require) and what it provides (export), and the build fails if dependencies are missing.
zero_magic.mental_model: The roadmap is a dependency graph: each module lists its prerequisites (dependencies) and its outputs (portfolio artifacts). Completing a module is like resolving a dependency — once it is done, downstream modules can use it.
performance_implications: Following the prerequisite order reduces concept-switching overhead and prevents relearning the same fundamentals.
The curriculum takes approximately 400-600 hours total; pacing yourself at 15-20 hours per week yields completion in 6-9 months.
Skipping modules to save time costs 3x more time later in debugging and re-learning.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Learners face decision paralysis with a large curriculum: which module next? Is this module required? What do I need to know first? The roadmap codifies the answers.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must trace their path from core-00-01 through the next 10 modules, listing each module's prerequisites and portfolio output, and explain why the order is structured that way.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Engineering teams use roadmap documents (RFCs, project proposals, quarterly OKRs) to communicate what will be built, in what order, and what each phase depends on. Reading and navigating the curriculum roadmap is practice for reading engineering roadmaps at work.
step_by_step_execution: Open curriculum/path.core.json and locate the modules array.
Identify your current module by its id and note its order number.
Check the next_item_ids to see which module comes next.
Verify you have completed all listed prerequisites before moving forward.
If the module has portfolio_output: true, plan to allocate extra time for the project artifact.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The curriculum roadmap is structured as a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where modules are nodes and prerequisites are edges. The topological ordering ensures that every module's prerequisites appear earlier in the sequence. The entry_item_ids and terminal_item_ids define the start and end points within each module.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without a roadmap, learners jump between topics, miss prerequisites, and never finish. The roadmap provides a flight plan that turns a mountain of content into a sequenced, achievable journey.

items.title: How to build a portfolio from this curriculum
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply How to build a portfolio from this curriculum in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Waiting until the end of the curriculum to start building a portfolio.
Treating portfolio projects as homework submissions rather than professional showcases.
Including every exercise instead of curating the best 3-5 projects.
execution_timeline: Identify which modules in the curriculum have portfolio_output set to true.
Complete the first portfolio module and polish the project to portfolio quality.
Add the project to your GitHub profile with a README and architecture diagram.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner has 10 half-finished projects in their GitHub but no polished, documented, deployable project.
failure_modes.cause: Quantity was prioritized over quality; no project was brought to portfolio-readiness.
failure_modes.fix: Pick the best project, finish it completely, write documentation, and remove or archive unfinished projects.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner's project README just says 'my project' with no explanation of architecture, tradeoffs, or how to run it.
failure_modes.cause: The project was treated as a coding exercise, not as a professional portfolio artifact.
failure_modes.fix: Write a README that includes: project description, architecture decisions, setup instructions, test commands, and lessons learned.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must evaluate three GitHub profiles and identify which has the most effective portfolio presentation.
Learner must describe what makes a project portfolio-worthy vs exercise-quality.
Learner must explain how their portfolio demonstrates each of the four hiring axes: code quality, design, communication, delivery.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's emphasis on documentation (go doc, README conventions, example functions) and simplicity (clean project layout, standard build commands) provides a template for what a professional Go portfolio should look like: clean, documented, and immediately runnable.
zero_magic.mental_model: Your portfolio is your engineering resume written in code. Each project is a case study demonstrating that you can design, implement, test, document, and deliver a working system.
performance_implications: A portfolio with 3 excellent projects gets 10x more interview callbacks than one with 15 mediocre projects.
Spending 8 hours polishing a project (README, test coverage, CI badge) increases its impact more than 8 hours of new features.
Interviewers spend 2-3 minutes per portfolio project; the README and architecture diagram must communicate value in that window.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Learners build dozens of projects during study but present none effectively to employers. This lesson teaches curation, presentation, and the skill of packaging work for consumption.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must take a completed curriculum project, write a professional README and architecture diagram, and present the project in a recorded 3-minute walkthrough that explains design decisions and tradeoffs.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Engineers at top companies are evaluated on their ability to communicate technical work. Architecture decision records (ADRs), project READMEs, and design docs are the professional equivalents of a curated portfolio. This skill is equally valuable internally for promotions.
step_by_step_execution: Complete a curriculum project with portfolio_output: true.
Write a README with: project description, architecture decisions, setup, test commands, and lessons learned.
Create an architecture diagram showing components, data flow, and key interfaces.
Push the project to GitHub with a clean commit history.
Practice a 3-minute walkthrough of the project focusing on design choices, tradeoffs, and what you would improve.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: A portfolio project differs from an exercise in three ways: it is complete (handles edge cases), documented (README, comments, architecture), and presentable (clean code, tested, deployable). The curriculum flags portfolio_output modules to ensure learners build these artifacts alongside skill development.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Employers do not hire based on what you have studied; they hire based on what you have built and can explain. A portfolio is the bridge between learning and being hired.

items.title: What is a program?
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply What is a program? in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Thinking a program is just a file you double-click, ignoring the compilation and loading steps.
Confusing source code (human-readable text) with the executable binary (machine code).
Assuming all programs are .exe files — not recognizing scripts, bytecode, or JIT-compiled programs.
execution_timeline: The programmer writes source code in a text editor and saves it to disk.
A compiler or interpreter translates the source into machine instructions the CPU can execute.
The operating system loads the program into memory, resolves dependencies, and transfers control to the entry point.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner writes a Go program that compiles but does nothing when run.
failure_modes.cause: The program has no main function or the main function returns immediately without logic.
failure_modes.fix: Ensure the package is declared 'package main' and contains a func main() that calls the intended logic.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner edits source code but the old behavior persists when they run the program.
failure_modes.cause: They ran the old compiled binary instead of recompiling — the source change was never built.
failure_modes.fix: Always recompile after editing: run 'go build' or 'go run .' to produce a fresh binary.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must distinguish between source code, compiled binary, and running process from a list of descriptions.
Learner must predict what happens when a program with a syntax error is submitted to the compiler.
Learner must explain why deleting the .go source file does not stop a running program.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go compiles source code into a single static binary with no external dependencies. The 'go build' command handles the entire pipeline: lexing, parsing, type-checking, code generation, and linking. Go programs start at package main's func main() — there is no hidden runtime initialization beyond what the Go runtime manages.
zero_magic.mental_model: Installing Go means downloading the distribution, setting GOROOT and GOPATH, and verifying with go version. The toolchain is self-contained — one download includes the compiler, linker, formatter, and package manager.
performance_implications: Interpreted programs (Python, Ruby) start instantly but run 10-50x slower than compiled code.
Compiled programs (Go, C, Rust) have a build step but run at near-native CPU speed.
JIT-compiled programs (Java, JavaScript V8) warm up over time, starting slow then approaching compiled speed.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New programmers conflate source code, executables, and running processes. This confusion makes debugging, compilation errors, and deployment seem like magic. A clear three-part model removes the mystery.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a Go program that prints 'Hello, program!', compile it with 'go build', run the resulting binary, then explain the role of each artifact (source, binary, process).
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every day, developers write code, compile it, and run it. When a build fails, they read compiler errors. When a binary crashes, they inspect the process. Understanding the source-binary-process pipeline is the foundation of all software engineering.
step_by_step_execution: Write source code in a text editor and save it as a .go file.
Run 'go build' — the compiler reads the source, checks types, generates machine code, and writes an executable.
Run the executable — the OS loader reads the binary, maps it into memory, and jumps to the entry point.
The CPU executes instructions sequentially, branching on conditions, looping, and calling functions.
When main returns or os.Exit is called, the process terminates and the OS reclaims its resources.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The Go compiler (gc) translates Go source through several stages: lexing splits text into tokens, parsing builds an AST, type-checking enforces correctness, SSA (static single assignment) optimization rewrites the IR, and code generation emits machine instructions. The linker resolves package imports into a single binary with a predetermined entry point.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Computers execute machine instructions, not human language. The concept of a 'program' bridges the gap — it is the unit of work that the OS understands. Without a clear definition, every compile error, segfault, or deployment failure feels inexplicable.

items.title: Source code, executable, and process
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Source code, executable, and process in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Believing source code and the running program are the same thing — editing source while the program runs does not change behavior.
Thinking the executable contains the source code — compiled binaries contain machine instructions, not human-readable text.
Assuming every running program corresponds to exactly one file on disk — daemons and interpreters blur this line.
execution_timeline: Source code is written as text and stored in a file on disk.
A compiler translates source into an executable file containing machine instructions and static data.
When run, the OS creates a process: a private virtual address space loaded with the executable's instructions.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer modifies source code while a long-running process is active and expects the change to take effect immediately.
failure_modes.cause: The running process loaded the old binary into memory; the new source must be recompiled and the process restarted.
failure_modes.fix: Stop the process, recompile, and start a new process from the updated binary.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer deletes the executable file while the process is still running and wonders why it continues to work.
failure_modes.cause: On Unix, the OS keeps the file's inode alive as long as a process holds it open; the file is only truly freed when the process exits.
failure_modes.fix: Restart the process to load the intended binary, or use a deployment strategy that avoids in-use file conflicts.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify which artifact (source, executable, or process) is affected by a given operation like editing, deleting, or killing.
Learner must explain why a compiled Go binary can run on a machine without Go installed.
Learner must trace the lifecycle of a program from keyboard input to terminal output through all three stages.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's toolchain explicitly separates these three stages: source files (.go) are input, 'go build' produces a standalone executable binary, and 'go run' combines both steps plus execution in one command. Go binaries are statically linked — the executable contains everything needed to run, including the Go runtime.
zero_magic.mental_model: Hello World is the minimal Go program: a package declaration, an import, and a main function calling fmt.Println. go run compiles and executes in one step. Every Go program starts with package main and func main.
performance_implications: Source code is human-readable and compressible; executables are larger (2-20 MB for Go) and optimized for the CPU.
The compilation step adds latency to the edit-run cycle — Go's fast compiler keeps this under 1 second for most projects.
Statically linked binaries are larger but eliminate runtime dependency resolution, making deployment simpler and faster.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Beginners conflate the static file they write with the dynamic process that runs. This causes confusion about edits, compilation errors, and debugging — understanding the separation clarifies the entire development workflow.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must create a Go source file, compile it to a binary, run the binary, then modify the source, recompile, and confirm the change only appears after restarting the process.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production deployments compile code into binaries (or containers), push those artifacts to servers, and restart processes. CI/CD pipelines automate this exact source-to-executable-to-process flow. Understanding each stage is essential for debugging, deployment, and incident response.
step_by_step_execution: Write source code in a text editor and save as main.go.
Run 'go build -o myapp' — the compiler reads main.go and produces a binary named myapp.
Run './myapp' — the OS loader reads the binary, creates a new process, and starts executing instructions.
The process runs until main returns, calling os.Exit to clean up resources.
The OS reclaims the process's memory, closes file descriptors, and removes the process entry from the process table.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: A Go binary uses the ELF (Linux) or PE (Windows) format. The OS loader reads the ELF/PE header, maps segments into virtual memory, resolves dynamic symbols (though Go prefers static linking), and jumps to the runtime entry point (_rt0_amd64_linux on amd64 Linux). The Go runtime then initializes the garbage collector, scheduler, and finally calls the user's main function.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without this distinction, every development action — editing, compiling, running, debugging — becomes muddy. Separating source, executable, and process gives developers precise language to discuss what is happening and why.

items.title: Files, bytes, directories, and paths
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Files, bytes, directories, and paths in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Confusing a file's name with its contents — renaming a .txt to .pdf does not make it a PDF.
Assuming paths like C:\\Users\\name\\file.txt mean the same on every OS — Linux uses forward slashes and no drive letters.
Thinking directories store files hierarchically — on disk, directories are just special files mapping names to inode numbers.
execution_timeline: The filesystem driver receives a path string from a system call like open() or ReadFile().
The driver traverses the directory tree, resolving each path component to an inode number.
When the target inode is found, the OS returns a file descriptor that the program uses to read or write bytes.

failure_modes.scenario: A program fails with 'file not found' even though the file exists in the same folder as the executable.
failure_modes.cause: The program's working directory is different from the executable's directory; relative paths resolve from the working directory, not the binary location.
failure_modes.fix: Use absolute paths or resolve paths relative to the executable location using os.Executable() or filepath.Abs().

failure_modes.scenario: A developer stores sensitive data in a world-readable file and is surprised when other users can read it.
failure_modes.cause: Files have permission bits that control read/write/execute access by owner, group, and others.
failure_modes.fix: Set appropriate permissions (0600 for secrets) and use OS-level access controls.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict whether a given path is absolute or relative and explain what the OS uses as the base for resolution.
Learner must explain why a directory listing shows file sizes in bytes and what a 'byte' actually represents.
Learner must determine whether two paths refer to the same file using inode comparison.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's os and path/filepath packages provide cross-platform filesystem access. os.Open, os.ReadFile, os.WriteFile, and filepath.Join abstract over OS differences. Go's embed package even allows embedding files directly into the binary at compile time.
zero_magic.mental_model: Go compiles to a native binary with no VM. The compiler type-checks, inlines, escapes-analyzes, and optimises before producing a static binary. This gives Go fast startup and simple deployment.
performance_implications: Reading a file byte-by-byte is 100-1000x slower than reading in large buffers due to syscall overhead.
SSDs provide ~500 MB/s sequential reads; HDDs provide ~150 MB/s. Random access is 10-100x slower on HDDs.
Directory traversal for deeply nested paths adds latency — each component requires a separate directory lookup.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Files are the fundamental storage abstraction in every OS. Without understanding how paths resolve, how bytes relate to content, and how directories organize data, every file operation feels fragile and unpredictable.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a Go program that creates a directory, writes a file with specific bytes, reads it back, and confirms the content matches using os and path/filepath packages.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every application reads configuration files, writes logs, processes data files, and manages assets. Understanding files, bytes, directories, and paths is essential for building reliable software that works across platforms.
step_by_step_execution: The OS mounts a filesystem (NTFS, ext4, APFS) that organizes data as a tree of directories and files.
A file is a named sequence of bytes stored in fixed-size blocks on disk, tracked by an inode containing metadata (size, permissions, timestamps).
A directory is a special file that maps names to inode numbers — ls/dir reads this mapping.
A path is a string of directory names separated by separators (slash, backslash) leading to a target file or directory.
When a program opens a path, the OS filesystem driver traverses the tree, resolving each component until it finds the target inode.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: On Linux, ext4 stores files in blocks (default 4 KB). Each file's inode (index node) stores 15 block pointers: 12 direct, 1 indirect, 1 doubly indirect, 1 triply indirect — supporting files up to 16 TB. NTFS uses MFT (Master File Table) entries instead of inodes. All filesystems use B-trees or similar structures for directory lookups.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without a filesystem, every program would need to manage raw disk sectors — an impossible burden. Files, directories, and paths give developers a simple tree-based abstraction that works across programming languages and operating systems.

items.title: Terminal basics
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Terminal basics in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Typing commands without understanding the working directory — running rm -rf * in the wrong directory can be catastrophic.
Confusing the terminal (the window) with the shell (the program running inside it) — they are different layers.
Using relative paths without checking the current directory first, leading to 'file not found' errors.
execution_timeline: The user opens a terminal emulator which starts a shell process (bash, zsh, or PowerShell).
The shell prints a prompt and waits for input — the user types a command and presses Enter.
The shell parses the input, resolves the command (built-in, alias, or PATH lookup), forks a child process, and waits for it to complete.

failure_modes.scenario: A beginner runs a command that modifies files but gets 'permission denied'.
failure_modes.cause: The user does not have write permission on the target directory or file.
failure_modes.fix: Use sudo (on Unix) or run as Administrator (on Windows), or change file permissions with chmod.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner runs a command with spaces in a file path and gets unexpected behavior.
failure_modes.cause: The shell splits arguments on spaces; the path is broken into multiple arguments.
failure_modes.fix: Quote the path: "My Documents/file.txt" or escape spaces: My\ Documents/file.txt.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must navigate to a specific directory using only cd and ls, then confirm the absolute path with pwd.
Learner must predict whether a given command is a shell built-in or an external program.
Learner must explain what happens when Ctrl+C is pressed while a command is running.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go developers use the terminal constantly: 'go run' to test code, 'go test' to verify correctness, 'go build' to produce binaries, 'go mod tidy' to manage dependencies, and 'go vet' to catch bugs. The go command is itself a terminal program with flags, arguments, and exit codes.
zero_magic.mental_model: A development environment pairs an editor with Go support, a terminal, Go tooling, and version control. The integrated terminal runs go build, go test, go fmt, and git without switching contexts.
performance_implications: Starting a new shell process for every command adds ~1-5ms overhead — negligible for interactive use but significant in tight loops.
Piping large datasets between commands avoids writing temp files to disk, reducing I/O by 10-100x.
Using shell built-ins (cd, echo, source) is faster than external commands since no process fork is needed.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New developers face the terminal with anxiety because every error looks like random noise. Understanding what the shell does — parsing, resolving, forking, waiting — transforms the terminal from a scary black box into a predictable tool.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must run three terminal commands, redirect output to a file using >, pipe data between two programs with |, and explain which process owns each step.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Professional developers spend hours daily in the terminal running builds, tailing logs, grepping code, managing git, deploying services, and debugging production. Terminal fluency is the difference between fighting the machine and commanding it.
step_by_step_execution: Open a terminal emulator — the shell process starts with your home directory as the working directory.
Type a command and press Enter — the shell parses the input line into a command name and arguments.
The shell searches PATH for an executable matching the command name and forks a child process.
The child process runs, reading from stdin and writing to stdout/stderr until it exits.
The shell collects the exit code, displays the next prompt, and waits for the next command.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Every terminal command creates at minimum one OS process. The shell uses fork+exec on Unix or CreateProcess on Windows. stdin/stdout/stderr are file descriptors inherited from the parent shell. The shell's job is to orchestrate these processes — piping fd 1 of process A to fd 0 of process B, collecting exit codes, and managing job control (foreground/background).
zero_magic.why_it_exists: The terminal predates GUIs and remains the most powerful interface for software development because it is composable, scriptable, remote-friendly, and consistent across machines. Learning the terminal is learning to speak the computer's native language.

items.title: Environment variables
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Environment variables in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Hard-coding sensitive values like API keys and database passwords in source code instead of using environment variables.
Assuming environment variables are automatically available in all contexts — child processes inherit them, but system services may not.
Modifying environment variables in one terminal and expecting them to persist in other terminals or after reboot.
execution_timeline: When a shell starts, it reads system-wide and user-specific initialization files (~/.bashrc, /etc/profile) to populate environment variables.
When the shell runs a command, it copies its entire environment block into the child process's address space.
The child process reads variables via getenv() (C) or os.Getenv() (Go) and behaves differently based on their values.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer sets an environment variable in a terminal, but the application running in that terminal does not see it.
failure_modes.cause: The variable was set after the application started, or the application was launched in a subshell with a cleared environment.
failure_modes.fix: Set the variable before launching the application, or use 'export VAR=value' and then start the app in the same shell.

failure_modes.scenario: A deployment script fails because a required environment variable is missing in the production environment.
failure_modes.cause: The variable is set in the developer's local shell config but not in the production deployment configuration.
failure_modes.fix: Use a .env file or deployment secrets manager to explicitly declare all required environment variables for each environment.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must set an environment variable in a terminal, run a Go program that reads it with os.Getenv, and confirm the value is passed correctly.
Learner must explain why changes to environment variables in a parent shell do not affect already-running child processes.
Learner must list three common environment variables (PATH, HOME, GOOS) and describe what each controls.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go reads environment variables for toolchain configuration: GOPATH (module cache location), GOROOT (Go installation root), GOOS/GOARCH (cross-compilation target), GOPROXY (module proxy), and GONOSUMCHECK (checksum database bypass). Go programs read config via os.Getenv() or the os.Environ() slice.
zero_magic.mental_model: The Go toolchain includes build, test, fmt, vet, mod, and run. Each tool serves a specific purpose. Learning them replaces the need for complex build systems or task runners.
performance_implications: Reading an environment variable is a fast in-memory lookup (~100ns), not a disk or network operation.
Setting many environment variables (1000+) slightly increases process startup time due to the memory copy at fork.
Environment variables are limited in size — typical max is 32KB on Windows, 2MB on Linux for the entire environment block.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Applications need configuration that differs between development, testing, and production without modifying code. Environment variables provide a universal, language-agnostic mechanism for injecting configuration at runtime.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a Go program that reads a DATABASE_URL environment variable, prints a connection string, and exits with a clear error if the variable is not set.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Environment variables are the standard mechanism for configuring Twelve-Factor Apps — database URLs, API keys, log levels, feature flags, and deployment-specific settings. Docker, Kubernetes, Heroku, and CI/CD systems all use environment variables for configuration injection.
step_by_step_execution: The OS kernel maintains an environment block for each process: a list of KEY=VALUE strings.
When a new process is spawned (fork+exec), the parent's environment block is copied into the child's address space.
The child process reads variables via language-specific APIs — os.Getenv in Go, getenv in C, process.env in Node.
The shell manages environment inheritance: export VAR=value marks a variable for export to child processes.
Shell initialization files (~/.bashrc, /etc/profile) pre-populate the environment at login.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: On Linux, the environment block is stored at the top of the process's stack as an array of null-terminated strings: environ. The execve syscall receives the environment as a parameter. On Windows, each process has an environment block in its PEB (Process Environment Block), inherited from the parent and stored in kernel memory.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without environment variables, configuration would require config files that must be managed across machines, or compile-time constants that require rebuilding. Environment variables provide simple, process-scoped configuration that works everywhere.

items.title: Exit codes
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Exit codes in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Ignoring exit codes entirely and assuming a program succeeded because no error text was printed.
Using exit code 0 for errors and non-zero for success — by convention, 0 means success, non-zero means failure.
Forgetting that a program exiting with code 0 may still have produced incorrect output — exit code only indicates the program ran without internal errors.
execution_timeline: A program completes its work and calls os.Exit(code) or returns from main, triggering process termination.
The OS kernel captures the exit code from the terminating process and stores it in the process table.
The parent process (usually the shell) reads the exit code via waitpid() and makes it available via $? (bash) or $LASTEXITCODE (PowerShell).

failure_modes.scenario: A CI pipeline step fails but the script continues running, masking the failure.
failure_modes.cause: The script did not check the exit code of the failed command and did not use set -e (bash) or $? checks.
failure_modes.fix: Add error checking after each command: 'command || exit 1' in bash, or use set -e to exit on any failure.

failure_modes.scenario: A program always exits with code 0 even when it encounters an unrecoverable error.
failure_modes.cause: The program does not call os.Exit(1) or return a non-zero code on error paths.
failure_modes.fix: Always return a non-zero exit code on error: os.Exit(1) for generic errors, or os.Exit(64) for usage errors.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must write a Go program that exits with code 42 and confirm the exit code using echo $? in bash.
Learner must explain the difference between a panic (non-zero exit + stack trace) and os.Exit (clean exit with code).
Learner must chain two commands with && and || and predict which runs based on exit codes.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go programs use os.Exit(code) for explicit exit codes and panic() for unrecoverable errors (which exits with code 2). The 'go vet' tool reports when os.Exit is called in unexpected places. Go tests use t.FailNow() and t.Fatal() to signal failure with appropriate exit codes.
zero_magic.mental_model: Compiler errors tell you exactly what is wrong and where. Read them from bottom to top: the last line is the root cause. Learning to read errors without panic is the highest-leverage skill for a new Go developer.
performance_implications: Reading an exit code is a zero-cost operation — the kernel already captures it during process termination.
Exit codes beyond 255 are truncated (modulo 256) on Unix — always use 0-255 range.
Exit code 128+N on Unix conventionally indicates termination by signal N (e.g., 130 = SIGINT, 137 = SIGKILL).
zero_magic.problem_solved: Programs need a simple, universal way to signal success or failure to their caller (shell, CI system, or another program). Exit codes provide this with a single integer — no parsing of output text required.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a Go program that accepts a filename argument, attempts to open it, exits with 0 on success and 1 on failure, and demonstrate both cases in the terminal.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: CI/CD pipelines, shell scripts, Makefiles, and container orchestrators all rely on exit codes to determine whether a step succeeded. 'docker run' exits with the container's exit code. 'kubectl apply' uses exit codes for success/failure signaling.
step_by_step_execution: A Go program completes normally — the runtime calls os.Exit(0) automatically when main returns.
If the program encounters an error, it calls os.Exit(1) (or another code) to signal failure.
The OS kernel stores the exit code in the process's exit status field in the process table.
The parent shell retrieves the exit code using waitpid() on Unix or GetExitCodeProcess on Windows.
The shell stores the exit code in $? (bash) or $LASTEXITCODE (PowerShell) for use in conditionals.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: On Linux, the exit syscall (nr 60 on x86_64) takes a single integer argument. The kernel sets the process's exit_code field in task_struct, wakes up the parent waiting on waitid(), and stores the code until the parent collects it with wait4() (zombie state). On Windows, ExitProcess sets the exit code in the EPROCESS block.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without exit codes, scripts and shells would need to parse program output to determine success — fragile, language-dependent, and slow. A single integer provides reliable, universal success/failure signaling.

items.title: How the OS manages processes
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply How the OS manages processes in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Thinking a 'process' is the same as a 'program' or a 'thread' — they are distinct OS abstractions.
Assuming every running program gets its own CPU core — the OS scheduler time-shares cores among all processes.
Believing a terminated process disappears instantly — it leaves a zombie entry until the parent collects its exit code.
execution_timeline: The user or another process requests a new process via fork() on Unix or CreateProcess() on Windows.
The OS allocates a Process Control Block (PCB), virtual address space, and initial thread for the new process.
The scheduler selects a ready process, assigns it a CPU core, and performs a context switch to execute its instructions.

failure_modes.scenario: A server starts many child processes but never calls wait(), creating zombie processes that exhaust the process table.
failure_modes.cause: Child processes that terminate need their exit code collected; otherwise they remain as zombies consuming a PID entry.
failure_modes.fix: Properly handle SIGCHLD signals or call wait()/waitpid() to reap child processes, or use double-fork technique.

failure_modes.scenario: A program spawns too many processes simultaneously, overwhelming the system and causing an Out-of-Memory (OOM) kill.
failure_modes.cause: Each process consumes memory for its address space; the OS has finite resources and may kill processes under memory pressure.
failure_modes.fix: Limit concurrent processes using worker pools, and set resource limits with ulimit or cgroups.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must run ps (or Task Manager) and identify which processes belong to the Go toolchain vs. the OS kernel.
Learner must explain why a CPU-bound process on a single-core machine appears to run 'at the same time' as other processes.
Learner must predict what happens to child processes when the parent process is killed.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's runtime manages goroutines as user-space threads multiplexed onto OS threads. Go programs typically use one OS thread per CPU core (GOMAXPROCS) and schedule thousands of goroutines across them. The os/exec package spawns external processes, and os.FindProcess looks them up by PID.
zero_magic.mental_model: A process is a running program with its own private memory space, file descriptors, and execution state. The OS kernel acts as a traffic cop — allocating CPU time, memory, and I/O resources among all running processes fairly.
performance_implications: Creating a new process is expensive (microseconds to milliseconds) — fork+copy-on-write or CreateProcess requires significant kernel work.
Context switching between processes is slower than between threads because the TLB (translation lookaside buffer) must be flushed.
The OOM killer activates when memory is overcommitted — processes can be killed arbitrarily if they consume too much memory.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Operating systems need to run multiple programs concurrently while keeping them isolated. The process abstraction provides memory isolation, fair CPU scheduling, and resource accounting.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a Go program that spawns a child process via os/exec, waits for it to complete, collects the exit code, and print the parent and child PIDs.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Web servers fork child processes (Apache), containers are isolated process trees (Docker), and shells spawn every command as a child process. Understanding process management is essential for system programming, deployment, and debugging.
step_by_step_execution: The OS boots, initializing the scheduler and the process table (a kernel data structure tracking all processes).
When fork() is called, the kernel copies the parent's PCB, creates a new virtual address space (with copy-on-write optimization), and assigns a new PID.
The scheduler maintains run queues — when a CPU core is free, it dequeues the highest-priority ready process and performs a context switch.
A context switch saves the current process's registers and program counter, then loads the next process's saved state.
When a process calls exit(), the kernel closes its file descriptors, frees its memory, stores the exit code, and transitions it to zombie state until the parent calls wait().
zero_magic.under_the_hood: On Linux, each process has a task_struct containing PID, parent PID, memory map (mm_struct), file descriptor table (files_struct), signal handlers, and scheduling priority. The scheduler on modern Linux (CFS — Completely Fair Scheduler) uses red-black trees to track process runtime and ensures fairness by selecting the process with the least runtime.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without OS-managed processes, every program would need its own CPU scheduler, memory isolation mechanism, and I/O multiplexer — essentially reimplementing the OS. Processes are the fundamental unit of workload isolation.
