items.title: Memory preview: stack vs heap
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Memory preview: stack vs heap in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Thinking 'memory' is a single uniform space — stack and heap have different allocation patterns, lifetimes, and performance characteristics.
Returning a pointer to a stack-allocated variable from a function — in Go this is safe (escape analysis), but in C this causes undefined behavior.
Assuming heap allocation is always bad — small, short-lived allocations on the stack are fast, but heap is necessary for dynamic lifetimes.
execution_timeline: When a function is called, the runtime pushes a stack frame containing local variables, return address, and saved registers.
When a function returns, its stack frame is popped, freeing all its local variables instantly.
When memory must outlive the function call (returned pointers, goroutine sharing), the runtime allocates on the heap via the memory allocator.

failure_modes.scenario: A Go program allocates many small objects in a tight loop, causing high GC pressure and degraded throughput.
failure_modes.cause: Every allocation on the heap adds work for the garbage collector, which must trace and free unreachable objects.
failure_modes.fix: Use object pools (sync.Pool), pre-allocate slices with make, or redesign to reduce allocation frequency.

failure_modes.scenario: A recursive function with no base case causes a stack overflow — the stack memory is exhausted.
failure_modes.cause: Each recursive call pushes a stack frame, and infinite recursion fills the limited stack space (1-8 MB per goroutine in Go).
failure_modes.fix: Add a proper base case, or convert the recursion to iteration (or tail recursion where supported).

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify whether a given variable is stack-allocated or heap-allocated based on its lifetime and usage pattern.
Learner must explain why Go can safely return a pointer to a local variable while C cannot.
Learner must predict the relative performance of stack vs. heap allocation for a given code pattern.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go uses escape analysis at compile time to decide stack vs. heap allocation. If a variable's address does not escape the function, it stays on the stack. If the compiler cannot prove the address is not used after the function returns, it allocates on the heap. Go's garbage collector handles heap reclamation automatically.
zero_magic.mental_model: The stack is a LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) structure for function-local data — like a stack of plates, the last one put on is the first taken off. The heap is a pool of memory for data that needs to live beyond its allocating function — like a storage unit where items can be retrieved in any order.
performance_implications: Stack allocation takes ~1-3 nanoseconds — just moving the stack pointer. Heap allocation takes ~10-100 nanoseconds plus GC overhead.
Frequent heap allocation generates garbage that the GC must collect, causing stop-the-world pauses (typically <1ms in Go).
Stack memory is cache-friendly (spatial locality). Heap memory can fragment and cause cache misses.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Not all data has the same lifetime. Stack memory auto-frees when functions return (fast), but only works for fixed-size, function-scoped data. Heap memory persists until explicitly freed or garbage-collected, at the cost of slower allocation and reclamation.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a Go program with two functions — one that allocates on the stack (local variable) and one that allocates on the heap (returned pointer) — and use 'go build -gcflags=-m' to verify the compiler's escape analysis decisions.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Performance-critical code in Go (network proxies, databases, game servers) minimizes heap allocations by designing data structures that stay on the stack or use object pools. Understanding stack vs. heap is essential for writing efficient Go programs.
step_by_step_execution: A function is called — the runtime pushes a stack frame onto the goroutine's stack (growing if needed).
Local variables are allocated by decrementing the stack pointer — no malloc, no GC tracking.
If the variable's address is returned or stored in a globally reachable location, escape analysis marks it for heap allocation.
Heap allocation calls Go's memory allocator (tcmalloc-inspired), which finds a free span in the heap arena.
When the heap object is no longer reachable, the garbage collector marks and sweeps it in a later GC cycle.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's stack grows dynamically (starting at 2 KB, growing up to 1 GB on 64-bit systems). Stack frames are copied on growth via stack copying — the GC updates all pointers in the old stack to point to the new location. Go's heap uses a page-based allocator with three tiers: page heap (large allocations), central free lists (medium), and per-P caches (small, fast).
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Memory is finite and must be reused. Stack vs. heap is the fundamental tradeoff between allocation speed and lifetime flexibility. Understanding this tradeoff is essential for writing correct, efficient programs in any language.

items.title: Git mental model
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Git mental model in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Thinking Git stores versions of files as deltas — Git stores full snapshots, not diffs.
Believing git commit saves changes to a remote server — commits are local until git push.
Confusing the working directory, the staging area (index), and the commit history as all the same thing.
execution_timeline: Git initializes a repository by creating a .git directory containing the object database, refs, and index.
When changes are staged (git add), Git hashes the current file content and stores it as a blob object.
When changes are committed (git commit), Git creates a tree object (directory snapshot) and a commit object pointing to the parent commit.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer runs git commit but forgets to git add first, and wonders why their changes were not committed.
failure_modes.cause: Git only commits changes that have been staged (added to the index). Unstaged changes are not included.
failure_modes.fix: Use 'git add <file>' to stage changes, or 'git commit -a' to automatically stage tracked files before committing.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer makes changes, commits them, then realizes they committed to the wrong branch.
failure_modes.cause: Git commits are attached to the current branch HEAD. The developer did not switch to the intended branch first.
failure_modes.fix: Use 'git log --oneline -1' to find the commit hash, then 'git cherry-pick <hash>' on the correct branch and 'git reset HEAD~1' on the wrong branch.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain the difference between the working directory, the staging area, and the commit history.
Learner must identify what happens to committed data when a branch is deleted.
Learner must explain why Git can reconstruct any version of any file from its object database.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go projects depend on Git for version control and module distribution. 'go mod download' fetches modules from Git repositories. 'go get' resolves package paths to Git remote URLs. The Go ecosystem on GitHub relies entirely on Git for collaboration, code review, and release management.
zero_magic.mental_model: Git is a content-addressable filesystem where every object is identified by its SHA-1 hash. A commit is a snapshot of the entire repository at a point in time, linked to its parent(s) in a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG). Branches are just movable pointers to commits.
performance_implications: Git operations are fast because they are local — no network needed for commit, diff, log, or branch operations.
Git's object store uses zlib compression, reducing disk usage by 30-70% compared to storing raw files.
Large binary files bloat Git history — Git is optimized for text files that diff well.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Developers need to track changes, collaborate on code, experiment safely, and revert mistakes. Git provides a distributed, offline-capable version control system that solves all these problems with a unified data model.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must initialize a new Git repository, create three commits, run 'git log --oneline --graph' and explain the commit graph, then use 'git diff' to show changes between two commits.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Git is the de facto standard for version control in the software industry. Every open-source project, every enterprise codebase, every CI/CD pipeline, and every deployment system integrates with Git. GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket host millions of repositories.
step_by_step_execution: Initialize a Git repository — this creates the .git directory with objects/, refs/, HEAD, and config files.
Create or modify files in the working directory — Git detects changes by comparing current file hashes with the index.
Stage changes with 'git add' — Git computes the SHA-1 hash of each file, stores the content as a blob in objects/, and updates the index.
Commit with 'git commit' — Git creates a tree object (listing blobs and subtrees), a commit object (linking to tree, parent, author, message), and advances the current branch pointer.
Push with 'git push' — Git uploads new objects to the remote, and the remote updates its refs to match.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Git's object database contains four types: blob (file content), tree (directory listing with mode/type/hash/name), commit (tree hash, parent hashes, author, committer, message), and tag (annotated tag pointing to a commit). Packfiles compress multiple objects together with delta encoding for efficient storage and transfer.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without version control, teams lose history, overwrite each other's changes, and cannot safely experiment. Git's distributed model, content-addressable storage, and branching model make it the most powerful and resilient version control system ever built.

items.title: Git basics: status, add, commit
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Git basics: status, add, commit in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Running git add . blindly without checking what files are being staged, accidentally committing build artifacts or secrets.
Writing meaningless commit messages like 'fix' or 'update' that provide no context for future developers.
Committing large binary files or dependency directories (node_modules, vendor) that bloat the repository permanently.
execution_timeline: git status shows the current state: staged changes, unstaged modifications, and untracked files.
git add <file> stages the current content of the file into the index (staging area).
git commit creates a new commit from the staged snapshot, advances the current branch, and opens an editor for the message.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer runs 'git commit' without configuring user.name and user.email and gets an error.
failure_modes.cause: Git requires identity information for every commit; it reads from git config user.name and user.email.
failure_modes.fix: Set global config: 'git config --global user.name "Your Name"' and 'git config --global user.email "you@example.com"'.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer stages a file, edits it again, then commits — the second edit is not included in the commit.
failure_modes.cause: git add captures the file's content at the moment of staging; subsequent edits are unstaged modifications.
failure_modes.fix: Always run 'git status' before committing, and re-stage changed files or use 'git commit -a' for tracked files.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must use git status to identify which changes are staged, unstaged, and untracked in a given scenario.
Learner must write a meaningful commit message following the conventional commit format (type: description).
Learner must use git diff --staged to review what will be committed before running git commit.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go developers use Git for every project: 'git init' creates a module, 'git add' and 'git commit' save work, 'git push' publishes code. Go's 'go mod vendor' creates a vendor directory that should be committed. GitHub Actions CI runs tests on every git push.
zero_magic.mental_model: The Git workflow is three baskets: the working directory (your current edits), the staging area (what you plan to commit), and the commit history (what has been saved). Changes flow left to right: working → staged → committed.
performance_implications: git add and git commit are local operations — no network, no latency. A typical commit takes under 100ms.
Large repositories (100k+ files) slow down git status — use .gitignore to exclude build artifacts and dependencies.
Git's index file scales with the number of tracked files — performance degrades linearly.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Developers need a repeatable workflow to save work-in-progress, review changes before committing, and build a clean history. The status-add-commit cycle provides a safe, structured way to record progress.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must create a repository, modify a file, stage it, make another modification, use git diff to see unstaged changes, re-stage, commit with a proper message, and confirm the commit with git log.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: The status-add-commit cycle is performed dozens of times daily by every professional developer. It is the fundamental rhythm of Git-based development, used in every codebase from small personal projects to monorepos at Google and Microsoft.
step_by_step_execution: Run 'git status' to see the current state — modified tracked files, staged files, and untracked files are listed separately.
Run 'git add <file>' or 'git add .' to stage changes — Git hashes file contents and updates the index.
Run 'git diff --staged' to review exactly what will be included in the next commit.
Run 'git commit -m "type: description"' to create a commit from the staged snapshot.
Run 'git log --oneline' to verify the commit appears at the top of the history.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The Git index (staging area) is a binary file at .git/index. It contains a sorted list of paths with their metadata (mode, SHA-1 hash, stage number). git add updates the hash in the index. git commit reads the index, builds a tree object from it, creates a commit object pointing to that tree, and writes the commit hash into the current branch ref.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without staging, every file change would be automatically included in the next commit — making partial commits, code review, and selective history impossible. The status-add-commit workflow gives developers fine-grained control over what enters the permanent record.

items.title: Branching and merging
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Branching and merging in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Working on the main branch for all changes and never using branches for features or bug fixes.
Creating long-lived feature branches that diverge significantly from main, leading to painful merge conflicts.
Deleting a branch without merging or rebasing it first, losing all commits that existed only on that branch.
execution_timeline: A branch is created from a starting commit — the branch pointer initially points to that same commit.
New commits on the branch advance the branch pointer forward while other branches remain at their positions.
When merged, Git finds the merge base (the common ancestor), applies the changes, and creates a merge commit with two parents.

failure_modes.scenario: A merge conflict occurs and the developer resolves it incorrectly, introducing a bug.
failure_modes.cause: Git cannot automatically merge changes that modify the same lines in different ways; it asks the developer to resolve manually.
failure_modes.fix: Use 'git mergetool' or manually edit conflicted files to keep the correct combination of changes, then 'git add' the resolved files.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer force-pushes (git push --force) to a shared branch, overwriting teammates' commits.
failure_modes.cause: Force push replaces the remote branch pointer without checking if new commits exist on the remote.
failure_modes.fix: Use 'git push --force-with-lease' instead, which aborts if the remote has commits you haven't seen.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must create a branch, make commits on it, and merge it into main using both 'git merge' and 'git rebase'.
Learner must resolve a simulated merge conflict correctly.
Learner must explain the difference between a fast-forward merge and a three-way merge.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go projects on GitHub use the standard branching model: main is stable, feature branches contain work-in-progress, and pull requests merge branches after review. Go modules use Git tags (v1.2.3) for release versioning.
zero_magic.mental_model: A branch is just a named pointer to a specific commit. Creating a branch costs nothing — it's just writing 20 bytes (a SHA-1 hash) to a file in .git/refs/heads/. The magic is that each branch can move independently as new commits are added, creating divergent histories.
performance_implications: Branch creation is instant and costs negligible disk space — Git stores commits in the shared object database, not per branch.
Merge conflicts require manual resolution, which can take minutes to hours depending on complexity.
Rebasing rewrites commit history (new hashes for existing changes), which is fast but forces other developers to re-sync.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Teams need to work on multiple features simultaneously without interfering with each other. Branching provides isolation for experimentation, and merging provides a controlled way to integrate completed work.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must create a feature branch, commit two changes, check out main and make a different change, merge the feature branch into main, and explain the resulting commit graph.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Branching strategies (Git Flow, GitHub Flow, Trunk-Based Development) are foundational to team software development. Every PR, every hotfix, every release candidate uses branches and merges. CI/CD pipelines are triggered by pushes to specific branches.
step_by_step_execution: Choose a starting commit (e.g., the latest main) and create a branch: 'git branch feature-x' or 'git checkout -b feature-x'.
Make commits on the branch — the branch pointer advances, main stays at the original commit.
When work is complete, switch to main and merge: 'git checkout main && git merge feature-x'.
Git finds the merge base (common ancestor of main and feature-x) and creates a merge commit combining both histories.
If there are conflicts, Git pauses the merge — resolve conflicts in the affected files, 'git add' them, and 'git merge --continue'.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Git merge performs a three-way merge using the merge base (B), the current branch tip (A), and the incoming branch tip (C). For each file, Git diffs B→A and B→C, then combines both diffs. If both diffs touch the same line differently, Git marks a conflict with <<<<<<<, =======, and >>>>>>> markers. 'git rebase' instead replays C's commits onto A, creating new commit objects with different hashes.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without branching, every developer would directly modify the same codebase, creating chaos. Branches provide parallel workspaces, and merges provide controlled integration — the foundation of collaborative software development.

items.title: GitHub workflow
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply GitHub workflow in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Making changes directly on the main branch instead of creating a feature branch and opening a pull request.
Pushing sensitive data (API keys, passwords) to a public GitHub repository and then deleting it — Git history still contains the secret.
Opening a pull request with a single commit that contains unrelated changes, making review difficult.
execution_timeline: A developer forks or clones a repository, creates a feature branch, and makes changes locally.
The developer pushes the branch to GitHub and opens a pull request against the target branch.
Team members review the PR, leave comments, request changes, and eventually approve and merge.

failure_modes.scenario: A pull request has merge conflicts and GitHub shows 'This branch has conflicts that must be resolved'.
failure_modes.cause: The target branch has changed since the feature branch was created, and Git cannot automatically merge.
failure_modes.fix: Resolve conflicts locally: 'git merge main' on the feature branch, resolve conflicts, commit, and push.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer pushes commits after a PR is approved but before it is merged, bypassing the review.
failure_modes.cause: New commits pushed to a PR branch are automatically included without re-review.
failure_modes.fix: Use branch protection rules requiring stale reviews to be dismissed when new commits are pushed.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify the correct sequence: fork → clone → branch → commit → push → PR → review → merge.
Learner must explain what 'upstream' and 'origin' remote names mean and how they differ.
Learner must describe what happens when a PR is merged via 'Create a merge commit' vs. 'Squash and merge' vs. 'Rebase and merge'.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The Go project itself uses GitHub with pull requests, code review, and CI. The standard library, community packages, and most Go tools follow the same fork-branch-PR-review workflow. GitHub Actions runs Go tests on every PR for most open-source Go projects.
zero_magic.mental_model: GitHub extends Git with a collaborative layer: remotes host branches for sharing, pull requests are proposals for merging branches with discussion attached, and actions automate verification. The workflow is propose-verify-merge.
performance_implications: Pushing to GitHub is network-bound — a typical push of a few commits takes 1-5 seconds.
Large PRs (1000+ lines changed) are slower to review and more likely to have merge conflicts.
GitHub Actions CI adds 1-10 minutes per PR depending on test suite size.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Distributed teams need a standardized way to propose changes, discuss them, verify correctness, and merge safely. GitHub's pull request workflow provides code review, CI automation, and merge management in one interface.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must create a fork of a repository, clone it, create a feature branch, push it, and create a pull request with a descriptive title and body explaining the change.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: GitHub is the largest code hosting platform in the world. Millions of developers use pull requests daily for code review, CI verification, and collaborative development. The PR workflow is the industry standard for team contribution.
step_by_step_execution: Fork the upstream repository on GitHub to create a personal copy under your account.
Clone your fork locally: 'git clone https://github.com/your-username/repo.git'.
Create a feature branch, make changes, commit logically, and push: 'git push origin feature-branch'.
On GitHub, open a pull request from your feature branch to the upstream repository's target branch.
Address review feedback with additional commits, then the maintainer merges the PR.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: A pull request is essentially a request to merge two Git refs. GitHub stores metadata (comments, reviews, status checks) in its database, linked to the PR number. When a merge button is clicked, GitHub performs 'git merge' on the server, or for squash merge, uses 'git merge --squash'. Branch protection rules are enforced server-side before the merge is allowed.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Direct push to shared branches causes chaos. The GitHub workflow formalizes code review, automated testing, and controlled integration — making collaboration scalable from solo developers to thousands of contributors.

items.title: Pull requests and code review
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Pull requests and code review in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Submitting a massive pull request with 50+ files changed and expecting a thorough review.
Taking code review feedback personally — review is about code quality, not personal criticism.
Merging a PR without waiting for CI checks to pass or without any approvals.
execution_timeline: A developer submits a PR with a descriptive title, body explaining the change, and related issue links.
Reviewers examine the diff, leave inline comments suggesting improvements or asking questions.
The author addresses feedback via additional commits, and the reviewers approve once all concerns are resolved.

failure_modes.scenario: A code review cycle takes 5+ days because the PR is too large and reviewers are overwhelmed.
failure_modes.cause: Large PRs are harder to review thoroughly; reviewers procrastinate on reviewing big changes.
failure_modes.fix: Break large changes into smaller, incremental PRs (under 300 lines changed). Each PR should do one thing.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer resolves a reviewer's comment with a force-push, losing the discussion history in the PR.
failure_modes.cause: Force-pushing after rebasing changes commit hashes, and GitHub may lose the connection between comments and code.
failure_modes.fix: Add new commits to address feedback instead of rebasing. If rebasing is necessary, only rebase before review, not after.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must write a PR description that explains what the change does, why it's needed, and how it was tested.
Learner must respond to a simulated code review comment professionally and make the requested fix.
Learner must identify which changes in a diff are related to the PR's purpose and which are incidental formatting changes.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go code review follows strict conventions documented in 'CodeReviewComments' on the Go wiki. Reviewers check for proper error handling, correct use of interfaces, test coverage, and idiomatic Go style. The 'go vet' and 'gofmt' tools automate style and correctness checks.
zero_magic.mental_model: Code review is pair debugging at scale — the author writes the solution, and reviewers validate assumptions, catch blind spots, and ensure consistency. The goal is not to find every bug but to share context, raise the team's collective code quality bar, and spread knowledge.
performance_implications: Each review cycle adds 1-24 hours to delivery time — smaller, focused PRs reduce this latency.
Thorough code review catches 60-70% of defects before they reach production, saving 10x the cost of fixing them later.
Mechanical style issues should be automated (formatters, linters) so reviewers can focus on logic and design.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Writing code alone leads to blind spots — the author knows what the code should do and may not notice edge cases, readability issues, or design flaws. Code review provides a second (and third, fourth) pair of eyes to catch issues early.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must review a provided pull request diff, leave at least three constructive comments, and explain the reasoning behind each comment.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every professional software team uses code review. Google mandates at least one review before any code is merged. Open-source projects like Go, Kubernetes, and VS Code require reviews from maintainers. Code review is the primary quality gate in modern software development.
step_by_step_execution: Open the PR on GitHub — review the Files Changed tab to see the full diff.
Read each changed file starting from the most critical (business logic) to the least (tests, config).
For each change, ask: is this correct? Is it clear? Is it tested? Could it break anything?
Leave inline comments for specific lines, and a summary comment with overall feedback.
The author addresses feedback, and the cycle repeats until approval — then the PR is merged.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: GitHub stores PR reviews as events linked to specific commits. Review comments on the 'Files changed' tab reference specific lines in specific diffs. 'Request changes' blocks merge via branch protection. 'Approve' signals the PR is ready to merge. The merge queue can enforce that status checks pass before allowing the merge button.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: No developer writes perfect code every time. Code review is a cheap, effective quality gate that also spreads domain knowledge across the team, reducing bus factor and onboarding time.

items.title: Web preview: client, server, DNS, and ports
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Web preview: client, server, DNS, and ports in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Thinking 'the internet' is a single entity — the web is one application (HTTP) running on top of the internet (TCP/IP).
Confusing a URL (like https://example.com/path) with the underlying IP address and port.
Believing that typing a domain name directly contacts the server — the browser first resolves DNS to get an IP address.
execution_timeline: A user types a URL in the browser — the browser checks its cache, then queries DNS to resolve the domain to an IP address.
The browser opens a TCP connection to the server at the resolved IP address on port 80 (HTTP) or 443 (HTTPS).
The browser sends an HTTP request, the server processes it and sends back an HTTP response with the requested content.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer hard-codes an IP address in their application configuration, and the service breaks when the server moves.
failure_modes.cause: IP addresses change when servers are redeployed or migrated; DNS names provide a stable address layer.
failure_modes.fix: Always use DNS hostnames instead of IP addresses for service endpoints, and use service discovery for dynamic environments.

failure_modes.scenario: A web application cannot connect to its database server, even though both are running.
failure_modes.cause: The database server is listening on localhost (127.0.0.1) but the web app is trying to connect via the external IP.
failure_modes.fix: Configure the database to listen on 0.0.0.0 or the correct network interface, and use the correct hostname from the web app.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must use 'nslookup' or 'dig' to resolve a domain name to an IP address.
Learner must explain the difference between a domain name, an IP address, and a port number.
Learner must trace the full path of an HTTP request from browser to server and back.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's net package provides DNS resolution (net.LookupHost, net.LookupMX), TCP/UDP connections (net.Dial), and HTTP client/server (net/http). Go's standard HTTP server listens on a port with http.ListenAndServe(\":8080\", handler). The 'net/http/httptest' package creates test servers without real ports.
zero_magic.mental_model: The web is a client-server model: browsers (clients) send requests to servers using the HTTP protocol over TCP connections. DNS translates human-friendly names (google.com) to machine-friendly IP addresses (142.250.80.46). Ports identify which application on the server should handle the request.
performance_implications: DNS lookups add 10-100ms to the first request to a domain — DNS caching (browser, OS, network) reduces this.
TCP connection setup requires a three-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK), adding ~1 RTT (round-trip time).
HTTPS adds one more RTT for TLS handshake — HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 reduce this with connection multiplexing and 0-RTT.
zero_magic.problem_solved: The web would not work without a standard way to locate servers (DNS), connect to them (TCP/IP), and identify which application handles the request (ports). Understanding these layers removes the magic from 'how does the browser load a webpage?'
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a Go program that resolves a domain name to its IP addresses using net.LookupHost, prints them, then opens a TCP connection to the server on port 80 and sends a raw HTTP GET request.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every web request uses DNS, TCP, IP, and ports — from loading a webpage to calling a REST API to connecting to a database. Understanding these fundamentals is essential for debugging network issues, configuring firewalls, and designing distributed systems.
step_by_step_execution: The user types 'https://example.com' into a browser and presses Enter.
The browser checks its DNS cache, then queries a DNS resolver (typically the ISP's or 8.8.8.8) to resolve 'example.com' to an IP address.
The browser opens a TCP connection to that IP address on port 443 (HTTPS) — the three-way handshake establishes the connection.
The browser performs a TLS handshake to encrypt the connection, then sends an HTTP GET request.
The server processes the request, sends an HTTP response with HTML content, and the browser renders the page.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: DNS is a hierarchical distributed database. The root DNS servers delegate to TLD servers (.com, .org), which delegate to authoritative nameservers (e.g., ns1.example.com). The resolver recursively queries these servers until it finds an A (IPv4) or AAAA (IPv6) record. TCP provides reliable, ordered delivery over IP, which is connectionless and best-effort. Ports (16-bit integers, 0-65535) are used by the OS to demultiplex incoming packets to the correct process.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: The internet would be unusable without these abstractions. DNS names are human-readable and stable. IP addresses are routable and unique. Ports enable multiple services on one machine. TCP provides reliability over unreliable IP networks.

items.title: HTTP request and response preview
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply HTTP request and response preview in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Thinking HTTP is the same as 'the internet' — HTTP is one protocol running on top of TCP/IP.
Believing every HTTP response contains HTML — responses can be JSON, XML, images, binary data, or any content type.
Assuming HTTP is always initiated by a client — server-sent events (SSE) and WebSockets enable server-initiated communication.
execution_timeline: A client (browser, curl, Go http.Client) sends an HTTP request consisting of a method, URL, headers, and optional body.
The request travels over TCP/IP to the server, which parses the request and routes it to the appropriate handler.
The server sends an HTTP response containing a status code, headers, and a body back to the client.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer sends a POST request but receives a 404 Not Found error.
failure_modes.cause: The URL path in the request does not match any route registered on the server.
failure_modes.fix: Check the server's route configuration and ensure the request URL matches exactly, including trailing slashes.

failure_modes.scenario: A client receives a 403 Forbidden error when trying to access a resource.
failure_modes.cause: The client does not have the required authentication or authorization to access the resource.
failure_modes.fix: Include proper authentication credentials (API key, JWT token, session cookie) in the request headers.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify the five components of an HTTP request: method, URL, headers, body, and protocol version.
Learner must explain the meaning of HTTP status codes 200, 301, 400, 404, and 500.
Learner must use curl to make a GET request and a POST request, and interpret the response.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's net/http package provides a complete HTTP client and server implementation. http.HandleFunc registers routes, http.Request represents incoming requests, http.ResponseWriter sends responses. The standard library's HTTP server is production-grade — used by Docker, Kubernetes, Prometheus, and many other projects.
zero_magic.mental_model: HTTP is a request-response protocol where a client sends a message (method + path + headers + body) and the server replies with another message (status code + headers + body). It is stateless — each request is independent, with no memory of previous requests.
performance_implications: HTTP/1.1 allows multiple requests per connection (keep-alive), reducing connection overhead from ~1 RTT per request to ~1 RTT per connection.
HTTP/2 multiplexes multiple requests over a single TCP connection, eliminating head-of-line blocking.
Each HTTP request adds 200-500 bytes of header overhead — minimize requests and use caching headers for performance.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Applications need a standard way to request and transfer data over the web. HTTP provides a universal protocol with well-defined methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE), status codes, headers, and content negotiation — understood by every web server, browser, and API client.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a Go program that starts an HTTP server on port 8080 with a single handler, then write a separate Go program that sends a GET request to http://localhost:8080 and prints the response body and status code.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: HTTP is the foundation of the web — every API (REST, GraphQL), every webpage, every image load, and every AJAX request uses HTTP. REST APIs, microservices, cloud services, and serverless functions all communicate over HTTP.
step_by_step_execution: A client creates an HTTP request with a method (GET) a URL (https://api.example.com/users) and optional headers (Authorization: Bearer <token>).
The client opens a TCP connection to the server and sends the request as text following the HTTP protocol format.
The server receives the request, parses it, and routes it to the registered handler for the /users path.
The handler processes the request, generates a response with a status code (200 OK) and a JSON body, and sends it back.
The client receives the response, reads the status code and body, closes the connection (or reuses it for keep-alive), and processes the data.
zero_magic.under_the_hood:
An HTTP/1.1 request looks like: GET /path HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com

. The server reads line by line until the empty line (

), then reads Content-Length bytes for the body. Status codes are three-digit integers: 1xx (informational), 2xx (success), 3xx (redirection), 4xx (client error), 5xx (server error). Content-Type and Content-Length headers determine how the client interprets the response body.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without a standard protocol, every application would invent its own communication format, making interoperability impossible. HTTP provides a universal, text-based, extensible protocol that works across programming languages, operating systems, and network architectures.

items.title: Install and verify Go
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Install and verify Go in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Downloading Go from an unofficial source or package manager and getting an outdated version.
Running go version before adding Go to the system PATH and getting a command-not-found error.
Installing Go but never verifying with go env GOROOT to confirm the toolchain location.
execution_timeline: Download the Go binary distribution from go.dev/dl and verify the checksum.
Run the official installer or extract the tarball to the target directory.
Add GOROOT/bin to the system PATH and confirm with go version.

failure_modes.scenario: Running go version returns the wrong version or a command-not-found error.
failure_modes.cause: GOROOT/bin is not on the system PATH, or a different Go installation shadows the intended one.
failure_modes.fix: Run where go or which go to find the active binary, then correct the PATH order or add GOROOT/bin.

failure_modes.scenario: go env GOROOT returns a directory that does not contain a bin/go executable.
failure_modes.cause: The GOROOT environment variable points to an incomplete or corrupted installation.
failure_modes.fix: Unset GOROOT (Go detects it from the binary location) or reinstall Go to the correct path.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must run go version and go env GOROOT and explain what each outputs.
Learner must intentionally corrupt PATH and restore it, showing they understand how the shell finds executables.
Learner must verify the installed Go matches the version they downloaded using go version.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The Go toolchain finds itself via GOROOT, which is embedded in the binary at build time. go env GOROOT reveals this path, and go version reports the exact build commit and toolchain version used by the compiler, linker, and standard library.
zero_magic.mental_model: Go installation is a self-contained directory tree. The go binary discovers GOROOT relative to its own location. GOPATH defaults to $HOME/go and is where go install places compiled binaries.
performance_implications: A correct installation saves hours of debugging. The download takes about 2 minutes on a typical connection.
Multiple Go versions can coexist; using a version manager (go install golang.org/dl/go1.X.X@latest) avoids PATH conflicts.
The go binary itself is about 15 MB and starts instantly — no heavy toolchain overhead.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Before writing Go code, learners need a working toolchain. This lesson ensures the go command is on PATH, the version is correct, and GOROOT points to a valid installation so subsequent tooling lessons work without errors.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must install Go (if not already installed), run go version, go env GOROOT, and go env GOPATH, then explain what each command tells them about their setup.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every Go developer runs go version to confirm the toolchain before starting a project. CI pipelines use fixed Go versions via go.mod directives. Container images pin Go versions to ensure reproducible builds across environments.
step_by_step_execution: Visit go.dev/dl and download the installer for your operating system.
Run the installer or extract the archive to a permanent location.
Open a new terminal and run go version to verify the installation.
Run go env GOROOT to see where Go is installed.
Run go env GOPATH to see where your workspace binaries will go.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The Go toolchain is a single self-contained directory. The go binary at GOROOT/bin/go embeds the GOROOT path at compile time and uses it to find the standard library source, compiler (compile), linker (link), and other tools. go env resolves all paths dynamically, showing where each component lives.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Go must be installed before any Go code can compile. This lesson removes the upfront friction of toolchain setup so learners can focus on Go fundamentals rather than installation troubleshooting.

items.title: Hello World
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Hello World in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Naming the file something other than main.go or a *_test.go pattern and expecting go run to find it.
Omitting the package main declaration and getting package is not a main package error.
Writing func main with incorrect capitalization or signature and getting a confusing linker error.
execution_timeline: Create a new directory and a file named main.go containing package main and func main().
Call fmt.Println inside main to print output to stdout.
Run go run . from the directory to compile and execute the program.

failure_modes.scenario: Running go run . produces package is not a main package.
failure_modes.cause: The file declares package something instead of package main.
failure_modes.fix: Change the package declaration to package main or run go run . on a different directory.

failure_modes.scenario: Running go run . produces undefined: fmt.
failure_modes.cause: The import fmt line is missing from the source file.
failure_modes.fix: Add import fmt at the top of the file, or use gofmt to insert it automatically.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must write a hello world program from memory without looking at examples.
Learner must modify the program to print a second line and run it again.
Learner must explain why Go requires package main and func main() for an executable.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The Go toolchain requires a main package with a main function as the entry point for executables. go build produces a binary named after the parent directory. The standard library's fmt package provides formatted I/O that mirrors C's printf family but is type-safe.
zero_magic.mental_model: A Go program is a collection of packages. Only the package named main with func main() produces an executable. Other packages are reusable libraries. This separation forces clear boundaries between executable entry points and library code from day one.
performance_implications: Hello World compiles in under one second. The resulting binary is about 1.5 MB due to the statically linked runtime.
The startup time is essentially instant — Go does not need a virtual machine or runtime initialization step.
Using fmt.Println for a single string is fine; for hot paths, consider fmt.Fprintf with a buffered writer.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Every language learner's first milestone is getting a program to compile and run. Hello World validates that the entire toolchain — editor, compiler, linker, and runtime — works end-to-end before tackling more complex concepts.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a hello world program, run it with go run ., modify it to print the current time using time.Now, and confirm the output.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Hello World is the canonical starting point for any Go project. Production Go services begin as a main.go with func main(), then grow into HTTP servers, CLI tools, or worker processes. The same package main convention applies at every scale.
step_by_step_execution: Create a new directory for your first Go program.
Create a file named main.go with package main and import fmt.
Define func main() and call fmt.Println("Hello, World!").
Open a terminal in that directory and run go run .
Verify the output reads Hello, World! and no errors appear.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: go run compiles the main package into a temporary directory, links the Go runtime statically, executes the binary, and discards the build artifacts. The Go linker includes the minimum runtime needed — garbage collector, scheduler, and defer stack — even for a trivial program that only calls fmt.Println.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Hello World is the simplest program that proves the toolchain works. It removes the biggest unknown in learning a new language: whether the computer is set up correctly to compile and run code.

items.title: go run
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply go run in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Running go run main.go when the package spans multiple files and expecting it to compile all of them.
Running go run from a directory that has no .go files and getting an cannot find package error.
Using go run for production deployments instead of go build to produce a standalone binary.
execution_timeline: Navigate to the directory containing the main package.
Run go run . to compile and execute the program.
Pass command-line arguments after the package pattern, e.g. go run . --flag value.

failure_modes.scenario: go run . succeeds but the program exits with no output and no error.
failure_modes.cause: The program compiled and ran but produced no stdout output because func main() was empty or only wrote to stderr.
failure_modes.fix: Check that main() contains a call to fmt.Println or similar output, and that output goes to os.Stdout.

failure_modes.scenario: go run . reports package is not a main package.
failure_modes.cause: The package declaration in the .go file is something other than package main.
failure_modes.fix: Change the package declaration to package main, or use go run ./path/to/main to specify a specific package.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must run a program with command-line arguments using go run . arg1 arg2.
Learner must explain why go run main.go compiles only one file but go run . compiles the whole directory.
Learner must identify whether go run produces a visible binary file on disk.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: go run is the most common command during development. The Go standard library build system uses go run internally for code generation tools (go generate). The playground at go.dev/play mirrors go run behavior in the browser.
zero_magic.mental_model: go run is a convenience shortcut for compile-link-execute-cleanup. It is equivalent to go build -o /tmp/random && /tmp/random && rm /tmp/random but handles the temporary file and cleanup automatically.
performance_implications: go run compiles from scratch every time. For large projects, use go test -count=1 or go build first to leverage cached compilation.
The compiled binary is placed in a temp directory and deleted after execution, so there is no disk pollution.
Startup overhead is the same as a compiled binary — go run does not add runtime latency.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Developers need a fast edit-compile-run loop. go run eliminates the separate build-and-run step, making iteration immediate. It also avoids cluttering the project directory with binary artifacts.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a program that reads os.Args and prints each argument, then run it with go run . hello world and verify the output.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: During development, go run is the primary iteration command. Many Go projects use go run ./cmd/app during development and switch to go build -o bin/app ./cmd/app for production. Short-lived scripts and tools are often run directly with go run.
step_by_step_execution: Navigate to the directory containing your main package.
Run go run . to compile and execute.
Observe that no binary remains in the directory after execution.
Run go build . and notice it produces an executable in the current directory.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: go run resolves the package pattern, compiles it to a temporary directory (os.TempDir()), links the binary, executes it with os.StartProcess, waits for it to complete, then removes the temp binary. If the program panics or crashes, the temp binary is still cleaned up after exit.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Developers need a one-step command to compile and run Go code during learning and prototyping. go run removes the compile-then-run friction so learners can focus on code behavior rather than build mechanics.

items.title: go build
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply go build in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Running go build on a library package and expecting an executable binary file to appear.
Forgetting to set GOOS and GOARCH for cross-compilation and distributing the wrong platform binary.
Running go build without the -o flag and not knowing where the binary was placed.
execution_timeline: Run go build . in the main package directory to produce an executable named after the directory.
Run go build -o bin/app . to specify the output path explicitly.
Use GOOS=linux GOARCH=amd64 go build for cross-compilation.

failure_modes.scenario: go build succeeds but no binary appears in the current directory.
failure_modes.cause: The package is not a main package; library packages compile but produce no executable.
failure_modes.fix: Verify the package declaration is package main, or use go build -o output.exe . to force an output path.

failure_modes.scenario: Cross-compiled binary fails to run on the target machine with exec format error.
failure_modes.cause: GOOS or GOARCH was set incorrectly, producing a binary for the wrong platform.
failure_modes.fix: Run go tool dist list to see valid OS/arch combinations, then rebuild with the correct GOOS/GOARCH.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain the difference between go build output for main vs library packages.
Learner must cross-compile a binary for linux/amd64 and verify its format with file or go tool objdump.
Learner must use -o to place the binary in a specific directory and confirm it exists.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The Go standard library is compiled with go build. Third-party tools are distributed via go install (which is go build plus binary placement). go build -ldflags="-s -w" strips debug info for smaller production binaries.
zero_magic.mental_model: go build is the compile-and-link step without execution. It produces a static binary containing the Go runtime and all dependencies, with no external DLLs or shared libraries required at runtime.
performance_implications: go build uses cached compilation — only changed files are recompiled. Consecutive builds of the same code complete in < 1 second.
The resulting binary is typically 5-20 MB for a small service due to the statically linked runtime.
Use -ldflags="-s -w" to reduce binary size by ~30% by stripping symbol tables and DWARF debug info.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Developers need a deployable artifact. go build produces a standalone executable that can be copied to any machine with the same OS/arch, with no runtime dependencies or interpreter required.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must build a main package with go build, run the binary, cross-compile it for a different OS, and verify the binary exists at the expected path.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production deployments use go build to create the binary that runs in a Docker container or on a server. CI pipelines run go build to verify the code compiles before running tests. Distribution channels use cross-compilation with go build to support multiple platforms.
step_by_step_execution: Navigate to a directory with a main package.
Run go build . to produce an executable.
Run go build -o bin/app . to place the binary in a subdirectory.
Set GOOS=linux GOARCH=amd64 and run go build . to cross-compile.
Verify the output file exists and is executable.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: go build invokes the Go compiler (compile) on each package, then the linker (link) to produce the final binary. The linker statically links the Go runtime, includes the garbage collector, scheduler, and memory allocator, and embeds metadata like build info and module version. The resulting binary is self-contained and position-independent.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Code that is not built cannot be deployed. go build is the gateway between source code and a runnable, distributable artifact. Understanding go build separates development from delivery.

items.title: go test
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply go test in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Naming test files something_test.go but forgetting the _test suffix and wondering why go test finds no tests.
Writing a Test function with the wrong signature (e.g., func TestFoo() instead of func TestFoo(t *testing.T)).
Running go test without -v and then not seeing which specific tests passed or failed.
execution_timeline: Create a file named *_test.go in the same package as the code being tested.
Write a function with signature func TestXxx(t *testing.T) containing test logic.
Run go test -v ./... from the module root to execute all tests verbosely.

failure_modes.scenario: go test reports no test files but the test file exists.
failure_modes.cause: The file does not match the *_test.go pattern, or it is in a different package with build tags that exclude it.
failure_modes.fix: Rename the file to match *_test.go and ensure build constraints (//go:build) do not exclude it from the current platform.

failure_modes.scenario: A test passes locally but fails in CI with a different Go version.
failure_modes.cause: The test relies on behavior that changed between Go versions (e.g., sort algorithm stability or map iteration order).
failure_modes.fix: Pin the Go version in go.mod and run go test with -count=1 to disable caching in CI.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must write a passing test and a failing test, then run go test -v and identify which passed and which failed.
Learner must explain why go test *_test.go fails with syntax errors while go test . succeeds.
Learner must run go test -run TestName to run a single test by pattern.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The Go standard library is tested entirely with go test. The testing package provides benchmarks (BenchmarkXxx), fuzzing (FuzzXxx), and subtests (t.Run). go test -cover reports code coverage. The testing/slogintest and testing/fstest packages provide example implementations for interface testing.
zero_magic.mental_model: go test compiles a special test binary that links the production code with test files. Each Test function is a separate entry point. The testing package provides t.Error/t.Fatal for failures and t.Log for diagnostics. Test files are excluded from production builds.
performance_implications: go test -count=1 disables test caching, forcing a fresh run every time. Without -count, go test caches results when neither code nor tests changed.
go test -short allows conditional test skipping for long-running tests.
go test -bench=. -benchmem runs benchmarks with memory allocation statistics.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Developers need a way to verify code correctness automatically. go test provides a built-in, standardized testing framework without requiring external test runners or assertion libraries.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a simple function, write a test file with a passing and failing test case, run go test -v, and explain the output.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: go test is run on every pull request in Go projects. CI pipelines fail on test failures. Coverage thresholds enforce minimum testing standards. Benchmarks with go test -bench detect performance regressions across commits.
step_by_step_execution: Create a file named main_test.go in the same directory as your main.go.
Add package main and import testing.
Write func TestHello(t *testing.T) that calls your function and uses t.Errorf on unexpected results.
Run go test -v to see the test execute.
Introduce a deliberate bug and re-run to see the failure output.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: go test compiles a virtual package combining the target package and its *_test.go files into a test binary. This binary is executed as a standalone program. Each Test function is discovered via reflection on the test binary's symbol table. The testing framework calls each in sequence, catching panics and reporting failures via the *testing.T argument.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Manual verification does not scale. go test provides a repeatable, automatable way to verify code behavior that integrates directly into development workflow and CI pipelines.

items.title: gofmt
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply gofmt in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Running gofmt without -w and wondering why the file did not change on disk.
Assuming gofmt handles all code style issues including naming conventions and comment placement.
Disabling gofmt in their editor because they disagree with tab-based indentation.
execution_timeline: Write Go source code with inconsistent formatting (tabs vs spaces, alignment, blank lines).
Run gofmt -w file.go to format and write changes in-place.
Verify the file now follows canonical Go formatting conventions.

failure_modes.scenario: Running gofmt produces no output and exits silently, but the file is still misformatted.
failure_modes.cause: The -w (write) flag was omitted, so gofmt only printed the formatted version to stdout instead of modifying the file.
failure_modes.fix: Add the -w flag: gofmt -w file.go. To preview changes without modifying, run gofmt -d file.go.

failure_modes.scenario: gofmt changes the file but introduces a syntax error that did not exist before.
failure_modes.cause: The original file had a syntax error, and gofmt cannot parse it; it will refuse to modify it. A different tool or manual edit must fix the syntax first.
failure_modes.fix: Run go vet or go build first to verify the file compiles, then run gofmt.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must deliberately misformat a Go file (wrong indentation, extra blank lines) and run gofmt -d to see the diff.
Learner must run gofmt -w and confirm the file was rewritten.
Learner must explain why gofmt uses tabs for indentation and spaces for alignment.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The Go standard library is formatted with gofmt before every commit. The gofmt -s flag simplifies unnecessary code (like redundant type declarations). gofmt -r rule applies mechanical refactoring patterns across a codebase.
zero_magic.mental_model: gofmt is an opinionated code formatter with zero configuration. There is no style guide to memorize — gofmt is the style guide. Every Go file in the ecosystem looks the same because gofmt eliminates formatting debates.
performance_implications: gofmt completes in milliseconds for typical files. It does not compile the code — it parses and reformats the AST.
Formatting early and often reduces merge conflicts because diff noise from style changes is eliminated.
Most editors run gofmt on save via gopls, making formatting invisible to the developer.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Teams waste time debating tabs vs spaces, brace placement, and alignment. gofmt eliminates all formatting discussions by enforcing a single, universal format that every Go developer uses.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a deliberately misformatted Go file, run gofmt -d to show the diff, then run gofmt -w to fix it, and verify the file is clean with gofmt -d (no output).
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every Go CI pipeline runs gofmt -l . to check for unformatted files. Code reviews reject PRs with formatting diffs. gofmt is the zero-config formatter that makes all Go code look uniformly readable.
step_by_step_execution: Write a Go file with inconsistent indentation (mix of tabs and spaces) and extra blank lines.
Run gofmt -d file.go to preview the formatting changes as a diff.
Run gofmt -w file.go to apply the formatting changes.
Open the file and confirm the formatting is consistent.
Run gofmt -l . to list all files that are not properly formatted.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: gofmt parses the Go source file into an AST, then walks the AST and prints each node using the standard go/printer formatting rules. The printer enforces tabs for indentation, specific spacing around operators, and consistent brace placement. If the file has syntax errors, gofmt refuses to format and reports the error instead.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Code formatting debates waste time and create noise in code reviews. gofmt eliminates this by providing a single, authoritative format that requires no configuration and produces consistent output across the entire Go ecosystem.

items.title: go vet
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply go vet in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Running go vet only once and ignoring its warnings because the code compiles and runs fine.
Confusing go vet warnings with compiler errors — vet warnings are suspicious patterns, not syntax errors.
Assuming go vet catches all bugs — it only detects specific known patterns, not logic errors or missing tests.
execution_timeline: Write Go code that compiles successfully but contains a suspicious pattern (e.g., unused variable, printf format mismatch).
Run go vet ./... from the module root to analyze all packages.
Read the vet warnings and fix the flagged issues even though the code compiles.

failure_modes.scenario: go vet reports Printf format %d expects int but got string.
failure_modes.cause: A format verb in fmt.Printf or fmt.Sprintf does not match the argument type.
failure_modes.fix: Change the format verb (e.g., %s for string) or convert the argument to the expected type.

failure_modes.scenario: go vet reports unreachable code but the logic seems correct.
failure_modes.cause: A return, panic, or os.Exit before the flagged line makes that line dead code.
failure_modes.fix: Remove the unreachable code or restructure the control flow so every path is reachable.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must write code with a printf format mismatch and observe go vet catching it.
Learner must write an unused variable and observe go vet detecting it.
Learner must explain why go vet warnings are not compiler errors but are still important to fix.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The Go standard library is vetted before every release. go vet is integrated into the go toolchain and runs specific analyzers for printf mismatches, unreachable code, shadowed variables, lock misuses, and test format issues. The golang.org/x/tools repository contains additional analyzers that can be added via go vet -vettool.
zero_magic.mental_model: go vet is a static analysis linter that finds suspicious code patterns — code that compiles but is probably wrong. Unlike gofmt which fixes style, go vet flags likely bugs.
performance_implications: go vet runs after compilation and is fast — typically less than a second per package.
CI pipelines should fail on go vet warnings, preventing suspicious patterns from reaching production.
go vet ./... on a large monorepo runs in parallel across packages and completes in seconds.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Many bugs are not syntax errors — they compile fine but behave unexpectedly. go vet catches common suspicious patterns like printf argument mismatches, unused variables, and dead assignments before they cause runtime failures.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a program with at least two go-vet-detectable issues (e.g., Printf mismatch and unused variable), run go vet ./..., read the warnings, and fix all of them.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: CI pipelines run go vet ./... in parallel with go test. Many organizations enforce zero go vet warnings in production code. go vet -vettool=$(which staticcheck) runs the staticcheck analyzer as a vet pass for deeper analysis.
step_by_step_execution: Write a Go program that has a fmt.Printf with mismatch between %d and a string argument.
Run go vet . and observe the warning.
Add an unused variable declaration and run go vet . again.
Fix both issues and re-run go vet . to confirm zero warnings.
Run go vet ./... from the module root to check all packages.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: go vet compiles each package, then runs a set of built-in analyzers on the compiled AST and type information. Each analyzer implements the go/analysis.Analyzer interface. Popular built-in analyzers include printf (checks format verb/argument matching), shadow (detects shadowed variables), copylocks (detects passing locks by value), and unreachable (detects dead code).
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Compilation success does not mean code correctness. go vet catches a class of bugs that the compiler does not check — suspicious patterns that are valid Go but likely unintended.

items.title: go doc
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply go doc in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Running go doc without arguments and getting confused by the large output of the current package.
Typing go doc fmt.Println when the correct syntax is go doc fmt.Println (capital P in Println matters).
Never writing doc comments because the code feels self-explanatory, then having no go doc output.
execution_timeline: Run go doc fmt to see documentation for the fmt package.
Run go doc fmt.Println to see documentation for a specific function.
Write a doc comment above a function and run go doc to confirm it appears.

failure_modes.scenario: go doc fmt.Println returns no output or doc not found.
failure_modes.cause: The identifier was misspelled or had incorrect capitalization (exported names must start with a capital letter).
failure_modes.fix: Check the exact spelling and capitalization: go doc fmt.Println (capital P, capital L).

failure_modes.scenario: Running go doc on a local function returns no documentation even though comments exist.
failure_modes.cause: The comments are not in the standard doc comment format (they must immediately precede the declaration with no blank lines).
failure_modes.fix: Place the comment directly above the declaration without blank lines between the comment and the func/type/const/var keyword.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must use go doc fmt to find the documentation for the Println function.
Learner must write a doc comment for a function and verify it appears in go doc output.
Learner must explain the difference between a package-level doc comment and a function-level doc comment.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The entire Go standard library is documented via doc comments. go doc extracts and formats these comments. pkg.go.dev renders the same documentation for all publicly available Go packages. The convention is: package comments start with Package <name>, function comments start with the function name.
zero_magic.mental_model: go doc reads doc comments from Go source files and displays them in the terminal. A doc comment is a plain text comment preceding a top-level declaration. The first sentence becomes the short summary; the full comment body is the detailed documentation.
performance_implications: go doc is instant — it reads source files directly without compiling.
Well-documented code is faster to onboard into, reducing the time to understand unfamiliar packages.
go doc requires no internet access; it works fully offline for any installed package.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Developers need quick access to documentation without leaving the terminal or opening a browser. go doc provides instant, offline access to package, type, function, and method documentation from any Go source file.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must use go doc to look up three different standard library functions, then write a doc comment for a custom function and verify it with go doc.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Professional Go developers use go doc constantly during development to check function signatures, available methods, and behavior. go doc is the first line of documentation before consulting web-based references.
step_by_step_execution: Run go doc fmt to view the fmt package documentation.
Run go doc fmt.Println to view Println's documentation.
Write a Go file with a commented function: // Add returns the sum of two integers.
Run go doc . to see your package's documentation.
Run go doc Add to see your function's documentation.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: go doc parses Go source files using the go/parser and go/ast packages. It extracts doc comments attached to top-level declarations. The formatting follows the Godoc format rules: indented lines are code blocks, blank lines separate paragraphs, and the first sentence is extracted as a short summary by tools like pkg.go.dev.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Documentation is useless if it is hard to access. go doc puts documentation at your fingertips without leaving the terminal, encouraging the habit of writing and reading doc comments.

items.title: Editor setup and gopls
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Editor setup and gopls in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Installing a Go extension without independently installing gopls and then getting no language features.
Assuming the editor catches all errors before running go build or go vet.
Disabling the Go extension because gopls startup feels slow, losing all IDE features.
execution_timeline: Install gopls via go install golang.org/x/tools/gopls@latest.
Install the Go extension for your editor (VS Code, Vim, Neovim, Emacs, etc.).
Open a Go file and verify that gopls provides completion, hover docs, and diagnostics.

failure_modes.scenario: The editor shows gopls: no packages found or gopls: package not found for every file.
failure_modes.cause: The module does not have a go.mod file, or gopls cannot find the module root.
failure_modes.fix: Run go mod init <module-name> in the project root, then reload the window.

failure_modes.scenario: gopls reports inconsistent diagnostics between editor and go build.
failure_modes.cause: The gopls version is outdated, or the editor is using a different Go version than the terminal.
failure_modes.fix: Run gopls version to check the version, then go install golang.org/x/tools/gopls@latest to update. Ensure GOROOT and PATH are consistent between editor and terminal.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must verify gopls is installed and running with gopls version.
Learner must open a Go file with a deliberate syntax error and observe the diagnostic in their editor.
Learner must use gopls completion (Ctrl+Space) to complete a function call and explain what happened.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The Go team develops gopls as the official language server. It powers all Go editor integrations. gopls provides completion, hover documentation, go-to-definition, references, rename, formatting (via gofmt), diagnostics, and code actions. The golang.org/x/tools/gopls package is updated alongside Go releases.
zero_magic.mental_model: gopls is the brain behind your Go editor. It reads your Go source files, builds an in-memory model of your codebase using the same analysis tools as go vet and go doc, and responds to editor requests (hover, go-to-def, completion) via the Language Server Protocol (LSP).
performance_implications: gopls indexes the module on first open, which can take 2-10 seconds for large modules.
Incremental updates after the initial index are sub-second. gopls watches file changes and re-indexes only modified files.
Memory usage is typically 100-300 MB for a medium-sized module. This is normal and expected for a language server.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without gopls, Go developers have no IDE features — no autocomplete, no go-to-definition, no inline errors. gopls provides these capabilities and ensures every editor has the same high-quality Go experience.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must install gopls if not already present, open a Go file, use hover to view documentation on fmt.Println, use go-to-definition to navigate to the standard library source, and fix a diagnostic shown by gopls.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every professional Go developer uses gopls daily. VS Code's Go extension, vim-go, and gopls Emacs mode all depend on gopls for the core editing experience. CI does not use gopls, but developers rely on it for real-time feedback.
step_by_step_execution: Verify gopls is installed: gopls version.
If not installed, run: go install golang.org/x/tools/gopls@latest.
Install your editor's Go extension (e.g., VS Code Go, vim-go, eglot for Emacs).
Open a Go file and verify features: hover over fmt.Println, trigger completion with ., go to definition on a function call.
Introduce a syntax error and observe the red squiggly underline with the error message.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: gopls implements the Language Server Protocol (LSP) — a JSON-RPC protocol for editor communication. It parses the module's Go files, resolves imports, builds a type-checked AST, and maintains a cache of symbols and their locations. When you hover over a symbol, gopls looks up its doc comment; when you go-to-definition, it returns the file, line, and column of the declaration.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Editors without language servers are just text editors with syntax highlighting. gopls transforms any LSP-compatible editor into a full-featured Go IDE, providing real-time feedback that accelerates learning and development.
