items.title: Reading compiler errors
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Reading compiler errors in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Panicking when a compiler error appears and trying random changes instead of reading the error message.
Only looking at the error type (e.g., syntax error) and ignoring the line number and column indicator.
Treating all compiler errors as fatal without understanding that Go produces multiple errors in one pass.
execution_timeline: Write Go code with a deliberate mistake (e.g., missing comma, wrong type, undeclared variable).
Run go build or go vet and observe the error output.
Read the file path, line number, column, error code, and message to locate and fix the issue.

failure_modes.scenario: Go reports declared and not used: x on line 5, but the variable x is clearly used on line 6.
failure_modes.cause: In Go, a variable is only considered used if it is read, not just assigned. An assignment without reading the value is unused.
failure_modes.fix: Use the variable in a read context (e.g., fmt.Println(x)), or declare it with _ = x to suppress the error intentionally.

failure_modes.scenario: Go reports cannot use str (type string) as type int in argument to add.
failure_modes.cause: A function expects an int but a string was passed. Go does not perform implicit type conversions.
failure_modes.fix: Convert the value explicitly, e.g., strconv.Atoi(str) to get an int, or change the argument type to match.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must produce three different compiler errors (syntax, type mismatch, unused variable) and explain each message.
Learner must identify the line and column from a Go compiler error message.
Learner must explain the difference between a compile-time error and a run-time panic.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's compiler produces error messages with a consistent format: file:line:col: error message. The Go compiler is designed to produce clear, actionable error messages. Go 1.20+ improved error messages with more context, including suggested fixes for common mistakes.
zero_magic.mental_model: Compiler errors are the Go compiler's way of saying I cannot translate this into machine code. The error message tells you exactly where (file:line:col) and why (error message). The first error is usually the real cause; later errors are often cascading effects.
performance_implications: Go's compiler stops after the first pass but may report multiple errors from that pass.
Fix errors from top to bottom — the first error is often the root cause of subsequent ones.
Reading and understanding the error message is faster than random trial-and-error debugging.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Compile errors are frustrating for beginners who do not know what the messages mean. This lesson teaches learners to read error messages systematically, reducing the time between error and fix.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write code that produces three different compiler errors, identify each error's location and cause from the message alone, and fix all three.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Professional Go developers encounter compile errors daily. The ability to quickly parse file:line:col messages and understand the error type directly impacts development speed. gopls shows these errors in-editor, but the same message format is used in CI logs.
step_by_step_execution: Write code with a deliberate syntax error (e.g., missing closing brace).
Run go build . and read the error: note the file, line number, column, and message.
Fix the syntax error.
Introduce a type mismatch error (passing string to an int parameter).
Run go build . and read the error, then fix it.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's compiler (cmd/compile) parses source into tokens, builds an AST, performs type checking, and generates machine code via SSA (Static Single Assignment) form. Errors are collected during each phase. The error format file:line:col is produced by the compiler's base position information, which tracks source locations through every transformation.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: New Go developers see compile errors as obstacles rather than information. Learning to read compiler errors systematically turns them from frustration into a fast feedback mechanism.

items.title: Reading runtime errors
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Reading runtime errors in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Seeing a panic traceback and closing the terminal without reading any of the stack frames.
Looking only at the panic message (e.g., nil pointer dereference) and ignoring the file and line where it happened.
Assuming a runtime panic means the entire program is broken, rather than fixing the specific nil check or bounds error.
execution_timeline: Write a program that triggers a runtime panic (e.g., nil pointer dereference, index out of range).
Run the program and observe the panic traceback output.
Read the traceback from top to bottom to find the exact line that caused the panic.

failure_modes.scenario: panic: runtime error: index out of range [5] with length 3.
failure_modes.cause: The code tried to access index 5 of a slice or array that only has 3 elements (indices 0, 1, 2).
failure_modes.fix: Check the slice length before accessing, or fix the loop bounds: for i := range slice instead of for i := 0; i <= len(slice); i++.

failure_modes.scenario: panic: runtime error: invalid memory address or nil pointer dereference.
failure_modes.cause: A pointer variable is nil but the code tried to access its field or method without a nil check.
failure_modes.fix: Add a nil check before dereferencing the pointer, or ensure the pointer is initialized before use.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must trigger a nil pointer dereference and identify the exact line from the traceback.
Learner must trigger an index out of range and explain how the traceback reveals the invalid index value.
Learner must recover from a panic using defer and recover and explain when that is appropriate.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go runtime panics include the goroutine stack trace showing every function call from the panic site up to main. The traceback lists file:line for each frame. go tool pprof can profile panics. The runtime/debug package provides Stack() for programmatic traceback access without panicking.
zero_magic.mental_model: A panic traceback is a call stack printed in reverse order. The top of the traceback is the panic site (the line that panicked). Each frame below shows who called whom. Read from top to bottom: the first frame is the bug, the last frame is main.
performance_implications: Printing a traceback is slow (millions of cycles) and should not happen in normal operation.
Panics are intended for truly exceptional conditions — not for regular error handling.
Recovering from a panic in a deferred function is the correct way to prevent crashes in long-running servers (e.g., HTTP handler per-request recovery).
zero_magic.problem_solved: Runtime errors crash programs without warning. Understanding panic tracebacks lets developers find the exact cause quickly instead of guessing where the crash occurred.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a program that panics with index out of range, read the traceback, identify the exact line, fix the bug, and confirm the program runs without panicking.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go services use panic recovery middleware to prevent crashes from taking down the entire server. When a panic does occur, the traceback is logged and analyzed to find the root cause. Runtime error reading is a critical debugging skill.
step_by_step_execution: Write a program that accesses slice[10] on a slice with 3 elements.
Run the program and observe the panic traceback.
Find the file and line number in the traceback.
Fix the out-of-bounds access by checking len(slice) before accessing.
Re-run to confirm the panic is resolved.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: When Go code panics (e.g., nil dereference, bounds check failure), the runtime unwinds the goroutine stack. Each frame's program counter is mapped back to a file:line through the binary's DWARF debug info (or the Go symbol table in stripped binaries). The traceback is printed to stderr, then the program exits with a non-zero status code unless a deferred recover() catches the panic.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Runtime crashes are inevitable in any non-trivial program. Reading panic tracebacks is the primary diagnostic tool for finding where and why a crash happened, making it one of the most important debugging skills.

items.title: Reading test failures
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Reading test failures in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Running go test without -v and wondering why only PASS or FAIL is shown with no details.
Using t.Fatal in a loop and stopping on the first failure instead of reporting all cases.
Ignoring the test name in the output and not knowing which specific test case failed.
execution_timeline: Write a test file with at least one test that will fail.
Run go test -v ./... to see verbose output.
Read the output: the failing test name, the failure message from t.Error/t.Fatal, and any log output.

failure_modes.scenario: go test -v shows a test failed but the error message says something unexpected.
failure_modes.cause: The test logic has a bug (wrong expected value, wrong input), or the production code has a bug.
failure_modes.fix: Read the test's expected vs actual values from the error message, then trace through the logic to find the discrepancy.

failure_modes.scenario: go test shows panic: runtime error: index out of range inside a test.
failure_modes.cause: The test code itself has a bug — usually accessing a test data slice beyond its length.
failure_modes.fix: Look at the traceback printed after the panic to find the exact line in the test file. Fix the test's data setup or access pattern.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must run go test -v on a failing test and explain which line of the error tells them the expected vs actual value.
Learner must use t.Run to create subtables and explain how subtest names appear in the output.
Learner must identify a test failure caused by incorrect setup (not a production code bug) from the error message.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's testing package reports failures with file:line information. t.Error marks the test as failed but continues execution. t.Fatal marks the test as failed and stops immediately. The -v flag prints all test names and log output. go test -json formats output as JSON for CI tooling.
zero_magic.mental_model: A test failure output tells you: which test (TestXxx), where (file:line), what was expected vs what was actual, and any additional log messages. The first failure message is the most actionable — fix it before worrying about cascading failures.
performance_implications: go test -v output can be very long for large test suites — pipe it through less or grep for specific test names.
Use go test -run ^TestName$ to run a single test and reduce noise.
Cascading failures happen when an early test failure leaves state that breaks later tests — fix from top to bottom.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Test output is meaningless if you cannot read it. This lesson teaches learners to parse go test -v output, understand failure messages, and locate the exact line causing a test to fail.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a test that fails, run go test -v, identify the test name, the file:line of the failure, the expected vs actual values, and fix the test or code.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: CI pipelines display go test -v output on every PR. Developers scan the output for FAIL lines, click through to the file:line in their editor, and fix the issue. Reading test output efficiently is a daily skill for every professional Go developer.
step_by_step_execution: Write a function and a test that intentionally asserts the wrong expected value.
Run go test -v and examine the output.
Identify the test name, the file:line of the failure, and the expected vs actual values.
Fix the test assertion (or the function) so the test passes.
Re-run go test -v to confirm the fix.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: go test compiles a test binary that links the testing framework. Each Test function is called with a *testing.T. When t.Error/Errorf is called, the framework records the failure message and file:line (using runtime.Caller). When t.Fatal/Fatalf is called, the framework records the failure and stops executing that test. After all tests complete, the framework prints the summary: number of tests passed, failed, skipped, and total duration.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Tests are only useful when their output tells you what went wrong. This lesson teaches the skill of reading test output to find the root cause of failures quickly.

items.title: Go module root basics
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Go module root basics in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Creating a go.mod with an incorrect module path that does not match the repository or import scheme.
Deleting go.mod thinking it is not needed because the program runs with go run.
Adding a dependency with go get but not running go mod tidy afterward.
execution_timeline: Run go mod init <module-path> in the project root to create go.mod.
Run go mod tidy to synchronize go.mod with the source code's imports.
Run go build or go test to verify the module resolves all dependencies.

failure_modes.scenario: go build reports missing go.sum entry for a dependency.
failure_modes.cause: A dependency was added (via import in code) but go mod tidy was not run to verify the checksum.
failure_modes.fix: Run go mod tidy to download the dependency and update go.sum. Never manually edit go.sum.

failure_modes.scenario: go build reports module declares its path as: example.com/foo but required as: example.com/foo/v2.
failure_modes.cause: The module path in go.mod does not match the import path used in the code, or the module has a major version mismatch.
failure_modes.fix: If you intend v2+, add /v2 to the module path in go.mod. Otherwise, update the import path to match go.mod.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must run go mod init and explain what the module path means.
Learner must add a dependency import, run go mod tidy, and explain what go.sum contains.
Learner must run go mod why -m <dependency> to understand why a dependency is included.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The Go standard library does not use modules internally (it has no go.mod), but every external Go project uses go.mod. The go.mod file defines the module path, Go version, and dependency requirements. go.sum contains cryptographic checksums of all dependency versions for verification.
zero_magic.mental_model: A go.mod file is the identity card of a Go module. It declares the module's import path prefix, the Go version, and the exact dependency versions the module requires. Subdirectories within the module are importable as <module-path>/<subdir>. The module root is wherever go.mod lives.
performance_implications: go mod tidy is fast (milliseconds for local modules) and should be run after every import change.
Keeping go.mod clean reduces download time in CI. Run go mod tidy before committing.
Using a Go module proxy (GOPROXY=proxy.golang.org or your own Athens server) speeds up dependency resolution in CI.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Before Go modules, there was no standard way to declare dependencies or version them. go.mod provides a single, canonical file that defines the module, its dependencies, and their versions, making builds reproducible.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must create a new module with go mod init, write a program that imports a standard library package, add an external dependency, run go mod tidy, explain what go.mod and go.sum contain, and build the program.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every Go project has a go.mod at its root. CI pipelines run go mod tidy -diff to check that go.mod is clean. Dependencies are updated by changing the require directive and running go mod tidy. Go 1.21+ supports toolchain directives in go.mod for automatic Go version management.
step_by_step_execution: Create a new directory for your module.
Run go mod init example.com/my-module to create go.mod.
Write a Go file that imports a standard library package and an external package.
Run go mod tidy to download dependencies and update go.mod and go.sum.
Run go build . to verify everything compiles.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: go mod init creates a go.mod file with the module path and Go version. go mod tidy scans all .go files in the module, resolves imports to their MVS (Minimum Version Selection), downloads missing modules, removes unused dependencies, and updates go.sum with hashes. The module cache lives at GOPATH/pkg/mod. Builds use the module cache without re-downloading.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Reproducible builds require explicit, versioned dependency declarations. go.mod is the foundation of Go's module system, enabling reproducible builds, semantic import versioning, and minimal version selection.

items.title: Tooling checklist
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Tooling checklist in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Checking only go version and assuming everything else is fine.
Running the checklist once and never re-verifying after changing editor or Go version.
Skipping gopls verification because the editor seems to work, then losing IDE features after an update.
execution_timeline: Verify Go installation: go version, go env GOROOT, go env GOPATH.
Verify basic commands: go run, go build, go test, go fmt, go vet, go doc.
Verify editor integration: gopls version, completion, hover diagnostics.

failure_modes.scenario: go version works but go env GOROOT points to a path that does not exist.
failure_modes.cause: GOROOT was set manually via environment variable and overrides the Go binary's built-in GOROOT.
failure_modes.fix: Unset the GOROOT environment variable. Go infers GOROOT from the binary's location automatically.

failure_modes.scenario: gopls reports gopls: no installed Go tools but go version works fine in the terminal.
failure_modes.cause: The editor's PATH or GOROOT is different from the terminal's. The editor may launch with a different shell environment.
failure_modes.fix: Ensure the editor is launched with the correct PATH. In VS Code, configure go.goroot and go.toolsManagement.autoUpdate in settings.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must run a complete tooling checklist: go version, go env, go run, go build, go test, go vet, go doc, and gopls version.
Learner must simulate a broken tool (e.g., broken PATH) and use the checklist to diagnose it.
Learner must explain the purpose of each item on the checklist.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The Go team provides a standard toolchain where every command follows the same pattern. The tooling checklist mirrors what professional Go developers run to verify their environment is working before starting a new project or after a toolchain update.
zero_magic.mental_model: The tooling checklist is a sanity check for your Go development environment.
performance_implications: Running the full checklist takes under 30 seconds and should be done after every Go version upgrade.
A broken tooling environment can waste hours of debugging time. The checklist catches issues early.
Document your checklist result (run it, save output) when setting up a new machine or CI environment.
zero_magic.problem_solved: After completing module 02, the learner has learned 14 separate tooling concepts. The checklist consolidates everything into a single rapid verification procedure they can run anytime they set up a new machine or encounter tooling issues.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must run every command on the tooling checklist, capture the output of each, and explain what each command verifies about their Go development environment.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Professional Go developers run these checks when setting up a new workstation, after updating Go, or when tooling behaves unexpectedly. CI environments run a similar set of checks (go version, go build, go test, go vet) on every pull request.
step_by_step_execution: Run go version and confirm the output matches your installed Go version.
Run go env GOROOT and go env GOPATH and ensure both point to valid directories.
Run go run . on a simple hello world program.
Run go build . and confirm a binary is produced.
Run go test . on a package with tests and confirm tests pass.
Run gofmt -d . and confirm no formatting differences exist.
Run go vet ./... and confirm zero warnings.
Run go doc fmt.Println and confirm documentation is displayed.
Run gopls version and confirm the language server is installed.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: This lesson synthesizes all previous module 02 lessons into a single integrated workflow. Each checklist item corresponds to one lesson: go version (core-02-01), hello world (core-02-02), go run (core-02-03), go build (core-02-04), go test (core-02-05), gofmt (core-02-06), go vet (core-02-07), go doc (core-02-08), gopls (core-02-09), reading errors (core-02-10, core-02-11, core-02-12), and go.mod basics (core-02-13).
zero_magic.why_it_exists: All the tooling knowledge in module 02 is only useful if the learner can verify their environment is working. The tooling checklist is the capstone that ties every lesson together into a repeatable verification procedure.

items.title: Values and expressions
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Values and expressions in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Confusing expression evaluation order - assuming left-to-right when Go evaluates operands as per spec.
Writing 42 + "hello" expecting implicit coercion - Go requires explicit type conversion.
Forgetting untyped constants have a default type that affects valid operations.
execution_timeline: Write a literal or expression with operators - Go parser builds AST, verifies type consistency.
At runtime, the expression evaluates left-to-right per operand with operator precedence rules.
The result value is assigned, returned, or used as an argument - no implicit magic.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer writes x / y with int operands expecting a fractional result.
failure_modes.cause: Integer division truncates toward zero in Go.
failure_modes.fix: Convert at least one operand to float64: float64(x) / float64(y).

failure_modes.scenario: A developer uses a && b || c without parentheses and gets unexpected behavior.
failure_modes.cause: Go precedence puts && higher than ||, so a && b || c is (a && b) || c.
failure_modes.fix: Add explicit parentheses to match intended grouping.

hidden_magic_checks: Predict result of mixed-type expression involving int, float64, and uint conversions.
Fix an expression causing compile error due to operator-type mismatch.
Explain why 1/2 evaluates to 0 and how to get 0.5.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's standard library follows the same expression semantics: io.Copy returns (int64, error), arithmetic on time.Duration uses explicit operands, comparison operators work only on compatible types.
zero_magic.mental_model: An expression is a recipe producing a value. Ingredients (operands) and tools (operators) combine in a precise order. If you know types and operators, you can trace every result.
performance_implications: Untyped constant expressions evaluate at compile time with arbitrary precision.
Short-circuit operators (&&, ||) skip right operand when left determines result.
Expressions with interfaces or string concat may allocate - benchmark hot paths.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New programmers assume expressions 'just work' without understanding precedence, typing, and evaluation order. This lesson makes evaluation explicit.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Write three expressions demonstrating operator precedence, integer vs float division, and short-circuit, then explain each result.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every Go program is a tree of expressions. From arithmetic in CLI tools to boolean logic in authorization, understanding expression evaluation is foundational.
step_by_step_execution: Identify expression and constituent operands and operators.
Check each operand's type - are they the same type? Need conversion?
Apply operator precedence for grouping - use parentheses if unsure.
Evaluate operands left to right per Go spec.
Apply operator to produce result with expected type.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go expressions are parsed into an AST. The type checker validates operator types match. At runtime, operands evaluate left-to-right, then operators apply by precedence. No hidden coercion exists.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Expressions are the smallest unit of computation. Making them explicit removes the first layer of magic from every program.

items.title: Variables
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Variables in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using := at package scope - short var declaration only works inside function bodies.
Shadowing a variable in inner scope expecting the outer value to change.
Declaring var x int then assigning x = "hello" - Go catches type mismatch at compile time.
execution_timeline: Declare using var or := - compiler allocates stack or heap depending on escape analysis.
Variable initializes to zero value if no explicit initializer given.
Assignment writes new value to existing memory location - no re-declaration.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer declares inside if block and tries to use outside.
failure_modes.cause: Go uses block scoping - := inside block is scoped to that block.
failure_modes.fix: Declare before the if with var, then assign inside.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer writes x := 1 inside a loop expecting persistence across iterations.
failure_modes.cause: Short var declaration inside loop creates new variable each iteration.
failure_modes.fix: Declare before the loop with var, then assign inside.

hidden_magic_checks: Trace variable scopes across function, if, for, and switch blocks.
Identify a shadowing bug where an outer variable is accidentally redeclared.
Explain when var is required over := (package scope, zero-value intent, multiple declarations).
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's standard library uses var for package-level config and := for concise locals. net/http declares DefaultTransport as var; handlers use :=.
zero_magic.mental_model: A variable is a named storage location with a fixed type. A labeled box: label = name, box size = type, contents = value. Box cannot change size (type), only contents.
performance_implications: Package-level var allocates at init time - zero overhead for declaration.
Local variables may be stack or heap based on escape analysis - use -gcflags=-m to check.
Wide structs assigned frequently may cause cache pressure - prefer pointers in hot paths.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Variables are the basic building block, yet beginners misunderstand scope, shadowing, and zero values. This lesson establishes precise semantics.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Write a program with package-level var, function-level :=, and a shadowing example, then explain each variable's scope and lifetime.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Variables appear in every Go program - from err in if err != nil to config structs. Solid understanding prevents entire categories of bugs.
step_by_step_execution: Determine required scope - package or local.
Choose var for explicit zero/package scope; := for local init.
Assign initial value if needed - Go sets zero value otherwise.
Use within scope - block-scoped.
Assign new values with =, matching declared type.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: var x int reserves space for an int-sized value. The variable is a named memory address. Go has no uninitialized variables - every declaration produces a valid zero value.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without precise understanding of declaration, assignment, and scope, every program has hidden assumptions about where values live and how long they persist.

items.title: Types
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Types in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Confusing int and int64 - same size on 64-bit but compiler treats as distinct types.
Assuming type alias allows mixing in operations without explicit conversion.
Believing zero value of every type is nil - struct zero is all-zero fields, bool is false.
execution_timeline: Define type with type keyword or use built-in - compiler registers name, size, method set.
Values created via literals, conversion, or zero-value initialization.
Type checker enforces operations only between compatible types - mismatches cause compile errors.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer writes type Celsius float64 then tries Celsius(100) + float64(32).
failure_modes.cause: Celsius is a named type distinct from float64.
failure_modes.fix: Explicitly convert: float64(Celsius(100)) + 32.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer assigns string to interface{} then calls .Len() on it.
failure_modes.cause: interface{} has no methods - type assertion needed.
failure_modes.fix: Use s.(string) to retrieve underlying string value.

hidden_magic_checks: Distinguish named types, defined types, and interface types.
Explain why [3]int and [4]int are different types.
Use type assertion to extract concrete value from interface{}.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go stdlib defines http.Handler, io.Reader, error as interfaces. os.File implements multiple interfaces. Every type describes the shape and behavior of values.
zero_magic.mental_model: A type is a blueprint specifying: (1) valid values, (2) permitted operations, (3) memory layout. int describes a number you can add; struct describes grouped fields; interface describes method sets.
performance_implications: Small types (bool, byte, int) pass in registers - zero allocation.
Interface values are two words (type + data pointer) - avoid in hot paths when concrete type known.
Named types have zero runtime overhead - compile-time concept only.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Types are everywhere, but beginners treat them as decoration rather than the correctness contract. This lesson makes the type system explicit.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Define a named type, attempt illegal operation with underlying type, fix with explicit conversion, explaining the compiler error.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Type safety is Go's primary guarantee. JSON unmarshaling, DB scanning, API parsing all rely on types. Understanding types means knowing what the compiler guarantees before code runs.
step_by_step_execution: Choose built-in type or define custom with type.
For structured data, use struct with typed fields.
For behavior, define interface with method signatures.
Create values via literals, conversion, or var.
Let compiler verify all operations are type-legal.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Every Go type has a fixed compile-time size. Type descriptors store name, size, alignment, method set. Interface values carry concrete type pointer + data pointer. Type system is nominal, not structural.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without explicit types, every value is a blob of bytes and every operation is a gamble. Types make data shape and behavior explicit.

items.title: Zero values
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Zero values in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Assuming pointers default to valid address instead of nil - dereferencing nil panics.
Thinking struct zero values are nil - struct zero has all fields at zero, valid and usable.
Forgetting map, chan, slice zero values are nil and cannot be written without init.
execution_timeline: Declare var x T without initializer.
Go assigns zero value: 0 for numeric, false for bool, "" for string, nil for pointer/map/slice/chan/interface.
Variable is safe to read (returns zero) but may need init before use (nil map write panics).

failure_modes.scenario: Developer creates struct with nil slice field and tries to index it.
failure_modes.cause: append works on nil slice but indexing panics.
failure_modes.fix: Use append or make([]T, len) before indexing.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer writes to nil map: var m map[string]int; m["key"] = 1.
failure_modes.cause: Assignment to nil map panics. Only reading from nil map is safe.
failure_modes.fix: Initialize with make(map[string]int) or literal.

hidden_magic_checks: State the zero value for int, string, bool, pointer, slice, map, chan, struct, interface.
Predict whether nil map read panics or returns zero value.
Initialize a struct so all fields are usable without further assignment.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: sync.Mutex zero value is ready to use - no constructor. bytes.Buffer zero is empty buffer. Go's zero value philosophy enables usable defaults.
zero_magic.mental_model: Every type has a default starting state. Like a new house: int = room with 0 on wall, string = empty sign, pointer = no address. Zero value ensures no uninitialized gaps.
performance_implications: Zero-initialized struct fields cost nothing - compiler sets memory to zero in one instruction.
Nil map reads safe and fast (no allocation), nil map writes panic - know which ops require init.
var buf bytes.Buffer avoids heap allocation vs new(bytes.Buffer) when compiler proves stack residency.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Many languages have uninitialized variables with garbage. Go guarantees every variable starts in known state. Know which zero values are usable vs dangerous.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Declare variables of five types without initializers, print zero values, then correctly initialize a nil map and nil slice.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every var without initializer relies on zero values. JSON reading, handler init, data pipelines all depend on knowing what zero value guarantees.
step_by_step_execution: Declare with var x T - no initializer.
Read variable - returns zero value.
Check if zero value is usable: struct OK, nil map read-safe, nil slice append-safe.
If more init needed, assign or use make/literal.
For maps, always init before writing: m := make(map[string]int).
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go compiler ensures every declared variable occupies memory. Without initializer, compiler emits zero-fill instruction. Pointers get nil. Key insight: zero values are not uninitialized - they are explicitly initialized to a known default.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Zero values eliminate entire class of bugs from uninitialized memory. In C, reading uninitialized var is UB. In Go, reading declared var always yields defined value.

items.title: Type conversions
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Type conversions in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Assuming Go has implicit numeric conversion - even int to int64 requires explicit T().
Confusing type conversion (T(x)) with type assertion (x.(T)) - conversion on concrete, assertion on interface.
Using strconv.Atoi instead of understanding string to int requires runtime parsing, not conversion.
execution_timeline: Write T(x) where T is target type, x is source - compiler checks if conversion is valid.
For numeric conversions, bit pattern is reinterpreted or truncated to fit target size.
For string <-> byte/rune conversions, runtime copies and encodes/decodes data.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer converts int64(999999999999) to int32 expecting preservation.
failure_modes.cause: int32 range -2147483648 to 2147483647 - larger values wrap silently.
failure_modes.fix: Check value before conversion or use int64 throughout.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer converts "hello" to []int expecting numeric array.
failure_modes.cause: string to []int is invalid - only string <-> []byte and string <-> []rune allowed.
failure_modes.fix: Use []byte("hello") for bytes or []rune("hello") for code points.

hidden_magic_checks: Distinguish safe, truncating, and invalid conversions.
Convert string to byte slice and back, explaining memory allocation.
Convert interface{} to concrete string using assertion and type switch.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go stdlib uses conversions pervasively: int64 for time.Duration, []byte for I/O, string <-> []byte for bufio.Scanner.
zero_magic.mental_model: A type conversion is an explicit instruction to reinterpret or transform a value from one type to another. Like translation - specify target language, accept possible meaning loss (truncation).
performance_implications: Numeric conversions between same-size types are free - bit pattern preserved.
Different-size conversions involve sign-extension or truncation - one CPU instruction.
string <-> []byte copies memory O(n) - use strings.Builder for large strings.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Type conversion is fundamental but beginners apply carelessly, causing truncation, panics, or compile errors. This lesson makes each conversion's cost and risk explicit.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Convert between int, int32, int64, float64, string, []byte, []rune in both directions, identifying safe, truncating, and invalid conversions.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: API handlers convert strings to numbers, DB drivers convert Go types to SQL types, JSON serialization converts structs to bytes. Every production Go program performs type conversions.
step_by_step_execution: Identify source value and its type.
Determine target type - is conversion defined in Go spec?
Write T(value) - compiler validates at compile time.
For numeric, consider range and precision.
For string, expect allocation - immutable string produces new copy.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go has no implicit numeric conversions. Compiler checks convertibility: numeric types, strings <-> []byte/rune, pointer/interface conversions. Runtime handles bit manipulation. Key difference from assertions: conversion works on concrete types at compile time.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Implicit type conversion is a common source of hidden bugs in other languages. Go's explicit policy makes every type change visible in source.

items.title: Constants
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Constants in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Assuming constants are variables that cannot change - they are compile-time values with no address.
Thinking const x float64 = 3.14 creates runtime float64 - untyped constants have arbitrary precision at compile time.
Confusing const grouping with iota - they work together but serve different purposes.
execution_timeline: Write const x = value or const x T = value - compiler evaluates at compile time.
Untyped constants (no explicit type) can be used in any type-compatible context without conversion.
Typed constants require conversion for mixed-type operations.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer writes const duration = time.Duration(5) * time.Second and gets compile error.
failure_modes.cause: Constants must be compile-time evaluable. Function calls like time.Duration(5) are runtime.
failure_modes.fix: Use const duration = 5 * time.Second or use runtime variable.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer tries &myConst.
failure_modes.cause: Constants have no memory address - substituted inline.
failure_modes.fix: Assign to variable first: v := myConst; then &v.

hidden_magic_checks: Distinguish typed vs untyped constants and explain when each is needed.
Identify valid constant initializers (compile-time) vs runtime-only expressions.
Explain why len(array) can be constant but len(slice) cannot.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go uses constants extensively: time.Second, http.StatusOK, os.O_RDONLY, io.EOF. math.Pi and math.E are untyped constants. Compile-time safety without runtime cost.
zero_magic.mental_model: A constant is a compile-time known value that never changes. Like a sticky note with a number - use wherever needed, cannot modify. Untyped = flexible sticky note that adapts to any type.
performance_implications: Constants have zero runtime cost - substituted inline at compile time.
Untyped constants avoid conversion overhead - compiler inserts correctly typed value at each use.
Large constant expressions evaluated at compile time with arbitrary precision.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Constants appear in every Go program, but compile-time nature, typing rules, and limitations confuse beginners. This lesson demystifies constants.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Write typed, untyped, and iota-based constants, then explain which operations are valid constant expressions and why.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Constants define HTTP status codes, time durations, config defaults, enum-like values. Go services rely on constants for error sentinels (io.EOF), permission modes, time arithmetic.
step_by_step_execution: Determine if value is truly compile-time fixed - if runtime input, use var.
Choose untyped for maximum flexibility.
Choose typed when constant should only work with specific type.
Group related with const (...) and iota for sequential.
Use by name - compiler inlines at each use site.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go compiler evaluates constant expressions with a special arbitrary-precision engine. Untyped integers can store millions of digits. Default type inferred from representation: 42 = int, 3.14 = float64, 'x' = rune.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Constants eliminate magic numbers and strings. Without them, code is full of raw values like 60, 404 - each requiring reader to infer meaning. Constants turn magic into documentation.

items.title: iota
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply iota in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Assuming iota always starts at 0 - resets per const block, increments by 1 per line.
Thinking iota works outside const declarations - only defined within const blocks.
Confusing iota's line-based counting with semantic values - reordering lines changes iota values.
execution_timeline: Open const (...) block, assign iota to first constant - compiler sets to 0.
Each subsequent line in same const block increments iota by 1.
Expression involving iota evaluated per line - const (A=iota; B) means A=0, B=1.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer writes const (A=iota; B=3; C=iota) expecting C=2.
failure_modes.cause: iota increments per line: A=iota=0, B=3, C=iota=line3=2.
failure_modes.fix: Understand iota is line-based, not assignment-based.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer adds blank line inside const block and iota increments unexpectedly.
failure_modes.cause: Each line increments iota. Blank lines skipped but do not reset.
failure_modes.fix: Avoid blank lines in const (...) with iota. Use comments instead.

hidden_magic_checks: Predict values of five constants using iota with custom expressions (e.g. 1 << iota).
Explain why adding a line between iota constants shifts subsequent values.
Implement bitmask enum using iota with shifts.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go stdlib uses iota in os (O_RDONLY etc), net (socket flags), database/sql (isolation levels). Iota is Go's tool for compact enum and flag definitions.
zero_magic.mental_model: Iota is a compile-time counter within const blocks. Like an auto-numbering machine - each line prints next number. Formulas (1 << iota) change what each number means.
performance_implications: Iota values resolved at compile time - zero runtime cost.
Bitmask flags (1 << iota) pack into single uint efficiently.
Unused iota constants do not consume memory - inlined.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Iota appears mysterious: compiler counter with no runtime equivalent. Beginners misuse it by misunderstanding line-based increment.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Define const block with A=iota, B, C_skip=_, D=1<<iota, E, predict values, then write byte-size enum (KB, MB, GB) with iota.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Iota defines HTTP methods, DB isolation levels, permission flags, byte-size constants. Production Go relies on iota for compact enum definitions.
step_by_step_execution: Start const (...) block.
Assign iota or expression with iota to first constant (starts 0).
Each line increments iota by 1.
Use _ to skip values.
Reset iota by starting new const (...) block.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: iota is a predeclared identifier representing untyped integer constant incrementing by 1 per line in const declaration. Compiler resets iota to 0 before each const block.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without iota, enum values would be manually numbered. Adding or removing requires renumbering. Iota automates numbering, making enum constants maintainable.

items.title: Boolean logic
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Boolean logic in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Assuming && and || evaluate both sides - Go short-circuits when left determines result.
Confusing bitwise (&, |) with logical (&&, ||) - & always evaluates both, && short-circuits.
Writing complex boolean without parentheses - Go requires explicit bool values, no truthiness.
execution_timeline: Write expression with &&, ||, ! and bool operands - compiler type-checks both sides are bool.
For &&: if left is false, right is never evaluated.
For ||: if left is true, right is never evaluated.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer writes if x != 0 && 10/x > 2 and expects division check first.
failure_modes.cause: Short-circuit stops when x==0 before division by zero.
failure_modes.fix: Correct - short circuit prevents panic. Add explicit guard if needed.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer writes if x || y where x and y are int.
failure_modes.cause: Go has no truthiness - && and || require bool operands.
failure_modes.fix: Use explicit: if x != 0 || y != 0.

hidden_magic_checks: Predict short-circuit results with side effects in right operand.
Rewrite complex boolean into clear if-else chain.
Distinguish logical (&&, ||) from bitwise (&, |) behavior.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go stdlib uses boolean logic: if err != nil && !errors.Is(err, io.EOF), for-range guards, test assertions. Go's explicit bool makes conditions visible.
zero_magic.mental_model: Boolean logic is true/false algebra. AND (&&) requires both true, OR (||) requires at least one, NOT (!) flips. Short-circuit is like lazy co-workers - first answer is enough.
performance_implications: Short-circuit saves CPU - order conditions with cheap/early-exit first.
Complex booleans in hot loops should be simplified.
Branch misprediction costs ~15 cycles - profile and simplify hot-path conditions.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Short-circuiting, operator precedence, and no-truthiness confuse beginners. This lesson makes boolean evaluation predictable.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Write function using short-circuit to safely access nil pointer field, then trace evaluation step by step.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every if, for condition, guard expression uses boolean logic. API authorization, validation, error handling depend on clear boolean expressions.
step_by_step_execution: Identify each sub-condition - must be bool.
Chain with && (all true) or || (any true).
Use parentheses for grouping.
Use ! for negation - consider writing positive case for clarity.
Rely on short-circuit: cheap/likely-false first with &&; likely-true first with ||.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Compiler generates conditional jump for each operator: && maps to JZ/JNZ, || maps to JZ/JNZ, ! maps to compare-and-invert. Side effects in right operand never execute if short-circuited.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without explicit boolean logic, every condition has hidden truthiness rules. Go's explicit bool means every condition's meaning is directly visible.

items.title: If and else
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply If and else in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Putting curly brace on wrong line - Go requires { on same line as if/else/for.
Else must be on same line as closing brace: } else {.
Deep if-else chain where switch would be clearer - 3+ branches read better as switch.
execution_timeline: Write if condition { body } - condition must be bool.
Chain with else if or else - checks top-down, executes first true branch.
Use if-init: if err := doSomething(); err != nil { return err }.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer writes if x = 5 { ... } assigning instead of comparing.
failure_modes.cause: Go catches at compile time - assignment in if condition is illegal.
failure_modes.fix: Use if x == 5 or if x := getValue(); x == 5.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer chains five if-else with same comparison.
failure_modes.cause: Deep chains are hard to read. Go switch is clearer.
failure_modes.fix: Refactor to switch x { case 1: ...; default: ... }.

hidden_magic_checks: Write if-else chain handling three error types using errors.Is/As.
Explain variable scoping in if-init - declared var scoped to if/else block.
Rewrite deeply nested if to early-return guard style.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go stdlib uses if constantly: if err != nil, if req.Method == http.MethodGet. if-init pattern scopes error variables to the check block.
zero_magic.mental_model: if is a fork in the road. Init statement (if x := compute(); x > 0) is a prep table - compute, check, both in same step before the fork.
performance_implications: If condition test costs few cycles - negligible in non-hot paths.
Deep nesting causes branch prediction pressure - prefer early returns.
if-init scoping lets compiler recycle stack space for init vars immediately.
zero_magic.problem_solved: if/else is basic control flow, but beginners misuse formatting, scope, and chain depth. This lesson establishes idiomatic patterns.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Write function using if-init, if-else chain for error handling, and early-return guard pattern.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Error handling is dominated by if err != nil. Config branches, request routing, validation all use if/else.
step_by_step_execution: Write if, then bool condition, then { on same line.
Body indented on next lines.
Use } else { on one line.
Use if init; condition { } to scope variables.
Prefer early returns over deep nesting.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's if doesn't need parentheses but requires braces. Init statement is unique Go feature - declares vars visible only within if/else block. Compiler generates conditional jump instruction.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Conditional branching is fundamental. Go's if makes every branch explicit, with no hidden fallthrough or truthiness.

Can identify common mistakes.
Can apply the concept in a small working program.
proof.practice_task: Complete a focused exercise that applies If and else.

verification.expected_output: Learner can demonstrate the concept and explain it.

items.title: Switch
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Switch in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Assuming implicit fallthrough like C - Go only executes matching case, stops automatically.
Forgetting that break in switch exits the switch - break is legal but not needed.
Using switch on non-comparable types (slice, map, func).
execution_timeline: Write switch expr { case val: ... } - compiler checks expr is comparable with each case.
Matching case found - Go executes that case body and exits switch.
If no match, default case (if present) runs - optional, can appear anywhere.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer uses switch on float64 expecting exact equality.
failure_modes.cause: Float comparison unreliable - NaN != NaN in IEEE 754.
failure_modes.fix: Use switch on int/string types, or if-else with epsilon for floats.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer writes type switch without .(type) syntax.
failure_modes.cause: Type switches need switch v := x.(type) syntax.
failure_modes.fix: Use switch v := x.(type) { case string: ...; case int: ... }.

hidden_magic_checks: Use type switch for three concrete types from interface{}.
Explain why two identical integer constants match different cases due to typing.
Write switch with no expression as if-else chain.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go stdlib uses switch for type switches, expression switches, and blank switches. net/http uses switch on request methods. fmt uses type switches for formatting.
zero_magic.mental_model: Switch is a multi-way branch checking a value against possibilities. Like a mail sorter - each letter goes into one bin. No hole in bin bottom (no fallthrough).
performance_implications: Dense integer cases compile to jump table - O(1).
Sparse/string cases compile to binary search - O(log n).
Type switches use interface's dynamic type pointer for O(1) dispatch.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Switch has Go-specific quirks: no fallthrough, type switch syntax, blank switch expression. This lesson makes switch predictable.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Write expression switch, type switch, and blank switch, explaining when each is appropriate over if-else.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: HTTP routing, error classification, config validation use switch. Go services rely on switch for clean multi-way branching.
step_by_step_execution: Identify expression to compare - must be comparable.
Write switch expr { case val: body }.
Use default: for catch-all.
For type switch: switch v := x.(type) { case Type: }.
For complex booleans: switch { case boolExpr: }.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Switch compiles differently per case values. Integer constants = jump table. String constants = binary search. Type switch compares dynamic type pointer against each case type.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Multi-way branching is inevitable. Go's switch makes branches explicit, prevents accidental fallthrough, keeps each case independent.

items.title: Loops
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Loops in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Forgetting Go only has for - three forms: for init;cond;post, for cond, for range.
Using for-range on string thinking it iterates bytes - it iterates runes, yielding byte index + rune.
Modifying range variable thinking it affects collection - range copies each element.
execution_timeline: Write for init;condition;post { body } - init runs once, condition checked each iteration.
Write for condition { body } - while-style, runs until condition false.
Write for { body } - infinite loop, exits via break or return.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer modifies slice length inside for i loop.
failure_modes.cause: len(slice) evaluated once before first iteration.
failure_modes.fix: Capture initial length: n := len(s); for i := 0; i < n; i++.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer uses range on pointer to array expecting copies.
failure_modes.cause: Range copies each element - modifying range var doesn't affect original.
failure_modes.fix: Access by index: for i := range arr { arr[i] = newValue }.

hidden_magic_checks: Write all three for forms: traditional, condition-only, for-range.
Explain why modifying range variable doesn't modify collection.
Break from nested loop correctly using labels.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go stdlib uses for everything: iterating request headers, scanning DB rows, copying between io.Reader/Writer. Single for keyword with three forms reduces complexity.
zero_magic.mental_model: A loop is a repeat instruction. Init sets up counters, condition says when to stop, post updates state. for-range is like conveyor belt - each item passes by.
performance_implications: Loop overhead is minimal - increment + branch costs ~1 cycle.
for-range on slice copies each element - use for i := range and slice[i] for structs.
for-range on string decodes each rune O(n) without allocating.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Go has one loop keyword but three forms, confusing beginners expecting while/do-while. This lesson clarifies for flexibility.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Write program using all three for forms, including labeled break from nested loop and for-range on string.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every server runs event loop. Every pagination loops over results. Every batch job iterates. Loops are backbone of repetition.
step_by_step_execution: Choose for form: init;cond;post for counter, cond for while-style, range for element iteration.
Write loop body - avoid modifying range variable.
Use break to exit, continue to skip.
Use labels for breaking nested loops.
Ensure loop makes progress toward termination.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Compiler translates for into branch instructions. For for-range, creates hidden iterator. Special handling for strings (rune decode) and maps (random order).
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Repetition is foundation of automation. Go's for makes repetition explicit - no hidden recursion or iterator magic.

items.title: Range
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Range in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Forgetting second range return - for i := range gives only index, use for i, v := range.
Assuming range over map yields insertion order - Go randomizes map iteration.
Modifying range value (v) thinking it updates slice - v is a copy.
execution_timeline: Write for k, v := range collection - compiler generates appropriate iterator.
For slices/arrays: index, element copy - stable low-to-high order.
For maps: key, value copy - random order by design. Strings: byte index, rune copy. Channels: received value.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer ranges over map, stores keys, expects same order.
failure_modes.cause: Go intentionally randomizes map iteration order.
failure_modes.fix: Maintain separate ordered slice of keys if order matters.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer ranges over channel without closing it.
failure_modes.cause: for v := range ch blocks until channel is closed.
failure_modes.fix: Ensure sender closes channel or use for { v, ok := <-ch; if !ok { break } }.

hidden_magic_checks: Range over slice, map, string, channel - explain what each iteration produces.
Predict output of ranging over map multiple times.
Collect only second value using _ for index/key.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go stdlib uses range extensively: for k,v := range headers for HTTP, for _,b := range []byte for byte processing, for range lines with bufio.Scanner.
zero_magic.mental_model: Range is a universal iterator that adapts to collection type. Like universal remote - pressing 'next' gives index+value on array, key+value on map, byte-index+rune on string.
performance_implications: Range over slice as fast as manual index loop - compiler optimizes.
Range over string decodes runes O(n) with decode cost per rune.
Range over map reads randomized key stream - runtime uses hash seed per map.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Range is Go's most common iteration idiom, but type-specific behavior, copy semantics, and map randomization surprise beginners.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Write program ranging over slice, map, string, and channel, printing type and value of each iteration variable.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Processing HTTP headers, scanning file contents, decoding request bodies, iterating query results all use range.
step_by_step_execution: Identify collection type.
Write for k,v := range - use _ to discard.
Slices: k=index, v=copy.
Maps: k=key, v=copy, random order.
Channels: one return value, loop ends when closed.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Range is a compiler primitive. For slices: simple index loop. For maps: iterator over hash table buckets, starting random. For strings: rune-decoding iterator. Range variable is always a copy.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Iteration is universal but each collection iterates differently. Range provides uniform interface without hiding type-specific behavior.

items.title: Arrays
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Arrays in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Confusing arrays with slices - [3]int and []int are different types.
Assuming arrays are reference types - arrays are values, passing [1024]int copies 8KB.
Thinking you can resize an array - size is part of type, fixed at compile time.
execution_timeline: Declare var arr [5]int - compiler allocates 5 contiguous int slots.
Access by zero-based index: arr[0] = 42.
Array passed by value - assigning or passing copies all elements.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer passes array to function expecting modifications to persist.
failure_modes.cause: Arrays are values - function receives copy.
failure_modes.fix: Pass pointer: func process(arr *[5]int), or use slice.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer writes a[5] for length-5 array causing panic.
failure_modes.cause: Arrays zero-indexed - valid indices 0 to len-1.
failure_modes.fix: Check bounds: if idx < len(a) { a[idx] = ... }.

hidden_magic_checks: Distinguish [5]int and []int, explaining type and memory implications.
Write function computing sum of any fixed-size array using pointer parameter.
Explain compile error from assigning [3]int to [4]int.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Arrays in stdlib are rare - slices preferred. Exceptions: [16]byte for crypto, [32]byte for SHA256, low-level os.File buffers.
zero_magic.mental_model: An array is fixed-length contiguous memory. Like parking lot with numbered spaces - size determined at construction, cannot add/remove spaces. Copying copies the entire lot.
performance_implications: Array access O(1) - direct memory offset.
Copying large arrays O(n) - prefer slices or pointers.
Arrays stack-allocated if small (<64KB), heap otherwise - check with -gcflags=-m.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Arrays are underused because slices are more flexible, but without understanding arrays, slice mechanics remain magical.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Allocate array, compute type/size, pass by value and pointer, explain copy behavior.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Arrays underpin slices - every slice has backing array. Cryptographic hashes produce [32]byte. Fixed-size network buffers use arrays.
step_by_step_execution: Determine element type and fixed length (part of type).
Declare: var arr [N]T or arr := [N]T{...}.
Access by index arr[i] - i in [0, N-1].
Pass by pointer *[N]T to avoid copy.
Use [...]T{...} for compiler-counted length.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: var arr [5]int creates 5 * 8 = 40 bytes contiguous. Length baked into type. Value type: arr1 = arr2 copies all elements. Indices bounds-checked at runtime.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Arrays are foundation of memory organization. Understanding value semantics and contiguous storage makes slice behavior predictable.

items.title: Slices
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Slices in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Treating nil and empty slices differently - len(nil) == 0, append works on nil.
Assuming slicing [:j] creates new copy - shares backing array with original.
Using append without saving return value: append(s, v) discards result.
execution_timeline: Create slice: make([]T, len, cap), []T{...}, or from array via arr[:].
Slice header (ptr, len, cap) allocated. Backing array stores elements.
Append may allocate new backing array if capacity insufficient.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer creates sub-slice s[2:5] and appends expecting original unaffected.
failure_modes.cause: Sub-slice shares backing array. Append may overwrite original.
failure_modes.fix: Use copy(dst, src) for independent copy.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer checks emptiness by comparing to nil instead of len(s) == 0.
failure_modes.cause: Both nil and empty have len 0, but JSON marshal null vs [].
failure_modes.fix: Use len(s) == 0 regardless of nil/empty distinction.

hidden_magic_checks: Explain three slice header fields and how they determine behavior.
Trace append affecting len, cap, backing array across multiple calls.
Use copy() for independent slice not sharing backing array.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Slices are universal sequence type. []byte for I/O, []string for args, []int for sort, []T for nearly every collection.
zero_magic.mental_model: A slice is a window into an array. Window has position (ptr), width (len), max width (cap). Slide window (reslice), but underlying array fixed. Wider window (append) needs new array if at max.
performance_implications: Slice access O(1) - pointer + offset.
append amortized O(1) - doubles capacity for growth.
Reslicing s[:0] with capacity retained avoids allocations.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Slices are most used data structure, but reference semantics, backing array sharing, and append confuse beginners.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Create backing array, two sub-slices sharing it, modify one, observe effect on other - then use copy for independence.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every DB result, file read, HTTP response body is a slice. JSON, string processing, protobuf all produce slices.
step_by_step_execution: Choose creation: make for specific len/cap, literal for known values, or slice from existing.
Access by index: s[i] - bounds checked at runtime.
Append: s = append(s, v) - always assign result.
Slice: s[low:high] creates new header sharing backing array.
Copy for independence: n := copy(dst, src) - preallocate dst.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Slice is struct{ ptr *T, len int, cap int } - 24 bytes. append checks len < cap; if true, sets s[len]=v and increments len; if false, calls runtime.growslice allocating new array.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Slices solve fixed-size array problem while providing uniform, safe, efficient view of sequential memory.

items.title: Slice length and capacity
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Slice length and capacity in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Confusing len and cap - len is current elements, cap is backing array size.
Assuming s[:cap(s)] is always safe - reslicing to cap reveals hidden backing elements.
Using make([]T, 5) for append-oriented usage instead of make([]T, 0, 5).
execution_timeline: Create with make([]T, len, cap) - allocates backing array of size cap.
Initial len elements zero-valued, accessible via index.
Elements len to cap-1 exist in backing array but not part of slice - accessing via reslice reveals them.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer uses s = append(s, v) starting with make([]int, 5), wonders why first 5 are 0.
failure_modes.cause: make([]int, 5) creates len=5, append adds at position 5.
failure_modes.fix: Use make([]int, 0, 5) for zero-length with capacity 5.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer removes last element via s[:len(s)-1] expecting memory cleared.
failure_modes.cause: Reslicing changes len but doesn't clear backing array element.
failure_modes.fix: Set element to zero before reslicing for pointer/reference types.

hidden_magic_checks: Draw memory layout of slice with len=3, cap=5 showing backing array.
Predict valid reslice operations (len-bound) vs revealing (cap-bound).
Explain append allocation behavior across 10 appends starting with cap=2.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go stdlib uses len/cap for buffer management. bufio.Scanner uses len for used bytes, cap for buffer size. sort uses reflect.SliceHeader.
zero_magic.mental_model: Length = cars on lot. Capacity = total spaces. Park more cars (append) up to capacity without building new lot. Lot full = new lot, cars move.
performance_implications: Reslicing O(1) - no allocation, just header update.
Starting cap=0 causes growth 0->1->2->4->8->... - preallocate with make([]T,0,n) to avoid.
Accessing backing array beyond len bypasses type safety.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Length vs capacity is unique to slices, root cause of slice bugs. Without explicit understanding, every reslice and append has hidden assumptions.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Create slice with make([]int, 2, 5), observe len/cap, append until capacity grows, explain each allocation.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: DB result batching, I/O buffer pooling, network packet processing depend on managing len/cap for allocation-free patterns.
step_by_step_execution: Use make([]T, 0, n) for pre-allocated capacity, zero initial length.
Use make([]T, n) when needing n zero-valued elements by index.
Check cap before append to predict allocation.
Reslice s[:n] reduces (n <= len) or extends (n <= cap) length.
Clear refs after removal for GC.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: len and cap are compiler intrinsics. For slices, returns header fields. make allocates backing array of cap size, initializes len elements to zero. append growth factor ~2x small, ~1.25x large.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Every append either fits (fast, no allocation) or allocates new backing array (slow). Knowing len vs cap makes append predictable.

items.title: Slice sharing and aliasing
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Slice sharing and aliasing in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Modifying through one sub-slice and surprised another shows the change.
Appending to sub-slice accidentally overwriting original elements.
Passing slice to function, appending inside, expecting caller's length to change.
execution_timeline: Create base slice s := []int{1,2,3,4,5} - backing array 5 elements.
Create sub-slice a := s[0:3] - shares backing array, len=3, cap=5.
Append to a until cap exceeded - if shared and cap allows, data corruption; if exceeded, new allocation.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer slices s[1:3], appends to result, checks s.
failure_modes.cause: Sub-slice cap = 5-1 = 4. Appending 2+ values overwrites s[3], s[4].
failure_modes.fix: Use copy: sub := make([]int, 2); copy(sub, s[1:3]).

failure_modes.scenario: Developer passes slice to function expecting len changes to propagate.
failure_modes.cause: Function receives slice header copy. ptr changes visible, len changes local.
failure_modes.fix: Return modified slice: s = fn(s), or pass *[]int.

hidden_magic_checks: Trace backing array state after three sub-slices created and modified.
Predict when append on sub-slice corrupts original data.
Explain difference between element modification (visible) and append (may or may not be visible).
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: bytes.Buffer Bytes() result shares backing buffer - callers must copy. bufio.Scanner returns shared []byte invalidated by next scan. Aliasing by design for performance.
zero_magic.mental_model: Sharing is group sharing single whiteboard. Each sub-slice writes on assigned section. Writing beyond section overwrites others' work. Only independent whiteboard (copy) prevents this.
performance_implications: Sharing avoids allocation - O(1) sub-slicing.
Aliasing bugs cause hard-to-debug memory corruption.
copy O(n) but eliminates aliasing - use for long-term independence.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Slice aliasing is most common subtle bug source. This lesson makes sharing contract explicit.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Create scenario where appending to sub-slice corrupts original, fix with copy, explain both steps.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: HTTP middleware chains share contexts, DB scanners share scan buffers, JSON decoders share internal buffers. Understanding aliasing prevents corruption.
step_by_step_execution: Check if sub-slice outlives operation - if so, copy.
Note cap derived from original cap minus start offset.
Assume corruption risk when appending to sub-slice unless copy used.
For functions modifying length, return slice or use *[]int.
Document sharing contracts explicitly.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: s[low:high] creates slice header where ptr=base+low, len=high-low, cap=cap(s)-low. Append within capacity overwrites original array at indices >= high. This is intentional and predictable.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Copying everything is safe but expensive. Sharing everything is fast but dangerous. Slice sharing makes tradeoffs explicit with full knowledge of consequences.

items.title: Maps
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Maps in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Reading nil map returns zero - no panic. Writing nil map panics.
Assuming map iteration order is deterministic - Go randomizes intentionally.
Using slices as map keys - slices not comparable. Use strings, ints, or struct keys.
execution_timeline: Create: m := make(map[K]V) or map[K]V{key: val}.
Write: m[key] = value - panics if nil, otherwise inserts/updates.
Read: v := m[key] - returns zero if missing. Use v, ok := m[key] for existence check.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer writes var m map[string]int; m["key"] = 1, getting panic.
failure_modes.cause: Nil map has no hash table allocated.
failure_modes.fix: Initialize with make(map[string]int) before writing.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer iterates over map expecting same order each run.
failure_modes.cause: Go adds random salt to hash seed per map instance.
failure_modes.fix: Collect keys into sorted slice and iterate that.

hidden_magic_checks: Write functions to insert, delete, and check existence in map.
Distinguish zero-value return from actual key presence via comma-ok.
Explain iteration order appears consistent until map grows.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Maps are most-used collection after slices. HTTP headers (map[string][]string), query params, JSON objects, config stores all use maps.
zero_magic.mental_model: A map is a hash table - key-value pairs where keys hash to storage location. Like coat check: give key, get ticket (hash), coat stored in numbered booth.
performance_implications: Map access/insert O(1) average, O(n) worst-case.
Map ops not goroutine-safe - concurrent read+write panics. Use sync.RWMutex.
Maps are reference types - passing shares underlying hash table.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Maps feel intuitive but nil safety, random iteration, and goroutine-unsafety surprise beginners.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Implement word-count using map, handle missing key with comma-ok, demonstrate nil-map assignment panic.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every API handler parses query params into maps. Every session store is a map. Maps are primary associative data structure.
step_by_step_execution: Initialize: m := make(map[K]V) - never write to nil map.
Read with comma-ok: if v, ok := m[key]; ok {...}.
Delete: delete(m, key) - safe if missing.
Iterate: for k, v := range m - expect random order.
Use sync.Map for concurrent access or protect with mutex.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: A map is pointer to runtime's hmap struct. make allocates hmap and bucket array. Key hashed using per-map random seed, placed in bucket. Missing key returns zero value.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Associative arrays are essential for non-trivial programs. Go's map provides fast key-value lookup with clean API, but safety rules must be explicit knowledge.

items.title: Comma-ok idiom
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Comma-ok idiom in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Forgetting comma-ok returns (value, bool) - using v := m[key] not knowing if key existed.
Dropping second return on type assertion - x.(T) panics if wrong; x, ok := x.(T) is safe.
Using comma-ok on channel without select - v, ok := <-ch returns false when closed and empty.
execution_timeline: Write v, ok := expression - map read, type assertion, or channel receive.
First return is result (or zero if !ok).
Second is bool: true = success, false = failure.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer writes val := m["missing"] treating val as existing.
failure_modes.cause: Map read returns zero for missing keys.
failure_modes.fix: Use val, ok := m["missing"]; if !ok { /* handle */ }.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer writes v := x.(string) where x holds int.
failure_modes.cause: This panics - type assertion fails.
failure_modes.fix: Use v, ok := x.(string); if !ok { /* handle mismatch */ }.

hidden_magic_checks: Use comma-ok for map lookup, type assertion, channel receive in sequence.
Identify which operations support comma-ok.
Explain why comma-ok preferred over separate 'contains' check for maps.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Comma-ok is pervasive: map lookups in templates, type switches in fmt, channel receives. gob encoder uses comma-ok for interface checks. reflect uses comma-ok for method lookups.
zero_magic.mental_model: Comma-ok is two-part answer: 'here's value, and yes/no - it worked.' Like vending machine: get snack and 'yes, here' or zero-snack and 'slot empty.'
performance_implications: Comma-ok on map avoids second lookup - one hash instead of two.
Comma-ok on type assertion avoids panic - one interface comparison.
Comma-ok on channel must be in select or for-range typically.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Comma-ok is Go idiom beginners don't know or forget, causing panics from failed assertions and silent data loss from missing keys.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Write function taking interface{} and safely extract string, int, []byte using comma-ok, returning errors for mismatches.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Comma-ok is standard for safe type extraction, safe map access, safe channel reading. Every Go codebase relies on it.
step_by_step_execution: Identify operation: map read, type assertion, or channel receive.
Assign both: v, ok := expr.
Check ok: if !ok { /* handle */ }.
Use value when ok is true.
Never use single-value assertions on interface{} from external sources.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Comma-ok is built into language for three operations. Map: hash lookup + found boolean. Type assertion: dynamic type comparison + true/false. Channel: read from buffer + true, or zero+false if closed and empty.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Single-value returns hide failure. Comma-ok makes success-or-failure explicit at every call site.
