items.title: Strings
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Strings in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Treating strings as mutable - strings are immutable byte sequences.
Using len(string) to count characters - len returns bytes, not runes.
Assuming string indexing yields a character - s[i] returns byte, not rune.
execution_timeline: Create via literal "hello", raw `raw`, or conversion from []byte.
Access by index: s[i] returns byte at position i.
Strings are immutable - modification creates new string.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer indexes into 'é' (2 bytes) getting garbled character.
failure_modes.cause: s[1] returns second byte of 2-byte UTF-8 sequence, not valid rune alone.
failure_modes.fix: Use for i, r := range s for rune iteration.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer builds string via s += str in loop, getting O(n^2).
failure_modes.cause: Each += creates new string copying entire buffer.
failure_modes.fix: Use strings.Builder for efficient string construction.

hidden_magic_checks: Explain string byte vs rune difference using multi-byte character.
Count characters using len and utf8.RuneCountInString, explain difference.
Rewrite string concat loop to use strings.Builder.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go stdlib treats strings as immutable []byte sequences. strings package provides search/replace/split/join. fmt handles formatting. net/http uses strings for headers.
zero_magic.mental_model: A string is a read-only view of a byte sequence. Sealed envelope: peek inside (read bytes), but cannot change message. Copy to whiteboard ([]byte) to edit.
performance_implications: String slice (s[i:j]) no allocation - new header sharing data.
string <-> []byte allocates and copies O(n).
strings.Builder avoids per-append allocation with exponential growth.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Strings seem simple but byte vs rune, immutability, encoding confuse beginners.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Write function reversing string byte-by-byte (wrong), then correctly by runes, explain difference.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every HTTP header, JSON key, file path, log line is a string. String processing is most common non-trivial Go task.
step_by_step_execution: Create with double quotes or backticks.
Access bytes via s[i].
Iterate runes: for i, r := range s.
Build with strings.Builder for efficiency.
Convert to []byte for mutation: b := []byte(s); modify; s = string(b).
zero_magic.under_the_hood: String is struct{ ptr *byte, len int } - 16 bytes. Pointer references immutable byte array. Multiple strings can share same array (slicing). Compiler ensures immutability.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Strings are universal medium for text. Understanding byte-level representation, immutability, and Unicode is essential.

items.title: Bytes
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Bytes in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Confusing []byte and string - same underlying data, different mutability and operation sets.
Assuming []byte auto-encodes UTF-8 - []byte is just bytes, no encoding implied.
Using string concatenation when []byte append is more efficient.
execution_timeline: Create via literal []byte{0x48}, make([]byte, n), or conversion from string.
Bytes are mutable - b[i] = 0xFF modifies in place.
Convert to/from string: string(b) or []byte(s) - both allocate and copy.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer creates var buf []byte and writes buf[0] without allocating.
failure_modes.cause: Nil slice cannot be indexed.
failure_modes.fix: Use make([]byte, n) to pre-allocate.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer uses bytes.Equal comparing same text in different encodings.
failure_modes.cause: bytes.Equal does byte-for-byte. Same text, different encoding = different bytes.
failure_modes.fix: Convert to same encoding before comparison.

hidden_magic_checks: Convert between string and []byte, explaining allocation cost.
Use bytes.Buffer and bytes.Reader for I/O.
Modify bytes in []byte and observe source string unaffected.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go stdlib uses []byte for all I/O: io.Reader/Writer operate on []byte. bytes provides Buffer, Reader, Split, Join. crypto uses []byte for keys.
zero_magic.mental_model: A byte slice is mutable sequence of raw 8-bit values. Workbench for rearranging bits. Strings = framed photographs (look only). Byte slices = loose photos (crop, recolor freely).
performance_implications: []byte to string allocates O(n) copy.
bytes.Buffer grows exponentially - prefer to manual append.
Preallocating make([]byte,0,size) reduces growth allocations.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Bytes are raw material of all data, but beginners confuse with strings and characters.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Write hex dump function taking []byte, print hex + ASCII, use bytes.Buffer to collect output.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: All Go I/O operates on []byte. Reading files, writing HTTP responses, encrypting, encoding JSON - all pass through byte slices.
step_by_step_execution: Choose []byte for mutable sequences, string for immutable text.
Use make([]byte, n) for fixed buffers, append/Buffer for dynamic.
Convert to string only when needed.
Use bytes.Split/Join/HasPrefix instead of converting to string.
For I/O, implement readers/writers with bytes.NewReader.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: []byte is regular slice with element type byte (uint8). bytes package optimized: bytes.Index uses assembly-level Rabin-Karp. string to []byte allocates new memory because strings are immutable.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Every piece of data is ultimately bytes. Understanding byte slices enables reading/writing formats, protocols, encodings with confidence.

items.title: Runes and Unicode
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Runes and Unicode in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Confusing 'character' in Go with single byte - rune can be 1-4 bytes in UTF-8.
Using string indexing for characters - s[i] gives byte, use for range for runes.
Assuming len(string) gives characters - it gives UTF-8 bytes.
execution_timeline: Write string literal with non-ASCII - compiler encodes as multi-byte UTF-8.
Iterate with for i, r := range s - decodes each rune, advances i by rune's byte width.
Convert to []rune for code point slice: runes := []rune(s).

failure_modes.scenario: Developer reverses string by swapping bytes, breaking multi-byte chars.
failure_modes.cause: Byte reversal produces invalid UTF-8 sequences.
failure_modes.fix: Convert to []rune, reverse, convert back: runes := []rune(s); reverse(runes); s = string(runes).

failure_modes.scenario: Developer counts characters using len(s) getting wrong count.
failure_modes.cause: len counts bytes, not code points. Accented/CJK chars are multi-byte.
failure_modes.fix: Use utf8.RuneCountInString(s) for character count.

hidden_magic_checks: Distinguish byte length, rune count, display width of string.
Write function extracting first N characters (not bytes) from string.
Explain why 'noël' has different byte lengths but same visual appearance.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go stdlib uses rune: fmt %c, strconv rune quoting, unicode classification/case conversion, utf8 encoding/decoding. Go strings are UTF-8 by convention.
zero_magic.mental_model: A rune is a Unicode code point - 32-bit integer representing one 'character'. Bytes = ink on page, runes = letters they form. Some letters use 1 byte (ASCII), others 2-4 bytes.
performance_implications: []rune(s) iterates the string decoding each rune - O(n) allocation for rune slice.
for range on string O(n) without allocating.
utf8.DecodeRuneInString O(1) per call.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Unicode/UTF-8 invisible in ASCII-only programs, explode into confusion with non-ASCII text. This lesson makes the relationship explicit.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Write function taking mixed ASCII + multi-byte string, output byte length, rune count, each rune's byte offset and hex value.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Internationalization, emoji handling (1-4 bytes), URL encoding, text processing depend on rune-aware operations.
step_by_step_execution: Write strings with Unicode literals - Go source is UTF-8.
Iterate runes: for i, r := range str.
Count runes: utf8.RuneCountInString(str).
Compare runes with == (uint32 value).
Convert to []rune for per-rune operations.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Rune is Go's int32 representing Unicode code point. String is byte sequence (not necessarily UTF-8). But for range, []rune, utf8 package assume UTF-8. Invalid UTF-8 produces replacement rune U+FFFD.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Real-world text contains more than ASCII. Without Unicode understanding, programs silently corrupt non-English text. Understanding runes means code works for everyone.

items.title: Pointers as addresses
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Pointers as addresses in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Assuming pointer arithmetic works - Go does not support pointer arithmetic.
Thinking pointers are only for large structs - pointers create aliases for shared access.
Confusing *T (pointer to T) with *p dereference - context determines meaning.
execution_timeline: Take address: p := &x - p is *T pointing to x's memory.
Read through pointer: v := *p - dereferences to get value.
Write through pointer: *p = newVal - modifies original variable.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer passes *uint64 and casts to *int32 expecting lower 32 bits.
failure_modes.cause: Go disallows unsafe pointer casting without unsafe package.
failure_modes.fix: Use encoding/binary to extract bytes, or unsafe only when performance-critical.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer returns pointer to local variable expecting it to be invalid.
failure_modes.cause: Go's escape analysis moves local vars to heap when address escapes.
failure_modes.fix: This is safe in Go. Compiler handles escape analysis.

hidden_magic_checks: Draw memory layout of pointer pointing to int, showing addresses.
Trace pointer aliasing: modify through one pointer, observe through another.
Explain why Go doesn't need pointer arithmetic.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go stdlib uses pointers for method receivers (func (t *T)), large struct params, shared state. io.Reader/Writer use pointers. database/sql uses *Row, *Rows.
zero_magic.mental_model: A pointer is a memory address - signpost saying 'data over here.' Instead of carrying data (copying), carry address (pointer). Multiple signposts can point to same house (aliasing).
performance_implications: Pointer indirection adds one memory fetch - 1 cycle if cached.
Passing *T instead of T for large structs avoids copying - 10x faster for 1KB+ structs.
Escape analysis moves pointer targets to heap - slower than stack.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Pointers fundamental to Go's pass-by-value, method receivers, escape analysis. Beginners overuse or avoid them entirely.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Write function taking *int, modify it, show caller's variable changed. Then return pointer to local var and explain why safe.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Method receivers use pointers. Large structs passed as pointers. DB rows, JSON decoders return pointers. Pointers essential for shared mutable state.
step_by_step_execution: Use & to take address: p := &x.
Use * to dereference: v := *p get, *p = v set.
Use *T param when function must modify original or T is large.
Use nil pointer checks: if p != nil.
Use unsafe.Pointer only with syscall/cgo - document safety.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go pointers are 8 bytes (64-bit) with virtual memory address. Pointer arithmetic forbidden. Dereferencing nil panics. new(T) returns *T. Method receivers distinguish value vs pointer - pointer allows mutation, avoids copy. Escape analysis determines stack vs heap.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Pointers connect variables to actual memory. Without pointers, cannot modify caller's state, share large data without copy, or represent optional values (nil).

items.title: Beginner pointer mistakes
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Beginner pointer mistakes in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Assigning to *p when p is nil panics - always check p != nil first.
Using & before literal like &42 - only variables have addresses. Use new(int).
Returning pointer to loop variable and using after loop - all captured pointers point to same final value.
execution_timeline: Beginner writes code that accidentally dereferences nil pointer.
Runtime detects nil dereference and panics with 'invalid memory address or nil pointer dereference'.
Developer must trace back why pointer was nil and add guard.

failure_modes.scenario: Beginner writes var p *int; *p = 42 expecting auto-init.
failure_modes.cause: Pointers are nil by default. Dereferencing nil panics.
failure_modes.fix: Initialize: p := new(int) or var x int; p := &x.

failure_modes.scenario: Beginner writes for _, v := range slice { ptrs = append(ptrs, &v) } and all point to last value.
failure_modes.cause: Go reuses single loop variable v per iteration - &v is same address.
failure_modes.fix: Copy inside loop: v := v; ptrs = append(ptrs, &v), or use &slice[i].

hidden_magic_checks: Fix nil pointer dereference by tracing root cause.
Explain loop variable capture bug and two ways to fix.
Distinguish &val vs new(Type) vs nil sentinel.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go stdlib avoids nil pointer panics by checking before dereference: if p != nil. net/http Request.Body can be nil - handlers must check.
zero_magic.mental_model: Beginner pointer mistakes share pattern: assuming pointer is valid when nil, or assuming Go magic. Fix: verify initialization before dereference.
performance_implications: Nil dereference panics are fast - one null check - but cause full stack unwind.
Checking p != nil costs one branch - negligibly cheap.
Loop variable capture copies value each iteration O(n).
zero_magic.problem_solved: Pointer mistakes are most common panic cause for beginners. Each mistake teaches important lesson about Go's memory model.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Identify and fix four pointer bugs: nil dereference, loop capture, pointer to literal, returning pointer to stack zero value.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every code review catches pointer mistakes. Service crashes from nil pointer panics are common. Knowing common errors eliminates runtime failures.
step_by_step_execution: Always check nil before dereference: if p != nil { *p = ... }.
Use new(T) or &var - never &literal.
In loops, capture copy: v := v or &slice[i].
Returning pointer to local var is safe - compiler handles escape.
Document nil meaning in pointer params.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Every Go program using pointers can crash from nil dereference. Runtime check inserted by compiler for every dereference. Loop capture bug fixed in Go 1.22 (per-iteration variable).
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Pointer mistakes are most common 'why did my program crash?' confusion. Cataloging top mistakes and fixes turns frustrating bugs into teachable moments.

items.title: Function basics
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Function basics in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Declaring a function inside another function and expecting it to be accessible outside — Go only supports package-level and method-level functions, not nested named functions.
Forgetting to call the function — writing `myFunc` instead of `myFunc()` compiles but does nothing, leading to confusing no-op behavior.
Omitting return statement in a function that declares return types — causes a compile error, but the error message may not clearly point to the missing return.
execution_timeline: Package init functions run automatically before main — their execution order follows import dependency order, not source file order.
The compiler checks function signatures (name, parameters, return types) at compile time — type mismatches fail early.
At runtime, function calls push a stack frame with parameters and return address — the callee executes and pops back to the caller.
Named return values are initialized to their zero values when the frame is allocated — a naked return returns the current values of those named variables.
After the function body completes, any deferred functions run in LIFO order before the function returns to its caller.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer writes `if err != nil { return }` inside a function that declares return values but omits the return statement entirely.
failure_modes.cause: Go requires an explicit return statement when return types are declared — the function must end with `return` or a terminating statement.
failure_modes.fix: Always end functions with return types with `return` followed by the values or named returns.

failure_modes.scenario: Using `os.Exit(1)` instead of `return` in main to signal failure — deferred functions don't run.
failure_modes.cause: os.Exit skips all deferred calls because it terminates the process immediately.
failure_modes.fix: Use `return` with a non-zero exit code via `os.Exit` only when no cleanup is needed, or use `defer` before os.Exit.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict what happens when a function with named return values panics before reaching the return statement.
Learner must identify why `defer` statements after `os.Exit` in main do not execute.
Learner must explain the difference between a function signature `func() int` and `func() (i int)` — both compile but handle returns differently.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The standard library uses function declarations for every entry point, handler, and utility. `http.ListenAndServe` takes a `func(http.ResponseWriter, *http.Request)` — this is Go's primary unit of behavior. Testing uses `func(*testing.T)` as the test function signature. Every concurrent goroutine starts with `go func()`.
zero_magic.mental_model: A function is a reusable block of code with a name, optional inputs (parameters), and optional outputs (return values). The function signature is the contract — it tells callers what to provide and what to expect back.
performance_implications: Function calls have a small overhead (push frame, jump, pop frame) — negligible for most code but measurable in tight loops.
Inlining (the compiler replacing a function call with the body) avoids this overhead for small/simple functions — the compiler decides automatically.
Closures (anonymous functions that capture variables) may allocate on the heap if the closure escapes — avoid in hot paths.
Deferred function calls have slightly more overhead than direct calls — fine for I/O cleanup but measurable in extremely tight loops.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Functions solve the problem of code organization and reuse. Without functions, every program would be one long flat list of statements — impossible to read, test, or maintain. Functions let developers name a block of logic, invoke it from anywhere, and compose larger behaviors from smaller ones.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a function that takes a slice of integers, computes the sum, and returns both the sum and a boolean indicating whether the result is even. They must also write a test that calls the function and verifies both return values.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every Go package uses functions. HTTP handlers are functions, database query wrappers are functions, middleware chains compose functions. In production Go code, functions are the boundary between packages — a package's exported functions define its API surface.
step_by_step_execution: Caller evaluates arguments and pushes them onto the call stack
The return address (next instruction after the call) is saved on the stack
Execution jumps to the function body — parameters are bound to the argument values
The function body executes line by line, possibly declaring local variables on the stack
When `return` is reached (or the function ends), return values are placed in the designated stack/memory area
The stack frame is popped and execution resumes at the saved return address in the caller
zero_magic.under_the_hood: In Go's compiled output, each function is a block of machine code with a known entry point. The calling convention passes arguments on the stack (or in registers on newer architectures). The stack frame holds the return address, parameters, local variables, and space for return values. Go's stack is dynamically grown — if a function call would overflow the current stack, the runtime copies the stack to a larger block, updating pointers.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Functions exist in every general-purpose programming language because they are the fundamental unit of procedural abstraction. Go's function model is intentionally simple — no default parameters, no function overloading, no optional arguments — because clarity and readability are preferred over syntactic convenience.

items.title: Parameters
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Parameters in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Declaring a function with parameters but never using them inside the body -- results in a compile error in Go.
Confusing arguments and parameters -- the parameter is the variable in the function signature, the argument is the value passed at the call site.
Modifying a parameter inside the function expecting the caller to see the change -- Go passes by value, so the original is unchanged unless a pointer is used.
execution_timeline: The caller evaluates each argument expression to a value before the call.
Go copies each argument value into the corresponding parameter variable on the stack.
The function body executes, using the parameter variables as local variables.
The function returns and the stack frame with parameter space is popped.
Concurrent goroutines each have their own copy of parameters -- no shared mutable state from function arguments.

failure_modes.scenario: A learner passes a large struct by value and discovers the function runs slowly.
failure_modes.cause: Every parameter in Go is passed by value -- a copy of the entire struct is made on each call.
failure_modes.fix: Pass the struct by pointer instead: `func f(s *LargeStruct)` to only copy the pointer (8 bytes).

failure_modes.scenario: A learner calls a function expecting side effects on a slice, but the caller's slice is unchanged.
failure_modes.cause: Slices are passed by value -- the header (pointer, length, capacity) is copied. Changes to length/capacity inside the function don't affect the caller's header.
failure_modes.fix: Return the modified slice or pass a pointer to the slice: `func f(s *[]int)`.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain what happens when a slice parameter has its length changed inside the function -- does the caller see it?
Learner must predict output when a pointer parameter is reassigned to nil inside the function.
Learner must compare behavior between passing a map by value vs passing a struct by value -- maps are reference types with a different copying behavior.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Every standard library function uses parameters. `http.HandlerFunc` takes `(http.ResponseWriter, *http.Request)`, `ioutil.ReadFile` takes `(string)`, `fmt.Sprintf` takes `(string, ...interface{})`. Parameter types define the contract for the function caller.
zero_magic.mental_model: A parameter is a local variable that receives its initial value from the caller's argument. The function signature declares what types the caller must supply, and the function body uses those values.
performance_implications: Parameters passed by value incur a copy cost proportional to the type size -- small types (int, bool, pointers) are essentially free.
Large struct parameters cause measurable copy overhead -- pass by pointer for structs larger than 64 bytes.
Interface parameters involve a dynamic dispatch -- the concrete type must be boxed into an interface value, which may escape to the heap.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without parameters, functions could only operate on global variables, making code impossible to test, reason about, or reuse in different contexts. Parameters make functions self-contained: all inputs are explicit in the signature, and the function depends only on its parameters (when it has no globals).
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a function `greet(name string, greeting string) string` that combines the greeting and name, then write tests for at least three different (greeting, name) pairs.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every HTTP handler, database query function, and API endpoint in Go uses parameters. A typical handler signature `func GetUser(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request)` takes the response writer and request as parameters. Parameters make functions testable by allowing different inputs to produce different outputs.
step_by_step_execution: Identify what inputs the function needs -- these become its parameters.
Choose a meaningful name and a type for each parameter in the function signature.
Use the parameter variables inside the function body as if they were local variables.
Call the function from another location, passing arguments that match the parameter types.
Remember that modifications to value-type parameters are invisible to the caller.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: In Go's calling convention, each parameter is allocated space in the caller's stack frame. The compiler ensures the argument expression is evaluated and copied into the parameter slot before the function call. For interface parameters, the compiler generates code to create an `iface` or `eface` struct (type pointer + data pointer) and passes that two-word value.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Parameters are the primary mechanism for supplying input to a function. Without them, every function would need to read from global state, making code tightly coupled and untestable. Go's explicit parameter lists make data flow visible at a glance.

items.title: Return values
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Return values in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Declaring return types but forgetting the `return` statement -- causes a compile error in Go.
Using unnamed return values and still using `return` with no values -- leads to zero values being returned silently.
Assuming a function with a return type but no `return` will panic at runtime -- it actually won't compile.
execution_timeline: Compiler checks that every code path through the function ends in a `return` statement matching the declared return types.
If using named return values, Go initializes them to their zero values when the stack frame is created.
When `return` is reached, the return values are placed in the designated stack slots.
The stack frame is popped and the caller reads the return values from the expected location.
If the function panics, return values are zero values -- the panic unwinds without setting them.

failure_modes.scenario: A function declares `(int, error)` but the developer returns only `42` without an error when there is no error.
failure_modes.cause: Go requires all return values in the return statement: `return 42, nil`.
failure_modes.fix: Always match the number and types of return values exactly: `return result, nil` when successful.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer writes `return` (a naked return) inside a function with unnamed return values.
failure_modes.cause: Naked return is only allowed when the function has named return values.
failure_modes.fix: Either name the return values or use explicit `return value1, value2` syntax.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify what happens when a function with named return values panics -- are the return values set?
Learner must predict the output of a function that uses deferred recovery and named return values.
Learner must explain why `return nil, nil` is valid for a `(interface{}, error)` return but causes a nil interface dereference if used incorrectly.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Every function in Go that produces output uses return values. `os.Open` returns `(*File, error)`, `strconv.Atoi` returns `(int, error)`, `io.ReadAll` returns `([]byte, error)`. The idiomatic Go pattern is to return results as first values and errors as the last return value.
zero_magic.mental_model: Return values are the function's output channels. The function signature declares what types of values it produces, and the caller receives those values.
performance_implications: Returning large structs by value copies the entire struct -- use pointers for large return types.
Returning an interface boxes the concrete type into an interface value -- may cause heap allocation.
Multiple return values are passed in a hidden struct or registers -- no heap allocation for small types.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without return values, functions could only communicate results through mutation (global variables, pointer parameters), which is error-prone and hard to trace. Return values make a function's output explicit, type-checked at compile time, and available to the caller as expressions.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a function with multiple return values (a quotient and remainder for integer division), handle the error case (division by zero), and write a test covering both success and failure paths.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Go's idiomatic `result, err` pattern is used in virtually every production Go codebase. Database `QueryRow` returns `(Row, error)`, HTTP clients return `(Response, error)`, and JSON functions return `(Output, error)`. This convention makes error handling explicit and chaining natural.
step_by_step_execution: Determine what values the function should produce -- these become its return types.
Declare the return types in the function signature: `func f() (int, error)`.
In each code path, place a `return` statement with values matching the declared types.
If using named return values, a bare `return` returns the current values of those variables.
The caller assigns or uses the return values immediately: `result, err := f()`.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go passes return values on the stack or in registers depending on the architecture and compiler version. For multiple return values, the compiler treats them as a hidden aggregate -- each return value occupies its own slot in the caller's frame. Named return values are just local variables that happen to be returned automatically on `return`.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Return values are how functions produce output. Without them, all effects would be side effects (mutation, I/O), making programs harder to reason about. Go's support for multiple return values makes error handling especially natural -- the error travels alongside the result.

items.title: Multiple return values
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Multiple return values in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Swapping the order of return values -- convention is result first, error last.
Using multiple return values for unrelated outputs instead of returning a struct.
Calling a function but only capturing the first return value with `_` for the error.
Forgetting to handle the error because multiple returns make it easy to ignore.
execution_timeline: The function computes all return values before the `return` statement.
The compiler verifies each return value matches its declared type.
The runtime places each return value in its designated slot in the caller's frame.
The caller destructures the return values into separate variables.
Go's garbage collector handles any heap-allocated return values after the caller is done with them.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer returns `result, err` but uses `result` before checking `err` -- nil pointer dereference at runtime.
failure_modes.cause: Convention says: check error first, use result second. Violating this order causes crashes.
failure_modes.fix: Always check `err != nil` before using the result value.

failure_modes.scenario: Using multiple return values for (min, max, sum, count) instead of a single struct.
failure_modes.cause: More than three return values becomes confusing -- the caller must remember the order.
failure_modes.fix: Group related values into a struct: `type Stats struct { Min, Max, Sum, Count int }`.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict the output when a function has two return values and the caller swaps their variable names.
Learner must explain why multiple return values are not tuples in the Python sense -- Go has no tuple type.
Learner must identify when to use multiple returns vs returning a struct.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Multiple return values are used throughout the standard library: `os.Open` returns `(*File, error)`, `strconv.ParseInt` returns `(int64, error)`, `bytes.SplitN` returns `([][]byte, int)`. The idiom of returning `(value, error)` is so universal that Go tooling and linters expect it.
zero_magic.mental_model: A function with multiple return values is like a machine with multiple output chutes. Each chute produces a specific type of output. The caller must be ready to catch all outputs -- Go does not allow ignoring return values.
performance_implications: Multiple return values have no additional overhead over a single struct return -- they are laid out as a contiguous block in the caller's frame.
Returning (value, error) avoids heap allocation that would be needed for a result pointer in languages with single returns.
The compiler can optimize multiple small returns into registers on supported architectures.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Many operations naturally produce multiple outputs: reading a file produces data and an error, dividing integers produces quotient and remainder, parsing input produces the value and the remaining string. Multiple return values make these natural pairs explicit in the type system without needing wrapper types.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a function `divideAndRemainder(a, b int) (int, int, error)` that returns quotient, remainder, and an error if b is zero. Then write a table-driven test covering normal and edge cases.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Go's `result, err` pattern is the standard for all I/O operations. `db.QueryRow(...).Scan(&id, &name)` uses multiple returns from Scan. `json.Marshal` returns `([]byte, error)`. The convention makes it impossible to accidentally use a result when the operation failed.
step_by_step_execution: List the multiple outputs the operation produces.
Declare them in the function signature: `func f() (T1, T2, error)`.
In the body, compute all outputs and return them in the declared order.
At the call site, capture with `v1, v2, err := f()`.
Always check `err` before using `v1` or `v2`.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The compiler treats multiple returns as an implicit struct -- each return value occupies a slot in the caller's stack frame. The return statement packs all values into their respective slots atomically. On amd64, small returns use registers; larger returns spill to the stack.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Multiple return values make error handling natural and ubiquitous in Go. Without them, errors would be hidden behind exceptions or status fields. Go's explicit `result, err` pattern forces the developer to acknowledge every error path at the call site.

items.title: Scope
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Scope in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Declaring a variable in an if statement and expecting it to be accessible after the if block.
Shadowing a package-level variable with a local variable of the same name inside a function.
Accessing a variable declared in a for loop outside the loop body.
Assuming variables declared in a switch case are accessible in subsequent cases.
execution_timeline: At compile time, the compiler determines the scope of every variable based on its declaration position.
Block-scoped variables (declared inside `{}`, `if`, `for`, `switch`) are accessible only within that block.
Inner scopes can shadow outer variables -- the compiler resolves each identifier to the innermost declaration.
At runtime, block-scoped variables are allocated on the stack and their lifetime ends when the block exits.
Closures that capture block-scoped variables keep them alive via heap escape.

failure_modes.scenario: A variable declared inside an `if` block with `:=` is used after the `if` block, causing 'undefined' compile error.
failure_modes.cause: The `:=` declares a new variable scoped to the if block, which ends at the closing `}`.
failure_modes.fix: Declare the variable before the `if` statement, then assign inside the block.

failure_modes.scenario: A package-level variable is shadowed by a local variable inside a function, causing unexpected behavior.
failure_modes.cause: The local declaration `:=` creates a new variable that hides the package-level one within the function body.
failure_modes.fix: Use assignment `=` instead of `:=` inside the function to reuse the package-level variable.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify shadowing in nested scopes and predict which variable a reference resolves to.
Learner must explain why loop variables captured by closures in pre-Go 1.22 had surprising behavior.
Learner must trace variable lifetimes across function calls and determine when they can be garbage collected.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's lexical scoping is consistent everywhere: `if`, `for`, `switch`, and bare `{}` blocks all create new scopes. The standard library uses block scoping extensively -- e.g., `if err := r.ParseForm(); err != nil { return err }` scopes the error variable to the if block.
zero_magic.mental_model: Scope is like nested rooms: a variable declared in a room is visible in that room and any rooms within it, but not outside. Each `{` opens a new room, each `}` closes it. If an inner room has a variable with the same name as an outer room, the inner one is what you see -- shadowing.
performance_implications: Block-scoped variables are allocated on the stack and freed when the block exits -- zero overhead.
Variables captured by closures escape to the heap -- can cause GC pressure in hot loops.
The compiler detects when a variable can be stack-allocated vs heap-allocated based on escape analysis.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Scope solves the problem of name collisions and variable lifetime management. Without scoping rules, every variable would need a unique name across the entire program. Scope ensures variables exist only where they are needed and don't interfere with unrelated code.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a program with nested scopes (a function containing a for loop containing an if statement, each with variables of the same name), predict the output, and explain which reference resolves to which declaration.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: In production Go code, the `if err := ...; err != nil { return }` pattern scopes error variables to exactly where they are needed. Loop counters are scoped to the loop. Every Go developer uses scope intuitively many times an hour.
step_by_step_execution: Identify where a variable needs to be accessible -- that determines its declaration scope.
Declare variables at the smallest scope that covers all usage sites.
Be aware that `:=` creates new variables in the current scope, while `=` assigns to existing variables.
Watch for shadowing when using `:=` in nested scopes.
When debugging, check which declaration a variable name refers to by looking at the innermost scope first.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The Go compiler tracks scope through a symbol table. Each scope is a nested entry in the table. When resolving an identifier, the compiler walks from innermost to outermost scope. The debugger (Delve) uses the same scoping rules.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Scope is fundamental to all programming languages. Without it, naming collisions would be inevitable in programs of any size. Go's lexical scoping is traditional and predictable -- no hoisting or implicit globals.

items.title: Call stack mental model
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Call stack mental model in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Thinking the call stack is infinite -- deep recursion causes stack overflow.
Confusing the stack (function calls) with the heap (dynamic allocation).
Expecting goroutines to share the same call stack -- each goroutine has its own.
Believing local variables are always on the stack -- they escape to the heap when the compiler decides they must.
execution_timeline: When function A calls function B, the runtime pushes a stack frame for B onto the call stack.
The frame contains B's parameters, local variables, and return address back to A.
When B returns, its frame is popped and execution resumes at the saved return address in A's frame.
If B calls C, another frame is pushed -- the stack grows downward in memory.
Each goroutine starts with a small stack (2 KB) that grows and shrinks as needed.

failure_modes.scenario: Recursive function causes stack overflow with a 'morestack' runtime error.
failure_modes.cause: Each recursive call pushes a new frame, and the stack eventually exceeds its allocated size.
failure_modes.fix: Convert recursion to iteration, increase stack size, or ensure the recursion terminates within a bounded depth.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer expects a variable to persist after the function returns because it is 'on the stack'.
failure_modes.cause: Stack-allocated variables are invalid once the function returns -- the memory is reused by subsequent calls.
failure_modes.fix: Use heap allocation when a value must outlive its declaring function.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict the stack contents when a recursive function calls itself 3 times.
Learner must explain why Go can have goroutines with very small stacks while threads need MB-sized stacks.
Learner must identify which variables escape to the heap in a given function.
Learner must trace the call stack from a panic output back to the source.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's garbage collector uses the call stack to find roots (local variables in active frames). The scheduler saves goroutine stacks when switching. `runtime.Caller` and `runtime.Callers` inspect the call stack programmatically. Stack traces from panics are the primary debugging tool.
zero_magic.mental_model: The call stack is like a stack of sticky notes. Each function call puts a new note on top. The note has the function's local variables and a 'return here' address. When the function finishes, the note is removed and execution follows the return address.
performance_implications: Stack allocation is faster than heap allocation -- just a pointer bump.
Deep function calls (especially recursion) use more stack space and may cause stack growth (slow).
Go's dynamic stack starts at 2 KB and grows in 2x increments -- stack copying involves pointer adjustment.
Avoid deep recursion in production -- use iteration when depth exceeds ~1000 calls.
zero_magic.problem_solved: The call stack solves the problem of tracking where to resume execution after a function returns. Without a stack, the program would have no memory of which function called which. The stack also provides automatic storage for local variables.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a recursive factorial function, add print statements to trace the call and return stack, run it, and explain the stack content at each level of recursion.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every time you see a panic stack trace in Go, you are reading the call stack. Debuggers display the call stack to show how execution reached the current line. Profilers sample the call stack to determine where CPU time is spent.
step_by_step_execution: When function A calls function B, picture execution of A pausing and B starting.
Go pushes a frame for B -- allocate space for parameters, locals, and return address.
B executes -- when it hits `return`, Go reads the return address from the frame.
Go pops B's frame and execution continues in A right after the call.
The call stack, visible via `debug.PrintStack()` or panic output, shows this history.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go does not use the hardware stack for goroutines -- each goroutine has its own stack allocated on the heap. The stack is dynamically grown: when a function call would exceed the current stack, the runtime allocates a larger stack (2x), copies all frames, and adjusts pointers.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: The call stack is a fundamental computer science concept that every programmer needs to understand to debug code, trace execution, and manage recursion. Go's stack model is particularly important because it enables lightweight goroutines.

items.title: Passing by value
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Passing by value in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Modifying a map inside a function and expecting the caller not to see the change -- maps are reference types.
Passing a struct to a function, modifying it, and expecting the caller to see the modification.
Thinking slices are 'passed by reference' -- the header is copied but the underlying array is shared.
Using `&variable` as an argument when the parameter is a value, not a pointer.
execution_timeline: The caller evaluates the argument expression and copies the result into the parameter slot.
For value types (int, struct, array), the entire value is copied.
For reference types (pointer, map, slice, channel, interface), the header/descriptor is copied, but the underlying data is shared.
The function operates on its copy -- changes to value-type parameters don't affect the caller.
When the function returns, the copied parameter space is reclaimed with the stack frame.

failure_modes.scenario: A function takes a struct parameter, assigns `p.Field = 42`, but the caller's struct doesn't change.
failure_modes.cause: The struct was passed by value -- the function received a copy. Modifications only affect the copy.
failure_modes.fix: Either pass a pointer `*Struct` or return the modified struct: `return p`.

failure_modes.scenario: A function takes a slice parameter and appends to it -- the caller's length doesn't show the new elements.
failure_modes.cause: The slice header (pointer, len, cap) was copied. `append` may reallocate, and the original header still points to the old array.
failure_modes.fix: Return the new slice: `return append(s, elem)` or pass a pointer to the slice: `func f(s *[]int)`.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict whether a map modification inside a function is visible to the caller.
Learner must explain why passing an array `[3]int` by value copies all 3 elements but a `[]int` only copies the header.
Learner must identify which types in Go are value types vs reference types.
Learner must trace the state of a slice after passing it to a function that does multiple `append`s.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go always passes by value. The standard library uses this consistently: `fmt.Fprint(w io.Writer, a ...interface{})` receives `w` as an interface value. `bytes.Buffer.Write` uses a pointer receiver because the buffer state must be mutated.
zero_magic.mental_model: Passing by value means the function gets its own copy of the argument -- like photocopying a document and handing the copy. For reference types (map, slice, pointer), the photocopy is of the address, so both copies point to the same underlying data.
performance_implications: Small types (int, bool, float64, pointers) are essentially free to copy.
Large structs cost proportional to their size -- use pointer parameters for structs > 64 bytes.
Interface parameters cause the concrete value to be boxed -- may allocate on the heap if the value is larger than two words.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Pass by value prevents functions from accidentally modifying the caller's data, making programs safer. The caller controls what the function sees: if mutation is desired, the caller explicitly passes a pointer. No hidden mutation.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a function that takes an int, a slice, and a map, modifies each, and then predict which changes the caller sees. Write tests to verify predictions.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: In production Go, pass by value is the default. When you read a config struct in an HTTP handler, you pass it by value to avoid accidental mutation. When you need to modify, you pass `*Config`. The caller always knows whether mutation is possible.
step_by_step_execution: When calling a function, Go copies the argument -- one level of indirection removal.
For value types (int, bool, string, struct, array): the entire value is copied.
For reference types (pointer, map, slice, channel, function, interface): the reference header is copied.
Mutations to value-type parameters are invisible to the caller.
Mutations to the contents of reference-type parameters may be visible to the caller.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The compiler determines whether to copy the argument to the stack or pass via registers. For `interface{}` parameters, the compiler generates an `eface` struct (type *uintptr, data unsafe.Pointer) and passes this two-word value.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Pass by value is Go's default because it makes data flow explicit. You can read a function signature and know what it can and cannot modify. This explicitness is central to Go's philosophy.

items.title: Pointer and value mutation behavior
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Pointer and value mutation behavior in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Thinking a function that receives a struct value can modify the caller's original — Go copies the entire struct, so modifications inside the function are lost when the function returns.
Using pointers everywhere to avoid copying without considering mutation semantics — passing a pointer allows the callee to modify the original, which may be unintended and cause hard-to-find bugs.
Mixing pointer and value receivers on the same type — some methods mutate (pointer receiver) and some do not (value receiver), making the API inconsistent and surprising callers.
Taking the address of a loop variable and storing it — the address is the same for every iteration, and by the time the stored pointer is used, the loop variable holds its final value.
Assigning a pointer to an interface variable — the interface holds a copy of the pointer, not a copy of the value, so mutations through the interface mutate the original.
execution_timeline: Allocate MyStruct{X: 10} on caller's stack at offset SP+0: [10 | 0 | 0 ...]
Call func(s MyStruct): allocate copy at callee's SP-8, copy all bytes: [10 | 0 | 0 ...]
Callee sets s.X = 20: callee's copy at SP-8 becomes [20 | 0 | 0 ...], caller's original at SP+0 still [10]
After return: callee's frame popped, caller's v.X is still 10
Call func(p *MyStruct): allocate pointer copy at callee's SP-8: p = 0x1234 (points to caller's SP+0)
Callee sets p.X = 20: write to address 0x1234 (caller's SP+0), caller's v.X becomes 20
failure_modes: Unintended mutation: a function receives a *Config, modifies a field, and the caller's Config is changed silently — the caller did not expect its config to be modified and may use stale values later.

Slice append ambiguity: append on a slice received by value may or may not modify the original backing array depending on capacity — if cap > len, append writes to the original backing array, mutating the caller's data; if cap == len, append allocates a new backing array, and the caller's slice is untouched.
Map nil assignment: assigning to a nil map panics, but assigning to a nil pointer to a struct panics differently — learners confuse nil map assignment panic with nil pointer dereference.

Interface mutation illusion: storing a *T in an interface{} and calling a method that mutates through the pointer works, but storing a T in an interface{} and calling the same method (value receiver) does not mutate — the interface holds a copy of T.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must trace the stack frames of a function that receives a struct by value, modifies it, and returns — explaining which stack frame each variable lives in and why the modification is invisible to the caller.

Learner must predict the output of a program that passes a slice to a function, appends to it, and prints both the caller's and callee's slice after the function returns — explaining the backing array sharing behavior.
Learner must explain why a map passed to a function can have elements added by the callee and visible to the caller, even though Go is pass-by-value — maps are pointers under the hood.
Learner must rewrite a buggy function that takes a struct by value and tries to modify it, changing it to use a pointer receiver and ensuring the caller sees the mutation.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go is always pass-by-value — every function parameter is a copy. The standard library exploits this: io.Reader's Read method takes a []byte (slice header is copied, but backing array is shared), time.Date takes all components by value, and json.Unmarshal takes an interface{} (the pointer inside the interface is copied, pointing to the original struct). Pointer receivers in methods are the idiomatic way to express mutation: func (p *MyStruct) Mutate().
zero_magic.mental_model: Go is pass-by-value, always. Every function parameter is a copy of the caller's argument. If the argument is a 100-byte struct, 100 bytes are copied onto the callee's stack. If the argument is a pointer (always 8 bytes on 64-bit), the pointer is copied — but both the original and the copy point to the same memory. Mutation through a copied pointer modifies the original memory. This is not pass-by-reference — it is pass-by-value where the value happens to be an address.
performance_implications: Passing large structs by value copies their entire memory — for structs > 64 bytes, the copy overhead can exceed the cost of a pointer dereference + allocation.
Passing pointers avoids copying the struct but adds heap-allocation pressure if the pointer escapes (e.g., returned or stored in an interface).
Escape analysis: returning a pointer to a local struct allocates on heap; returning a struct by value stays on stack.
Slice and map headers are small (24 bytes for slice, 8 bytes for map) — passing them by value copies only the header, not the backing data.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers from Java or Python backgrounds assume objects are passed by reference and modifications in functions are visible to callers. They learn Go is pass-by-value but then discover that map and slice modifications inside a function ARE visible to the caller — creating confusion about when mutation applies. This lesson makes the memory model explicit: every parameter is a copy, but the semantics of what is copied (value vs pointer, slice header vs backing array, map descriptor) determines mutation visibility.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a program that passes a struct, a slice, and a map to a function, mutates each inside the function, and prints both inside and outside. They must explain for each type whether the mutation is visible to the caller and why — tracing the stack and heap allocation.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every Go codebase depends on understanding value vs pointer mutation semantics. Misunderstanding causes data races (multiple goroutines holding pointers to the same struct), silent data corruption (modifying a slice backing array shared with another caller), and logic bugs (trying to append to a slice inside a function and expecting the caller's length to update).
step_by_step_execution: Caller creates v := MyStruct{Name: "alice", Age: 30} on its stack frame
Caller calls updateName(v, "bob") — copies entire MyStruct onto callee's stack
Callee's v is an independent copy at a different memory address
Callee sets v.Name = "bob" — modifies its own copy only
Callee returns — its stack frame is reclaimed
Caller prints v.Name — still "alice", because the original was never touched
Caller calls updateNamePtr(&v, "bob") — copies the address of v (8 bytes) onto callee's stack
Callee's p points to caller's v at its original address
Callee sets p.Name = "bob" — dereferences the pointer and writes to the caller's original
Caller prints v.Name — now "bob"
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's calling convention (Go 1.17+) passes function arguments and return values in registers (up to a certain size), then on the stack for larger structs. For a struct passed by value, the compiler emits a memcpy of the struct's bytes from the caller's frame to the callee's frame (or registers). For a pointer, only the 8-byte address is copied. Maps and channels are implemented as pointers to runtime descriptors (hmap for maps, hchan for channels) — when you pass a map to a function, you copy the pointer, not the entire descriptor. Slices are structs {ptr, len, cap} — the header is copied, but the ptr field points to the same backing array. This is why map modifications and slice element changes inside a function are visible to the caller, but slice append (which may change len/cap) is not.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Pass-by-value is Go's default because it eliminates the ambiguity of pass-by-reference: in C++, a function signature can be void f(T), void f(T&), or void f(const T&) — all three look the same at the call site but have different mutation semantics. In Go, if you see f(v), the caller's v is never modified. If the function needs to modify the caller's data, it must take a pointer, and the call site has f(&v) which makes the intent explicit. This explicitness prevents entire classes of bugs where a function silently mutates its arguments.

items.title: Errors as values
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Errors as values in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Assigning the result of a function call to a variable but checking a different variable for the error — the error is silently discarded.
Using _ = fmt.Sprintf() to ignore an error return, believing the function never fails in practice — Sprintf documents it returns an error for a reason.
Nesting if err != nil blocks five levels deep instead of returning early — the happy path is buried in indentation and the error paths are indistinguishable.
Checking err != nil but doing nothing meaningful with the error — logging and continuing as if nothing happened, leaving the system in an unknown state.
failure_modes: Silent discard: a function returns an error, the caller assigns to _ and proceeds — the system continues in a degraded state with no indication that something failed.
Error swallowed by defer: a cleanup function returns an error but the caller uses defer and never checks the return value — the cleanup failure is invisible.
Shadowed err: in a loop, err is declared with := inside the loop body, shadowing the outer err — the loop's last error is never visible after the loop ends.
Sentinel error comparison with ==: errors.New returns a new pointer each time, so err == io.EOF fails if the error was wrapped with fmt.Errorf.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must trace the error return path of a function with 3 exit points and identify which errors are checked and which are discarded.
Learner must rewrite nested if-err blocks using early returns and explain why the refactored version is safer.
Learner must identify a shadowed err variable in a loop and explain what the caller sees after the loop exits.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's standard library treats errors as values everywhere: os.Open returns (*File, error), json.Decode returns error, http.Get returns (*Response, error). The convention 'if err != nil { return err }' appears in virtually every Go function. Unlike exceptions, errors in Go are ordinary values that can be stored, compared, wrapped, and inspected.
zero_magic.mental_model: An error is a value, not a control flow mechanism. If a function can fail, it returns an error as a regular return value — the caller decides what to do with it. There is no throw, no catch, no stack unwinding. The error value is just data: it can be logged, wrapped, compared, or ignored (but should not be).
performance_implications: Every if err != nil check is a branch that the CPU must predict — on modern CPUs, the predictable 'no error' path is fast, but error paths incur branch misprediction penalties.
Wrapping errors with fmt.Errorf("context: %w", err) allocates a new string — on hot paths with predictable errors, consider sentinel errors and direct comparison to avoid allocation.

Returning an error requires the caller to check it — if a caller checks errors on only 80% of calls, 20% of failures are invisible. The performance cost of checking is negligible (~1ns), but the cost of NOT checking can be a production outage.
errors.Is and errors.As walk the error chain — chains longer than 3-4 levels have linear traversal cost. Keep error chains shallow for frequently-checked errors.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Exception-based languages hide error handling in catch blocks far from the failure site, making it easy to forget that a function can fail. Go's errors-as-values approach forces the developer to handle errors at the call site, making failure paths visible and explicit in the code.
production_examples: HTTP handler pattern: every call to r.FormValue, json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode, and w.Write is checked for error before proceeding — a malformed request is rejected early rather than producing a partial response.
Multi-step initialization: each step (config load, DB connect, server start) returns an error; startup fails fast on the first error rather than continuing with nil pointers.
Deferred cleanup with error check: a file open is deferred with a closure that captures the error from file.Close, ensuring cleanup failures are not silently lost.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a function that reads a file, parses JSON, and processes each record — with every error path explicitly handled. They must then explain what happens at each failure point and why ignoring any single error would produce incorrect behavior.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go services use errors-as-values for every I/O operation, every database call, every API request, every configuration parse. Skipping an error check in production means a partial failure is invisible until it cascades into a full outage.
step_by_step_execution: Caller invokes os.Open(filename) — the function returns (*File, error)
Caller checks if err != nil — if non-nil, the *File is invalid and must not be used
Caller returns early or handles the error — the happy path continues only with a valid *File
Later, file.Read(buf) returns (int, error) — n may be > 0 even when err == io.EOF; caller must process n bytes before checking the error
Caller defers file.Close() — Close returns an error that is silently discarded unless captured via a named return or closure
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The error type in Go is a built-in interface: type error interface { Error() string }. Any type with an Error() string method satisfies it. errors.New returns a pointer to a simple struct with a string field. fmt.Errorf uses errors.New internally after formatting the string. When you return err, you return an interface value — if the underlying type is *os.PathError, the interface is non-nil even if the pointer is nil (see the nil interfaces lesson). The 'if err != nil' check is a runtime interface nil check comparing both the type and data pointers.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Go chose errors-as-values over exceptions because exceptions create invisible control flow — every statement can potentially throw, and the catch site may be arbitrarily far from the throw site. By making errors ordinary return values, Go ensures that failure handling is visible in the function signature and explicit at every call site.

items.title: Error wrapping
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Error wrapping in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using `fmt.Errorf` with `%w` but not importing the `errors` package.
Wrapping errors in a loop -- each wrap adds another layer, making the chain harder to read.
Wrapping an error with `fmt.Errorf` but not using `%w` -- the wrapping is lost and `errors.Is` won't match.
Wrapping errors unnecessarily -- not every error needs to be wrapped.
execution_timeline: An error occurs at the lowest level and is returned upward.
A mid-level function wraps the error with `fmt.Errorf("context: %w", err)`.
The wrapped error preserves access to the original via `errors.Is` and `errors.As`.
The top-level caller uses `errors.Is` to check for specific sentinel errors through the chain.
Go 1.20+ allows wrapping multiple errors with `errors.Join`.

failure_modes.scenario: Wrapping with `%v` instead of `%w` breaks `errors.Is` matching.
failure_modes.cause: `%v` formats the error as a string, losing the wrapping chain.
failure_modes.fix: Use `%w` in `fmt.Errorf` to preserve the error chain.

failure_modes.scenario: Double-wrapping at every level creates deeply nested errors, slow to unwrap.
failure_modes.cause: Each wrapping adds another layer. Deep chains (10+) slow down `errors.Is`.
failure_modes.fix: Only wrap at semantic boundaries, not at every function.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict whether `errors.Is(err, target)` returns true given a chain of wraps.
Learner must explain the difference between `fmt.Errorf` with `%w` vs custom error types implementing `Unwrap()`.
Learner must identify when wrapping is appropriate vs sentinel or custom error types.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The standard library uses wrapping in `net/http`: `http.ErrServerClosed` uses `errors.Is`. `os.PathError` has an `Unwrap` method. `net.OpError` wraps the underlying syscall error.
zero_magic.mental_model: Error wrapping is like putting an error in an envelope with a note. The envelope has your message, and the original error is inside. `errors.Is` opens all envelopes to find the original.
performance_implications: Wrapping via `fmt.Errorf("...%w...", err)` allocates a new error value -- measurable in hot paths.
`errors.Is` traverses the chain via `Unwrap()` -- O(n) where n is chain depth.
Custom error types with `Unwrap()` are cheaper than `fmt.Errorf` wrapping.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without wrapping, errors at higher levels have no context. Wrapping preserves the original error while adding the current layer's context, enabling meaningful messages without losing error identity.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a three-layer function chain where the top wraps the middle's error and the middle calls the bottom that returns a sentinel error. Write tests showing `errors.Is` finds the sentinel through the chain.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go services wrap errors at every I/O boundary: `db.Query` wraps with the query name, `http.Handler` wraps with the endpoint, `storage.Get` wraps with the key.
step_by_step_execution: At the error source, create or return the base error.
At each call level, decide if this layer adds meaningful context.
If yes, wrap: `return fmt.Errorf("doing step X: %w", err)`.
If no, return the error directly without wrapping.
At the top level, use `errors.Is` or `errors.As` to inspect the chain.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: `fmt.Errorf` with `%w` creates a `*fmt.wrapError` struct storing the format string and wrapped error. Its `Unwrap()` returns the inner error. `errors.Is` walks the chain by calling `Unwrap()` repeatedly.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Error wrapping solves the tension between preserving error identity (for programmatic checks) and adding context (for human readability).

items.title: errors.Is
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply errors.Is in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using `errors.Is` with a non-sentinel error value.
Calling `errors.Is(err, target)` with an error not created with `errors.New` or `fmt.Errorf`.
Checking `err == sentinel` instead of `errors.Is(err, sentinel)` when err might be wrapped.
Using `errors.Is` in an if statement but continuing execution without handling the case.
execution_timeline: A function returns an error, possibly wrapped at multiple levels.
The caller calls `errors.Is(err, target)` to check the error chain.
`errors.Is` calls `Unwrap()` to walk the chain.
If a match is found, `errors.Is` returns true.
The caller branches on the result to handle specific error conditions.

failure_modes.scenario: `errors.Is(err, target)` returns false even though the error originated from the sentinel.
failure_modes.cause: The error was wrapped with `%v` instead of `%w`, breaking the chain.
failure_modes.fix: Use `%w` in fmt.Errorf or a custom type with `Unwrap()`.

failure_modes.scenario: Using `err == io.EOF` works in simple cases but fails when wrapped.
failure_modes.cause: `==` compares identity, not the chain.
failure_modes.fix: Always use `errors.Is(err, io.EOF)` to walk the wrapping chain.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain when `errors.Is` returns false for a wrapped sentinel.
Learner must compare `errors.Is` with direct `==` comparison and explain which is safer.
Learner must trace `errors.Is` through a chain of 3 wrapped errors.
Learner must identify a bug where `==` is used instead of `errors.Is`.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The standard library uses `errors.Is` throughout: `os.IsNotExist(err)` calls `errors.Is(err, fs.ErrNotExist)`. `io.EOF` checks use `errors.Is(err, io.EOF)`. `errors.Is` is the standard way to check error identity.
zero_magic.mental_model: `errors.Is` is like a detective following a chain of envelopes. Each envelope has a `Unwrap()` that opens to reveal the next. The detective checks each envelope until finding the target or running out of envelopes.
performance_implications: `errors.Is` walks the chain -- O(n) comparisons where n is wrap depth.
For deeply wrapped errors (5+ layers), the cost is measurable but negligible in I/O-bound code.
Avoid creating 50+ level deep error chains.
zero_magic.problem_solved: `errors.Is` solves the problem of checking error identity through arbitrary wrapping layers. Without it, every error check would need to pattern-match strings or compare directly, both of which fail when errors are wrapped.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must create a sentinel error, a function that returns it wrapped in multiple layers, and write tests showing `errors.Is` correctly identifies the sentinel through the chain.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: `errors.Is(err, fs.ErrNotExist)` is used in every Go file-reading codebase. `errors.Is(err, io.EOF)` in every parsing library. `errors.Is(err, context.Canceled)` in every concurrent Go service.
step_by_step_execution: Import the `errors` package and have the target sentinel error.
Call `errors.Is(err, sentinel)` where `err` is the error to check.
`errors.Is` first compares the top-level error with `==`.
If no match, it calls `Unwrap()` and repeats until the chain is exhausted.
Return true if any error in the chain is the sentinel; false otherwise.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: `errors.Is` compares using `==`, then checks for an `Is(err error) bool` method, then calls `Unwrap()` to walk the chain. The chain walk is depth-first.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: `errors.Is` enables reliable error inspection without breaking abstraction boundaries. Functions can wrap errors with context, and callers can still check for specific conditions.

items.title: errors.As
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply errors.As in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using `errors.As` when `errors.Is` would suffice -- As is for type extraction, Is is for value equality.
Forgetting that `errors.As` requires a pointer to the target type.
Using `errors.As(err, &target)` where target is already nil -- the assignment happens on match.
Checking `errors.As` return value but then using target without verifying the assertion succeeded.
execution_timeline: An error occurs that may be a specific custom error type wrapped at multiple levels.
The caller calls `errors.As(err, &target)` where target is a pointer to the error type.
errors.As walks the chain via Unwrap, looking for any assignable error.
When found, it sets the target to the matched error and returns true.
The caller accesses type-specific fields or methods on the target.

failure_modes.scenario: Calling `errors.As(err, target)` instead of `errors.As(err, &target)` -- runtime panic or compile error.
failure_modes.cause: errors.As needs a pointer to assign the matched value.
failure_modes.fix: Always pass `&target`: `var myErr *MyErrorType; errors.As(err, &myErr)`.

failure_modes.scenario: errors.As finds the outermost match, not the innermost.
failure_modes.cause: errors.As walks from outermost to innermost and returns the first match.
failure_modes.fix: Structure custom error types deliberately, knowing errors.As matches the first in the chain.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain when to use errors.As vs errors.Is vs a regular type assertion.
Learner must predict which type errors.As matches in a chain with multiple types.
Learner must identify whether errors.As can match an interface type.
Learner must explain why errors.As requires a pointer argument.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The standard library uses errors.As for extracting `*os.PathError`, `*net.DNSError`, and `*url.Error`. Package `json` uses errors.As with `*json.SyntaxError` and `*json.UnmarshalTypeError`.
zero_magic.mental_model: errors.As is like a detective looking for a specific person in a chain of nested rooms. Each room (error) might contain the target type. When found, the detective hands you a reference to that person.
performance_implications: errors.As walks the chain performing type assertions -- O(n) where n is chain depth.
Each step involves reflection-like assignment -- slightly more expensive than errors.Is.
In hot paths, avoid deep error chains if using errors.As frequently.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Many errors in Go carry structured data (HTTP status code, SQL error code, filename). errors.As extracts the concrete error type from the wrapping chain so the caller can access type-specific fields.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must create a custom error type `HTTPError` with Code and Message fields, write a function returning this error wrapped in fmt.Errorf, and write a test using errors.As to extract and inspect the HTTPError fields.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go uses errors.As to extract `*net.DNSError` for DNS server IP, `*pgconn.PgError` for PostgreSQL error codes, and custom domain errors with correlation IDs.
step_by_step_execution: Define the target variable as a pointer: `var target *MyError`.
Call `errors.As(err, &target)`.
If it returns true, target now points to the matched error.
If it returns false, target is unchanged.
Always check the return value before using target.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: errors.As uses reflection-like assignment via the reflectlite package. It walks the error chain by calling `Unwrap()`. For each error, it checks if the error implements the target interface or if a concrete type can be assigned.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without errors.As, structured errors would be useless at higher levels -- their context is wrapped away. errors.As lets errors carry data through the wrapping chain while preserving extractability.
