items.title: Validation
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Validation in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Validating only at the UI layer and not in the domain/business logic layer.
Returning `error` from validation but not providing which field failed or why.
Using magic numbers or hardcoded strings for validation rules instead of constants or config.
Performing validation after the operation has already started modifying state.
execution_timeline: Input arrives at the system boundary (HTTP request, CLI args, file read).
Raw input is parsed into typed Go values (string to int, JSON to struct).
Validation checks run against the typed values: required fields, ranges, formats, business rules.
If validation fails, an error is returned with information about what failed and why.
If validation passes, the validated data is passed to the business logic.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer validates an email with a regex that rejects valid emails (+ addressing).
failure_modes.cause: Email validation is tricky -- a simple regex cannot match RFC 5322.
failure_modes.fix: Use Go's `net/mail.ParseAddress` for email validation.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer skips input length validation and a malicious user sends a 1 MB string.
failure_modes.cause: The system blindly accepted large input without checking size constraints.
failure_modes.fix: Always validate input size/length before processing: reject early, reject fast.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify validation bugs: missing required field checks, wrong range limits, incorrect format validation.
Learner must explain the difference between client-side validation (UX) and server-side validation (security).
Learner must design a validation function that returns structured errors per field.
Learner must distinguish between validation and authentication/authorization.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's standard library validates in `net/http.Request.ParseForm`, `json.Decoder.Decode`, `strconv.Atoi`. Third-party packages like `go-playground/validator` provide struct tag-based validation.
zero_magic.mental_model: Validation is a gatekeeper at the system boundary. Raw input arrives unchecked; the gatekeeper inspects it and either passes it through or rejects it with a reason. Garbage in, not passed through.
performance_implications: Validation is typically fast (microseconds per field) compared to I/O or business logic.
Expensive validation (regex, DB lookups) should be used sparingly or cached.
Validate at the boundary once, not repeatedly in each layer.
Short-circuit validation: check the cheapest checks first (length, required), then progressively more expensive checks.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without validation, bad input reaches core business logic and causes crashes, data corruption, or security vulnerabilities. Validation ensures that only well-formed, safe data enters the system.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a `ValidateUser` function that takes a `User` struct (Name, Email, Age) and returns a `[]ValidationError` where each error has a Field and Message. Include tests for: missing name, invalid email, negative age, and a valid user.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every web framework validates incoming JSON. Every database layer validates SQL queries. Every CLI validates flags and arguments. Validation is the first step in every non-trivial Go function that accepts external input.
step_by_step_execution: Parse raw input into Go types (string to int, JSON to struct, etc.).
Run structural validation: required fields, type correctness, range limits, format patterns.
Run business rule validation: uniqueness checks, state machine transitions, authorization.
Collect all validation failures (don't stop at first error when possible).
Return the complete error list to the caller with actionable messages.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Struct tag validators use reflection to read struct tags and iterate over fields. For each field, they apply the registered validation function. Custom validators can access the full struct for cross-field validation.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Validation is the first and most important defense against bad data. Centralized validation at boundaries keeps core logic clean and focused on happy-path behavior.

items.title: Orchestration
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Orchestration in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Making a function do too many things -- violating single responsibility.
Mixing business logic, I/O, and error handling with no structure in an orchestrator.
Skipping error handling in an orchestrator and letting a failed step corrupt the result.
Writing orchestrators that are untestable because they hardcode dependencies.
execution_timeline: Orchestrator receives the initial input or request.
Step 1: validate input -- if failed, return error early.
Step 2: fetch required data (DB, API) -- if failed, return error with context.
Step 3: process/transform data -- if failed, return error.
Step 4: persist or respond with the result -- if failed, may need to roll back.
Orchestrator returns the final result or the first error encountered.

failure_modes.scenario: An orchestrator calls steps A, B, C and step B fails, but step C still runs.
failure_modes.cause: The orchestrator didn't check the error from step B before proceeding.
failure_modes.fix: Check errors after each step and return immediately on failure.

failure_modes.scenario: An orchestrator performs DB writes in step B and step C fails -- partial DB writes remain.
failure_modes.cause: The orchestrator has no rollback or compensation logic.
failure_modes.fix: Use transaction boundaries for DB operations, or implement compensation/rollback in error paths.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify which responsibilities belong in an orchestrator vs in individual steps.
Learner must explain the testability benefits of orchestrators that accept interfaces.
Learner must design an orchestrator that handles partial failure with rollback.
Learner must identify error handling gaps in a given orchestrator implementation.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's standard library orchestrates in `net/http` -- the server accepts a connection, reads the request, finds the handler, calls it, writes the response. `database/sql` orchestrates connections: open, execute, close.
zero_magic.mental_model: An orchestrator is like a stage manager in a theater production. The manager doesn't act or build sets -- they coordinate: cue lights, cue sound, cue actor. If the lights fail, the manager stops the show.
performance_implications: Orchestrators add minimal overhead (function call + error check per step).
Well-structured orchestrators enable parallel steps via goroutines.
Orchestrators that hardcode I/O dependencies are harder to stub for benchmarks.
Sequential orchestrators are bounded by the slowest step.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Complex operations need multiple steps -- validate, fetch, process, persist. Without orchestration, business logic is scattered or duplicated. Orchestration provides a single visible entry point defining order, error handling, and data flow.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write an orchestrator `CreateOrder(userID string, items []Item) (Order, error)` that: validates items, checks inventory, calculates total, creates order. Each step is a separate function. Write tests verifying the orchestrator stops on step failure.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every HTTP handler in a production Go service is an orchestrator. Service layer orchestrators coordinate: load user, check permissions, execute operation, audit log, return result. Clean Architecture uses orchestrators at the use-case layer.
step_by_step_execution: Define the operation as a sequence of clear steps, each with single responsibility.
Write each step as a separate function with the parameters and return values it needs.
In the orchestrator, call each step in order, checking errors after each call.
Handle rollback/compensation for steps that cannot be safely retried.
Return the final result or the first error, wrapped with context about which step failed.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The orchestrator pattern maps to Go's sequential execution model. Each function call is synchronous -- the next step runs only after the previous step returns. For parallel steps, use sync.WaitGroup + goroutines.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without explicit orchestration, multi-step operations become tangled spaghetti code. Orchestration separates the 'what order' from the 'how', making both individually testable and understandable.

items.title: defer mechanics
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply defer mechanics in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Assuming deferred calls run immediately when the function exits — they run when the surrounding function returns, not when the block ends.
Capturing loop variables in a defer inside a loop — the defer captures the variable's address, and by the time the defer runs, the loop variable has advanced to its final value.
Using defer in a tight loop — each deferred call allocates a stack entry that is not freed until the function returns, causing O(n) memory growth.
Assuming deferred recover catches panics in a different goroutine — recover only works in the same goroutine as the panic.
execution_timeline: func openAndProcess(name string) error called
→ f, err := os.Open(name) — file opened successfully
→ defer f.Close() pushed onto defer stack — f.Close() will run when openAndProcess returns
→ buf := make([]byte, 1024) allocated
→ n, err := f.Read(buf) — read 512 bytes
→ process(buf[:n]) called
→ process panics — stack unwinding begins
→ defer stack is LIFO: any defers in process run first, then control returns to openAndProcess
→ openAndProcess's defer stack: [f.Close()]
→ f.Close() runs — file descriptor is closed even though process panicked
→ panic continues unwinding up the call stack
failure_modes: Defer in loop: for i := 0; i < n; i++ { defer f.Close() } — all n file descriptors stay open until the function returns, causing 'too many open files'.
Defer with closure capturing mutable value: defer func() { fmt.Print(i) }() in a loop — all defers see the final value of i, not each iteration's value.
Defer after error: defer f.Close() is placed after os.Open succeeds, but if another deferred cleanup runs before f.Close(), it may assume f is still open.
Defer recover in wrong goroutine: go func() { defer recover() }() does not catch a panic in the parent goroutine.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict the output of a function with 3 deferred calls that capture variables by value and by reference.
Learner must explain why a defer inside a loop leaks resources even though each defer individually closes a single file.
Learner must determine whether a deferred recover in a goroutine catches a panic from the main goroutine.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Defer is used pervasively in Go: closing files, unlocking mutexes, closing HTTP response bodies, recovering from panics, timing functions with defer since time.Now(), and rolling back transactions. The standard library relies on defer for every resource cleanup path.
zero_magic.mental_model: Defer is a stack of cleanup functions attached to the current function call. When the function returns (normally or via panic), the stack is popped LIFO. Each deferred call's arguments are evaluated at the defer site, but the function body executes at return time.
performance_implications: Each defer allocation costs approximately 50ns — negligible for a handful of defers, but significant in tight loops.
Go 1.14+ optimizes defer in non-panic paths to run as inline cleanup, making the overhead ~10ns instead of ~50ns.
Defer in a hot loop creates O(n) defer entries that are not freed until the function returns — consider explicit cleanup instead.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without defer, every function with multiple return paths must duplicate cleanup code (close file, unlock mutex) on every exit path. One forgotten cleanup in a new return path causes a resource leak. Defer guarantees cleanup runs exactly once, regardless of how the function exits.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a function that opens 3 files, defers each close, processes data, and handles errors at each step. They must trace which deferred closes run when a panic occurs in the processing step, and explain why the LIFO order matters.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every Go HTTP handler defers response body close. Every mutex lock is paired with defer unlock. Every database transaction defers rollback on error and commit on success. Defer is the standard Go pattern for resource lifecycle management.
step_by_step_execution: Go compiler encounters defer f.Close() at line 7
Compiler emits code to push a defer record onto the goroutine's defer stack at runtime
Arguments are evaluated immediately: f is evaluated now, Close is resolved, but Close is NOT called yet
Function continues execution — reads, processes, computes
Function hits return statement — or panic occurs
Runtime pops the defer stack (LIFO): most recently deferred call runs first
Each deferred call executes with its captured arguments
After all defers run, the function's return values are passed to the caller — or the panic continues unwinding
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's runtime maintains a per-goroutine linked list of _defer structs. When the compiler encounters a defer statement, it calls runtime.deferproc to allocate a _defer node, populate it with the function pointer and arguments, and prepend it to the goroutine's defer list. When the function returns, the epilogue calls runtime.deferreturn, which pops _defer nodes from the list and executes them. In Go 1.14+, non-defer-panic paths are optimized so that deferred calls are inlined at each return site rather than going through the runtime — this makes the common case much faster.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Languages without defer (C, early C++) require explicit cleanup on every code path. One missed cleanup path causes a resource leak that may not be detected until production. Defer makes cleanup automatic and guaranteed — it is the Go answer to RAII and try-finally.

items.title: defer for cleanup
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply defer for cleanup in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Assuming deferred functions run after the function returns -- they run when the enclosing function reaches its end, including on panic.
Putting `defer` after the resource acquisition -- if the acquire fails, `defer` on a nil resource causes panic.
Deferring a function that modifies a loop variable -- all defer calls see the final value of the variable.
Using `defer` in a tight loop -- each defer incurs overhead and runs at function return, not loop iteration end.
execution_timeline: Resource is acquired (file opened, mutex locked, DB connection obtained).
Immediately after acquisition, `defer f()` is registered -- f runs when the function returns, LIFO with other defers.
The function body uses the resource (read, write, query).
If a panic occurs, all deferred functions in the panicking goroutine still execute.
When the function returns, deferred functions run in reverse declaration order.
After all defers complete, the function returns to its caller.

failure_modes.scenario: A file open fails, returning nil file. Deferred `file.Close()` panics on nil.
failure_modes.cause: `defer file.Close()` was written without checking if `file` is nil.
failure_modes.fix: Only defer close after a successful open: check `err` first.

failure_modes.scenario: A deferred function modifies named return values unexpectedly.
failure_modes.cause: The defer function runs after return values are set but before the caller receives them.
failure_modes.fix: Be explicit about named return modifications in deferred functions.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict the execution order of multiple defers in a function with several return paths.
Learner must explain why defer runs before os.Exit, and what happens if a deferred function calls os.Exit.
Learner must trace a function with defer + panic + recover and predict the return values.
Learner must identify when defer should be used vs explicit cleanup (tight loops).
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The standard library uses defer everywhere: `os.File.Close()`, `sql.Rows.Close()`, `net.Conn.Close()`, `sync.Mutex.Unlock()`, `http.Response.Body.Close()`. Defer is the idiomatic Go way to ensure cleanup regardless of return path.
zero_magic.mental_model: Defer is like writing a 'to-do on exit' list. When you acquire a resource, you immediately add a cleanup task. As you acquire more, new tasks go to the top. When the function exits, Go reads the list from top to bottom and does each task.
performance_implications: Each defer adds ~50 ns overhead for registration and execution -- negligible for I/O-bound code.
In tight loops (millions of iterations), defer inside the loop becomes measurable.
Deferred functions that capture variables may cause heap allocation if the closure escapes.
Multiple defers have cumulative overhead -- combine cleanup into fewer deferred calls.
zero_magic.problem_solved: In languages without defer, cleanup code must be written before every return statement, leading to duplicated close calls and bugs. Defer ensures cleanup always runs, even on panic, without duplicating code.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a function that opens a file, writes data, handles errors, and closes the file. They must modify it to have two return points and show that `file.Close()` runs at both.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every file operation, mutex lock, HTTP response body read, and database connection in production Go uses defer. `sync.Mutex` is always paired: `mu.Lock(); defer mu.Unlock()`.
step_by_step_execution: Acquire the resource (open file, lock mutex, query DB connection).
Check the acquisition error -- do not defer cleanup on failed acquisition.
Immediately after successful acquisition, write `defer resource.Close()`.
Use the resource in the function body with confidence that cleanup will run.
For multiple resources, the defer order is LIFO.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The compiler registers a deferred function in a linked list on the goroutine. Each `defer` adds a `_defer` struct containing the function pointer and arguments. On return or panic, the runtime walks this chain in LIFO order.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Defer solves the fundamental problem of resource cleanup in languages with early returns and panics. Without defer, every function needs manual cleanup before every return statement.

items.title: panic and recover
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply panic and recover in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using panic for routine error handling instead of returning errors — panic should be for exceptional conditions, not regular error flow.
Calling recover outside a deferred function — recover() returns nil if not called directly inside a defer.
Assuming recover catches panics from other goroutines — recover only works in the goroutine that panicked.
Panicking in a library function that the caller cannot recover from — library code should return errors, not panic.
execution_timeline: func A calls func B which calls func C
→ C encounters invalid state: index out of bounds
→ Go runtime panics: stack unwinding starts
→ C's deferred functions run (LIFO)
→ C's defers complete, unwinding continues to B
→ B's deferred functions run (LIFO)
→ B has a defer with recover()
→ recover() catches the panic value, stops unwinding
→ B returns normally (not panicking)
→ A continues execution normally — the panic did not reach A
failure_modes: Silent swallow: a deferred recover catches a panic, logs nothing, and returns — the panic is invisible, and the system continues in an unknown state.
Double panic: a panic occurs inside a deferred function that is already handling a panic — the second panic overrides the first, and the program crashes with the second panic's value.
Goroutine isolation: one goroutine panics and is not recovered — the entire program crashes, even though other goroutines are unaffected.
recover outside defer: a function calls recover() directly (not in a defer) — it returns nil and the panic continues unwinding.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict whether recover catches a given panic based on the defer nesting and goroutine boundaries.
Learner must identify whether a codebase uses panic for control flow (bad) or for truly exceptional conditions (acceptable).
Learner must explain why a panic in a net/http handler does not crash the server (the server has a deferred recover in its connection handler).
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The Go standard library uses panic internally for exceptional conditions: index out of bounds, nil pointer dereference, type assertion failure on non-comma-ok form. The net/http server wraps every handler in a deferred recover, so a handler panic does not crash the server. The encoding/json package panics internally during encoding and recovers to produce an error — a controversial pattern.
zero_magic.mental_model: Panic is an emergency stop, not a return value. When a function panics, the call stack unwinds, running deferred cleanups, until either a recover catches it or the program terminates. Recover is the emergency brake: it stops the unwinding and lets the goroutine continue.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without panic/recover, every function would need to thread error returns through deep call stacks for truly exceptional conditions (nil pointer, bounds check). Panic provides a fast path for unrecoverable errors, and recover provides a boundary where external effects are rolled back before the error propagates to the user.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a function that spawns a goroutine that panics, and explain why the parent goroutine's deferred recover does not catch it. Then they must write a correct pattern for recovering panics in goroutines using a recover inside the goroutine's own defer.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go HTTP servers use recover in middleware to convert handler panics into 500 responses instead of crashing. CLI tools use recover to print a stack trace and exit gracefully. Batch processing pipelines use recover per-item to skip a single malformed record without aborting the entire batch.
step_by_step_execution: panic(v) called: value v is saved as the panic argument
Go runtime begins stack unwinding: current goroutine only
At each stack frame, deferred functions run in LIFO order
If any deferred function calls recover(), the panic stops: the panic value is returned, unwinding halts
If no recover is found after all frames are unwound, the goroutine's stack trace and the panic value are printed to stderr
The process exits with non-zero status — no other goroutines are notified
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The Go runtime maintains a per-goroutine panic stack. When panic is called, runtime.gopanic creates a _panic struct and begins unwinding. For each frame, it iterates the defer list (which is also per-goroutine) and executes each deferred function. If a deferred function calls runtime.gorecover, it marks the panic as recovered and stops unwinding. If the deferred function itself panics (a double panic), runtime.gopanic checks for the double-panic flag and, if set, exits with a fatal error and the second panic's stack trace. The net/http server leverages this by having each connection handler run in its own goroutine with a deferred recover that converts panics to HTTP 500 responses.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Go's panic/recover exists as a structured alternative to C's setjmp/longjmp and C++'s exceptions. Unlike exceptions, panic is not meant for control flow — it is a last-resort mechanism for truly exceptional conditions that should crash the program if not recovered. The convention is: panic for programmer errors (nil dereference, out of bounds), return errors for everything else.

items.title: Custom error types
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Custom error types in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Defining a custom error type when a simple sentinel error would suffice.
Making the custom error type too complex -- errors should carry only what callers need.
Forgetting to implement the `Error() string` method -- the type won't satisfy `error`.
Comparing custom errors with `==` instead of `errors.Is` -- `==` fails when wrapped.
execution_timeline: Define a struct type implementing the `error` interface (has `Error() string`).
The custom error can carry additional fields: code, HTTP status, underlying wrapped error.
When the error is created, relevant fields are set by the constructor.
The error is returned up the call stack, possibly wrapped with fmt.Errorf.
The caller uses `errors.As` to extract the custom error and access its fields.

failure_modes.scenario: A custom error type embeds `error` but doesn't forward `Unwrap()` properly.
failure_modes.cause: Go doesn't auto-generate `Unwrap()` for embedded interfaces.
failure_modes.fix: Explicitly implement `Unwrap() error` that returns the embedded error.

failure_modes.scenario: A custom error type has too many fields that only the creator fills.
failure_modes.cause: The error type was designed with false future needs instead of YAGNI.
failure_modes.fix: Start minimal: add fields only when a real caller needs them.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify which custom error fields are useful to callers vs purely informational.
Learner must explain when to use custom error types vs fmt.Errorf vs sentinel errors.
Learner must design a custom error that wraps another error and preserves errors.Is compatibility.
Learner must evaluate when an error deserves a custom type.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The standard library defines custom error types: `*os.PathError` (Op, Path, Err), `*net.DNSError` (Name, Server, IsTimeout), `*json.SyntaxError` (Offset), `*url.Error` (Op, URL, Err).
zero_magic.mental_model: A custom error type is like a specialized alert form. A sentinel error is a simple post-it note. A custom error type is a structured form with labeled fields carrying actionable information.
performance_implications: Custom error types are heap-allocated structs -- each error creation allocates.
If errors are created in hot paths, allocation costs add up.
Consider sentinel errors or pre-allocated error instances for hot paths.
Wrapping adds another allocation -- balance information with allocation cost.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Sentinel errors and fmt.Errorf tell you something went wrong but don't carry structured data. Custom error types carry the context handlers need: HTTP status, validation field, database constraint.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must define a `ValidationError` type with fields (Field string, Message string, Value interface{}), implement error interface, and write tests using errors.As to extract and inspect ValidationError fields.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: API services define `APIError { Code int, Message string }` for structured JSON responses. Database layers define `DBError { Code string, Message string, Constraint string }`. Custom error types are standard for structured error data.
step_by_step_execution: Define a struct for your error: `type MyError struct { Code int; Message string }`.
Implement `func (e *MyError) Error() string` -- satisfies the error interface.
Optionally implement `Unwrap() error` if wrapping another error.
Create errors with a constructor: `return &MyError{Code: 404, Message: "not found"}`.
Callers use `errors.As(err, &myErr)` to extract and inspect the structured error.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: A custom error type satisfies `error` by implementing `Error() string`. The interface itself is a two-word struct (type pointer + data pointer). errors.As uses type assertion to extract the custom type.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Sentinel errors can only carry identity. Custom error types carry context. Without them, callers would need to parse error strings (brittle, locale-dependent, slow) or check every possible sentinel.

items.title: Structs
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Structs in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Forgetting that struct fields are comma-separated -- missing a comma on multi-line declarations causes compile error.
Declaring a struct type inside a function and trying to use it outside -- struct types are block-scoped.
Comparing structs with `==` when they contain slices or maps -- incomparable types cause compile error.
Assuming zero values of struct fields are nil for all types -- int fields are 0, bool fields are false.
execution_timeline: At compile time, the struct type definition is registered and the compiler calculates size and alignment.
When a struct variable is declared, all fields are initialized to their zero values.
When a struct literal is used, each field is set to the provided value.
When the struct is passed or assigned, the entire struct is copied (unless using a pointer).
The struct persists until it goes out of scope or is garbage collected.

failure_modes.scenario: Positional struct initialization breaks when fields are reordered.
failure_modes.cause: Positional initialization depends on field declaration order.
failure_modes.fix: Always use named field initialization: `Struct{Field: val}`.

failure_modes.scenario: A large struct copied many times causes performance degradation.
failure_modes.cause: Structs are value types -- every assignment and function call copies the entire struct.
failure_modes.fix: Use pointers for large structs.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict the zero values of all fields in a struct with mixed types.
Learner must explain why struct comparison fails when the struct contains a slice.
Learner must identify which initialization style is safer and why.
Learner must understand alignment padding in memory layout.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Structs are the primary data structure in Go. `http.Request`, `os.File`, `bytes.Buffer`, `net.Conn` are all structs. The standard library organizes almost all complex data through structs.
zero_magic.mental_model: A struct is a container that groups related data fields into a single value. The struct bundles these together as one unit.
performance_implications: Structs are value types -- assignment and pass-by-value copy the entire struct (O(n) for n fields).
Large structs (> 64 bytes) benefit from pointer usage to avoid copy overhead.
Struct fields are laid out contiguously with alignment padding -- field order can affect size.
The compiler optimizes small structs into registers on some architectures.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without structs, every program's data would be independent variables with naming conventions. Structs group related data into a named type with field access by name, making code self-documenting and type-safe.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must define a `Product` struct (ID int, Name string, Price float64, InStock bool), create instances using named initialization, write a formatting function, and write tests verifying field access.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Structs are used in every Go codebase for domain models (`Order`, `User`, `Product`), configuration (`Config`), API request/response bodies (`CreateUserRequest`), and JSON messages.
step_by_step_execution: Identify data that belongs together -- these become struct fields.
Define the struct type with typed fields: `type Person struct { Name string; Age int }`.
Create instances with named field initialization.
Access fields with dot notation: `p.Name`, `p.Age`.
Pass structs by value or pointer depending on mutation needs.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: A struct is laid out as a contiguous block of its fields in declaration order. The compiler adds alignment padding between fields. Reordering fields from largest to smallest alignment reduces padding.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Structs are the fundamental building block for organizing related data in Go. They enable type-safe, self-documenting code with field-level access control.

items.title: Methods
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Methods in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Defining a method inside another method -- Go doesn't support nested methods.
Forgetting that the method receiver is passed by value -- modifications don't affect the original.
Declaring a method on a non-local type (from another package) -- compile error.
Calling a method with pointer receiver on a non-addressable value -- compile error.
execution_timeline: The compiler registers the method as a function with the receiver as the first parameter.
When called, the receiver is bound to the object.
For value receivers, the object is copied; for pointer receivers, the pointer is passed.
The method body executes with access to the receiver's fields.
The method returns -- pointer receiver modifications are visible to the caller.

failure_modes.scenario: A method on a struct tries to modify a field, but the original is unchanged.
failure_modes.cause: The method has a value receiver -- it received a copy.
failure_modes.fix: Change to pointer receiver: `(s *MyStruct)`.

failure_modes.scenario: A method is called on a nil pointer receiver and panics.
failure_modes.cause: Go allows calling methods on nil pointers -- the body must check for nil.
failure_modes.fix: Add a nil check at the method start.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict whether a value receiver method can modify the original.
Learner must explain what happens when a method is called on a nil pointer receiver.
Learner must identify whether a method should use value or pointer receiver.
Learner must determine the method set of a type vs its pointer.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Methods are used throughout the standard library: `bytes.Buffer.Write`, `http.Request.WithContext`, `os.File.Read`, `sql.DB.Query`. Methods attach behavior to types without classes or inheritance.
zero_magic.mental_model: A method is a function that belongs to a type. A Person struct with a Greet() method that says 'Hello, my name is X'. The method accesses the Person through the receiver.
performance_implications: Method calls are as fast as function calls -- the receiver is just the first parameter.
Value receiver methods copy the receiver -- expensive for large structs.
Pointer receiver methods avoid the copy overhead.
The compiler may inline small methods.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without methods, you'd write separate functions for every type combination. Methods attach behavior to types naturally: `person.Greet()` vs `company.Greet()`. This organizes behavior with the data it operates on.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must define a `BankAccount` struct with `Deposit(amount float64)`, `Withdraw(amount float64) error`, `Balance() float64` methods. Write tests showing Deposit and Withdraw modify the balance.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every Go type with behavior uses methods. HTTP handlers implement `ServeHTTP`. The `error` interface requires `Error() string`. Custom types use methods for JSON marshaling. Methods are standard for attaching behavior to types.
step_by_step_execution: Define the type (often a struct).
Decide whether the method needs to modify the receiver (pointer receiver) or not (value receiver).
Write the method: `func (r ReceiverType) MethodName(args) returnType { body }`.
Call the method on an instance: `instance.MethodName(args)`.
Go handles pointer/value conversion where possible.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Methods are syntactic sugar for functions with a receiver parameter. `func (t T) Foo()` becomes `func Foo(t T)`. The compiler determines the method set of each type.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Methods provide the 'behavior attached to data' paradigm. Go's method system is simpler than classes (no inheritance, no constructors, no overloading) but provides the essential capability.

items.title: Pointer receivers
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Pointer receivers in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using a pointer receiver when the method doesn't modify the receiver -- be consistent but don't over-use pointers.
Forgetting that pointer receiver methods are not in the method set of a non-pointer type variable.
Calling a pointer receiver method on a non-addressable value (e.g., function return value).
Modifying the pointer receiver itself inside the method -- only changes the local pointer.
execution_timeline: The caller has a pointer or an addressable value.
When the method is called, the pointer is passed -- no copy of the struct.
Inside the method, `s.Field = val` modifies the struct through the pointer.
If the method modifies the pointer itself (`s = nil`), only the local copy is affected.
After the method returns, the caller sees all field-level modifications.

failure_modes.scenario: Pointer receiver method called on a function return value: `GetStruct().Method()` -- compile error.
failure_modes.cause: The return value is not addressable.
failure_modes.fix: Assign the return to a variable first: `s := GetStruct(); s.Method()`.

failure_modes.scenario: Method sets `s = nil` expecting the caller's pointer to become nil.
failure_modes.cause: The pointer itself is passed by value.
failure_modes.fix: Return the modified value or use pointer-to-pointer if reassignment is needed.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict whether a field modification in a pointer receiver method is visible to the caller.
Learner must explain why `GetStruct().Method()` fails when Method has a pointer receiver.
Learner must identify which types/methods require pointer vs value receivers.
Learner must trace the effect of a pointer receiver method modifying multiple fields.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Standard library uses pointer receivers for mutation: `*bytes.Buffer.Write`, `*sql.DB.Query`, `*http.Request.WithContext`. Mutating methods use pointer receivers; accessor methods may use value receivers for small types.
zero_magic.mental_model: A pointer receiver is like giving someone the key to your house instead of a photo. With the key (pointer), they can rearrange furniture (modify fields). With a photo (value), they can only describe what they see.
performance_implications: Pointer receiver avoids copying the entire struct -- essential for large structs.
Accessing fields through a pointer has one extra indirection.
Pointer receivers can cause heap allocation if the struct escapes.
The compiler can inline pointer receiver method calls.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without pointer receivers, methods could only read from a struct, never modify it. Methods would need to return the modified struct. Pointer receivers enable mutation without reassignment overhead.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must create a `Counter` struct with `Increment()` (pointer receiver) and `Value() int` (value receiver). Write tests showing Increment persists and Value reads correctly.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every Go type needing mutation uses pointer receivers: `*http.Request`, `*os.File`, `*bytes.Buffer`, `*sql.DB`, `*net.Conn`. Factory functions return pointers. Pointer receivers are standard for stateful types.
step_by_step_execution: Determine if the method needs to modify receiver fields (use pointer).
Also use pointer receiver if the struct is large (avoids copy).
Define: `func (s *MyStruct) Mutate() { s.Field = newVal }`.
Call on a pointer or addressable value -- Go auto-converts when addressable.
Non-addressable values (function returns, map elements) cannot use pointer methods directly.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: A pointer receiver method receives a pointer (8 bytes on 64-bit). Go auto-converts `value.Method()` to `(&value).Method()` when addressable. This works for variables, struct fields, array elements, but not map elements or function returns.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Pointer receivers enable mutation without copying large structs, making code both correct (mutations persist) and efficient (no copying).

items.title: Value receivers
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Value receivers in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using a value receiver for a large struct and wondering why the method is slow.
Assuming a value receiver method can modify the original -- it receives a copy.
Using value receivers for methods that should handle nil receivers.
Forgetting value receiver methods work on both pointer and value types.
execution_timeline: The method is called on a value or pointer.
If called on a pointer, Go dereferences and copies the pointed-to value.
The method body receives a copy -- all field accesses are on the copy.
Any modifications to the copy are lost when the method returns.
The original struct is completely unchanged.

failure_modes.scenario: Value receiver method modifies a field expecting the original to change.
failure_modes.cause: The method receives a copy -- modifications affect the copy only.
failure_modes.fix: Change to pointer receiver for mutations.

failure_modes.scenario: Assuming value receiver is always cheap, but the struct is large.
failure_modes.cause: Each call copies the entire struct -- significant overhead for hot paths.
failure_modes.fix: Switch to pointer receiver to avoid the copy.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict whether a value receiver method can modify the original.
Learner must explain when to choose value vs pointer receiver.
Learner must identify performance characteristics of value receivers for large structs.
Learner must determine why methods on small immutable types (time.Time) use value receivers.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Standard library: `time.Time` methods use value receivers -- `time.Now().Add(1*time.Hour)` returns a new Time. `net.IP.IsLoopback()` uses value receiver. Small, immutable types use value receivers.
zero_magic.mental_model: A value receiver is like taking a photograph and describing it. You can talk about it, write on the photo, but the original object stays unchanged. The photo is a snapshot at call time.
performance_implications: Value receivers copy the entire struct -- O(size) per call.
For small structs (< 64 bytes), copy is cheap and often faster than pointer indirection.
For large structs (KB+), value receivers cause measurable copying overhead.
The compiler can sometimes optimize away the copy for read-only methods.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Value receivers make the contract clear: this method does not modify the receiver. For small immutable types, value receivers signal operations return new values. Useful for concurrency safety.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must create a `Point` struct with `DistanceToOrigin() float64` (value receiver) and `Scale(factor float64)` (pointer receiver). Write tests showing DistanceToOrigin doesn't modify Point.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: `time.Time` uses value receivers exclusively. Small data types like `net.IP` and value objects use value receivers. Use value receivers for immutability semantics.
step_by_step_execution: Choose value receiver when the method does not modify the receiver.
Also choose for small, immutable types where copy cost is negligible.
Define: `func (s MyStruct) Accessor() ReturnType { body }`.
Call on any value: `val.Accessor()` or `ptr.Accessor()`.
Treat the receiver as read-only in the method body.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: When a value receiver method is called on a pointer, the compiler inserts a `*ptr` dereference and copies the value. The copy is on the caller's stack if small enough, or on the heap if escape analysis says so.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Value receivers make immutability explicit. They are the default choice for small types because they are simple, safe for concurrent use, and often faster than pointer receivers.

items.title: Receiver sets
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Receiver sets in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Assuming a value type automatically satisfies an interface that requires pointer receiver methods — T has access to T's method set (value receivers only), *T has access to *T's method set (all methods including pointer receivers). A value can only call pointer receiver methods if it is addressable (e.g., a map element is not addressable).
Passing a value to a function that expects an interface with pointer receiver methods — the value does not satisfy the interface; the compiler rejects it with 'does not implement'.
Not understanding that a slice of T must be converted to a slice of the interface type manually — even if T satisfies io.Writer, []T does not automatically satisfy []io.Writer.

Assuming that a value receiver method on *T also appears in T's method set — only *T's method set includes both value and pointer receiver methods; T's method set includes only value receiver methods.

Calling pointer receiver methods on non-addressable values (results of function calls, map index expressions) — the compiler rejects them because the value is not addressable and cannot be implicitly referenced.
execution_timeline: Compiler encounters: var w io.Writer = myWriter{} — checks if myWriter (value) satisfies io.Writer interface (Write(p []byte) (n int, err error))
→ Compiler checks myWriter's method set: only methods with value receivers are included
→ If Write has a pointer receiver (*myWriter), the compiler rejects: myWriter does not implement io.Writer (Write method has pointer receiver)
→ If Write has a value receiver (myWriter), the compiler accepts: myWriter implements io.Writer
→ Compiler encounters: var w io.Writer = &myWriter{} — checks *myWriter's method set: all methods (value and pointer receivers) are included
→ If Write has a pointer receiver, the compiler accepts: *myWriter implements io.Writer
→ Runtime call w.Write(data): if w holds a pointer, Go automatically dereferences it for value receiver methods; if w holds a value, it must be addressable to call pointer receiver methods
failure_modes: Interface satisfaction failure: a library defines an interface with pointer receiver methods; the learner passes a value instead of a pointer — the compiler error does not immediately indicate the receiver mismatch, and the learner adds a pointer wrapper or type alias incorrectly.

Non-addressable value: the learner writes mappedStruct[key].Method() where Method has a pointer receiver — Go does not allow taking the address of a map element, so the call is rejected. The learner must copy the value to a temporary variable first.

Slice of interface conversion: the learner has []*myWriter and tries to pass it where []io.Writer is expected — Go requires explicit conversion with a loop, and the learner is surprised that the conversion does not happen automatically.

Method set hiding: the learner defines a type alias (type MyWriter myWriter) and expects MyWriter to satisfy the same interfaces as myWriter — the method set is empty for type aliases of named types; methods are not inherited.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict which of the following compile: var s fmt.Stringer = MyType{}, var s fmt.Stringer = &MyType{} given that String() has a value receiver; then explain why.

Learner must write a type with both value and pointer receiver methods, then an interface that requires a pointer receiver method — demonstrate that the value type does not satisfy the interface and explain the compiler error.
Learner must explain why m[key].Method() (where m is a map[K]T and Method has a pointer receiver) does not compile, and write the correct workaround.
Learner must write a generic function that accepts both value and pointer types satisfying an interface using constraints.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's method sets are the foundation of interface satisfaction. The standard library uses method sets to define contracts: io.Writer requires Write with value or pointer receiver, http.Handler requires ServeHTTP with value or pointer receiver. The encoding/json package's Marshaler interface requires MarshalJSON with a value receiver (valued). The sort package's Interface requires Len, Less, Swap — typically implemented with value receivers on a slice type. Go's compiler uses method set rules to determine interface satisfaction at compile time, with no runtime dispatch overhead.
zero_magic.mental_model: Every type T has a method set containing all methods declared with a T receiver. Every pointer type *T has a method set containing all methods declared with either a T receiver or a *T receiver. Interface satisfaction checks the method set of the concrete type: if the variable is T, only T's method set counts; if the variable is *T, *T's method set (which includes T's methods) counts. Addressability determines whether you can call a pointer receiver method on a value — you can if and only if the value's address can be taken.
performance_implications: Pointer receiver methods incur a nil check at call time — if the pointer is nil, the method may or may not panic depending on whether it accesses the receiver's fields.
Value receiver methods copy the entire struct on each call — for large structs (8+ fields, contains slices/maps/strings which copy the header), this adds allocation and GC pressure.
Calling a value receiver method through an interface allocates — Go boxes the value to store it in the interface, heap-allocating it; calling through a pointer type does not allocate.
Method set lookups are compile-time in Go — there is no runtime method dispatch penalty like in dynamic languages; the compiler resolves the method at compile time.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers do not understand why their type does not satisfy an interface. They see a confusing compiler error ('does not implement' or 'cannot call pointer method on') and do not know the method set rules. They write workarounds like empty interface assertions or unsafe pointer conversions. This lesson makes the method set rules explicit: value type T has only value receiver methods, pointer type *T has both. This is the single most common source of interface confusion in Go.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a type with at least two methods (one value receiver, one pointer receiver), then write code that demonstrates: (1) a value of the type can call the value receiver method but NOT the pointer receiver method unless assigned to a variable, (2) a pointer to the type can call both methods. They must also write an interface that requires a pointer receiver method and explain why a value type does not satisfy it. They must write a test that proves their compiler errors are expected.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every Go program that uses interfaces encounters method set rules. When passing http.Handler implementations, the handler type must have ServeHTTP in its method set. When implementing database/sql Scanner, the Scan method must use a pointer receiver because it modifies the receiver. When implementing sort.Interface on a slice type, value receivers are idiomatic. When implementing json.Marshaler, value receivers are common because serialization does not mutate the receiver.
step_by_step_execution: Define type Counter struct { n int } with two methods: (c Counter) Value() int (value receiver) and (c *Counter) Inc() (pointer receiver)
var c Counter — c.Value() compiles (value receiver is in T's method set); c.Inc() compiles (c is addressable, Go takes &c implicitly)
var m map[string]Counter — m["k"].Value() compiles (value receiver, no address needed); m["k"].Inc() does NOT compile (map elements are not addressable)
temp := m["k"]; temp.Inc(); m["k"] = temp — workaround: copy to addressable variable, mutate, write back
var v Counter; var i interface{ Inc() } = v — does NOT compile: Counter does not implement Inc() because Inc is in *Counter's method set
var i interface{ Inc() } = &v — compiles: *Counter's method set includes Inc()
f() method call on non-addressable result: getCounter().Inc() — does NOT compile (function call result is not addressable)
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The Go compiler maintains two method tables per type: one for T (methods with T receivers) and one for *T (methods with T + *T receivers). Interface satisfaction checks whether the method set of the concrete type (T or *T) contains all methods of the interface. This is resolved at compile time: the compiler generates an interface dispatch table (itab) for each concrete type-interface pair, stored in the binary. At runtime, the itab pointer is stored alongside the value in the interface struct (interface{typecode, value}). The method call is a pointer lookup through the itab — one indirect call, no reflection, no type switching.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Receiver sets (method sets) exist because Go distinguishes between value and pointer receivers to control mutation semantics. A value receiver promises not to mutate the receiver; a pointer receiver can mutate it. This distinction extends to interface satisfaction: a value type T cannot automatically satisfy an interface requiring pointer receiver methods because doing so would allow mutation through a copy. The method set rules prevent this accidentally unsafe pattern at compile time. Without understanding method sets, developers write type-assertion-heavy code or use reflection instead of letting the compiler verify interface satisfaction.

items.title: Composition
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Composition in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Trying to use inheritance patterns in Go -- Go uses composition, not inheritance.
Creating deep nesting of structs inside structs -- prefer flat composition.
Using embedding when a named field would be more explicit.
Forgetting that interfaces compose too -- different from struct composition.
execution_timeline: Identify the behaviors the type needs -- define interfaces.
Identify the data the type needs -- define structs.
Define small, focused types -- each has one responsibility.
Compose larger types by embedding smaller types or using named fields.
The composed type gains promoted methods from embedded types.

failure_modes.scenario: Manager struct embeds Employee, overrides a method, gets surprising dispatch.
failure_modes.cause: Go doesn't have method overriding -- outer shadows inner.
failure_modes.fix: Use explicit delegation instead of relying on shadowing.

failure_modes.scenario: Two embedded types have the same method name -- compile error.
failure_modes.cause: Go cannot promote both conflicting methods.
failure_modes.fix: Explicitly define the method on the outer type, delegating appropriately.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain the difference between composition (has-a) and inheritance (is-a).
Learner must predict which methods are promoted when A embeds B.
Learner must identify when to use embedding vs named fields.
Learner must design a composed type from 3 smaller types and trace method promotion.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Standard library uses composition: `os.File` composes reader/writer, `bytes.Buffer` composes a byte slice. HTTP handlers compose middleware via function wrapping. `io.ReadWriter` composes Reader and Writer.
zero_magic.mental_model: Composition is like LEGO bricks. Build small, single-purpose bricks (types) and snap them together. Each brick handles its responsibility. The composed object has all brick capabilities through method promotion.
performance_implications: Composition has zero runtime overhead over flat structs.
Method promotion is compile-time -- no runtime dispatch overhead.
Delegation methods add one function call of overhead.
Embedded types don't create indirection unless pointers are used.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Inheritance hierarchies become deep and fragile. Composition builds types from small, independently testable pieces. Changes to a component affect only types embedding it, not their dependents.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must define `Logger` with `Log(string)`, `Engine` with `Start()`, then compose into `Car` embedding both. Write tests showing promoted methods work on Car.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go codebases compose service structs: `type UserService struct { db *sql.DB; logger *zap.Logger; cache *redis.Client }`. APIs compose middleware: `auth(rateLimit(handler))`.
step_by_step_execution: Break down the type's responsibilities into components.
Define each component as a separate type.
Compose by embedding (for promoted methods) or named fields (for explicit access).
Use component methods via dot notation in the composed type.
If ambiguity arises, define the method explicitly on the outer type.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: When A embeds B, the compiler inlines B's fields into A's memory layout. B's methods are promoted to A's method set via auto-generated forwarding methods. This is resolved at compile time.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Composition is Go's alternative to inheritance. It builds complex types from simple pieces without fragile deep hierarchies. It's the primary code reuse mechanism in Go.

items.title: Embedding
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Embedding in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Confusing embedding (struct-in-struct without field name) with composition via named fields.
Expecting embedding to work like inheritance -- embedding promotes methods, not polymorphic behavior.
Embedding the same type twice -- compile error.
Forgetting that embedding a type exposes its methods through the outer type accidentally.
execution_timeline: Declare a struct with an embedded type: `type A struct { B }`.
At compile time, B's fields and methods are promoted to A.
Access B's fields directly: `a.FieldFromB`.
If A has a field with the same name, A's takes precedence.
If two embedded types have the same method, accessing it on A causes compile error.

failure_modes.scenario: Embeds a type and unintentionally exposes its methods as part of the outer type's API.
failure_modes.cause: Embedding promotes all exported methods.
failure_modes.fix: Use a named field instead: `type A struct { b B }`.

failure_modes.scenario: Two embedded types implement the same interface -- calling that method is ambiguous.
failure_modes.cause: Go cannot determine which to promote.
failure_modes.fix: Define the method explicitly on the outer type.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict whether a field access resolves to the outer type or embedded type.
Learner must explain why embedding a type implementing io.Reader makes the outer implement it too.
Learner must handle same-name fields between outer and embedded types.
Learner must resolve ambiguity from two embedded types with the same method.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Embedding in standard library: `sync.Mutex` embedded in types needing locking. `bytes.Buffer` embeds a byte slice. `embed.FS` embeds file system. Embedding is Go's compositional reuse mechanism.
zero_magic.mental_model: Embedding is like clicking two LEGO bricks together. The combined piece has all the studs (methods) of both bricks. Interior surfaces (fields) are accessible from outside. It's structural composition with automatic forwarding.
performance_implications: Embedding has zero runtime cost -- compile-time only.
Promoted methods compile as direct calls, not dynamic dispatch.
Accessing promoted fields is direct memory access.
Embedding doesn't cause heap allocation.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without embedding, every method from component types needs manual forwarding. Embedding automates this: `type Server struct { *http.Server; Logger }` promotes all methods automatically.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must define a `Mutex` with `Lock()`/`Unlock()`, embed it in a `Counter`, and write tests showing promoted methods work on Counter.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production services embed `sync.Mutex` for thread safety. API handlers embed `base.Handler` for common methods. `embed.FS` embeds file systems. Embedding is universal for compositional reuse.
step_by_step_execution: Identify a type whose fields/methods to promote.
Declare without field name: `type Outer struct { Inner }`.
Access Inner's fields directly: `outer.Field`.
Outer automatically satisfies interfaces Inner satisfies.
If naming conflict, define the method on Outer to resolve.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The compiler builds a flattened field layout. For `type A struct { B; x int }`, B's fields are at offset 0, then x. Method promotion generates forwarding methods in the outer type's method table.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Embedding provides automatic method/field promotion, eliminating boilerplate forwarding code. Build small types and snap them into larger types with zero wiring code.

items.title: Interfaces
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Interfaces in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Declaring an interface and implementing it on a pointer receiver, then calling methods through a value type that does not satisfy the interface — the compiler error says 'does not implement' but the learner does not see why the method set is empty.
Assigning a nil *T to an interface variable — the interface value is non-nil (it has a type and a nil pointer), so 'if err == nil' evaluates to false even though the underlying value is nil.
Assuming io.Reader closes after EOF because Read returns (0, io.EOF) — the learner forgets that Read can return (n, io.EOF) with n > 0, requiring the caller to process n bytes before handling EOF.
failure_modes: Nil interface panic: a function returns nil explicitly (return nil) and the caller assigns to an error interface — works. But if the function returns (*MyError)(nil), the interface is non-nil, and 'if err != nil' is always true, masking the nil value.

Interface boxing overhead: calling a method through an interface allocates the value on the heap if the concrete type is not a pointer — hot loops through io.Writer calls can generate unexpected GC pressure.

Dynamic dispatch invisible to inlining: the compiler cannot inline interface method calls because the concrete type is unknown at compile time — performance-sensitive code may silently run slower than expected.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict whether a given concrete type satisfies an interface at compile time WITHOUT running the code.
Learner must determine whether an interface variable is nil — given a *int that is nil assigned to an interface{} — and explain why.
Learner must identify the difference between a type assertion with and without the comma-ok pattern and predict which panics.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The entire Go standard library is organized around interfaces: io.Reader, io.Writer, http.Handler, error, fmt.Stringer, sort.Interface. The Go proverb 'accept interfaces, return structs' derives from the fact that interfaces provide decoupling at call sites without forcing heap allocation on return values.
execution_timeline: var r io.Reader = &os.File{} → interface value: two words — (type *os.File, pointer to os.File struct on heap). The concrete value is boxed into the interface, but since it's a pointer, no additional allocation occurs.
var r io.Reader = bytes.NewBuffer([]byte{...}) → NewBuffer returns *bytes.Buffer which is a pointer — stored in interface as (type *bytes.Buffer, data pointer).

var w io.Writer = myWriter{} where myWriter is a struct value → interface value: (type myWriter, copy of struct value on heap). The struct is implicitly allocated on the heap because interface values hold a copy of the concrete data.
zero_magic.mental_model: An interface is a contract: 'anything with these methods satisfies me.' At runtime, an interface value is a pair: (type, pointer). The type tells Go which concrete method to call. The pointer holds the data (either a pointer to the value or the value itself boxed on the heap). Checking interface equality checks both the type and the data pointer.
performance_implications: Interface method call is an indirect function call through the itable — roughly 2-3x slower than a direct call, but negligible outside hot loops.
Assigning a non-pointer struct to an interface causes a heap allocation (escape to heap) — avoid in tight loops by using pointer receivers.
Type assertions are O(1) with a hash lookup on the type — they are fast but not free; repeated assertions in a loop should be hoisted.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Interfaces are Go's primary abstraction mechanism, but their runtime representation (itable, data pointer, nil semantics) is invisible to learners who only see the source code. This lesson makes the two-word interface value tangible, so learners stop treating interfaces as 'magic abstract types' and start treating them as concrete data structures.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a program that: (1) assigns a nil *os.File to an error interface, (2) checks if the error is nil, (3) explains why the result is surprising, and (4) fixes it with an explicit nil return.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every Go production service uses interfaces for HTTP handlers, database drivers, mock testing, middleware decoupling, and structured logging backends. Understanding interface nil semantics prevents a class of production bugs where error checks silently pass nil-as-non-nil.
step_by_step_execution: Compiler sees assignment of concrete value to interface variable — it checks the method set of the concrete type against the interface at compile time
If the method set satisfies the interface, the compiler emits code to build an itable (interface table) mapping interface methods to concrete methods at runtime
At runtime, the assignment creates a two-word interface value: first word is a pointer to the itable+type info, second word is a pointer to the data (or the data itself if <= one word)
When an interface method is called, Go looks up the concrete method via the itable and dispatches — this is always a single indirect call, not a search
When checking interface == nil, Go compares both words — if either word is non-zero, the interface is non-nil
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's interface value is a runtime struct: iface for non-empty interfaces (has methods) and eface for empty interface{} (no methods). Both contain a type pointer and a data pointer. The type pointer points to an itable (interface table) that maps interface method offsets to concrete method addresses — computed once at the assignment point and cached. When a concrete value fits in one machine word (e.g., bool, int, pointer), it is stored directly in the data pointer slot without additional allocation. Larger values are heap-allocated and the data pointer points to the copy.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Interfaces exist in Go to enable decoupling without inheritance. Unlike Java's classes or C++'s virtual inheritance, Go interfaces are implicitly satisfied — no 'implements' keyword. This means packages can define interfaces that existing types already satisfy, enabling safe, non-breaking abstraction across package boundaries.

items.title: Small interfaces
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Small interfaces in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Defining interfaces with 10+ methods -- they should be 1-3 methods at most.
Creating an interface before it has two concrete implementations -- premature abstraction.
Putting setters and getters in an interface -- use exported fields or methods that do something.
Making interfaces that mirror a concrete type's methods exactly -- that's indirection, not abstraction.
execution_timeline: Identify a behavior that multiple types could provide -- becomes a method in the interface.
Define the interface with minimum methods: `type Writer interface { Write(p []byte) (n int, err error) }`.
Implement implicitly -- any type with matching methods satisfies it.
Use the interface as a parameter type to accept any implementation.
Add methods only when new callers genuinely need them (YAGNI).

failure_modes.scenario: Defines a 15-method Database interface before writing any implementation.
failure_modes.cause: Premature abstraction -- designed upfront guessing future needs.
failure_modes.fix: Start with concrete types. Extract interface when you have two implementations.

failure_modes.scenario: Interface has one method with 5 parameters and 3 returns -- violating 'small'.
failure_modes.cause: The method does too much.
failure_modes.fix: Break into smaller methods or use a function type.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify whether a 4+ method interface should be split.
Learner must explain why `io.Reader` (one method) is well-designed.
Learner must determine if a given interface is the right abstraction level.
Learner must identify premature interface extraction.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Standard library's best interfaces are tiny: `io.Reader` (1), `io.Writer` (1), `io.Closer` (1), `fmt.Stringer` (1), `error` (1). Larger ones like `http.Handler` (1) are single-method. Small interfaces compose via embedding.
zero_magic.mental_model: A small interface is like a power outlet -- it defines one specific capability. Any device providing that capability can plug in. The interface doesn't care how -- only that the capability is provided.
performance_implications: Small interfaces are cheap -- interface value is two words (type + data pointer).
Method calls through an interface involve a dynamic dispatch table lookup (~1-3 ns).
Small interfaces enable more efficient testing (smaller fakes) and better composition.
Compiler cannot inline interface method calls.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Large interfaces create coupling -- every implementation must implement all methods. Small interfaces are easy to implement, easy to compose, and changes rarely break existing code.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must identify three separate behaviors in a `FileProcessor`, extract each as a small interface (1-2 methods), and compose them. Write tests with stub implementations.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go defines interfaces like `Storer { Store(key string, data []byte) error }`, `Notifier { Notify(msg Message) error }`, `Cache { Get(key string) ([]byte, bool) }`. Each is easily mocked.
step_by_step_execution: Identify a capability multiple implementations could provide.
Define the interface with minimum methods to express that capability.
Prefer 1-method interfaces -- add only when callers need them.
Compose interfaces: `type ReadWriter interface { Reader; Writer }`.
Use as parameter: `func Process(r io.Reader) error`.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Interface values are two-word structures: type pointer (itab) and data pointer. Method calls look up the function in the itab's function table. Single indirection for dispatch.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Small interfaces are Go's design philosophy: prefer small, composable pieces over large monolithic abstractions. They make code testable, flexible, and understandable.

items.title: Interface embedding
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Interface embedding in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Embedding an interface in a struct and forgetting to provide an implementation -- nil pointer panic.
Creating circular dependencies when embedding interfaces across packages.
Using interface embedding when explicit named fields would be clearer.
Embedding too many interfaces into one monolithic combined interface.
execution_timeline: Define small interfaces: `Reader`, `Writer`.
Compose into larger: `type ReadWriter interface { Reader; Writer }`.
Types implementing all embedded interfaces satisfy the composed interface.
Structs can embed interfaces too -- expect concrete values assigned.
Methods dispatch through each embedded interface's itab.

failure_modes.scenario: Struct embeds `io.Reader` without initializing it -- calling Read panics.
failure_modes.cause: The embedded interface field is nil.
failure_modes.fix: Always initialize embedded interfaces in the constructor.

failure_modes.scenario: Interface embeds A and B, but type only implements A -- compile error.
failure_modes.cause: The composed interface requires all embedded interfaces.
failure_modes.fix: Implement all embedded interfaces independently.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict which methods are on a composed interface.
Learner must explain what happens when struct embedding an interface doesn't initialize it.
Learner must determine if a type satisfies a composed interface.
Learner must compose 3 small interfaces into one and verify.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Interface embedding is everywhere: `io.ReadWriter`, `io.ReadWriteCloser`, `io.WriteCloser`. `http.Handler` composes with middleware. `sort.Interface` has 3 methods together for convenience.
zero_magic.mental_model: Interface embedding is like a multi-tool combining simple tools. A `ReadWriter` is a Swiss Army knife with knife (Reader) and scissors (Writer). Use as either, and satisfies any interface needing either.
performance_implications: Interface embedding adds no runtime overhead.
Method dispatch through composed interface is identical to single interface.
The itab contains all methods from all embedded interfaces.
Struct embedding interfaces adds one pointer per embedded interface.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without embedding, you'd need ReadWriter, ReadWriteCloser, etc. Embedding lets you compose these from Reader, Writer, Closer, eliminating combinatorial explosion.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must define Reader, Writer, Flusher interfaces, compose into ReadWriteFlusher, create a Buffer implementing all three, and write tests showing it satisfies the composed interface.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go uses `io.ReadWriter` for buffered I/O. Database drivers compose Querier, Executor, Transactor. `http.ResponseWriter` embeds `io.Writer`. Interface embedding builds complex contracts from simple parts.
step_by_step_execution: Define small, focused interfaces (1-3 methods).
Compose into richer interfaces: `type MyInterface interface { InterfaceA; InterfaceB }`.
Types automatically satisfy the composed interface if they implement all embedded ones.
Use composed interface as parameter to accept all capabilities.
For structs, embed interfaces to declare required capabilities as fields.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The compiler builds the method table by concatenating all embedded interface methods. Duplicate methods must have identical signatures. The itab entry points to the concrete implementation.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Interface embedding builds complex behavior contracts from small, reusable pieces. It's Go's mechanism for interface composition, following the same philosophy as struct embedding.

items.title: Stringer
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Stringer in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Infinite recursion in String() -- using `%v` on the same type inside String().
Creating String() that modifies the receiver -- Stringer should be read-only.
Forgetting that String() must be defined on the type, not a pointer (unless both needed).
Overriding String() but not considering pointer vs value receiver implications for fmt.
execution_timeline: Type implements `String() string` -- satisfies fmt.Stringer.
When passed to fmt.Print, fmt.Sprintf, etc., Go checks if the type implements Stringer.
If yes, Go calls String() and uses the result for formatting.
If no, Go uses default struct/type formatting with field values.
The `%v` verb uses Stringer if available; `%+v` adds field names for structs.

failure_modes.scenario: String() uses `fmt.Sprintf("%v", p)` -- infinite recursion.
failure_modes.cause: `%v` on the receiver calls String() again.
failure_modes.fix: Format fields explicitly: `return fmt.Sprintf("Name: %s", p.Name)`.

failure_modes.scenario: String() defined on `*Person` but fmt.Println receives a value.
failure_modes.cause: Only `*Person` satisfies Stringer, not Person.
failure_modes.fix: Define String() on the value type or always pass pointers.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict fmt output for types with and without Stringer.
Learner must identify infinite recursion in String().
Learner must determine value vs pointer receiver for String().
Learner must explain `%v`, `%+v`, `%#v` differences.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Standard library uses Stringer pervasively: `time.Time.String()`, `net.IP.String()`, `url.URL.String()`. All custom types in standard library implement Stringer for debugging and display.
zero_magic.mental_model: Stringer is the 'make this type printable' interface. It's a name tag: 'when asked what I am, tell them this.' Without it, Go prints raw struct fields. With it, you control the representation.
performance_implications: String() called every time the value is formatted -- avoid heavy computation.
String() formatting many fields may allocate -- use strings.Builder.
String() on hot paths should be lightweight or lazy.
Complex implementations can be memoized for immutable types.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Default Go struct formatting shows all fields -- great for debugging but verbose. Stringer lets types define their own human-readable representation.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must define a `User` struct with Name, Email, Role, implement `String() string`, and write tests showing custom formatted output.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every custom Go type that is logged should implement Stringer. URL, IP, MAC address types implement Stringer for display. Error types implement Error() for human-readable messages.
step_by_step_execution: Define `func (t MyType) String() string`.
Format the type's meaningful data into a human-readable string.
Use `strings.Builder` for efficient string construction.
Avoid infinite recursion -- don't use `%v` on the same type inside String().
Use pointer receiver if the type is large (avoids copy).
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The `fmt` package does a type assertion to check for `fmt.Stringer`. If yes, calls `String()`. If no, falls to default formatting. The check is O(1) -- just an itab comparison.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Stringer provides human-readable output for custom types. Without it, every type would output raw struct fields -- verbose and not what you want for logging or display.
