items.title: Type assertions
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Type assertions in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using type assertion on a nil interface value -- panics (use comma-ok form).
Forgetting the comma-ok idiom: single-value form panics on failure.
Asserting to the wrong type -- must match exactly, not just implement the same interface.
Using assertions instead of interface methods -- if asserting frequently, reconsider interface design.
execution_timeline: At runtime, an interface value contains a concrete type and concrete value.
The assertion `x.(ConcreteType)` checks the concrete type inside x.
If match (and comma-ok), `ok` is true and `val` is the concrete value.
If no match, `ok` is false and `val` is zero value.
If no comma-ok and assertion fails, runtime panics.

failure_modes.scenario: Asserts `x.(*os.File)` on an io.Reader that might be a *bytes.Buffer -- runtime panic.
failure_modes.cause: Assumes a concrete type that may not match.
failure_modes.fix: Use comma-ok form: `f, ok := x.(*os.File); if !ok { handle }`.

failure_modes.scenario: Asserts to an interface instead of concrete type -- valid but often not intended.
failure_modes.cause: Asserting to interface checks if x implements that interface.
failure_modes.fix: This is valid Go -- ensure it's intentional.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict whether a type assertion will succeed given an interface value.
Learner must explain single-value vs comma-ok assertions.
Learner must identify when assertions are necessary vs when methods suffice.
Learner must determine what happens asserting a nil interface.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Standard library uses assertions: `json.Decoder` asserts `io.ByteReader`, `net/http` asserts `http.Hijacker` for WebSocket, `sort.Interface` unwraps `sort.Reverse`.
zero_magic.mental_model: A type assertion asks 'are you actually a specific brand?' The generic USB device (interface) is asked: are you a printer? If yes, printer-specific features are accessible.
performance_implications: Type assertions are fast -- compare interface type pointer against target type.
Comma-ok adds a branch but no measurable overhead.
Assertions on interfaces are O(1) -- pointer comparison.
Heavy assertion usage can indicate poor interface design.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Sometimes you need concrete type behavior the interface doesn't provide. Type assertions let you safely check if an interface value wraps a specific concrete type and access its unique capabilities.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write `typeName(x interface{}) string` using a type switch returning the type name. Write tests for int, string, bool, slice, unknown.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go uses type assertions for: checking if ResponseWriter supports Hijacker, if Reader supports Seeker, if error is a custom type (via errors.As).
step_by_step_execution: Start with the interface value.
Use comma-ok form: `val, ok := x.(ConcreteType)`.
If ok, use val with concrete type methods/fields.
If not ok, handle gracefully.
For multiple types, use a type switch.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The interface value stores an itab pointer (or eface for empty interfaces). The assertion compares the type pointer against the target. If match, the concrete value is extracted from the data pointer.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Type assertions bridge generic interface code with concrete type-specific code. Essential for handling special cases, accessing type-specific features, and implementing stub/mock substitution.

items.title: Type switches
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Type switches in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Forgetting the default case in a type switch -- unexpected types pass silently.
Using `x := x.(type)` but then using x as the wrong type in a case arm.
Thinking type switches only work with `interface{}` -- they work with any interface.
Expecting fallthrough -- Go type switches don't fall through.
execution_timeline: The type switch examines the dynamic type of the interface value.
Each case lists a type -- if matched, the case body executes.
The variable in each case arm is automatically cast to the case's type.
The `default` case matches any type without a specific case.
The switch completes after the first matching case.

failure_modes.scenario: Type switch for int, string, bool but no default -- unexpected []byte passes silently.
failure_modes.cause: Without default, unhandled types do nothing.
failure_modes.fix: Always include a `default` case.

failure_modes.scenario: Uses x in a case arm as a type different from the case -- compile error.
failure_modes.cause: x is shadowed to the case's type in each arm.
failure_modes.fix: x changes type per arm -- use a different name if original interface needed.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict which case a type switch executes for a given value.
Learner must explain what type x has in each case arm.
Learner must distinguish type switches from regular switches on reflect.Type.
Learner must choose between type switch and sequential errors.As calls.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Standard library uses type switches: json.Decoder switches on JSON value types. fmt formatting uses type switches. path/filepath uses type switches for error checking.
zero_magic.mental_model: A type switch is like a package sorting machine. Packages arrive on a conveyor belt (interface). The machine checks each label (dynamic type). Books go to books bin, Electronics to electronics bin, unknown to default.
performance_implications: Type switches compile efficiently -- jump table for concrete types.
O(N) worst case (linear scan), often optimized to O(1) via hash-based dispatch.
Type switches are faster than sequential if-assertions.
Resolved at runtime, not compile time.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Sequential type assertions are verbose. A type switch provides clean, readable multi-type dispatch in a single structure.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write `formatValue(x interface{}) string` with a type switch returning specific formats for int, float64, string, bool, and default. Write tests for each case.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go uses type switches for: JSON unmarshaling (different JSON types), error classification (net.Error, *os.PathError, custom errors), logging argument formatting, template function arguments.
step_by_step_execution: Start with the interface variable.
Write `switch v := x.(type) {`.
List cases: `case int:`, `case string:`, `case MyType:`.
In each arm, v has the case's type -- use directly.
Always include `default:` for unexpected types.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The compiler generates type assertions wrapped in a switch. It can optimize using the type pointer from the interface's itab. For interface{}, the compiler may use a hash-based jump table for O(1) dispatch.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Type switches provide safe, readable multi-type dispatch for interface values. Essential for any code handling multiple concrete types through a single interface.

items.title: Nil interfaces
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Nil interfaces in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Writing return nil, err in a function that returns (MyCustomError, error) — the nil is typed as *MyCustomError(nil), making the error interface non-nil. The caller sees err != nil even though there was no error.
Checking if err == nil after calling a function that returns (*MyError)(nil) — the condition is false because the interface has a type even though the pointer is nil.
Using if err != nil { return err } in a loop where err is an interface — the first non-nil error is returned, but subsequent iterations may see a nil concrete value wrapped in a non-nil interface.
Comparing two interface values that each contain a nil *T — they compare equal. Comparing a nil interface with an interface containing nil *T — they compare NOT equal.
failure_modes: Masked nil error: a function returns (*MyError)(nil) instead of nil — the call-site error check passes (err != nil is true), and code proceeds with a nil value, causing a nil pointer dereference when accessing fields on the supposed error.

Marshaling nil interface values: json.Marshal returns null for a nil interface but error for an interface holding a nil pointer — JSON consumers may not distinguish these, leading to silent data loss.

Logging nil interface values: fmt.Printf("%v", err) prints <nil> for a nil interface but prints the concrete type name for an interface holding a nil pointer — operators misread logs and assume no error occurred.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must determine, for each of 5 code snippets, whether err == nil is true or false, and explain the two-word representation.
Learner must write a correct error-return pattern that prevents the typed-nil bug, without using reflect.
Learner must explain why a nil *os.PathError assigned to an error variable is not a nil error, and what happens in a type switch.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The Go compiler team considered making typed nil assignment to interface produce nil, but decided against it because it would break the rule that interface values are (type, value) pairs. The standard library everywhere uses the pattern: if the concrete error type is meaningful, return it typed; for simple sentinel errors, use errors.New or fmt.Errorf which return an untyped nil interface on success.
execution_timeline: var e error = nil → e = {type: nil, data: nil} — truly nil interface
var p *os.PathError = nil → p stores nil pointer
e = p → e = {type: *os.PathError, data: nil} — NON-nil interface because type is set!
e == nil → false — even though the underlying data is nil
if e != nil { return e } → returns non-nil interface that wraps nil pointer
caller checks err == nil → false — the nil error was not detected
zero_magic.mental_model: An interface value is a box with two compartments: one holds the type tag, one holds the data. A nil interface has both compartments empty. An interface holding a nil pointer has the type compartment filled and the data compartment empty — the box is NOT empty because the type tag is present. The '== nil' check looks at the whole box, not just the data.
performance_implications: Type assertions on interface values that hold nil concrete values do NOT panic as long as the type matches — they return (nil, false) for comma-ok, or a zero-value for direct assertions.

Comparing interface values with == compares both type and data — if the concrete type is comparable and the data matches, they are equal. For non-comparable types (slices, maps), comparison panics at runtime.
Reflection on interface values with nil concrete data still works — reflect.ValueOf(e).IsNil() returns true even though e != nil.
zero_magic.problem_solved: The typed-nil interface bug is the most common subtle bug in Go production code. A function returns a nil *T in an error interface, the caller checks err != nil (which is true), and proceeds with a nil value that panics. This lesson makes the two-word interface representation visible so that every learner can predict exactly when an interface is nil and when it is not.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a program that: (1) defines a custom error type, (2) creates a function that returns the custom error type as error, (3) returns nil explicitly, (4) demonstrates that err != nil is false, then (5) changes the function to return (*customError)(nil) and demonstrates that err != nil is now true, and (6) fixes the pattern so that nil errors are properly detected.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every Go production service that defines custom error types (API errors, database errors, domain errors) must handle the typed-nil interface pattern. Bugs from this pattern have caused production outages in Kubernetes, Docker, and etcd. The fix is always: return nil as the interface type directly (return nil, nil) rather than returning a typed nil (return (*MyError)(nil), nil).
step_by_step_execution: Function signature: func doSomething() error — return type is the error interface
Inside function: var err *MyError = nil — err is a nil pointer of type *MyError
Inside function: return err — compiler sees *MyError assigned to error interface
Runtime: runtime.convT2E or convT2I wraps *MyError into eface/iface — type = *MyError descriptor, data = nil
Caller: result, err := doSomething() — err is interface{type: *MyError, data: nil}
Caller: if err != nil — runtime compares both type and data; type is non-nil → condition is TRUE
Caller: uses err — calls err.Error() which dereferences nil *MyError → PANIC: nil pointer dereference
Fix: return nil — compiler sees untyped nil assigned to error interface → err = {type: nil, data: nil}
Caller: if err != nil — both type and data are nil → condition is FALSE, correct behavior restored
zero_magic.under_the_hood: An interface value in Go is implemented by the runtime as an iface (for non-empty interfaces) or eface (for empty interface{}). Both contain a _type pointer and an unsafe.Pointer for data. When you assign a nil *T to an interface, the _type pointer is set to the type descriptor for *T, and the data pointer is set to nil. The runtime's nil check (iface == nil) checks that BOTH the _type and data are nil — if either is non-nil, the interface is non-nil. This is by design: an interface that knows its type but has no data is not the same as an interface that doesn't know its type at all.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: The two-word interface representation exists because Go interfaces are structurally typed — the type information must be preserved at runtime for type assertions, type switches, and reflection. If assigning a typed nil to an interface produced a nil interface, type assertions would lose the concrete type information, breaking reflect and any code that depends on knowing the underlying type.

items.title: Package names
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Package names in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using generic names like `common`, `utils`, `helpers` -- dumping grounds.
Naming package same as primary export (package `config` with type `Config`) -- stutter.
Using underscores in package names -- Go convention is lowercase single-word.
Mixing plural and singular inconsistently.
execution_timeline: Create a package directory and choose a short, lowercase name.
Write `package name` at the top of each .go file.
The package name becomes the last import path segment.
Exported names (capitalized) are accessible to importers.
The package doc comment describes the package's purpose.

failure_modes.scenario: Creates `package common` containing User, Config, Logger, DB mixed together.
failure_modes.cause: The name is too generic -- anything 'common' goes there.
failure_modes.fix: Split into focused packages: user, config, logger, db.

failure_modes.scenario: Package `config` exports `Config` struct -- usage is `config.Config{}` (stutter).
failure_modes.cause: Package and type names repeat.
failure_modes.fix: Rename package to `cfg` or rename the type.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify stutter in package-type naming.
Learner must rename a utils package into focused packages.
Learner must explain why `package fmt` is a good name.
Learner must determine singular vs plural naming.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Standard library packages set convention: `http`, `json`, `xml`, `fmt`, `io`, `os`, `net`. Short, lowercase, single-word, descriptive. Package name describes what it provides.
zero_magic.mental_model: A package name is like a department name. You don't say 'Common Things Department' -- Engineering, Sales, Marketing. Each has clear responsibility.
performance_implications: Package names don't affect runtime performance.
Well-named packages improve developer productivity.
Stutter adds character count but no performance impact.
Import paths affect compile-time lookup.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without naming conventions, packages accumulate unrelated functionality. Naming conventions make code predictable: `http` is about HTTP, `json` is about JSON encoding.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must identify 3 standard library packages with good names, explain why each is appropriate, and rename 3 bad hypothetical packages into focused alternatives.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go follows: `api`, `store`, `worker`, `server`, `config`, `middleware`. Kubernetes uses `pkg/api`, `pkg/client`. Docker uses `cli`, `client`, `daemon`.
step_by_step_execution: Decide the package's single responsibility -- suggests its name.
Choose short, lowercase word describing what it provides.
Avoid stutter: if it provides Config type, don't name it config.
Write package declaration at the top of every file.
Keep name short (1-3 syllables).
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Package name must match across files in a directory. The compiler resolves import path to directory, reads .go files, verifies same package name. Package name is the prefix for exported identifiers.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Package names are the primary organizational unit. Good names make code navigable, understandable, maintainable. First thing developers see when reading a new codebase.

items.title: Export rules
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Export rules in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Assuming 'exported' means 'public' -- exported is accessible from other packages.
Using ALL_CAPS for exported constants -- Go doesn't use ALL_CAPS.
Thinking unexported functions cannot be tested -- test from same package.
Forgetting types with unexported fields cannot be constructed cross-package.
execution_timeline: Compiler checks if each identifier starts with a capital letter.
If capitalized, exported -- accessible via `packagename.Ident`.
If lowercase, unexported -- only within same package.
The access check happens at compile time.
Embedded types bring their own export rules.

failure_modes.scenario: Struct with all unexported fields tried to be created from another package.
failure_modes.cause: Fields unexported can't be set externally.
failure_modes.fix: Provide a constructor: `func NewMyType() MyType` or export fields.

failure_modes.scenario: Exports everything 'just in case' and loses API control.
failure_modes.cause: Every identifier becomes part of public API -- can't change without breaking.
failure_modes.fix: Export only what consumers need. You can export later but not un-export.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify which identifiers are exported vs unexported.
Learner must explain why unexported field structs need constructors cross-package.
Learner must determine export status of promoted methods from embedded types.
Learner must identify why a compile error is due to export rules.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Standard library: `os.Open` exported, internal error types unexported. `http.Handler` exported, `http.serveMux` internals unexported. Internal packages have stricter rules.
zero_magic.mental_model: Export rules are like building access control. Exported identifiers are the public lobby -- anyone enters. Unexported identifiers are back offices -- only employees (same package) enter.
performance_implications: Export status is compile-time only -- zero runtime impact.
Inlining across packages is unaffected by export status.
Unexporting reduces public API surface, easing future refactoring.
Internal packages (internal/) enforced by compiler.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without export rules, every identifier in every package would be accessible everywhere -- impossible to refactor or hide details. Export rules give control over the public API surface.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must create two packages: `store` (exported Store, unexported internals) and `main` (using store). Write tests showing which identifiers compile cross-package.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every Go package uses export rules. Exported types, functions, constants form the API. Helper functions, internal types remain unexported. `internal/` enforces package-group visibility.
step_by_step_execution: Design the public API: which types/functions/constants do consumers need?
Capitalize those identifiers.
Keep everything else lowercase.
Use `internal/` for code shared within a module but not externally.
Never export 'just in case' -- export on demand.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Compiler checks export rules at name resolution. When resolving `pkg.FuncName`, verifies FuncName is capitalized. Also enforces `internal/` package restrictions.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Export rules define the boundary between public API and private implementation. Essential for encapsulation -- without them, refactoring would break consumers.

items.title: Project layout
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Project layout in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Flat directory with 20+ .go files in one package -- breaks clarity.
Mixing multiple packages in the same directory -- one package per directory.
Putting all business logic in main.go -- main should be thin.
Creating unnecessarily deep directory trees -- 3-4 levels is enough.
execution_timeline: Create top-level directory named after the project.
Place cmd/ for executable entry points.
Place internal/ for private packages.
Place pkg/ for reusable public packages (optional).
Place business logic in domain-specific packages.
Place tests/ for integration tests.

failure_modes.scenario: All code in main.go -- 5000+ lines.
failure_modes.cause: No package organization.
failure_modes.fix: Extract cohesive groups into separate packages under internal/.

failure_modes.scenario: Package `repo` directly imported by `handlers` creating tight coupling.
failure_modes.cause: Direct import couples handler to repository implementation.
failure_modes.fix: Define interfaces in consuming package, implement in provider.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify which layer a piece of code belongs in.
Learner must explain why placing business logic in main.go is problematic.
Learner must design a package layout for a web service.
Learner must determine correct placement of internal/.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Standard library: `net/http`, `encoding/json`, `io` -- flat packages. Go tools use `cmd/` for multiple binaries. Modern Go projects follow `internal/` for private packages.
zero_magic.mental_model: Project layout is like a building floor plan. cmd/ is the lobby entrance. internal/ is the employee-only area. pkg/ is the public conference room. Each area has a clear purpose.
performance_implications: Well-structured projects compile faster -- better cache utilization.
Circular imports prevent compilation -- structure forces good design.
Layer violations cause runtime coupling but no direct performance cost.
internal/ enforcement is compile-time only.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without standard project layout, every project has a unique structure making it hard to navigate. Standard layout makes Go projects predictable and easy to understand for new contributors.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must design a directory structure for a web service with: HTTP handlers, business logic, data storage, and CLI commands. They must explain each directory's purpose.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Kubernetes, Docker, Prometheus, and most popular Go projects follow the standard Go project layout. `cmd/`, `internal/`, `pkg/` are recognizable conventions across the Go ecosystem.
step_by_step_execution: Identify the binaries to build -- each gets a cmd/ subdirectory.
Identify code that shouldn't be imported externally -- place in internal/.
Organize business logic by domain, not by layer.
Keep main packages thin -- parse flags, call logic, handle results.
Test from the consumer's perspective using external tests.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The `internal/` package restriction is enforced by the compiler: packages importing from another module's internal/ must share a common root. This enables module-level encapsulation beyond package-level export rules.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Standard project layout makes Go projects navigable and predictable. Without it, every project would need individual orientation, wasting developer time.

items.title: Modules
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Modules in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Not initializing a go.mod file -- can't manage dependencies properly.
Committing go.mod and go.sum without running `go mod tidy` first.
Editing go.mod by hand instead of using `go mod` commands.
Understanding module paths but not module versioning semantics.
execution_timeline: Run `go mod init <module-path>` to create go.mod.
Import dependencies in code with their full import paths.
Run `go mod tidy` to update go.mod with precise versions.
Build/test the module -- Go downloads dependencies automatically.
Repeat tidy before committing to ensure consistency.

failure_modes.scenario: Running `go build` in a project without go.mod -- 'could not determine module path' error.
failure_modes.cause: Go requires a module to manage dependencies since 1.16.
failure_modes.fix: Run `go mod init <module-path>` in the project root.

failure_modes.scenario: Hand-editing go.mod to add dependencies -- format errors.
failure_modes.cause: go.mod has a specific format the toolchain manages.
failure_modes.fix: Use `go get <package>` or edit go.mod, then run `go mod tidy`.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain what go.mod defines at minimum.
Learner must demonstrate running go mod init and go mod tidy.
Learner must identify the difference between GOPATH and module mode.
Learner must verify go.mod consistency with go mod verify.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Every modern Go project uses modules. The standard library itself is built with modules. Third-party tools, frameworks, and libraries all declare module paths in their go.mod files.
zero_magic.mental_model: A module is a self-contained unit of Go code with a version. It's like a book: go.mod is the title page (module name, version, dependencies). Other modules can reference this book by its ISBN (module path).
performance_implications: Modules don't affect runtime performance.
Module resolution happens at compile time -- larger dependency graphs compile slower.
The module cache avoids re-downloading.
Go's minimal version selection avoids diamond dependency conflicts.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Before modules, Go developers used GOPATH -- all code in a single workspace with no versioning. Modules solve dependency management with explicit versioning, minimal version selection, and reproducible builds.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must create a new module, add a third-party dependency, write code using it, run `go mod tidy`, and explain what go.mod and go.sum contain.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every Go project since 1.16 uses modules. `go.mod` is the standard way to declare dependencies. CI pipelines run `go mod tidy` and check for consistency. Go modules are the foundation of Go's ecosystem.
step_by_step_execution: Run `go mod init example.com/myproject` in the project root.
Write Go code importing packages from the standard library or external modules.
Run `go get example.com/somepackage@v1.2.3` to add a dependency.
Run `go mod tidy` to clean up the dependency list.
Commit go.mod and go.sum together.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: go.mod uses HCL-like syntax declaring the module path, Go version, and dependencies with versions. go.sum contains cryptographic hashes of each dependency module. Go's minimal version selection picks the minimum required version that satisfies all imports.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Go modules solve the dependency management problem that plagued pre-1.11 Go. They provide reproducible builds, versioned dependencies, and a decentralized module ecosystem.

items.title: go.mod
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply go.mod in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Editing go.mod by hand instead of using `go get` or `go mod edit`.
Setting go version to latest without checking compatibility.
Committing go.mod with untidy dependency entries.
Using `replace` directives in go.mod unnecessarily.
execution_timeline: Run `go mod init example.com/mymodule` -- creates go.mod with module path and Go version.
Add imports to code -- Go toolchain discovers required dependencies.
Run `go mod tidy` -- adds direct and indirect dependencies with precise versions.
Run `go build` or `go test` -- uses go.mod for dependency resolution.
Commit go.mod alongside go.sum for reproducible builds.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer changes go version in go.mod from 1.21 to 1.23, but uses 1.21 features only.
failure_modes.cause: Unnecessary version bump may cause compatibility issues with some dependencies.
failure_modes.fix: Keep go version at the minimum Go version your code actually requires.

failure_modes.scenario: Using `go get` without `@version` gets the latest -- may introduce breaking changes.
failure_modes.cause: Latest may include API-breaking changes.
failure_modes.fix: Pin to specific versions: `go get example.com/pkg@v1.2.3`.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain each section of go.mod (module path, go version, require, exclude, replace).
Learner must demonstrate adding a dependency via go get.
Learner must identify when replace directives are appropriate.
Learner must understand the difference between direct and indirect dependencies.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Every Go project uses go.mod. The standard library modules each have go.mod files. `go mod tidy` is the standard command for cleaning up go.mod.
zero_magic.mental_model: go.mod is like a shopping list for your project's dependencies. It says: 'I need these packages at these versions.' When you build, Go fetches exactly what's on the list.
performance_implications: go.mod resolution happens at compile time -- no runtime impact.
More dependencies increase compile time slightly.
The module cache avoids repeated downloads.
replace directives only affect local builds.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Go.mod solves the problem of declaring and versioning dependencies. Before modules, GOPATH mode had no versioning -- you got whatever was on disk. go.mod provides explicit, versioned, reproducible dependency declarations.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must create a new module, add two dependencies (one standard, one third-party), run go mod tidy, and explain each section of the resulting go.mod file.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every Go project since 1.16 has a go.mod. CI systems run `go mod tidy && go mod verify` to check consistency. go.mod is the first file a developer checks when understanding a project's dependencies.
step_by_step_execution: Create a new module: `go mod init example.com/myapp`.
Add imports in code for external packages.
Fetch dependencies: `go get example.com/some/lib@v1.0.0`.
Clean up: `go mod tidy`.
Verify: `go mod verify` -- commit go.mod and go.sum.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: go.mod uses a line-based format with directives: `module`, `go`, `require`, `exclude`, `replace`. The `go` directive specifies the language version, not the toolchain version. `require` lists dependencies with semantic import versioning.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: go.mod is the foundation of Go's dependency management. It provides explicit, versioned, and reproducible module definitions that the Go toolchain uses for building, testing, and vendoring.

items.title: go.sum
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply go.sum in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Committing go.sum without go.mod -- they must go together.
Manually editing go.sum -- it's auto-generated and must not be hand-edited.
Ignoring go.sum checksum mismatches -- indicates tampering or corruption.
Not committing go.sum -- breaks reproducible builds for other developers.
execution_timeline: Run `go mod tidy` -- Go computes checksums for all direct and indirect dependencies.
Go writes the checksums to go.sum -- one entry per module version.
On subsequent builds, Go verifies downloaded modules match go.sum entries.
If checksums don't match, the build fails with a security error.
go.sum is updated automatically when dependency versions change.

failure_modes.scenario: go.sum checksum mismatch prevents build -- security error.
failure_modes.cause: The downloaded module content doesn't match the recorded checksum.
failure_modes.fix: Run `go mod tidy` to update checksums, or investigate if tampering occurred.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer deletes go.sum and tries to rebuild without running go mod tidy.
failure_modes.cause: Build fails because no checksums to verify against.
failure_modes.fix: Run `go mod tidy` to regenerate go.sum from go.mod.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain what go.sum contains and why it exists.
Learner must demonstrate running go mod tidy to generate go.sum.
Learner must identify what happens on checksum mismatch.
Learner must explain the difference between go.sum and a lock file.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Every Go module uses go.sum. The Go checksum database (sum.golang.org) provides publicly verifiable checksums. `GONOSUMCHECK` and `GONOSUMDB` environment variables control checksum verification for private modules.
zero_magic.mental_model: go.sum is like a fingerprint database for your dependencies. Each module version has a unique fingerprint (checksum). Before using a dependency, Go checks that its fingerprint matches the recorded one. This prevents accidentally or maliciously modified dependencies.
performance_implications: Checksum verification adds minimal overhead to builds.
Checksum lookups are cached locally.
No runtime performance impact.
The checksum database adds a network round-trip on first use.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without go.sum, a dependency could be silently modified between builds -- either accidentally (different version resolved) or maliciously (supply chain attack). go.sum ensures every build uses exactly the same dependency content.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must create a module, add a dependency, run go mod tidy, inspect go.sum, and explain why entries change when dependency versions change.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: CI pipelines check go.sum consistency. Module proxies cache checksummed modules. go.sum is co-committed with go.mod in every Go project.
step_by_step_execution: Initialize a module with go mod init.
Add imports and dependencies.
Run `go mod tidy` -- generates go.sum automatically.
Commit go.mod and go.sum together.
On dependency updates, run `go mod tidy` again to update go.sum.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: go.sum contains lines like `module version h1:hash`. The hash is a cryptographic checksum using the Go checksum format (h1:). The `go mod verify` command checks that locally cached modules match go.sum entries.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: go.sum provides supply-chain security and build reproducibility. Without it, there's no guarantee that the code you're building against is the same code that was originally intended.

items.title: Dependency management
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Dependency management in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Not pinning dependency versions -- using latest causes unpredictable builds.
Adding dependencies for trivial functionality that could be written in a few lines.
Not running `go mod tidy` before committing -- leaves unnecessary or stale entries.
Committing vendor directory without running `go mod vendor` first.
execution_timeline: Add import statements for external packages in Go source files.
Run `go mod tidy` -- resolves all imports to specific versions.
The Go toolchain downloads missing modules to the module cache.
Build compiles using the resolved dependency versions.
Run `go mod verify` to confirm module integrity.

failure_modes.scenario: A dependency upgrade breaks the build unexpectedly.
failure_modes.cause: Using `go get -u` upgraded a minor version with breaking changes.
failure_modes.fix: Pin versions explicitly: `go get example.com/pkg@v1.2.3`.

failure_modes.scenario: Two modules require different versions of the same dependency -- version conflict.
failure_modes.cause: Go's minimal version selection picks the minimum required version; but some features may be missing.
failure_modes.fix: Use `go mod graph` to understand the dependency tree, then update or replace as needed.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must demonstrate adding a dependency and running go mod tidy.
Learner must explain Go's minimal version selection (MVS).
Learner must identify stale dependencies with `go mod why`.
Learner must demonstrate updating a dependency to a specific version.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The Go module system uses minimal version selection (MVS) for dependency resolution. `go mod graph` shows the dependency tree. `go mod why` explains why a dependency is needed. `go mod vendor` creates a vendor directory.
zero_magic.mental_model: Dependency management is like planning a potluck dinner. Each guest (module) brings certain dishes (packages). The organizer (go mod tidy) makes sure there's no conflict (version mismatch) and the meal is complete (all dependencies satisfied).
performance_implications: More dependencies increase compile time and binary size.
The module proxy (proxy.golang.org) speeds up downloads.
Vendored dependencies avoid network access during builds.
Dependency graph depth affects resolution time.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without dependency management, every project manually manages third-party code. Dependencies conflict, versions are uncontrollable, and updates are risky. Go modules provide automatic, versioned, and conflict-free dependency management.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must add a dependency to an existing module, update it to a newer version, use `go mod graph` to visualize dependencies, and use `go mod vendor` to create a vendor directory.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go services manage dozens or hundreds of dependencies via go.mod. CI pipelines enforce tidy state. Dependency updates are managed carefully with semantic versioning awareness.
step_by_step_execution: Identify a needed external package and its import path.
Add the import to your Go source file.
Run `go mod tidy` to resolve and record the dependency.
Use `go get package@version` to control specific versions.
Run `go mod vendor` to create a vendor directory for offline builds.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Minimal version selection (MVS) works by walking the dependency graph and picking the minimum version of each module that satisfies all requirements. MVS guarantees reproducible builds -- given the same inputs, the same versions are always selected.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Dependency management is essential for any non-trivial project. Go modules provide a robust, deterministic, and easy-to-use system that avoids the dependency hell problems of other ecosystems.

items.title: Workspaces
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Workspaces in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Creating a workspace when a single module would suffice.
Forgetting to run `go work sync` after changing workspace modules.
Committing go.work when it should be local/go.work is gitignored.
Adding replace directives in individual go.mod files instead of using workspace.
execution_timeline: Create a go.work file: `go work init ./module1 ./module2`.
The workspace file declares multiple modules that should be used together locally.
Build commands resolve module references within the workspace first.
Changes in one module are immediately visible to dependent workspace modules.
Run `go work sync` to synchronize dependency requirements across workspace modules.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer creates a workspace for a single-module project -- unnecessary overhead.
failure_modes.cause: go.work is useful for multi-module workflows only.
failure_modes.fix: For a single module, just use go.mod directly.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer edits go.mod in one workspace module, but other modules don't pick up the change.
failure_modes.cause: go work sync wasn't run after the change.
failure_modes.fix: Run `go work sync` to synchronize dependency versions across workspace modules.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain when to use workspaces vs single modules.
Learner must demonstrate creating a workspace with go work init.
Learner must explain why go.work should not be committed.
Learner must demonstrate using workspace for local module development.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go workspaces were introduced in Go 1.18 for multi-module development. The Go toolchain itself uses workspaces internally. `go.work` is typically local and gitignored; it doesn't affect other developers or CI.
zero_magic.mental_model: A Go workspace is like a shared desk where multiple projects (modules) can be developed together. When one project changes, the others see the changes immediately. The go.work file is the desk's arrangement plan.
performance_implications: Workspace resolution adds negligible compile-time overhead.
Local replace directives in go.mod are faster than workspace lookup.
Workspace doesn't affect runtime performance.
go.work is not used by the module proxy.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without workspaces, developing multiple dependent modules locally requires `replace` directives in each go.mod, which must be removed before publishing. Workspaces provide a clean way to develop multiple modules together without modifying go.mod files.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must create two modules where one depends on the other, set up a workspace with `go work init`, make a change in the dependency module, and verify the dependent module sees the change immediately.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Workspaces are used when developing libraries alongside applications, when monorepos contain multiple modules, and when contributing changes across multiple repositories simultaneously.
step_by_step_execution: Create or have two modules where one depends on the other.
Run `go work init ./module1 ./module2` to create go.work.
Make changes in the dependency module.
Build/test the dependent module -- changes are picked up immediately.
Run `go work sync` to synchronize dependency versions.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: go.work uses a similar format to go.mod with `go`, `use`, and `replace` directives. The `use` directive points to local module directories. When resolving imports, Go checks workspace modules before the module cache or proxy.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Workspaces solve the local module development problem without polluting go.mod with replace directives. They enable smooth multi-module development workflows common in larger projects.

items.title: Documentation comments
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Documentation comments in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Writing documentation comments that just repeat the function name.
Not documenting exported identifiers at all -- go vet will warn.
Using `//` instead of `// ` (double-slash space) -- formatting tools expect the space.
Writing implementation details instead of documenting the contract/documentation vs comment confusion.
execution_timeline: Place a `// Package` comment above the package declaration describing the package's purpose.
Place `// FunctionName` comments above each exported function describing its behavior, parameters, and return values.
Place `// TypeName` comments above exported types explaining what they represent.
Run `go vet` to check for missing documentation on exported identifiers.
Run `go doc` or `pkgsite` to view the rendered documentation.

failure_modes.scenario: Exported function has no doc comment -- go vet reports 'exported function should have comment or be unexported'.
failure_modes.cause: Go convention requires doc comments for all exported identifiers.
failure_modes.fix: Add a // FunctionName comment before the function declaration.

failure_modes.scenario: Doc comment describes how the function works instead of what it does.
failure_modes.cause: Doc comments should describe the contract (what), not the implementation (how).
failure_modes.fix: Rewrite the comment to focus on caller-facing semantics.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify missing doc comments with go vet.
Learner must write effective doc comments for an exported function.
Learner must explain the difference between doc comments and regular comments.
Learner must use go doc to view rendered documentation.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Every standard library package has doc comments. `go doc` generates documentation from comments. `pkgsite` provides web-based documentation. `//go:embed` and other directives also use comment-style annotations.
zero_magic.mental_model: Documentation comments are like a user manual for your code. They're not implementation notes -- they tell other developers what your code does, what it expects, and what they can rely on. Good documentation makes code usable without reading the source.
performance_implications: Doc comments have zero runtime cost -- stripped during compilation.
Good documentation reduces developer time spent reading source code.
go doc rendering adds no overhead to builds or execution.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without documentation comments, developers must read source code to understand each function's behavior. Documentation comments provide a quick, consistent way to understand what code does without reading its implementation.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a package with doc comments for the package, two exported functions, and one exported type. They must run `go vet` to verify no missing comments, and use `go doc` to view the rendered output.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every well-maintained Go project has doc comments. `go vet` enforces the convention. `go doc` provides command-line documentation. Go's standard library documentation is the gold standard for doc comments.
step_by_step_execution: Start each exported identifier with a comment: `// FunctionName does X.`.
Use complete sentences describing the behavior, parameters, and returns.
Document edge cases and panics explicitly.
Place package-level doc comment before the `package` declaration.
Run `go vet ./...` to check for missing doc comments.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's doc comment format uses a simple convention: the comment immediately preceding a top-level declaration documents that declaration. `go doc` and `pkgsite` parse these comments and display them as documentation. There's no markup language -- just plain text with code examples.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Documentation comments are Go's built-in documentation mechanism. They keep documentation close to the code it describes, reducing the chance of outdated docs. Every exported identifier should have a doc comment as a Go convention.

items.title: Why testing exists
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Why testing exists in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Treating tests as an afterthought -- writing them after the code is 'done'.
Not understanding that tests are executable documentation.
Believing manual testing is sufficient for correctness.
Testing only happy paths and skipping error cases.
execution_timeline: Write a failing test first (red).
Write minimal code to make the test pass (green).
Refactor the code while keeping tests green (refactor).
Run the full test suite before every commit.
Tests serve as regression protection for future changes.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer deploys code that passes manual testing but breaks in production.
failure_modes.cause: Manual testing missed edge cases that automated tests would catch.
failure_modes.fix: Write automated tests covering edge cases, not just happy paths.

failure_modes.scenario: A team has no tests and is afraid to refactor.
failure_modes.cause: Without test safety net, every change risks breaking existing behavior.
failure_modes.fix: Start by writing tests for the most critical and most changed code paths.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain why testing is essential for maintaining code quality.
Learner must describe the test pyramid and where unit tests fit.
Learner must identify why manual testing alone is insufficient.
Learner must explain how tests enable safe refactoring.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Testing is built into Go's toolchain with `go test`. The standard library's `testing` package provides test framework, benchmarks, fuzzing, and coverage. Go's design philosophy treats testing as a first-class development activity.
zero_magic.mental_model: Tests are a safety net. They catch regressions, document expected behavior, and make refactoring safe. The test suite is the executable specification of what the code should do. If the tests pass, the code works as intended.
performance_implications: Tests increase compile time (test binary compilation) but catch bugs early.
A comprehensive test suite is faster than debugging production issues.
Table-driven tests reduce code duplication and run efficiently.
Beware of slow tests that discourage running them frequently.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without tests, there's no automated way to verify correctness. Changes break existing functionality silently. Tests provide a repeatable, automated verification mechanism that catches regressions and documents expected behavior.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must explain the Red-Green-Refactor cycle, write a simple test for a function, run it with `go test -v`, and explain what the test output means.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every production Go service has an extensive test suite. CI pipelines run tests on every pull request. Code review requires new tests for new code. Testing is not optional in professional Go development.
step_by_step_execution: Identify a function or behavior to test.
Write a test function in a _test.go file.
Run with `go test -v ./...`.
See the test fail (red), then fix the code (green).
Run the full suite before committing.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's test runner compiles test files (those ending in _test.go) alongside the package. Each Test function is called with a *testing.T parameter. The test passes if no failure methods (t.Error, t.Fatal) are called before the function returns.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Testing exists to provide confidence in code correctness. Without tests, every code change is a risk. Testing is the foundation of maintainable, reliable software.

items.title: Unit testing
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Unit testing in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Writing integration tests instead of unit tests -- testing too many layers at once.
Testing private functions through the public API -- acceptable but sometimes calls for internal tests.
Not using table-driven tests for multiple test cases -- repetitive test code.
Ignoring test output and not reading test failures.
execution_timeline: Identify a single unit (function, method) to test in isolation.
Set up the test inputs and expected outputs.
Call the unit with the inputs.
Compare the actual output to the expected output.
Report failure if they don't match, pass if they do.

failure_modes.scenario: A unit test depends on a database connection -- slow, flaky, not a unit test.
failure_modes.cause: The test is not isolated -- it tests across boundaries.
failure_modes.fix: Mock or stub the database dependency to test the logic in isolation.

failure_modes.scenario: A test passes but the code fails in production.
failure_modes.cause: The test doesn't cover the actual failure modes.
failure_modes.fix: Test edge cases: empty inputs, nil values, boundary conditions.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify whether a test is a unit test or integration test.
Learner must write a unit test that tests a function in isolation.
Learner must explain the FIRST principles (Fast, Isolated, Repeatable, Self-verifying, Timely).
Learner must identify test dependencies that should be mocked.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The standard library uses unit tests extensively. `go test` is the command. Test files are named `*_test.go`. Test functions take `*testing.T`. The `go test -short` flag skips slow integration tests.
zero_magic.mental_model: A unit test is like testing a single gear from a clock. You take the gear out, spin it with known force, and check that it rotates the expected amount. You don't care about the rest of the clock for this test.
performance_implications: Unit tests are fast (microseconds per test).
Fast tests encourage running them frequently.
Unit tests compile alongside the package -- no separate compilation step.
Use -count=1 to disable test caching during development.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without unit tests, every code change requires manual verification of every affected code path. Unit tests provide fast, automated verification of individual components in isolation.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a unit test for a function that calculates the area of a rectangle (including edge cases: zero, negative, large values). Run with `go test -v`.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Unit tests are the foundation of Go testing. Every standard library package has unit tests. Most functions in Go production codebases have associated unit tests.
step_by_step_execution: Create a file named `something_test.go` in the same package.
Import the `testing` package.
Write `func TestSomething(t *testing.T) { ... }`.
Call your function with inputs and compare to expected outputs.
Use `t.Errorf` or `t.Fatalf` to report failures.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's test runner discovers tests by looking for `func TestXxx(t *testing.T)` in _test.go files. Each test is run sequentially unless subtests are used. The -v flag shows verbose output including each test's result.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Unit testing provides fast, reliable verification of individual code units. It enables continuous integration, refactoring safety, and documented expected behavior.

items.title: Table-driven tests
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Table-driven tests in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Writing separate test functions for each test case instead of using table-driven tests.
Not including a test name in each table entry -- failures are hard to identify.
Using the same test inputs as the code -- tests should cover edge cases.
Forgetting to add new cases to the table when adding new code paths.
execution_timeline: Define a struct for test cases with name, inputs, and expected output.
Define a slice of test cases covering expected behavior and edge cases.
Iterate over the slice, running each case as a sub-test (optional but recommended).
For each case, call the function under test and compare results.
Report failures with the test case name for easy identification.

failure_modes.scenario: Adding a new test case requires writing a new Test function -- lots of boilerplate.
failure_modes.cause: Without table-driven tests, each case is a separate function.
failure_modes.fix: Refactor to a table-driven test: one Test function, many cases.

failure_modes.scenario: Table-driven test fails but the error message doesn't identify which case failed.
failure_modes.cause: Test case names aren't included in failure messages.
failure_modes.fix: Use `t.Run(name, func(t *testing.T) { ... })` for named subtests.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must recognize when table-driven tests are appropriate.
Learner must write a table-driven test with at least 5 cases.
Learner must explain why table-driven tests reduce code duplication.
Learner must include edge cases in the test table.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Table-driven tests are the standard test pattern in Go. The standard library uses them extensively. They reduce boilerplate and make adding new test cases trivial.
zero_magic.mental_model: A table-driven test is like a recipe book with multiple recipes. Each recipe (table row) has ingredients (inputs), expected result, and a name. The cook (test function) follows each recipe and checks that the result matches expectations.
performance_implications: Table-driven tests reduce code duplication -- easier to maintain.
All cases run in the same Test function -- slightly faster than separate functions.
Subtests can be run individually with `go test -run TestName/CaseName`.
Coverage is measured across all cases.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without table-driven tests, each test case requires a separate function or duplicated test logic. Adding a new case means copying and pasting the entire test structure. Table-driven tests make adding cases trivial.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must rewrite a set of 5 separate test functions into a single table-driven test with subtests using `t.Run`. Run with `-v` to show each subtest.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Table-driven tests are the idiomatic Go testing pattern. They are used in the standard library, open source projects, and production codebases. Code reviews expect table-driven tests for functions with multiple cases.
step_by_step_execution: Define a test case struct: `type testCase struct { name string; input int; expected int }`.
Define a slice of test cases covering normal and edge cases.
Use `for _, tc := range tests { ... }` to iterate.
Use `t.Run(tc.name, func(t *testing.T) { ... })` for named subtests.
Report failures with tc.name for easy identification.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Table-driven tests work because Go's testing framework runs Test functions. Inside the function, you control the loop and reporting. `t.Run` creates a subtest that appears in the test output hierarchy.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Table-driven tests reduce boilerplate and make Go testing efficient. Adding a new test case is as simple as adding a row to the table. They're the idiomatic Go testing pattern.

items.title: Subtests
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Subtests in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Not using subtests for table-driven tests -- all cases run in one blob.
Sharing t between parent and subtests incorrectly.
Using `t.Fatal` in a subtest instead of `t.Error` -- Fatal stops the parent test too.
Running subtests in parallel without understanding race conditions.
execution_timeline: Call `t.Run(name, func(t *testing.T) { ... })` inside a Test function.
The parent test continues while the subtest runs.
Subtests can have their own setup and teardown (no shared state issues).
Subtests can be run individually: `go test -run TestName/CaseName`.
Subtests support parallel execution with `t.Parallel()`.

failure_modes.scenario: A subtest calls t.Fatal -- the parent test also stops, even if other subtests remain.
failure_modes.cause: t.Fatal stops the current goroutine, which stops the parent.
failure_modes.fix: Use t.Error for non-critical failures in subtests.

failure_modes.scenario: Subtests share a loop variable without capturing it inside the closure.
failure_modes.cause: The loop variable changes before the closure executes.
failure_modes.fix: Capture the loop variable: `tc := tc` or pass it as a parameter.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must write subtests for a table-driven test.
Learner must explain the difference between t.Error and t.Fatal in subtests.
Learner must run a specific subtest with -run flag.
Learner must demonstrate t.Parallel() in subtests.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Subtests are used throughout the standard library for organizing test cases. `go test -run` enables targeted subtest execution. t.Cleanup works naturally with subtests for per-case cleanup.
zero_magic.mental_model: Subtests are like chapters in a test book. The Test function is the book, each t.Run is a chapter. You can read (run) the whole book or jump to a specific chapter. Each chapter has its own setup and teardown.
performance_implications: Subtests add minimal overhead per subtest.
Parallel subtests can significantly speed up test suites on multi-core machines.
Individual subtest execution enables targeted debugging.
Subtests improve CI output by showing which specific case failed.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without subtests, a failing test case requires running the entire test function to diagnose. Subtests provide granular failure reporting and individual test case execution.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a table-driven test with at least 3 subtests, demonstrate running a specific subtest with -run, and show t.Error and t.Fatal differences.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Subtests are standard in Go testing. Table-driven tests use subtests for each case. CI systems show individual subtest pass/fail. Debuggers can target specific subtests.
step_by_step_execution: In a Test function, define a slice of test cases.
Use `t.Run(name, func(t *testing.T) { ... })` for each case.
Inside the subtest closure, run the case and report failures.
Run all subtests: `go test -v`.
Run a specific subtest: `go test -v -run TestName/CaseName`.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: t.Run starts a new goroutine for each subtest. The parent test waits for all subtests to complete before returning. The testing framework tracks each subtest as a separate entry in the test result hierarchy.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Subtests provide granular test organization and execution. They enable individual test case debugging, parallel execution, and clearer test output.

items.title: t.Cleanup
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply t.Cleanup in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Forgetting that t.Cleanup runs in LIFO order, not FIFO.
Using defer for cleanup instead of t.Cleanup -- defer runs on function return, t.Cleanup runs when the test and all its subtests complete.
Registering cleanup in a helper function that's called from multiple tests -- cleanup registers per test.
Not understanding that t.Cleanup can be called multiple times.
execution_timeline: Test starts and acquires resources (temp dir, DB connection, file).
Register cleanup with t.Cleanup(func() { ... }) -- runs when the test completes.
The test body executes, using the resources.
When the test and all its subtests complete, cleanup functions run in LIFO order.
Resources are released deterministically.

failure_modes.scenario: Cleanup registered in a subtest runs after the subtest, not after the parent test.
failure_modes.cause: t.Cleanup is scoped to the test it's registered in.
failure_modes.fix: Register cleanup in the correct scope (parent test for test-level cleanup, subtest for per-case).

failure_modes.scenario: Using defer for file cleanup -- if the test has subtests, the file closes before subtests run.
failure_modes.cause: Defer runs when the function returns, but subtests may still run.
failure_modes.fix: Use t.Cleanup instead of defer for test-scoped cleanup.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain when t.Cleanup runs vs defer.
Learner must demonstrate t.Cleanup for temp file cleanup.
Learner must explain LIFO execution order of multiple t.Cleanup calls.
Learner must identify when to use t.Cleanup vs defer.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The testing package provides t.Cleanup as the standard test teardown mechanism. It's recommended over defer for test cleanup because it integrates with subtests and parallel execution correctly.
zero_magic.mental_model: t.Cleanup is like checking luggage at an airport. You hand over items (register cleanup) as you acquire them. When you leave (test completes), items are returned in reverse order (LIFO). This ensures proper resource release order.
performance_implications: t.Cleanup adds negligible overhead.
Proper cleanup prevents resource leaks in long-running test suites.
Multiple cleanup registrations are efficient -- stored in a stack.
Cleaning correctly prevents test pollution across test cases.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without t.Cleanup, tests need manual teardown at each return point, or defer that doesn't integrate with subtests. t.Cleanup provides automatic, LIFO-ordered cleanup that respects subtests and parallel execution.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a test that creates a temp file, registers t.Cleanup to remove it, writes to the file, reads it back, and verifies content. Show that the temp file is removed after the test.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: t.Cleanup is used for cleaning up temp directories, closing test databases, stopping test servers, and restoring global state. It's the standard teardown mechanism in modern Go tests.
step_by_step_execution: Acquire a resource (temp dir, file, DB connection).
Register cleanup: `t.Cleanup(func() { /* release resource */ })`.
Test body uses the resource.
When test completes, cleanup runs automatically.
Multiple cleanups run in LIFO order.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: t.Cleanup adds a cleanup function to an internal stack in the test's internal state. When the test completes (pass, fail, or panic), the stack is popped in LIFO order. Cleanups registered in subtests run when the subtest completes.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: t.Cleanup provides deterministic, automatic test teardown that correctly handles subtests and parallel execution. It's more reliable than defer for test-scoped cleanup.

items.title: Test fixtures
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Test fixtures in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Creating test fixtures inside test functions instead of using testdata/ directory.
Modifying test fixtures during tests -- they should be read-only.
Not using t.TempDir() for temporary test directories.
Hardcoding file paths that break when tests are run from different working directories.
execution_timeline: Place fixture files in `testdata/` subdirectory of the package.
Test opens and reads fixture files using relative paths from the testdata directory.
Test creates temporary files using t.TempDir() for output files.
Test processes fixture input and compares with expected output.
t.Cleanup removes any temporary files created during the test.

failure_modes.scenario: Test modifies a fixture file -- subsequent test runs fail or produce different results.
failure_modes.cause: Fixtures should be read-only inputs.
failure_modes.fix: Use t.TempDir() to copy fixtures before modification.

failure_modes.scenario: Fixture path is hardcoded to an absolute path -- tests fail on other machines.
failure_modes.cause: Absolute paths don't transfer between environments.
failure_modes.fix: Use relative paths from the package directory (test runner sets working directory to the package directory).

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain the purpose of the testdata directory.
Learner must create a test fixture and write a test that uses it.
Learner must use t.TempDir() for temporary test files.
Learner must explain why fixtures should be read-only.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The standard library uses `testdata/` extensively. `go test` treats testdata as a special directory (it's not compiled). The `testing` package provides `t.TempDir()` for temporary directories that are automatically cleaned up.
zero_magic.mental_model: Test fixtures are like photographs of expected test inputs and outputs. You keep the photo album (testdata directory) with your tests. Each test opens the relevant photo and checks current output against it.
performance_implications: File I/O for fixtures is slower than in-memory data -- use for integration-style tests.
t.TempDir() is fast and automatically cleaned.
Fixture files should be small to keep tests fast.
Large fixtures slow down test execution.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without test fixtures, test data must be created inline, making tests verbose and data hard to reuse across tests. Fixtures provide reusable, version-controllable test data in a standard location.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must create a testdata directory with a sample input file, write a test that reads the file, processes it, and verifies the output against a golden file.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go tests use fixtures for JSON input samples, SQL migration files, configuration files, and binary test data. The testdata directory is a Go convention recognized by the toolchain.
step_by_step_execution: Create a `testdata/` directory in the package.
Place fixture files (input data, expected outputs) in testdata.
In tests, open fixtures with relative paths from the package.
Use `t.TempDir()` for temporary output files.
Clean up with t.Cleanup (or let t.TempDir handle it).
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The `go test` command changes the working directory to the package directory before running tests. This means relative paths to testdata resolve correctly regardless of where `go test` is invoked from. The testdata directory itself is skipped by the Go compiler.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Test fixtures provide reusable test data in a standard location. They keep test code clean and data separate from logic.

items.title: Golden files
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Golden files in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Hand-writing expected output files -- they should be generated by running the test once.
Not updating golden files when expected output changes -- tests fail silently.
Storing large binary files in golden files -- prefer text formats.
Not using -update flag workflow for maintaining golden files.
execution_timeline: Run tests with `-update` flag to generate or update golden files (if your test framework supports it).
Review the generated golden file content for correctness.
Commit the golden file alongside the test code.
Future test runs compare actual output against the golden file.
When output intentionally changes, re-run with -update and review the diff.

failure_modes.scenario: A test fails because the golden file doesn't match, but the output is actually correct.
failure_modes.cause: The golden file is outdated from a previous change.
failure_modes.fix: Update the golden file by running with the update flag, or delete and regenerate.

failure_modes.scenario: Golden file diff is ignored during code review -- wrong output gets committed.
failure_modes.cause: Golden files are committed alongside code and must be reviewed.
failure_modes.fix: Always diff golden file changes in code review.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must write a golden file test that compares output to a golden file.
Learner must implement an update flag for regenerating golden files.
Learner must explain the workflow for updating golden files.
Learner must identify when golden file tests are appropriate.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Golden file testing is used in the Go standard library for code generation tests, formatter tests (gofmt), and compiler output tests. The `testing/golden` package (Go 1.24) provides built-in golden file support.
zero_magic.mental_model: A golden file is like a master recording of expected output. The test plays back the code, records the output, and compares it to the master recording. If they match, the test passes. If not, either the code or the master needs updating.
performance_implications: Golden file comparison is fast -- byte comparison.
Generating golden files is a write-once cost.
Large golden files (MB+) slow down test reading.
Diff output for golden file failures shows exact line differences.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without golden files, expected output must be hardcoded in tests, making tests verbose and brittle. Golden files provide an external source of truth for expected output that's automatically comparable.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a function that generates a formatted string, write a golden file test that compares the output to a stored golden file, and implement an update flag.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Golden files are used for testing code generators, formatters, serializers, and any code that produces predictable output. They prevent regression in output format.
step_by_step_execution: Define the flag: `var update = flag.Bool("update", false, "update golden files")`.
Run the function and capture its output.
If -update is set, write output to golden file.
Otherwise, read the golden file and compare to output.
Report diff if they don't match.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Golden file testing typically uses `flag` for the update flag, `os.ReadFile`/`os.WriteFile` for file operations, and string/byte comparison for the diff. Go 1.24's `testing/golden` package provides a standardized implementation.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Golden files make output comparison tests maintainable. When output changes intentionally, you update the file rather than the test code. They provide clear diffs when tests fail.

items.title: Testable design with io.Writer
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Testable design with io.Writer in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Hardcoding fmt.Print in functions instead of accepting io.Writer.
Writing to stdout directly in business logic -- impossible to test output.
Creating test-specific output paths instead of using io.Writer abstraction.
Passing strings around instead of io.Writer for output destinations.
execution_timeline: Accept `w io.Writer` as a parameter instead of writing to stdout.
In production, pass `os.Stdout` (or any writer).
In tests, pass `bytes.Buffer` as the writer.
After the function executes, inspect the buffer contents.
Assert on the buffer content to verify output correctness.

failure_modes.scenario: Function writes to stdout via fmt.Println -- can't capture output in tests.
failure_modes.cause: The output destination is hardcoded.
failure_modes.fix: Accept io.Writer parameter and write to it with fmt.Fprintln.

failure_modes.scenario: Function returns a string but the caller needs to write it somewhere specific.
failure_modes.cause: Returning strings is fine, but if the function should write to flexible destinations, use io.Writer.
failure_modes.fix: Accept io.Writer or return string -- choose based on usage pattern.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must refactor a function that prints to stdout to accept io.Writer.
Learner must write a test using bytes.Buffer as the writer.
Learner must explain why io.Writer makes code testable.
Learner must identify untestable output code in a review.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The standard library uses io.Writer everywhere: `fmt.Fprintf(w, ...)`, `http.ResponseWriter` implements io.Writer, `json.NewEncoder(w)`, `io.Copy(dst, src)`. io.Writer is the standard abstraction for output destinations.
zero_magic.mental_model: io.Writer is like a universal output socket. Your function writes data to this socket. In production, the socket is connected to stdout or a file. In tests, it's connected to a buffer you can inspect. The function doesn't know or care where the output goes.
performance_implications: io.Writer adds a method call overhead per write -- negligible.
Buffered writers improve performance for many small writes.
bytes.Buffer as test writer is fast (in-memory).
Interface dispatch for io.Writer is cheap.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Functions that write directly to stdout are untestable -- you can't capture their output. By accepting io.Writer, the same function works for stdout, files, network connections, or buffers, and is trivially testable.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a function `greet(w io.Writer, name string)` that writes a greeting to w. Write a test using bytes.Buffer, pass two different names, and verify the output contains the expected greeting.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: CLI tools accept io.Writer for output. HTTP handlers write to ResponseWriter (which implements io.Writer). Logging libraries accept io.Writer as output targets. It's the fundamental Go output abstraction.
step_by_step_execution: Change function signature from returning string to accepting `w io.Writer`.
Use `fmt.Fprintf(w, ...)` instead of `fmt.Sprintf(...)` or `fmt.Print(...)`.
In production, call the function with `os.Stdout` or a file.
In tests, create a `bytes.Buffer`, pass it as the writer.
After the call, inspect `buf.String()` for assertions.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: io.Writer is a single-method interface: `Write(p []byte) (n int, err error)`. bytes.Buffer implements it by appending to its internal byte slice. os.Stdout implements it by writing to the OS file descriptor.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Testable design with io.Writer decouples output generation from output destination. It makes code testable, flexible, and composable following Go's small interface philosophy.
