items.title: Fakes before mocks
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Fakes before mocks in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Reaching for mocking frameworks before trying Go interfaces with simple fakes.
Creating overly complex mock setups that need maintenance.
Using mocks for types that could be real implementations.
Not understanding that fakes (simple implementations) are often better than mocks.
execution_timeline: Define an interface for the dependency: `type Store interface { Get(id string) (Value, error) }`.
Create a fake implementation for tests: `type FakeStore struct { data map[string]Value }`.
The fake has a simple in-memory implementation of the interface.
In tests, create a FakeStore and inject it into the code under test.
Test assertions verify behavior using the fake's known state.

failure_modes.scenario: Using a mocking framework to create a mock that returns different values on each call -- complex setup.
failure_modes.cause: Mocking frameworks create expectation-based mocks that are fragile.
failure_modes.fix: Use a fake (simple in-memory implementation) that's easier to set up and understand.

failure_modes.scenario: The mock setup doesn't match the actual call -- test fails with unexpected invocation.
failure_modes.cause: The mock expects specific parameters that differ from actual calls.
failure_modes.fix: Fakes don't have this problem -- they implement the interface with real logic.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain the difference between fakes, stubs, and mocks.
Learner must create a fake implementation of an interface for testing.
Learner must identify when a fake is better than a mock.
Learner must recognize mocking framework overuse in a codebase.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's standard library encourages fakes over mocks. `net/http/httptest` provides fakes (NewServer, NewRecorder). `io` package types are easily faked with `bytes.Buffer`. The Go philosophy favors simple implementations over expectation-based mocks.
zero_magic.mental_model: A fake is like a flight simulator for testing pilots. It doesn't mock individual instrument readings -- it runs a simplified but complete version of the flight software. The pilot (test) can fly patterns without taking off.
performance_implications: Fakes are faster than real implementations (no I/O, no network).
Fakes are simpler than mocking framework setups.
Fake maintenance is proportional to interface complexity.
No mock framework overhead (no expectations to verify).
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without fakes, tests use real implementations that are slow, flaky, or impossible to control. Fakes provide fast, reliable, controllable test doubles without the complexity of mocking frameworks.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must define an interface `EmailSender { Send(to, subject, body string) error }`, create a `FakeEmailSender` that stores sent emails in a slice, and write a test using the fake to verify email content.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go codebases use fakes for databases (in-memory maps), HTTP clients (returning canned responses), and email senders (capturing sent messages). Fakes are the preferred test double approach.
step_by_step_execution: Define the interface the code depends on.
Create a fake struct implementing the interface with in-memory state.
In the fake, track calls and store results for later assertion.
Inject the fake into the code under test.
After the test, inspect the fake's state to verify behavior.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: A fake is a struct that implements an interface with simplified but functional logic. It doesn't use expectation libraries -- it just records operations and provides accessors for test assertions. Fakes are idiomatic Go because they rely on interfaces, which Go satisfies implicitly.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Fakes provide simple, maintainable test doubles that are easier to understand and less brittle than mocking framework expectations. They're the Go-idiomatic approach to test isolation.

items.title: Mocking tradeoffs
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Mocking tradeoffs in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using mocks for every dependency -- including trivial ones.
Creating brittle tests that break when implementation details change.
Over-specifying mock expectations (exact call counts, exact parameters) when not needed.
Using mocks instead of real implementations for stable, fast dependencies.
execution_timeline: Identify the dependency that needs isolation (external service, database, random).
Define an interface for the dependency.
Create a fake implementation for simple cases, or use a mocking framework for complex verification.
Set up mock expectations for the specific calls you need to verify.
Inject the mock, run the test, verify expectations.

failure_modes.scenario: A mock expects a specific call order that's not guaranteed -- test fails intermittently.
failure_modes.cause: Mock's expectation-based matching is too strict.
failure_modes.fix: Use fakes instead of mocks, or loosen mock expectations (allow any order).

failure_modes.scenario: Refactoring internal implementation breaks tests, even though behavior is correct.
failure_modes.cause: Mocking implementation details instead of behavior.
failure_modes.fix: Mock at the boundary (interface), not the implementation.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify when a mock is appropriate vs when a fake suffices.
Learner must explain the tradeoffs of mock-based testing.
Learner must write a test using a mocking framework (gomock, testify/mock).
Learner must identify mock overuse in a codebase.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The standard library avoids mocking frameworks -- it prefers fakes and integration tests. External testing libraries like `gomock`, `testify/mock`, and `moq` provide mocking capabilities. Go's interface system makes any mocking approach feasible.
zero_magic.mental_model: Mocking is like hiring a actor to play the role of a dependency in a play (test). The actor follows a script (expectations) and you verify they performed correctly (verify). But the actor only pretends -- there's no real implementation behind it.
performance_implications: Mocking frameworks add test overhead for setup and verification.
Mock-based tests are slower than fake-based tests due to expectation matching.
Brittle mocks increase maintenance cost.
Mock generation tools add build-time overhead.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Mocks provide precise verification of how code interacts with dependencies -- which methods were called, with what parameters, in what order. This is valuable for coordination code where call patterns matter.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a service that calls an external API, use testify/mock to mock the API client, write tests that verify the correct API calls are made with the right parameters.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Mocks are used when testing interaction patterns: verifying that a service calls a repository in the right order, that a handler sends the correct HTTP request, or that a retry mechanism correctly re-calls on failure.
step_by_step_execution: Identify the boundary interface to mock.
Generate or write a mock implementation.
In the test, set up mock expectations (what should be called, with what args).
Inject the mock into the code under test.
After execution, verify that all expected calls were made.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Mocking frameworks generate types that implement the target interface. They track expected calls via an expectation chain. `EXPECT().Method(args).Return(values)` builds the expectation. `Ctrl.Finish()` verifies all expectations were met.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Mocks provide precise interaction testing that fakes can't. When you need to verify that specific calls were made with specific parameters, mocks are the right tool.

items.title: Debugging mindset
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Debugging mindset in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Treating debugging as guesswork instead of systematic investigation.
Making random changes to see if the bug goes away -- cargo cult debugging.
Not reproducing the bug consistently before trying to fix it.
Skipping the step of understanding the root cause before applying a fix.
execution_timeline: Reproduce the bug consistently -- identify the exact steps and inputs.
Form a hypothesis about the root cause.
Test the hypothesis (add logging, check values, trace execution).
If hypothesis is wrong, form a new one based on what was learned.
When root cause is identified, implement the fix and verify the bug is gone.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer changes code randomly hoping to fix a bug -- introduces new bugs.
failure_modes.cause: No systematic approach -- guessing instead of investigating.
failure_modes.fix: Reproduce first, then isolate the cause, then fix.

failure_modes.scenario: Bug is 'fixed' but reappears later because the root cause wasn't addressed.
failure_modes.cause: Surface-level fix instead of root cause analysis.
failure_modes.fix: Understand why the bug occurred, not just how to make it go away.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must describe the systematic debugging process.
Learner must explain why reproducing the bug is the first step.
Learner must demonstrate hypothesis-driven debugging.
Learner must identify cargo cult debugging in a scenario.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's tooling supports systematic debugging: stack traces on panic, `go vet` for suspicious constructs, `go test -v` for test failures, Delve for interactive debugging, and profiling tools for performance issues.
zero_magic.mental_model: Debugging is like being a detective at a crime scene. You gather evidence (logs, stack traces, variable values), form theories (hypotheses), test each theory, and eliminate possibilities until only the cause remains. Random guessing is not detective work.
performance_implications: Systematic debugging is faster than guesswork in the long run.
Adding logging adds overhead but provides invaluable debugging data.
Debug builds are slower but give better information.
Investing in reproducible test cases pays off for complex bugs.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without a systematic approach, debugging becomes frustrating trial and error. A structured mindset turns debugging from guesswork into methodical investigation, reducing time to fix and preventing recurring bugs.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must debug a provided buggy Go program: reproduce the bug, identify the root cause using debugging techniques, fix it, and explain the systematic process they followed.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Professional debugging follows scientific method: hypothesis, prediction, experiment, analysis. Senior engineers spend significant time debugging and use systematic approaches rather than random changes.
step_by_step_execution: Reproduce the bug consistently -- know exactly how to trigger it.
Observe the failure behavior -- what's different from expected?
Form a hypothesis about the root cause.
Test the hypothesis (check variable values, add logging, trace execution).
When confirmed, implement the fix and verify all tests pass.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go programs produce deterministic output for given inputs -- this makes reproduction reliable. Panics produce stack traces with file and line numbers. The race detector catches concurrency bugs. Delve provides step-by-step execution.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: A debugging mindset transforms frustration into methodical problem-solving. Every developer spends significant time debugging -- a systematic approach makes this productive rather than painful.

items.title: Reading stack traces
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Reading stack traces in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Ignoring the stack trace and searching for the bug by reading code randomly.
Not recognizing that the top of the stack trace is the panic location.
Getting confused by the goroutine label and assuming it's part of the call trace.
Not looking at file and line numbers in stack traces.
execution_timeline: A panic or error occurs -- Go prints a stack trace to stderr.
Read the top frame first -- this is where the panic/error occurred.
Note the file:line number -- this is where to start investigating.
Read subsequent frames to understand how execution reached that point.
Look for your own code in the trace (not just standard library frames).

failure_modes.scenario: Developer sees a nil pointer dereference but looks at the wrong line in the stack trace.
failure_modes.cause: The actual cause is a few lines before where the panic occurs.
failure_modes.fix: Look at the line indicated by the stack trace, then check preceding statements for the nil assignment.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer ignores goroutine labels and misses the goroutine that panicked.
failure_modes.cause: Stack traces for concurrent programs show multiple goroutines.
failure_modes.fix: Find the goroutine marked with the panic/recover status.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must read a panic stack trace and identify the file, line, and function where the panic occurred.
Learner must trace back from the panic line to find the root cause.
Learner must identify which frames are from their code vs standard library.
Learner must explain the goroutine dump format in stack traces.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Every Go program produces stack traces on panic. `debug.PrintStack()` dumps the current stack. `runtime.Caller` inspects the stack programmatically. Loggers use stack traces for error reporting.
zero_magic.mental_model: A stack trace is a breadcrumb trail from where the program crashed back to where it started. The top frame is the crash site. Each frame below is one step in the call chain. Read top to bottom to trace execution backwards.
performance_implications: Generating stack traces is fast -- just frame pointer traversal.
Formatting the trace has minimal overhead.
Dumping stack traces in hot loops is expensive.
Stack trace depth increases with function call depth.
zero_magic.problem_solved: When a program crashes, you need to know where and how it reached that point. Stack traces provide the exact call sequence, file names, and line numbers -- the primary tool for crash diagnosis.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must cause a nil pointer dereference in a Go program, read the stack trace, identify the file, line number, and function where the panic occurred, and explain the call path that led there.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Stack traces are the first thing developers look at when a Go program crashes. Every production error monitoring tool (Sentry, Datadog) captures and displays stack traces. They're essential for post-mortem debugging.
step_by_step_execution: When a panic occurs, look at the first line after 'panic:' -- that's the crash location.
Note the file:line number and function name.
Scroll down to see the call path -- your code is likely in the middle frames.
If the runtime frames dominate, scroll past them to find your code.
Use the file:line information to open the source and investigate.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Stack traces are generated by the runtime's stack walking code. Each frame stores the program counter and stack pointer. The runtime walks the linked list of frames, resolving program counters to function names and line numbers via the symbol table.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without stack traces, crash diagnosis would require recreating the entire execution path manually. Stack traces provide instant visibility into where the crash occurred and how execution reached that point.

items.title: Debugging panics
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Debugging panics in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Not handling panic recovery at appropriate boundaries (goroutine boundaries, API boundaries).
Recovering from a panic and continuing as if nothing happened -- the program state may be corrupted.
Calling recover() outside a deferred function -- it returns nil.
Not logging the panic details before recovering.
execution_timeline: A panic occurs due to nil pointer dereference, index out of bounds, or explicit panic() call.
The current function stops executing and begins unwinding the stack.
Deferred functions in the current function run.
If any deferred function calls recover(), the panic stops and the function returns normally.
If no defer recovers, the panic continues unwinding up the stack, potentially crashing the program.

failure_modes.scenario: A goroutine panics and crashes the entire program because the panic wasn't recovered.
failure_modes.cause: Each goroutine must have its own recover or the panic crashes the whole process.
failure_modes.fix: Add a defer/recover at the top of every goroutine entry point.

failure_modes.scenario: A handler recovers from a panic but continues to process the request with corrupted state.
failure_modes.cause: Recovering doesn't restore the program state -- only prevents the crash.
failure_modes.fix: After recover, log the error and return a clean error response.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain when recover() returns nil.
Learner must write a deferred function that recovers from a panic.
Learner must explain why recover must be in a deferred function.
Learner must identify goroutine boundaries that need panic recovery.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The standard library recovers at goroutine boundaries: `net/http` recovers from handler panics and returns 500. `database/sql` recovers from driver panics. `encoding/json` recovers from marshal panics.
zero_magic.mental_model: A panic is like a fire alarm in a building. Normal execution stops (unwinding). People evacuate (defers run). The fire marshal (recover) can declare it a false alarm, and the building returns to normal operation. Without recovery, the building burns down (program crashes).
performance_implications: Panic/recover is slower than error returns -- use errors for expected failures.
Recover adds minimal overhead when no panic occurs.
Panicking across many stack frames is expensive.
Use defer for recover, not for normal control flow.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without recover, any panic crashes the entire program, including other goroutines. Recover allows programs to handle unexpected failures gracefully at boundary points, logging the error and continuing to serve.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a function that intentionally panics, a deferred recover that catches the panic, and tests showing the function returns an error instead of crashing. Write a goroutine that recovers from panic without crashing the main program.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: HTTP servers recover from handler panics to return 500 instead of crashing. Long-running goroutines in background workers recover to continue processing. Database drivers recover from panics without crashing the application.
step_by_step_execution: Place `defer func() { if r := recover(); r != nil { /* handle */ } }()` at the start of the function.
When a panic occurs, the deferred function runs.
recover() returns the panic value (or nil if no panic).
In the recover block, log the error and return a clean error response.
Without recover, the panic propagates up and eventually crashes.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The runtime tracks panicking goroutines. When a panic occurs, the runtime walks the defer chain. For each deferred function, if it calls recover(), the panic state is cleared and the goroutine resumes normal execution. Otherwise, the unwinding continues.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Debugging panics is a daily reality for Go developers. Understanding stack traces, panic/recover mechanics, and goroutine boundaries is essential for building resilient Go programs.

Can apply the concept in a small working program.
proof.practice_task: Complete a focused exercise that applies Debugging panics.

verification.expected_output: Learner can demonstrate the concept and explain it.

items.title: Delve basics
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Delve basics in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Not installing Delve when trying to debug Go programs.
Trying to use Delve on an optimized binary -- set -gcflags='-N -l' to disable optimization.
Starting a debugging session without a clear goal.
Not knowing the basic Delve commands (break, continue, print, next, step).
execution_timeline: Install Delve: `go install github.com/go-delve/delve/cmd/dlv@latest`.
Start debugging: `dlv debug` (debug current package) or `dlv test` (debug tests).
Set breakpoints: `break main.go:10` or `break package.Function`.
Run the program: `continue`.
When a breakpoint hits, inspect variables: `print variableName`, `locals`.

failure_modes.scenario: Delve can't find source lines or shows wrong line numbers.
failure_modes.cause: Optimized compilation reorders code. Debug info may be incomplete.
failure_modes.fix: Compile with `-gcflags='-N -l'` to disable optimization for accurate debugging.

failure_modes.scenario: Breakpoint set on a function but never hits.
failure_modes.cause: The function may be inlined by the compiler, removing the breakpoint target.
failure_modes.fix: Disable inlining with `-gcflags='-l'` or set breakpoint at a line number instead.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must install Delve and start a debugging session.
Learner must set a breakpoint and inspect a variable.
Learner must step through code line by line.
Learner must use Delve to debug a failing test.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Delve is the standard Go debugger, replacing GDB for Go debugging. It understands Go's runtime, goroutines, and data structures. The VS Code Go extension and GoLand use Delve as their debugging backend.
zero_magic.mental_model: Delve is like a microscope for your running code. You can pause execution at any point (breakpoint), examine the current state (variables, goroutines, stack), and step forward one instruction at a time to observe how state changes.
performance_implications: Debugging with Delve slows execution significantly (10-100x slower).
Disabling optimization (-N -l) makes binaries larger and slower.
Breakpoints add overhead even when not hit.
Delve is not suitable for production debugging.
zero_magic.problem_solved: fmt.Println debugging is limited -- you can't inspect arbitrary variables or control execution flow. Delve provides full interactive debugging: set breakpoints, inspect values at any point, step through code, and understand complex call chains.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must create a Go program with a bug, start a Delve debugging session, set a breakpoint at the buggy line, inspect the relevant variables, step through execution, and identify the bug.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Delve is used for debugging complex logic, understanding unexpected behavior, and stepping through unfamiliar code. It's essential for debugging concurrent programs where print statements are insufficient.
step_by_step_execution: Run `dlv debug` in your package directory.
Set a breakpoint: `break main.go:20` (file:line).
Start execution: `continue`.
When breakpoint hits: `print variableName` to inspect.
Step: `next` (next line), `step` (into function), `continue` (to next breakpoint).
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Delve uses the operating system's process tracing facilities (ptrace on Linux, mach exceptions on macOS). It controls the target process, injects breakpoint instructions, and reads/writes registers and memory to inspect state.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Print-based debugging reaches its limits with complex bugs, concurrent code, and unfamiliar codebases. Delve provides the interactive debugging capabilities that professional developers need.

items.title: Breakpoints
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Breakpoints in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Setting breakpoints on every line instead of strategic locations.
Not removing debug print statements after finding the bug.
Setting breakpoints in optimized code -- may not trigger correctly.
Forgetting that breakpoints persist across debugging sessions if not removed.
execution_timeline: Identify the suspicious code area where the bug might be.
Set a breakpoint at a key line: `break file.go:42`.
Run the program: `continue`.
Execution pauses at the breakpoint -- inspect state.
Step through subsequent lines to observe behavior.

failure_modes.scenario: Breakpoint set on a blank line or comment -- Delve can't set breakpoint there.
failure_modes.cause: Breakpoints require executable lines.
failure_modes.fix: Set breakpoints on actual code lines that generate instructions.

failure_modes.scenario: Breakpoint in a function inside a loop -- hits many times, slowing execution.
failure_modes.cause: Loop breakpoints trigger on every iteration.
failure_modes.fix: Use a conditional breakpoint: `break file.go:10 if i == 5` to only stop on specific iterations.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must set a breakpoint at a specific file and line.
Learner must set a breakpoint on a function name.
Learner must use conditional breakpoints.
Learner must list, clear, and manage breakpoints.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Breakpoints are the fundamental Delve feature. `break` creates them, `breakpoints` lists them, `clear`/`clearall` removes them. Conditional breakpoints use `if` expressions.
zero_magic.mental_model: Breakpoints are like checkpoints in a video game. You place them at critical locations. When the program reaches a checkpoint, the game pauses and you can look around (inspect state). Then you resume and continue to the next checkpoint.
performance_implications: Breakpoints slow execution because the program must stop and hand control to the debugger.
Conditional breakpoints evaluate the condition on every execution -- slower.
Too many breakpoints make debugging impractical.
Breakpoints in hot loops cause thousands of stops.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without breakpoints, you can't pause code execution at a specific point. You add print statements everywhere and rebuild. Breakpoints let you surgically inspect state at exactly the right moment without modifying code.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a function with a loop, start a Delve session, set a breakpoint inside the loop, use a conditional breakpoint to stop on a specific iteration, and inspect the loop variables.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Breakpoints are the most-used Delve feature. Developers set breakpoints at the start of suspicious functions, at key decision points, and inside loops. They're the first step in any debugging session.
step_by_step_execution: Identify the file and line to pause execution.
In Delve: `break file.go:42`.
Run `continue` -- program runs until the breakpoint.
Inspect variables with `print x` or `locals`.
Continue (`continue`) or step (`next`, `step`) from the breakpoint.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Delve sets breakpoints by replacing the instruction at the target address with a software interrupt instruction (int3 on x86). When the CPU executes this instruction, the target process stops, and Delve catches the signal.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Breakpoints are the most powerful debugging tool. They let you pause execution at exactly the right point without modifying code, enabling precise inspection of program state.

items.title: Stepping through code
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Stepping through code in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Stepping into standard library functions when the bug is in application code.
Using `next` when `step` is needed -- confusing the two.
Not stepping through the code systematically.
Stepping too quickly and missing the critical state change.
execution_timeline: Pause at a breakpoint.
Use `next` to execute the current line and stop at the next line in the same function.
Use `step` to go into a function call and stop at the first line inside.
Use `stepout` to complete the current function and stop at the caller.
Continue stepping through the code path that contains the bug.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer steps into fmt.Printf internals instead of their own function.
failure_modes.cause: step goes into any function call, including standard library.
failure_modes.fix: Use `next` instead of `step` to skip into library calls.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer steps over a function call with `next` that contains the bug.
failure_modes.cause: next skips the entire function -- you miss what happens inside.
failure_modes.fix: If the bug might be inside the function, use `step`.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must demonstrate using step and next correctly.
Learner must distinguish when to use step vs next vs stepout.
Learner must step through a recursive function.
Learner must use stepout to exit a deep call chain.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Delve's step commands are: `step` (into), `next` (over), `stepout` (return). These are the core navigation commands for controlling execution flow during debugging.
zero_magic.mental_model: Stepping through code is like reading a book one word at a time. `next` reads the current sentence and moves to the next. `step` follows a footnote and comes back. `stepout` skips back to the main text from a footnote.
performance_implications: Stepping is slow -- each step executes one line and returns control to the debugger.
Stepping through loops is tedious for many iterations.
Use conditional breakpoints combined with continue for loops.
Stepping through standard library code is typically unnecessary.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without stepping, you can only observe code at breakpoints -- you miss the intermediate state changes. Stepping lets you observe every line's effect, making it possible to find exactly where state becomes incorrect.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must set a breakpoint at a function call, use step to enter the function, step through the function body line by line, observe how a variable changes, and use stepout to return to the caller.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Stepping is used to trace the exact execution path through unfamiliar code, to observe state changes in detail, and to understand complex algorithms one step at a time.
step_by_step_execution: At a breakpoint, use `next` to execute the current line and stop at the next.
Use `step` to enter a function call and stop at its first line.
Use `stepout` to complete the current function and stop at the caller.
Use `continue` to run to the next breakpoint.
Use `restart` to start the debugging session over.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: step sets a breakpoint on the next instruction (next line), step sets a breakpoint at the target function entry, stepout sets a breakpoint at the return address. Each is a temporary breakpoint that's removed after it triggers.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Stepping provides line-by-line execution control, the finest granularity for observing program behavior and finding bugs.

items.title: Inspecting variables
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Inspecting variables in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Forgetting to print pointer values and printing the address instead of the value.
Not inspecting variables before the buggy line -- the bug may be in state setup.
Only printing scalars and not inspecting struct, slice, map contents.
Not using `whatis` to understand a variable's type when confused.
execution_timeline: At a breakpoint, use `print variableName` to inspect a variable.
Use `locals` to see all local variables in the current function.
Use `whatis variableName` to see the type of a variable.
Use `print &variable` to get a pointer's address.
Use `print *pointer` to dereference a pointer.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer prints a pointer and sees the address, not the value.
failure_modes.cause: print on a pointer shows the address.
failure_modes.fix: Use `print *pointer` to dereference and see the value.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer inspects a variable after the buggy line -- the value is already corrupted.
failure_modes.cause: The bug occurred before the current line.
failure_modes.fix: Set an earlier breakpoint or step through the code.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must inspect a struct variable and its fields in Delve.
Learner must dereference a pointer to see its value.
Learner must use `locals` to see all variables.
Learner must inspect slice length, capacity, and elements.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Delve's inspect commands: `print`, `locals`, `whatis`, `args`. These are used constantly during debugging to understand the program state at any point.
zero_magic.mental_model: Inspecting variables is like taking inventory in a store. You list all items (locals), check specific items (print), understand what type of item it is (whatis), and look inside containers (dereference pointers, inspect struct fields).
performance_implications: Variable inspection has no performance impact on the running program.
Printing large data structures is slow in Delve.
Evaluating complex expressions may trigger method calls with side effects.
Use simple variable names and fields for fast inspection.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without variable inspection, you can only guess what values your variables hold at runtime. Inspection lets you see the exact state at any point, making it easy to identify where values become incorrect.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must set a breakpoint in a function that manipulates a struct, inspect the struct's fields before and after modification, dereference a pointer, and use `locals` to see the full local variable state.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Variable inspection is the most frequent debugging action. Developers check function parameters at entry, intermediate values during computation, and return values before the function returns to verify correctness.
step_by_step_execution: At a breakpoint, type `print variableName`.
For pointers, use `print *ptr` to see the pointed-to value.
For structs, use `print structVar.FieldName` for specific fields.
Use `locals` to see all local variables.
Use `whatis x` to understand a variable's type.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Delve reads the target process's memory through the OS debugging interface. It parses the DWARF debug information to know variable types and locations (register, stack, heap). It formats the value according to Go's formatting rules.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Variable inspection is essential for understanding why code behaves unexpectedly. Without it, debugging would be entirely based on print statements and guesswork.

items.title: Debugging tests
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Debugging tests in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using fmt.Println debugging when Delve would be faster.
Not knowing that `dlv test` exists for debugging tests.
Trying to debug a passing test instead of a failing one.
Running the full test suite when only one test needs debugging.
execution_timeline: Identify the failing test: `go test -v -run TestName`.
Start Delve with `dlv test -- -test.run TestName`.
Set breakpoints in the test function or the code it calls.
Run `continue` to execute until a breakpoint.
Inspect variables and step through the failing test case.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer tries to debug a test with print statements and rebuilds repeatedly.
failure_modes.cause: Frequent rebuilds slow down debugging significantly.
failure_modes.fix: Use `dlv test` for interactive debugging -- no rebuilds needed.

failure_modes.scenario: Delve test session starts but no breakpoints are hit.
failure_modes.cause: The test function name might not match the -test.run pattern.
failure_modes.fix: Verify the test name with `go test -list`.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must start a Delve debugging session for a specific test.
Learner must set a breakpoint inside a test function.
Learner must step through test execution.
Learner must use Delve to find the root cause of a test failure.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: `dlv test` is the standard way to debug Go tests. It combines the Go test runner with Delve's debugging capabilities, allowing breakpoints, stepping, and variable inspection inside test code.
zero_magic.mental_model: Debugging tests with Delve is like having a time machine for your test execution. You can pause at any test step, look at the state, and step forward to see how things evolve -- without modifying your test code.
performance_implications: Debugging tests with Delve is slower than running them directly.
Use `-gcflags='-N -l'` to ensure accurate line number mapping.
Disable parallel test execution during debugging.
Only debug the specific failing test.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without Delve, debugging failing tests requires print statements and repeated rebuilds. Delve lets you interactively debug tests, inspecting state at any point and tracing execution without code modifications.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must have a failing test, start `dlv test -- -test.run FailingTest`, set a breakpoint at the assertion line, inspect the actual and expected values, step through the test to find why they differ.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Developers routinely use `dlv test` to debug test failures. It's especially useful for table-driven tests where multiple cases run -- set breakpoints inside the loop to inspect specific cases.
step_by_step_execution: Open the test file and note the test function name.
Run `dlv test -- -test.run TestName`.
Set breakpoints inside the test or the code it tests.
Type `continue` to start test execution.
When breakpoint hits, inspect variables and step through code.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: `dlv test` compiles the test binary with debug information, then executes it under Delve's control. The `--` separates Delve arguments from test arguments. `-test.run` is passed to the Go test runner to select a specific test.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Debugging tests is the most common debugging scenario. Delve's test debugging support makes finding test failures faster and more precise than print-based debugging.

items.title: Refactoring safely
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Refactoring safely in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Refactoring without tests first -- no safety net.
Changing too much at once instead of small, verifiable steps.
Refactoring and adding features simultaneously.
Not running the full test suite after each refactoring step.
execution_timeline: Identify the code to refactor and what improvement you want.
Ensure comprehensive test coverage of the code area.
Make one small refactoring change.
Run the test suite to verify nothing broke.
Commit the change. Repeat until the refactoring is complete.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer refactors a large function without tests -- introduces bugs.
failure_modes.cause: No regression detection without tests.
failure_modes.fix: Write tests before refactoring, covering all existing behavior.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer renames a function but forgets to update one caller -- compilation fails.
failure_modes.cause: Manual rename is error-prone.
failure_modes.fix: Use IDE refactoring tools or grep to find all call sites.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain why tests are essential before refactoring.
Learner must identify the steps in a safe refactoring workflow.
Learner must demonstrate extracting a function from a longer function.
Learner must refactor without changing behavior (verified by tests).
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's tooling supports refactoring: `gofmt` standardizes formatting, `go vet` catches suspicious patterns, `go test` verifies behavior, `gorename` renames identifiers, and `guru` provides code analysis.
zero_magic.mental_model: Refactoring is like renovating a house. You don't tear down all walls at once. You renovate one room, test the structure, then move to the next room. Tests are your building inspector -- they tell you if the renovation weakened the structure.
performance_implications: Refactoring without tests is faster initially but causes bugs that are expensive to fix later.
Good test coverage makes refactoring safe and fast.
Small refactoring steps prevent long debugging sessions.
IDE tools make Go refactoring efficient.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Code degrades over time without refactoring. But refactoring is risky -- changes can introduce bugs. Safe refactoring requires a systematic approach with test coverage as the safety net.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must take a long function with duplicated code, extract the duplicate code into a helper function, rename variables for clarity, and verify all tests still pass after each change.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production codebases are constantly refactored: renaming for clarity, extracting functions for reuse, simplifying complex conditionals, and restructuring packages. Safe refactoring is an essential engineering skill.
step_by_step_execution: Write or verify test coverage for the code to refactor.
Make one small, atomic change (rename, extract, inline, simplify).
Run `go test ./...` to verify nothing broke.
Commit the change with a clear message.
Repeat steps 2-4 until the refactoring is complete.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's fast compilation enables rapid refactoring feedback. `go test` compiles and tests in seconds. `gofmt` ensures consistent formatting. `go vet` detects type and logic issues. `go doc` helps understand existing interfaces.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Refactoring safely is essential for maintaining code quality over time. Without safe refactoring practices, code degrades, technical debt accumulates, and the codebase becomes harder to change.

items.title: Fuzz testing
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Fuzz testing in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Writing fuzz tests for functions that don't benefit from fuzzing (simple deterministic logic).
Not providing good seed corpus entries -- the fuzzer starts from scratch.
Running fuzz tests without a time limit -- they run indefinitely.
Not understanding that fuzzing finds different bugs than unit tests.
execution_timeline: Write a fuzz target function: `func FuzzXxx(f *testing.F)`.
Add seed corpus entries with `f.Add(input)`.
Run with `go test -fuzz=FuzzXxx`.
The fuzzer generates random inputs and calls the target.
If a crash is found, the input is saved to testdata/fuzz/.

failure_modes.scenario: Fuzz test finds a crash but the developer doesn't save the failing input.
failure_modes.cause: Without saving the input, the bug can't be reproduced.
failure_modes.fix: Go's fuzzer automatically saves crashing inputs to testdata/fuzz/FuzzName/.

failure_modes.scenario: Fuzz test runs slowly because each iteration does I/O.
failure_modes.cause: Fuzz tests should be fast and CPU-bound.
failure_modes.fix: Design fuzz targets to test decoding/parsing logic, not I/O operations.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must write a fuzz target for a string parsing function.
Learner must run a fuzz test and find a crash.
Learner must explain what seed corpus entries are for.
Learner must understand when fuzzing is appropriate.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go 1.18 introduced built-in fuzzing. The standard library uses fuzzing in `net/http`, `encoding/json`, `archive/tar`, `compress/gzip`, and other parsing packages. Fuzzing is a first-class testing feature.
zero_magic.mental_model: Fuzzing is like having a million monkeys typing random inputs to your program. The fuzzer is smarter than monkeys -- it uses coverage guidance to find inputs that explore new code paths. When a crash occurs, the input is saved for reproduction.
performance_implications: Fuzzing is CPU-intensive -- it generates and tests millions of inputs.
Fuzz targets should be fast (milliseconds per iteration).
Coverage-guided fuzzing adds overhead for coverage tracking.
Run fuzzing in CI with a time limit (e.g., 10 minutes).
zero_magic.problem_solved: Unit tests only cover what the developer thinks to test. Fuzzing automatically generates unexpected inputs, finding edge cases that human testers miss. It's complementary to unit tests.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a function that parses a string (e.g., a CSV line), write a fuzz target for it, run the fuzzer until it finds a crash, examine the crashing input, and fix the bug.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Fuzzing is used for security-critical code: parsers, decoders, network protocol implementations. The Go standard library runs fuzz tests in CI. Fuzzing found hundreds of CVEs in various projects.
step_by_step_execution: Identify a function that takes input from the outside (parsers, decoders).
Write `func FuzzXxx(f *testing.F) { f.Fuzz(func(t *testing.T, input string) { /* test function */ }) }`.
Add seed corpus: `f.Add("valid,input")`.
Run: `go test -fuzz=FuzzXxx -fuzztime=10s`.
When a crash is found, examine the failing input in testdata/fuzz/.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's fuzzer uses coverage-guided mutation: it tracks which code paths each input covers, then mutates inputs to explore uncovered paths. Crashing inputs are minimized to the smallest failing input before saving.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Fuzz testing finds edge cases and bugs that unit tests miss. It's especially valuable for security, parsing, and data validation code where unexpected inputs are common.

items.title: Race detector preview
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Race detector preview in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Ignoring the race detector's warnings -- assuming they're false positives.
Not testing with -race during development.
Adding mutexes everywhere without understanding the actual race condition.
Only running the race detector in CI and not during development.
execution_timeline: Enable the race detector: `go test -race ./...`.
Run tests or the program with race detection enabled.
If a race is detected, the runtime prints a detailed report: goroutine 1 writes, goroutine 2 reads, with stack traces.
Analyze the report to understand which variables are accessed unsafely.
Fix the race (add mutex, use channels, use atomic operations, or restructure).

failure_modes.scenario: Developer sees a race report but doesn't understand which access is the problem.
failure_modes.cause: Race reports show conflicting accesses but don't say which is wrong.
failure_modes.fix: Both accesses are the problem -- concurrent access without synchronization is always undefined behavior.

failure_modes.scenario: Developer adds a mutex but the race persistss.
failure_modes.cause: The mutex might not be held on all code paths accessing the variable.
failure_modes.fix: Use the race detector to verify the fix -- it should show no races.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain what the race detector detects.
Learner must run tests with the race detector enabled.
Learner must read a race detector report and identify conflicting accesses.
Learner must fix a simple race condition using a mutex.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The race detector is built into the Go toolchain. It's enabled with `-race` flag on `go test`, `go build`, `go run`. The Go team recommends running tests with -race in development and CI.
zero_magic.mental_model: The race detector is like a security camera watching your program for simultaneous, unsynchronized access to the same memory. When two goroutines access the same variable without synchronization, the camera captures the moment and shows you the video (stack traces).
performance_implications: Running with -race makes programs 5-10x slower and uses more memory.
Race detection adds overhead to every memory access.
Only use -race during development and testing, not in production.
The overhead is worth the cost for finding concurrency bugs.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Data races cause bugs that are intermittent, hard to reproduce, and devastating in production. The race detector automatically finds these bugs by instrumenting all memory accesses and detecting simultaneous unsynchronized access.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a program with a data race (concurrent map write), run it with `go run -race`, see the race report, fix the race with a mutex, and verify `go run -race` reports no races.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: The race detector is used in development and CI for every concurrent Go program. It's particularly important for services, workers, and any program using goroutines with shared memory.
step_by_step_execution: Enable race detection: `go test -race ./...` or `go run -race main.go`.
Run the program normally -- if a race exists, it will be reported.
Read the race report: W1 (goroutine writing), R2 (goroutine reading), both with stack traces.
Add synchronization (mutex, channel, atomic) between the conflicting accesses.
Re-run with -race to verify the fix.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The race detector (ThreadSanitizer) instruments every memory access in the compiled program. It maintains a shadow memory tracking which goroutine last accessed each memory location. When two goroutines access the same location without synchronization, it reports the conflict.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Data races are the most common concurrent programming error. They cause intermittent, hard-to-debug crashes and corruption. The race detector makes them visible and fixable.

items.title: CLI args
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply CLI args in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Assuming access command-line arguments passed to the program does something it does not — read the package docs instead of guessing behavior.
Hardcoding CLI args values instead of parameterizing them — this makes tests impossible and changes painful.
Copying CLI args code from StackOverflow without understanding the edge cases in your specific context.
execution_timeline: Read the os.Args documentation to understand the expected inputs and outputs.
Write a minimal CLI args program that demonstrates the core contract: create input, invoke, inspect output.
Add edge-case handling: empty input, nil values, large payloads, invalid data.
Wrap the CLI args logic in a test that verifies both success and failure paths.
Refactor into a reusable helper if the same pattern appears in multiple call sites.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer uses CLI args without checking the error return value, assuming the operation always succeeds.
failure_modes.cause: In Go, os.Args signals failure through an error return value, not a panic or exception. Ignoring the error means the program continues with an undefined state.
failure_modes.fix: Always check the error from os.Args and handle it explicitly — return it to the caller, log it and retry, or convert it to a sentinel error.

failure_modes.scenario: A team uses CLI args in a hot loop without understanding the allocation pattern, causing GC pressure.
failure_modes.cause: The os.Args call allocates memory on every invocation, and the loop runs thousands of times without reusing buffers.
failure_modes.fix: Pre-allocate buffers outside the loop, use sync.Pool for reusable objects, or restructure to process data in batches.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict what happens when access command-line arguments passed to the program receives a nil input instead of a valid value.
Learner must explain why two calls to os.Args with the same input return different results (stateful or time-based behavior).
Learner must find and fix a bug where CLI args is used without proper error handling in a real code snippet.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's standard library provides os.Args as the idiomatic way to access command-line arguments passed to the program. The CLI args pattern appears throughout the Go ecosystem — in the standard library, popular frameworks, and production codebases — because it aligns with Go's philosophy of explicit, composable abstractions.
zero_magic.mental_model: The pipeline may produce errors, partial results, or side effects. Each stage is independent and testable. The contract is: given valid input, produce valid output or an explicit error.
performance_implications: The performance cost of CLI args depends on allocation patterns — each os.Args call may allocate memory for the result.
Avoid premature optimization: write clear CLI args code first, profile to find bottlenecks, then optimize specific allocations.
Benchmark CLI args with realistic data sizes to understand the performance characteristics before optimizing.
zero_magic.problem_solved: This lesson explains the problem CLI args solves: read program arguments from the command line. Without CLI args, developers would need to implement access command-line arguments passed to the program manually, leading to inconsistent behavior, duplicated effort, and subtle bugs.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a CLI args program that handles success and error cases, test it with edge cases (empty, nil, invalid input), and explain the tradeoffs of different CLI args approaches.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Professional Go engineers use CLI args daily in production services, CLI tools, API servers, and data pipelines. Understanding CLI args is essential for writing idiomatic, maintainable Go code that teams can confidently deploy and extend.
step_by_step_execution: Import the os.Args package and identify the function signature: what inputs does it take, what does it return?
Call the function with known valid input and verify the output is correct.
Call the function with edge-case input (empty, zero, nil, large) and observe the behavior.
Check the error return value on every call and handle failures explicitly.
Write a table-driven test that covers the success case, each error case, and each edge case.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Under the hood, os.Args interacts with the Go runtime and operating system. Understanding this helps developers diagnose performance issues, debug unexpected errors, and write correct concurrent code. The key insight is that access command-line arguments passed to the program is not magic — it follows well-defined Go language semantics and OS conventions.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: CLI args exists because every Go program needs a standard, well-understood way to access command-line arguments passed to the program. Without it, every team would build their own ad-hoc solution, leading to inconsistent behavior, more bugs, and harder onboarding. Go's approach is to provide one clear, well-tested mechanism in the standard library.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer uses JSON marshal without checking the error return value, assuming the operation always succeeds.

beginner_mistakes: Assuming design consistent error response structures does something it does not — read the package docs instead of guessing behavior.
Hardcoding API error shape values instead of parameterizing them — this makes tests impossible and changes painful.
Copying API error shape code from StackOverflow without understanding the edge cases in your specific context.
execution_timeline: Read the error response struct pattern documentation to understand the expected inputs and outputs.
Write a minimal API error shape program that demonstrates the core contract: create input, invoke, inspect output.
Add edge-case handling: empty input, nil values, large payloads, invalid data.
Wrap the API error shape logic in a test that verifies both success and failure paths.
Refactor into a reusable helper if the same pattern appears in multiple call sites.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer uses API error shape without checking the error return value, assuming the operation always succeeds.
failure_modes.cause: In Go, error response struct pattern signals failure through an error return value, not a panic or exception. Ignoring the error means the program continues with an undefined state.
failure_modes.fix: Always check the error from error response struct pattern and handle it explicitly — return it to the caller, log it and retry, or convert it to a sentinel error.

failure_modes.scenario: A team uses API error shape in a hot loop without understanding the allocation pattern, causing GC pressure.
failure_modes.cause: The error response struct pattern call allocates memory on every invocation, and the loop runs thousands of times without reusing buffers.
failure_modes.fix: Pre-allocate buffers outside the loop, use sync.Pool for reusable objects, or restructure to process data in batches.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict what happens when design consistent error response structures receives a nil input instead of a valid value.
Learner must explain why two calls to error response struct pattern with the same input return different results (stateful or time-based behavior).
Learner must find and fix a bug where API error shape is used without proper error handling in a real code snippet.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's standard library provides error response struct pattern as the idiomatic way to design consistent error response structures. The API error shape pattern appears throughout the Go ecosystem — in the standard library, popular frameworks, and production codebases — because it aligns with Go's philosophy of explicit, composable abstractions.
zero_magic.mental_model: API errors must carry a code, a message, and optionally details in a consistent structure. A uniform error shape lets clients parse failures programmatically instead of scraping response text.
performance_implications: The performance cost of API error shape depends on allocation patterns — each error response struct pattern call may allocate memory for the result.
Avoid premature optimization: write clear API error shape code first, profile to find bottlenecks, then optimize specific allocations.
Benchmark API error shape with realistic data sizes to understand the performance characteristics before optimizing.
zero_magic.problem_solved: This lesson explains the problem API error shape solves: design a consistent JSON error response format for your API. Without API error shape, developers would need to implement design consistent error response structures manually, leading to inconsistent behavior, duplicated effort, and subtle bugs.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a API error shape program that handles success and error cases, test it with edge cases (empty, nil, invalid input), and explain the tradeoffs of different API error shape approaches.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Professional Go engineers use API error shape daily in production services, CLI tools, API servers, and data pipelines. Understanding API error shape is essential for writing idiomatic, maintainable Go code that teams can confidently deploy and extend.
step_by_step_execution: Import the error response struct pattern package and identify the function signature: what inputs does it take, what does it return?
Call the function with known valid input and verify the output is correct.
Call the function with edge-case input (empty, zero, nil, large) and observe the behavior.
Check the error return value on every call and handle failures explicitly.
Write a table-driven test that covers the success case, each error case, and each edge case.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Under the hood, error response struct pattern interacts with the Go runtime and operating system. Understanding this helps developers diagnose performance issues, debug unexpected errors, and write correct concurrent code. The key insight is that design consistent error response structures is not magic — it follows well-defined Go language semantics and OS conventions.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: API error shape exists because every Go program needs a standard, well-understood way to design consistent error response structures. Without it, every team would build their own ad-hoc solution, leading to inconsistent behavior, more bugs, and harder onboarding. Go's approach is to provide one clear, well-tested mechanism in the standard library.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer uses OpenAPI basics without checking the error return value, assuming the operation always succeeds.
failure_modes.cause: In Go, OpenAPI / Swagger signals failure through an error return value, not a panic or exception. Ignoring the error means the program continues with an undefined state.
failure_modes.fix: Always check the error from OpenAPI / Swagger and handle it explicitly — return it to the caller, log it and retry, or convert it to a sentinel error.

failure_modes.scenario: A team uses OpenAPI basics in a hot loop without understanding the allocation pattern, causing GC pressure.
failure_modes.cause: The OpenAPI / Swagger call allocates memory on every invocation, and the loop runs thousands of times without reusing buffers.
failure_modes.fix: Pre-allocate buffers outside the loop, use sync.Pool for reusable objects, or restructure to process data in batches.

items.title: HTTP Client
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply gRPC and Protocol Buffers in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Defining a new proto message type for every RPC call instead of reusing common message types across the service boundary.
Treating gRPC as a drop-in replacement for REST without designing for streaming, backpressure, or connection reuse.
Forgetting to set client-side deadlines and server-side timeouts — a stuck gRPC stream can hold resources indefinitely.
execution_timeline: The developer defines service and message types in a .proto file using Protocol Buffers IDL.
The protoc compiler generates Go client and server stubs from the .proto definition.
The server registers the generated service implementation and starts a gRPC listener.
The client dials the server address, creates a stub, and calls RPC methods as if they were local functions.
Under the hood, the gRPC layer serializes messages with protobuf, multiplexes streams over HTTP/2, and manages connection lifecycle.

failure_modes.scenario: A production gRPC endpoint suddenly spikes latency under moderate load.
failure_modes.cause: No client-side deadline was set, so the client retries indefinitely, compounding connection pressure on the server.
failure_modes.fix: Always set a context.WithTimeout on the client call and configure server-side keepalive enforcement.

failure_modes.scenario: After adding a new field to a proto message, old servers crash when receiving messages from new clients.
failure_modes.cause: The new field was added without considering wire-format backward compatibility — existing servers reject unknown fields.
failure_modes.fix: Follow protobuf backward-compatibility rules: never remove or renumber fields, use reserved for deleted fields, and deploy servers before clients.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict the wire format change when a proto field is renamed but its tag number stays the same.
Learner must explain why turning off HTTP/2 makes gRPC unusable and why upgrading HTTP/1.1 connections to h2c works.
Learner must identify why two proto files with the same package name but different directory paths cause compilation conflicts.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's net/http provides http.Get, http.Post, and http.Client for outgoing requests. Production code creates a custom http.Client with a configured Timeout and Transport. The Transport handles connection pooling, TLS, and proxy settings. Response.Body must always be closed.
zero_magic.mental_model: An HTTP client sends requests to external services and processes their responses. Go's http.Client manages connection pooling, timeouts, and transport configuration. Always close response bodies, configure timeouts, and reuse a single client across requests.
performance_implications: Protobuf binary encoding is 3-10x faster and produces smaller payloads than JSON — critical for high-throughput inter-service communication.
HTTP/2 multiplexing allows many concurrent RPCs over a single TCP connection without head-of-line blocking.
Server streaming avoids the overhead of creating a new connection or request per message when sending real-time event streams.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Making HTTP requests to external services is every networked application's basic need. Without understanding Go's http.Client, developers use the zero-value client (no timeout), fail to close response bodies (leaking goroutines), and miss connection pooling defaults.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must define a .proto service with at least one unary RPC and one server-streaming RPC, generate Go client and server code, implement both sides, and demonstrate the streaming response working end-to-end.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: gRPC is the standard for inter-service communication in cloud-native architectures. Companies like Uber, Netflix, and Docker use gRPC for service-to-service RPC, replacing REST in high-throughput paths where contract enforcement and performance matter.
step_by_step_execution: Install protoc and the Go protobuf plugins (protoc-gen-go, protoc-gen-go-grpc).
Write a .proto file defining the service, RPC methods, and message types.
Run protoc to generate Go client and server stubs.
Implement the server: register the generated service on a gRPC server.
Implement the client: dial the server, create a stub, and invoke RPC methods.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: gRPC uses HTTP/2 as its transport. Each RPC call maps to an HTTP/2 stream, allowing multiple concurrent calls over a single TCP connection. Protocol Buffers encode messages as binary using tag-length-value encoding, where each field is identified by its unique tag number rather than its name — this is why renaming a field is safe but renumbering breaks compatibility.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Every Go service communicates with other services over HTTP. The http.Client has subtle defaults — no timeout, shared transport, body-drain requirements — that silently cause production outages.

items.title: DELETE operations
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply DELETE operations using sqlc-generated code in Go.

beginner_mistakes: Running DELETE without a WHERE clause in an automated migration or script — this truncates the entire table without the ability to roll back individual rows.
Cascading deletes that remove more data than the caller expected because foreign-key CASCADE propagates to related tables silently.
Hard-deleting rows that are referenced by foreign keys in other tables — this causes constraint violations or orphaned records.
execution_timeline: The application receives a DELETE request with the resource identifier (ID).
The handler authorizes that the caller owns or can delete the target resource — check ownership or roles before proceeding.
The repository executes a parameterized DELETE statement with the resource ID in the WHERE clause.
The database acquires a row-level lock, removes the row, and cascades the deletion to dependent rows if configured.
The application returns a 204 No Content or 200 OK with a deletion confirmation.

failure_modes.scenario: A DELETE on a parent row fails with a foreign-key constraint violation because child rows still reference it.
failure_modes.cause: The schema has foreign-key constraints but no ON DELETE CASCADE, so deleting the parent violates referential integrity.
failure_modes.fix: Decide on a deletion strategy: CASCADE (delete children), SET NULL (orphan children), or require the caller to delete children first.

failure_modes.scenario: An API client accidentally deletes a critical production resource — there is no recovery path.
failure_modes.cause: The DELETE endpoint has no authorization check, confirmation step, or soft-delete mechanism.
failure_modes.fix: Implement soft-delete (is_deleted flag or deleted_at timestamp) for critical resources and add role-based access control to destructive operations.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict the database state after a DELETE with a WHERE clause that matches zero rows — the statement succeeds but affects zero rows.
Learner must explain why a hard DELETE returns 204 No Content while a soft DELETE returns 200 OK with the updated record.
Learner must identify why deleting from a table with a foreign-key constraint can succeed even when child rows exist (ON DELETE SET NULL or CASCADE).
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: sqlc generates DELETE functions that accept the deletion criteria as parameters and return the number of affected rows. The generated code uses parameterized queries to prevent SQL injection and handles RETURNING clauses for PostgreSQL.
zero_magic.mental_model: DELETE is a destructive operation that removes rows permanently (hard delete) or marks them as inactive (soft delete). The WHERE clause is the safety mechanism — without it, every row in the table is removed. Soft-delete adds a recovery window by marking the row instead of removing it.
performance_implications: Hard DELETE frees physical disk space (post-VACUUM) but generates WAL entries proportional to the size of the deleted rows.
Soft DELETE (setting a flag) is faster — it updates a single column instead of removing the row — but the table grows over time as old rows accumulate.
Bulk DELETEs in a single transaction acquire row locks on all matching rows — large bulk deletes should be batched to avoid exhausting lock tables.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Removing data from a database requires careful handling of referential integrity, authorization, and recovery. A hard DELETE permanently removes rows, while soft-delete preserves data for audit and recovery at the cost of table growth. Go programs need a safe interface for both strategies.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement a soft-delete endpoint that sets a deleted_at timestamp instead of removing the row, add a recovery endpoint that clears the deleted_at flag, and demonstrate that queries filter out soft-deleted rows by default.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Most production systems use soft-delete for user-facing resources (accounts, documents, orders) to provide recovery and audit trails. Hard DELETE is reserved for cleaning up temporary data, test fixtures, or PII after the legal retention period expires.
step_by_step_execution: Define the DELETE query in a .sql file with sqlc annotations.
Run sqlc generate to produce the Go DELETE function.
In the HTTP handler, authorize the DELETE request — verify ownership or role.
Execute the generated DELETE function and check the number of affected rows — zero means the resource did not exist.
Return 204 No Content on success, 404 Not Found if no rows were affected.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: PostgreSQL MVCC handles DELETE by marking the row as dead rather than immediately reclaiming the space. Dead rows are later removed by VACUUM. Each DELETE writes a WAL entry recording the deletion. ON DELETE CASCADE is handled by the database automatically by deleting child rows in the same transaction.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Data has a lifecycle: it is created (INSERT), read (SELECT), modified (UPDATE), and eventually removed (DELETE). Without a principled DELETE mechanism, databases accumulate stale, untrusted data. Soft-delete provides a safety net — recovery is possible as long as the row has not been hard-deleted and VACUUM has not reclaimed the space.

items.title: UPDATE operations
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply UPDATE operations using sqlc-generated code in Go.

beginner_mistakes: Performing a SELECT before every UPDATE to fetch the current row, then sending the unchanged fields back — this doubles the database round trip and is the most common ORM-style anti-pattern.
Writing UPDATE statements that set every column regardless of what actually changed — this causes unnecessary write locks and invalidates cache entries for unaffected columns.
Forgetting to add a WHERE clause to an UPDATE — without it every row in the table is overwritten.
execution_timeline: The application receives a partial or full update request from the client (HTTP PATCH or PUT).
The handler validates the input and authorizes that the caller owns or can modify the target resource.
The repository layer constructs an UPDATE statement with only the columns that changed, using parameterized placeholders.
The database acquires a row-level lock, applies the update, and returns the number of affected rows.
The application invalidates any cached representation of the updated resource and returns the updated state to the caller.

failure_modes.scenario: Two concurrent requests update different columns on the same row — the second write silently overwrites the first.
failure_modes.cause: No optimistic locking or version check. Each UPDATE overwrites the entire row without verifying that the underlying data has not changed since it was read.
failure_modes.fix: Add a version_number or updated_at column and include it in the WHERE clause: UPDATE SET x = $1 WHERE id = $2 AND version = $3.

failure_modes.scenario: A PATCH endpoint returns the full updated row, but an intermediate middleware mutates the response body, causing the client to see stale data.
failure_modes.cause: The middleware reads and buffers the response body without accounting for the repository returning a fresh object.
failure_modes.fix: Return the object after the UPDATE completes, log the before-and-after diff, and ensure no middleware caches the pre-update response.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must predict what happens when an UPDATE statement has no WHERE clause and the table has one million rows.
Learner must explain why UPDATE ... RETURNING * is more efficient than a separate SELECT after UPDATE in PostgreSQL.
Learner must identify the bug in a Go handler that calls UPDATE then immediately reads from a read replica that has replication lag.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: sqlc generates UPDATE functions that accept Go struct pointers and return the number of affected rows. The generated code uses parameterized queries and supports RETURNING clauses for databases like PostgreSQL that support them.
zero_magic.mental_model: An UPDATE is a targeted mutation that should affect only the rows explicitly identified by the WHERE clause. Unlike a full replacement (PUT), a partial update (PATCH) changes only the fields the client sends while preserving all other column values.
performance_implications: Each UPDATE acquires a row-level lock — long-running transactions that hold UPDATE locks block concurrent writes and reduce throughput.
Partial updates (PATCH) reduce write amplification and lock contention compared to full-row replacement (PUT).
Adding a RETURNING clause eliminates the need for a separate SELECT to fetch the updated row.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Modifying existing records in a database requires atomic, isolated writes that do not corrupt concurrent reads or other writes. Go programs need a safe, idiomatic way to perform targeted mutations — UPDATE provides the SQL mechanism, and sqlc generates the type-safe Go bindings that prevent SQL injection and misspelled column names.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement an HTTP PATCH handler that updates a subset of resource fields using sqlc-generated UPDATE code, includes optimistic locking with a version field, and returns the updated row.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: UPDATE is the second-most common SQL operation after SELECT in production systems. Every API that modifies resources — user profiles, order status, document content — relies on UPDATE with appropriate WHERE clauses and concurrency control.
step_by_step_execution: Define the UPDATE query in a .sql file using sqlc annotations.
Run sqlc generate to produce the Go UPDATE function.
In the HTTP handler, parse the request body into an update struct — only set fields that the client provided.
Call the generated UPDATE function, passing the ID and the partial update struct.
Return the updated resource to the caller (via RETURNING or a subsequent SELECT).
zero_magic.under_the_hood: PostgreSQL MVCC does not overwrite rows in place. An UPDATE creates a new version of the row and marks the old version as dead, later reclaimed by VACUUM. This means UPDATE generates write-ahead log (WAL) entries proportional to the size of the updated columns.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Production data changes over time — user profiles are edited, order statuses advance, document content is revised. Without a safe, concurrency-controlled UPDATE mechanism, every write risks corrupting other transactions or losing data due to lost-update anomalies.
