items.title: Threat modeling
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Threat modeling in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Drawing threat models after an incident instead of during design — post-hoc models miss the assumptions that led to the breach.
Modeling only external threats and ignoring insider risk — most breaches involve compromised credentials.
Stopping at diagram creation without tracing trust boundaries, data flows, and attack surfaces through the codebase.
execution_timeline: Identify the system components and draw the trust boundary around your service.
Map data flows: every request path from client through middleware, handler, service, and database.
Identify assets: secrets, user data, session tokens, API keys.
List threats per asset using STRIDE: Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Info Disclosure, DoS, Elevation.
Document mitigations: which threats are accepted, mitigated, or transferred.

failure_modes.scenario: A team threat-models after deployment and realizes the session management exposes user tokens in logs.
failure_modes.cause: Threat modeling was treated as a documentation exercise, not a discovery process.
failure_modes.fix: Threat-model during design, before writing code. Each threat should produce a test case.

failure_modes.scenario: A threat model identifies 50 threats but the team mitigates none because 'it is just an internal service.'
failure_modes.cause: Risk appetite was not defined before modeling.
failure_modes.fix: Classify each threat by impact and likelihood. Mitigate high+likely threats immediately.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must draw a threat model for a Go HTTP service that accepts user uploads and identify three threats.
Learner must trace a data flow from HTTP request to PostgreSQL and identify where trust boundaries exist.
Learner must explain why threat modeling before implementation catches more vulnerabilities than pen testing after.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's net/http stack is a natural place to apply threat modeling: each middleware layer (TLS, auth, rate limiting, validation) corresponds to a trust boundary. Go's explicit error handling forces developers to consider failure modes at every layer.
zero_magic.mental_model: Threat modeling is an architectural review: at every trust boundary, ask 'what could go wrong?' Each answer becomes a security requirement.
performance_implications: Threat modeling during design costs 1-2 hours but prevents vulnerabilities that would take days to fix in production.
Automated threat modeling tools reduce the time to about 30 minutes per service.
Post-breach threat modeling is 10x more expensive.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without threat modeling, security is reactive: you fix vulnerabilities after they are found by pen tests or attackers. Threat modeling makes security proactive by identifying risks before code is written.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must threat-model a Go API gateway: draw the trust boundary, identify at least 4 threats using STRIDE, and write acceptance criteria that would prevent each threat.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go services at Google, Stripe, and Docker use threat modeling as a standard design-phase activity. Security review gates in CI/CD require an up-to-date threat model.
step_by_step_execution: Identify the system scope and trust boundaries (network, auth, data layers).
Map data flows: ingress -> TLS -> auth -> middleware -> handler -> service -> DB.
Apply STRIDE per data flow element. Document each threat and its mitigation.
Write acceptance criteria that become test cases.
Review the threat model when the system architecture changes.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Threat modeling in Go follows STRIDE/LINDDUN like any language but benefits from Go's explicit error handling. Go's io.Reader/Writer interfaces make data flow tracing straightforward.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Security vulnerabilities in production cost 100x more than the same vulnerabilities caught during design. Threat modeling shifts security left.

items.title: Trust boundaries
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Trust boundaries in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Drawing trust boundaries around deployment boundaries instead of data flow boundaries.
Assuming the network perimeter is the only trust boundary — in modern architectures, boundaries exist between services and even between goroutines.
Placing the trust boundary inside the request handler instead of at the middleware layer.
execution_timeline: Identify all data entry points: HTTP handlers, queue consumers, gRPC streams, cron jobs.
For each entry point, determine the authentication level: anonymous, authenticated, admin.
Draw the trust boundary at every transition from a lower trust level to a higher one.
Implement the boundary as Go middleware that validates trust before passing to the handler.
Test the boundary: verify lower-trust requests cannot reach higher-trust components.

failure_modes.scenario: A Go service exposes a health check that leaks database connection strings because it runs inside the trust boundary.
failure_modes.cause: The health check is in the same handler chain as authenticated endpoints.
failure_modes.fix: Register health check on a separate mux that runs before authentication middleware.

failure_modes.scenario: A microservice trusts headers from the API gateway (X-User-Id) without verifying them.
failure_modes.cause: No cryptographic proof of trust between services.
failure_modes.fix: Use signed tokens (JWT with verification) or mTLS for inter-service communication.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify all trust boundaries in a Go monorepo with three services and an API gateway.
Learner must write middleware that enforces a trust boundary: external requests must pass auth before reaching the handler.
Learner must explain why a database connection is not a trust boundary but the SQL query that reads user data is one.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's net/http middleware pattern is ideal for trust boundaries. Each middleware is a gate that checks trust before passing the request deeper. http.Handler composes these gates naturally.
zero_magic.mental_model: A trust boundary is any point where data crosses from less trusted to more trusted. The network edge is one boundary; every RPC call and database query is another. Each is a gate that validates trust before allowing passage.
performance_implications: Moving authentication from per-handler to middleware reduces duplicated trust checks by 90%.
Each trust boundary adds about 100us to request latency.
Too many boundaries inside a service adds latency without proportional benefit.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without explicit trust boundaries, a service cannot distinguish legitimate requests from compromised internal ones. Every handler must trust everything before it.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must write a Go HTTP service with three trust zones: external, authenticated, and admin. Each zone must be enforced by middleware.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go services use trust boundaries to isolate public API vs admin API, user data vs system data, and staging vs production.
step_by_step_execution: Identify privilege levels: anonymous, user, admin, system.
Map request paths to privilege levels.
Implement middleware per privilege level: authMiddleware checks JWT, adminMiddleware checks role.
Register handlers on separate sub-muxes behind their respective middleware.
Test: verify each endpoint is accessible only at its required privilege level.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Trust boundaries in Go are implemented by composing http.Handler with middleware. This is the 'wall and gate' pattern: the wall separates trust zones, and the gate controls passage.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without trust boundaries, any compromised component can access any resource. In zero-trust architecture, every access decision is made at the resource boundary.

items.title: Input validation for security
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Input validation for security in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Validating input format but not semantics — checking email has @ but not that the domain exists.
Validating input on the client side only — client validation is for UX, server validation is for security.
Rejecting input that is 'too long' but accepting any length below the maximum without considering memory impact.
execution_timeline: Define the expected schema and constraints for each input field.
Implement validation as middleware or at the handler boundary before any business logic.
Use a validation library (go-playground/validator) or write explicit validation functions.
Validate each field: type, format, length/range, business rules.
Return structured 400 error responses with field-level messages.

failure_modes.scenario: A Go handler accepts a price field and validates it is a number but not that it is positive. An attacker sets a negative price.
failure_modes.cause: Validation checked type but not the business constraint.
failure_modes.fix: Use a domain type with Validate() that checks business rules.

failure_modes.scenario: A file upload endpoint accepts any filename. An attacker uploads '../../../etc/passwd'.
failure_modes.cause: Filename used without sanitizing path traversal.
failure_modes.fix: Use filepath.Base(), reject filenames containing '..' or '/'.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must write a Go handler that validates user registration and returns field-level errors.
Learner must explain why server-side validation is necessary even with client-side validation.
Learner must implement input validation that rejects SQL injection attempts in a search parameter.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's explicit error handling pairs with input validation: each check returns an error accumulated into a structured response. Packages like go-playground/validator provide struct tag-based validation.
zero_magic.mental_model: Input validation is a series of gates: type check -> format check -> length check -> business rule check. Each gate passes data through or rejects it. Data is not trusted until it has passed every gate.
performance_implications: Validation at the handler boundary costs about 10-50us per field.
Rejecting invalid input early is 100x cheaper than rejecting after a database write.
Validation libraries add about 100KB to the binary.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without input validation, every downstream component must handle malicious input. A SQL injection in a search field becomes a database breach. Input validation is the first defense.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement input validation for a Go HTTP endpoint accepting name, email, age. Invalid fields return 400 with field-specific error messages.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every production Go API validates input at the handler boundary. Frameworks like Echo and Gin provide built-in validation; net/http services use middleware or per-handler validation.
step_by_step_execution: Define a Go struct with validation tags.
In the handler: decode JSON, validate, return 400 on failure, proceed on success.
For business rules, add a custom validator or check after struct validation.
Never pass raw input to database queries — always use parameterized queries.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: encoding/json validates JSON structure (types, syntax). Additional validation uses struct tags or explicit Validate() methods. go-playground/validator uses reflection for struct tags.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Input is the attacker's primary vector. Without validation, every input is an attack surface. With validation, the attack surface is reduced to the validation logic itself.

items.title: Authentication vs authorization
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Authentication vs authorization in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Confusing authentication (who you are) with authorization (what you can do).
Implementing authorization as an afterthought in handlers instead of a separate middleware layer.
Using the same middleware for both auth and authz — mixing them makes either hard to test independently.
execution_timeline: Request arrives at the HTTP handler chain.
Authentication middleware extracts and validates credentials. If fails: return 401.
Store identity in context on success.
Authorization middleware reads identity from context and checks permissions. If fails: return 403.
If both pass: pass to the handler.

failure_modes.scenario: An API checks the user is authenticated but never checks resource ownership. User A deletes User B's data.
failure_modes.cause: Authentication passes but authorization is missing.
failure_modes.fix: Add authorization check: if resource.OwnerID != ctx.UserID { return 403 }.

failure_modes.scenario: An admin endpoint checks the role is admin but does not re-authenticate. A stolen session still has admin access.
failure_modes.cause: Authorization is checked once at login but never re-verified.
failure_modes.fix: Re-authenticate for sensitive operations. Shorten session TTL for admin.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain the difference between 401 (unauthenticated) and 403 (unauthorized).
Learner must write a Go middleware chain: first authenticates, second authorizes.
Learner must implement resource-level authorization: user can only edit their own profile.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's context is the standard carrier for authentication results. authMiddleware sets ctx.UserID; authzMiddleware reads it and checks against the resource.
zero_magic.mental_model: Authentication answers 'who are you?' Authorization answers 'are you allowed to do that?' First is about identity proof; second is about permissions. Separate middleware: if auth fails -> 401, if authz fails -> 403.
performance_implications: Authentication adds about 5-20ms per request (token verification).
Authorization adds about 1-5ms per check (cache lookup).
Caching authz decisions can reduce time to under 100us.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without separating auth and authz, all access control is implicit — checking 'is the user logged in?' but not 'is this the right user?' leads to the most common web vulnerability.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement a Go HTTP API with: login endpoint (returns JWT), GET /profile/:id (auth + authz), and GET /admin/users (auth + admin role).
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go services separate auth and authz into distinct middleware. Auth middleware (JWT verification) runs first. Authz middleware (RBAC/ABAC) runs second.
step_by_step_execution: Implement authentication middleware: extract token, validate, set ctx.UserID.
Implement authorization middleware: extract ctx.UserID, load permissions, check resource.
Register middleware: r.Use(authMiddleware); r.Use(authzMiddleware).
For resource-level authz: handler extracts resource ID, checks ownership against ctx.UserID.
Return 401 for auth failures, 403 for authz failures.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Auth middleware validates credentials and stores identity in context. Authz middleware reads identity and compares against resource ownership or roles. Go's mux composes these as separate wrappers.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Authentication without authorization is a door that opens for anyone with a key. Authorization controls which rooms they can enter.

items.title: Password hashing
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Password hashing in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using fast hash functions (SHA-256) for passwords — fast hashes can be brute-forced at billions of attempts/second on GPU.
Implementing custom password hashing logic — use bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2 from the Go stdlib.
Storing bcrypt hashes in a VARCHAR(60) field without testing with the slowest cost parameter.
execution_timeline: User submits password over HTTPS.
Server hashes using bcrypt: hash, err := bcrypt.GenerateFromPassword(password, 12).
Server stores the hash string — never the plaintext password.
During login: err := bcrypt.CompareHashAndPassword(storedHash, submittedPassword).
If err == nil, password matches.

failure_modes.scenario: A team uses SHA-256(salt + password). After a breach, attackers crack 80% of passwords within 24 hours on GPU.
failure_modes.cause: SHA-256 is designed for speed. Fast hashing is the opposite of what password storage needs.
failure_modes.fix: Replace with bcrypt (cost 12) which is deliberately slow.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer hashes passwords with bcrypt cost 4 (default). Each hash takes 5ms. An attacker cracks all in minutes.
failure_modes.cause: The cost parameter was too low.
failure_modes.fix: Use cost 10-12 as the minimum. Benchmark to find highest cost under 500ms.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain why SHA-256 is unsuitable for password hashing.
Learner must implement user registration and login using bcrypt with cost 10.
Learner must write a benchmark measuring bcrypt at cost 10, 12, and 14 and explain their production choice.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's golang.org/x/crypto/bcrypt provides GenerateFromPassword and CompareHashAndPassword. Go also provides argon2 for applications requiring the latest password hashing algorithm.
zero_magic.mental_model: Password hashing is a one-way function with a work factor. The hash is deliberately slow: slow for the attacker (brute force), manageable for the server (login). Cost parameter tunes this.
performance_implications: bcrypt at cost 10 takes about 100ms per hash.
Increasing cost by 1 doubles the time: cost 12 = about 400ms.
Argon2id is memory-hard, making GPU attacks much harder.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Plaintext passwords in a database are catastrophic: a single SQL injection exposes all user passwords. Hashing converts passwords into irreversible values to protect them even if the database is compromised.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must register two users with the same password and verify their bcrypt hashes differ (demonstrating salting). Then implement a login handler with bcrypt.CompareHashAndPassword.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: bcrypt is the industry standard for password hashing in Go. Large-scale Go services (Uber, Docker) use bcrypt for user authentication.
step_by_step_execution: Benchmark bcrypt at costs 10, 12, 14 on your hardware. Pick the highest cost under 500ms.
Register: hash, err := bcrypt.GenerateFromPassword(password, cost). Store as string in DB.
Login: err := bcrypt.CompareHashAndPassword(storedHash, submittedPassword).
Never log the password, hash, or comparison result.
Consider argon2id for new applications.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: bcrypt runs the Blowfish key schedule with password and salt for 2^cost iterations. Each iteration depends on the previous one, making it inherently sequential and impossible to parallelize on GPU.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Users reuse passwords across services. A breach that exposes plaintext passwords compromises users everywhere. Hashing limits damage to a single breach.

items.title: Sessions and cookies
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Sessions and cookies in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Storing sessions only in memory with no persistence — all sessions are lost on restart.
Using predictable session IDs — session tokens must be cryptographically random.
Not setting HttpOnly and Secure flags on session cookies — exposes session ID to XSS and man-in-the-middle.
execution_timeline: User authenticates (login endpoint). Server creates a session.
Server generates a random session ID and stores session data.
Server sets a cookie: http.SetCookie(w, &http.Cookie{Name: "session", Value: sessionID, HttpOnly: true, Secure: true, SameSite: http.SameSiteStrictMode}).
Client sends cookie on subsequent requests.
Server middleware loads session from cookie value, validates expiry, sets user identity in context.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer stores the entire user object in a signed cookie without server-side validation. A user modifies their role claim and escalates privileges.
failure_modes.cause: The cookie was signed but the server did not re-validate the session against a server-side store.
failure_modes.fix: Store only a session ID in the cookie. Keep session data server-side (Redis, DB).

failure_modes.scenario: Session cookies are not rotated after login — a fixed session ID is vulnerable to session fixation attacks.
failure_modes.cause: The server accepts the session ID the client provides without generating a new one after authentication.
failure_modes.fix: Always generate a new session ID after login. Never accept a pre-login session ID for authenticated requests.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain the security properties of HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite cookie attributes.
Learner must implement a session middleware in Go that loads session from Redis by cookie value.
Learner must explain why session IDs must be generated by a CSPRNG (crypto/rand) not math/rand.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's net/http provides cookie support via http.Cookie and http.SetCookie. Sessions are typically managed by gorilla/sessions or custom implementations using Redis with JSON serialization.
zero_magic.mental_model: A session is a temporary association between a user and server-side state. The session ID is a key to a lockbox on the server: the client has the key, but the lockbox contents never leave the server.
performance_implications: Session lookup adds about 1-5ms with Redis. In-memory sessions are faster but cannot survive restarts.
Session size affects performance — store only essential data (user ID, role, expiry).
Session cleanup (expired sessions) should run as a background goroutine on a ticker.
zero_magic.problem_solved: HTTP is stateless; without sessions, every request would require full authentication. Sessions provide a balance: authenticate once, carry a proof of authentication on subsequent requests.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement a session-based authentication system in Go: login endpoint creates a Redis-backed session, middleware loads the session on each request, and a logout endpoint destroys the session.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Sessions are used in web applications, admin panels, and any service where users authenticate via browser. Go services use Redis-backed sessions for horizontal scaling.
step_by_step_execution: Create session struct: type Session struct { UserID string; Role string; ExpiresAt time.Time }.
Generate session ID: b := make([]byte, 32); rand.Read(b); sessionID := hex.EncodeToString(b).
Store in Redis: redisClient.Set(ctx, "session:"+sessionID, sessionJSON, ttl).
Set cookie: http.SetCookie(w, &http.Cookie{Name: "sid", Value: sessionID, HttpOnly: true, Secure: true, Path: "/", SameSite: http.SameSiteStrictMode}).
Load in middleware: cookie, _ := r.Cookie("sid"); sessionJSON, _ := redisClient.Get(ctx, "session:"+cookie.Value).Result().
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Sessions in Go are typically cookie-based: the server sets a cookie with the session ID on login, the browser sends it on every request, and the server looks up session data from a backing store (memory, Redis, DB). The session ID must be cryptographically random to prevent guessing.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without sessions, users would re-authenticate on every page load. Sessions provide persistent authentication with server-side control (revocation, expiry, concurrent session limits).

items.title: JWT implementation
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply JWT implementation in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Not validating the algorithm header — accepting 'alg: none' tokens bypasses verification entirely.
Using symmetric signing (HS256) for multi-service architectures — the shared secret must be distributed to every service, increasing exposure.
Not setting expiration (exp claim) — tokens that never expire cannot be revoked and remain valid indefinitely.
execution_timeline: User authenticates. Server creates a JWT with claims: sub (user ID), role, exp (expiration), iat (issued at).
Server signs the JWT with RS256 (RSA private key) or ES256 (ECDSA private key).
Server returns the JWT in the response body (Bearer token pattern).
Client sends the JWT in the Authorization header: Bearer <token>.
Server middleware parses the JWT, validates the signature, checks exp, and extracts claims into context.

failure_modes.scenario: A server accepts 'alg: none' JWT tokens. An attacker creates an unsigned token with admin claims and gains full access.
failure_modes.cause: The JWT library was configured to accept the 'none' algorithm.
failure_modes.fix: Always set a whitelist of accepted algorithms: token, err := jwt.Parse(tokenString, func(t *jwt.Token) (interface{}, error) { if _, ok := t.Method.(*jwt.SigningMethodRSA); !ok { return nil, fmt.Errorf("unexpected signing method") }; return publicKey, nil }).

failure_modes.scenario: A developer uses HS256 and hardcodes the secret in the source code. The secret is leaked in a public GitHub repo.
failure_modes.cause: Symmetric signing requires every service to know the secret. Asymmetric signing (RS256) keeps the private key on the auth service only.
failure_modes.fix: Use RS256 or ES256. Distribute only the public key to verifying services.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain the difference between symmetric (HS256) and asymmetric (RS256) JWT signing and when each is appropriate.
Learner must implement JWT issuance and verification middleware in Go using golang-jwt/jwt.
Learner must identify the security flaw in a JWT implementation that does not validate the 'exp' claim.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's golang-jwt/jwt library is the standard JWT implementation. It supports multiple signing methods, provides middleware patterns for parsing and validation, and integrates with net/http context for claim storage.
zero_magic.mental_model: A JWT is a self-contained credential: the server signs a payload, and any service with the public key can verify it without contacting the issuing server. It is like a passport — it contains identity information, is tamper-evident (signature), and expires.
performance_implications: JWT verification (RS256) costs about 1-5ms per token on modern hardware.
JWT issuance costs about 5-20ms (RSA signing is more expensive than verification).
Parsing and validating claims adds about 10-50us once the signature is verified.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Stateless authentication: the server does not need to store session state. Any service can verify a JWT using the public key alone, enabling distributed auth without shared session stores.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement a Go HTTP service that issues JWTs on login and verifies them on protected endpoints. The implementation must use RS256, validate exp and nbf claims, and reject the 'none' algorithm.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: JWTs are used in: OAuth2 access tokens, OIDC ID tokens, service-to-service auth, and API authentication. Major Go services use golang-jwt/jwt for JWT handling.
step_by_step_execution: Generate RSA key pair: openssl genrsa -out private.pem 2048; openssl rsa -in private.pem -pubout -out public.pem.
Issue JWT: claims := &jwt.MapClaims{...}; token := jwt.NewWithClaims(jwt.SigningMethodRS256, claims); tokenString, err := token.SignedString(privateKey).
Parse and verify: token, err := jwt.Parse(tokenString, func(t *jwt.Token) (interface{}, error) { return publicKey, nil }).
Extract claims: if claims, ok := token.Claims.(jwt.MapClaims); ok && token.Valid { userID := claims["sub"].(string) }.
Set claims in context: ctx = context.WithValue(ctx, "user", claims).
zero_magic.under_the_hood: A JWT is three base64url-encoded segments separated by dots: header.payload.signature. The header contains the algorithm and type. The payload contains claims. The signature is the cryptographic output of signing header + payload with the private key. Verification recomputes the signature with the public key and compares.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Traditional session tokens require server-side storage and a database lookup on every request. JWTs eliminate the lookup by encoding identity directly in the token, enabling stateless authentication at scale.

items.title: JWT risks
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply JWT risks in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Not validating the algorithm header — accepting 'alg: none' tokens bypasses verification entirely.
Using HS256 in multi-service architectures — every service must hold the shared secret, increasing exposure.
Failing to validate the exp claim — tokens without expiration remain valid indefinitely if leaked.
Storing sensitive data in JWT claims — the payload is base64-encoded, not encrypted.
execution_timeline: Client authenticates and receives a JWT from the auth service.
Client includes the JWT in the Authorization header of subsequent requests.
Server middleware parses the JWT header to determine the signing algorithm.
Server validates the signature using the configured public key or HMAC secret.
Server checks standard claims: exp, nbf, iss, aud.
Server extracts custom claims and injects them into the request context.
Handler logic uses the context claims for authorization decisions.

failure_modes.scenario: A server is configured to accept 'alg: none' tokens. An attacker crafts an unsigned JWT with elevated privileges and gains admin access.
failure_modes.cause: The JWT library was initialized without an algorithm whitelist, accepting the 'none' algorithm.
failure_modes.fix: Always validate the algorithm: use ParseWithClaims with a keyfunc that checks the token header algorithm matches the expected method.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer uses HS256 with a weak secret. An attacker brute-forces the secret and forges arbitrary tokens.
failure_modes.cause: Symmetric signing with a weak secret is vulnerable to offline brute-force attacks.
failure_modes.fix: Use RS256 or ES256 with a properly generated key pair. Rotate keys periodically.

failure_modes.scenario: A service does not validate the exp claim. A stolen token is used months after its intended expiry.
failure_modes.cause: The JWT parsing code only checks the signature, not the expiration claim.
failure_modes.fix: Always enable required claim validation: parser := jwt.Parser{ValidMethods: []string{"RS256"}} and validate exp, nbf.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain why 'alg: none' is dangerous and how to prevent it.
Learner must write a JWT middleware that validates signature, exp, and nbf claims.
Learner must identify the security flaw in a JWT config that uses HS256 across multiple services.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's golang-jwt/jwt v5 is the standard JWT library. It provides jwt.Parse, jwt.ParseWithClaims, and middleware patterns. Go's explicit error handling ensures every parsing failure produces an error the caller must handle — there are no silent fallbacks.
zero_magic.mental_model: A JWT is a tamper-evident credential container. The server signs a JSON payload, producing a compact string any service can verify with the public key.
performance_implications: JWT verification with RS256 takes 1–5ms per token on modern hardware.
JWT issuance (RSA signing) takes 5–20ms — signing is more expensive than verification.
Parsing claims adds negligible overhead (~10–50µs) once the signature is verified.
Blacklisting individual tokens requires a server-side check, defeating the stateless benefit.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Stateless authentication: the server does not store session state. Any service can verify a JWT using the public key alone, enabling distributed auth without a shared session store or database lookup per request.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement a Go HTTP service that issues JWTs on login and verifies them on protected endpoints. The implementation must use RS256, validate exp and nbf, reject 'alg: none', and extract claims into the request context.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: JWTs are the foundation of OAuth2 access tokens, OIDC ID tokens, and service-to-service authentication. Major Go projects use golang-jwt/jwt for token handling in production.
step_by_step_execution: Generate RSA key pair: openssl genpkey -algorithm RSA -out private.pem -pkeyopt rsa_keygen_bits:2048; openssl pkey -in private.pem -pubout -out public.pem.

Issue token: claims := jwt.MapClaims{"sub": userID, "exp": time.Now().Add(time.Hour).Unix()}; token := jwt.NewWithClaims(jwt.SigningMethodRS256, claims); tokenStr, err := token.SignedString(privateKey).
Parse and verify: token, err := jwt.Parse(tokenStr, func(t *jwt.Token) (interface{}, error) { return publicKey, nil }).
Check token.Valid and extract claims: if claims, ok := token.Claims.(jwt.MapClaims); ok && token.Valid { ... }.
Inject user identity into the request context: ctx = context.WithValue(r.Context(), contextKeyUser, claims).
zero_magic.under_the_hood: A JWT is three base64url-encoded segments separated by dots: header.payload.signature. The header specifies the algorithm and token type. The payload contains registered claims (iss, sub, exp) and custom claims. The signature is the cryptographic output of signing header+payload with the private key. Verification recomputes the signature using the public key and compares.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Session tokens require server-side storage and a database lookup on every authenticated request. JWTs eliminate the lookup by encoding identity directly in the token, enabling stateless authentication at scale without a shared session database.

items.title: OAuth2 and OIDC overview
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply OAuth2 and OIDC overview in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Implementing OAuth2 from scratch instead of using a proven library — the specification has many edge cases.
Confusing OAuth2 (authorization delegation) with OIDC (authentication) — OAuth2 alone does not verify user identity.
Not validating the id_token signature and claims in OIDC — accepting unverified tokens allows impersonation.
Storing client secrets in client-side code — public clients cannot keep secrets.
execution_timeline: Client requests authorization from the resource owner (user).
Client redirects the user to the authorization server with client_id, redirect_uri, and scope.
User authenticates and consents on the authorization server.
Authorization server redirects back to the client with an authorization code.
Client exchanges the authorization code for an access token (and id_token in OIDC) at the token endpoint.
Client uses the access token to call the protected resource API.
Resource server validates the access token before serving the request.

failure_modes.scenario: A client does not validate the redirect_uri. An attacker initiates the flow and captures the authorization code via a crafted redirect target.
failure_modes.cause: The client registered a wildcard redirect_uri pattern or did not enforce exact match.
failure_modes.fix: Always register and enforce exact redirect_uri matching. Reject any redirect that does not match exactly.

failure_modes.scenario: A resource server accepts any access token without validation, trusting the format alone.
failure_modes.cause: The resource server does not validate the token signature, issuer, or audience.
failure_modes.fix: Always validate the token signature, iss, and aud claims against the authorization server's well-known configuration.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain the difference between OAuth2 and OIDC.
Learner must enumerate the OAuth2 grant types and when each is appropriate.
Learner must describe the PKCE flow and why it is required for public clients.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's golang.org/x/oauth2 package provides a robust OAuth2 client implementation. Coreos/go-oidc/v3 adds OIDC support. These libraries handle token exchange, refresh, and validation using Go's http.Client interface and context cancellation.
zero_magic.mental_model: OAuth2 is a valet key system for APIs. The user gives the client a limited-access token (valet key) instead of their password. OIDC adds an identity layer: the client also receives a signed ID card (id_token) proving who the user is.
performance_implications: OAuth2 token exchange adds one HTTP round trip to the authorization server per session.
Token introspection (opaque tokens) requires a database call per request; JWTs are self-validating.
Refresh token rotation adds a storage cost but improves security.
OIDC id_token verification adds ~1–5ms for signature validation.
zero_magic.problem_solved: OAuth2 solves delegated authorization: a third-party client can access a user's resources without ever seeing the user's password. OIDC adds authentication: the client can verify the user's identity through a signed id_token from a trusted provider.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must configure a Go service as an OAuth2 client using golang.org/x/oauth2, implement the authorization code flow with PKCE, and explain the difference between access tokens and id_tokens.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: OAuth2 and OIDC are the foundation of modern identity federation. Google, GitHub, Microsoft, and Auth0 all use OAuth2/OIDC. Go services integrate with these providers via golang.org/x/oauth2.
step_by_step_execution: Register the client at the authorization server: obtain client_id and client_secret.
Configure the OAuth2 config: conf := &oauth2.Config{ClientID, ClientSecret, Endpoint, Scopes, RedirectURL}.
Redirect the user to AuthCodeURL: http.Redirect(w, r, conf.AuthCodeURL(state, oauth2.AccessTypeOffline), http.StatusFound).
Handle the callback: exchange the code for tokens: tok, err := conf.Exchange(ctx, r.URL.Query().Get("code")).
Use the access token: client := conf.Client(ctx, tok); resp, err := client.Get("https://api.example.com/resource").
zero_magic.under_the_hood: OAuth2 defines four roles: resource owner, client, authorization server, and resource server. The authorization code flow uses a one-time code that the client exchanges for tokens, preventing the token from being exposed in the redirect URL. OIDC extends OAuth2 with the id_token (JWT) and UserInfo endpoint.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Before OAuth2, applications asked for user passwords directly (password anti-pattern). OAuth2 decouples authentication from authorization, allowing users to grant limited access without sharing credentials.

items.title: API keys
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply API keys in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using API keys as the sole authentication mechanism for human users — API keys lack granular revocation and rotation.
Logging API keys in request logs or error messages — keys in logs are accessible to anyone with log access.
Storing API keys in plaintext in the database — a database breach exposes all keys.
Generating predictable API keys — sequential or time-based keys are guessable.
execution_timeline: Client registers and receives a generated API key.
Client includes the API key in the Authorization header or query parameter.
Server receives the request and extracts the API key.
Server hashes the API key and looks it up in the database.
Server checks the key's expiration, rate limits, and permissions.
Server attaches the tenant/user identity to the request context.
Handler executes the business logic.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer stores API keys in plaintext. An attacker gains read access to the database and exfiltrates every active key.
failure_modes.cause: The API keys were stored as raw strings instead of hashed values.
failure_modes.fix: Hash API keys with bcrypt or SHA-256 before storing. Only the hash is stored; the plaintext key is returned once on creation.

failure_modes.scenario: An API key is logged in the access logs. An attacker with log access uses the key to call the API.
failure_modes.cause: The logging middleware logs the full request, including the Authorization header.
failure_modes.fix: Redact sensitive headers in logging middleware: log.Printf("Authorization: %s", redactAuthHeader(r.Header.Get("Authorization"))).

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must implement API key generation using crypto/rand.
Learner must hash API keys before storing and describe why.
Learner must write a middleware that extracts, validates, and rate-limits API keys.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go services use middleware to extract API keys from headers, validate them against a database or cache, and attach the client identity to the request context. Common patterns use httputil and the context package for clean propagation.
zero_magic.mental_model: An API key is a shared secret between the client and the server. It identifies the client and is used for authentication, not authorization.
performance_implications: API key lookup adds 1–10ms depending on database vs. cache hit.
bcrypt hash verification adds ~10–100ms per request depending on the cost factor.
SHA-256 hashing is ~100x faster than bcrypt but lacks built-in cost factor.
Caching validated keys in memory reduces latency to <1µs per lookup.
zero_magic.problem_solved: API keys provide a simple, revocable authentication mechanism for programmatic access. Unlike passwords, API keys are designed for automation: they have no human interaction at login, can be scoped to specific permissions, and can be rotated independently of user accounts.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement an API key authentication system in Go: key generation with crypto/rand, key hashing with SHA-256, a middleware that extracts and validates keys, and a test that verifies valid keys pass and invalid keys are rejected with 401.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: API keys are used by Stripe, Twilio, GitHub, and most SaaS platforms. A Go API gateway typically validates API keys as the first middleware in the chain before routing to backend services.
step_by_step_execution: Generate key: bytes := make([]byte, 32); _, err := rand.Read(bytes); key := hex.EncodeToString(bytes).
Hash key: hash := sha256.Sum256([]byte(key)); store hash, not plaintext.
Store in database: INSERT INTO api_keys (key_hash, client_id, permissions, expires_at) VALUES ($1, $2, $3, $4).
Middleware: extract key from Authorization: Bearer header, hash it, look up hash in database.
Validate: check expires_at, check permissions for the requested resource, attach client info to context.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: An API key is a cryptographically random string, typically 32–64 bytes encoded as hex or base64url. The server stores only a hash of the key, so a database breach does not expose active keys. Key validation involves hashing the presented key and looking up the hash.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: User-based authentication (username/password) is not suitable for programmatic access. API keys provide a machine-friendly credential that can be scoped, rotated, and revoked independently of user accounts.

items.title: RBAC
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply RBAC in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Hardcoding role checks in business logic instead of using a policy layer — changes require code deploys.
Using only roles without resource-level scoping — an admin role should not grant access to all resources by default.
Storing role assignments in application code rather than a database.
Implementing RBAC without default-deny — missing role checks implicitly allow access.
execution_timeline: User authenticates and their identity is established.
Server loads the user's roles from the database or identity provider.
Server attaches roles to the request context.
Request reaches an endpoint with an RBAC guard.
Guard checks if the user's role includes the required permission for the action.
If permitted, the handler executes. If denied, the server returns 403 Forbidden.
Audit log records the access decision.

failure_modes.scenario: A user with role 'editor' can delete resources because the delete permission was not scoped by role.
failure_modes.cause: The RBAC check only verifies the user is authenticated, not that their role has the specific permission.
failure_modes.fix: Implement granular permission checks: if !user.HasPermission("resource:delete") { http.Error(w, "forbidden", http.StatusForbidden); return }.

failure_modes.scenario: A new endpoint is added without an RBAC guard. Every authenticated user can access it, including those without the required role.
failure_modes.cause: Missing authorization middleware on the new route.
failure_modes.fix: Use middleware-based RBAC that applies default-deny to all routes. Explicitly whitelist public routes.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must design an RBAC data model with roles, permissions, and role-permission mappings.
Learner must implement an RBAC middleware that checks permissions before allowing handler execution.
Learner must write tests that verify authorized access succeeds and unauthorized access returns 403.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go RBAC implementations typically use middleware chains. A common pattern loads user roles into a struct attached to the request context, and a permission-checking middleware returns 403 when the required permission is missing. Casbin is the most popular Go RBAC/ABAC library.
zero_magic.mental_model: RBAC is a role-based permission matrix. Users are assigned roles, roles are assigned permissions, and permissions grant access to actions. It is a many-to-many relationship: one user can have multiple roles, one role can grant multiple permissions.
performance_implications: Role lookup adds 1–5ms with a database query or <10µs with cached roles.
Permission checks are O(1) with a hash set lookup.
Complex RBAC hierarchies can increase check time to O(log n) with tree-based role structures.
Caching role-permission mappings in memory reduces latency to <1µs per check.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Without RBAC, authorization logic is scattered across the codebase as ad-hoc if-statements. RBAC centralizes access control into a declarative policy layer that non-engineers can audit. It separates who can do what from how it is implemented.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement RBAC in a Go HTTP service with role-permission mappings, a middleware guard, and tests. The implementation must support multiple roles per user and deny access by default.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: RBAC is the standard authorization model for enterprise applications. Kubernetes, AWS IAM, and GitHub all use role-based access control. Go services implement RBAC via Casbin or custom middleware.
step_by_step_execution: Define roles and permissions in the database: roles(id, name), permissions(id, action, resource), role_permissions(role_id, permission_id).
Load user roles on login: SELECT r.name, p.action, p.resource FROM users_roles ur JOIN roles r JOIN role_permissions rp JOIN permissions p ...
Create a User struct with roles and a HasPermission method: func (u *User) HasPermission(action, resource string) bool { ... }.
Write RBAC middleware: func RBAC(action, resource string) func(http.Handler) http.Handler { return func(next http.Handler) http.Handler { ... } }.
Apply middleware to routes: r.With(RBAC("delete", "post")).Delete("/posts/{id}", handler).
zero_magic.under_the_hood: RBAC has three core entities: users, roles, and permissions. Permissions are typically expressed as action:resource pairs (e.g., post:create, post:delete). A permission check iterates the user's roles and looks up the intersection of role permissions with the required permission.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Ad-hoc if-statements for authorization do not scale. RBAC provides a maintainable, auditable, and testable authorization layer that separates access policy from business logic.

items.title: ABAC and policy checks
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply ABAC and policy checks in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Implementing ABAC without a policy engine, leading to convoluted if-else chains in handlers.
Mixing attribute definitions across layers — defining resource ownership in the handler instead of the policy layer.
Writing policies that are too permissive because of missing attribute constraints.
Failing to audit policy evaluation results — ABAC decisions are hard to debug without logs.
execution_timeline: User authenticates and their attributes (department, clearance level, region) are loaded.
Request arrives with resource attributes (resource type, owner, classification).
Environment attributes (time of day, IP range, request method) are collected.
Policy engine evaluates all attributes against the defined policies.
Engine returns allow or deny with an optional reason.
Middleware enforces the decision and logs the audit trail.

failure_modes.scenario: A policy allows access to documents based on department, but does not include resource ownership. A user in Finance can access every Finance document, not just their own.
failure_modes.cause: The ABAC policy lacks the resource.owner == user.id constraint.
failure_modes.fix: Add resource ownership to the policy: effect: deny unless resource.owner == user.id || user.role == "admin".

failure_modes.scenario: A policy evaluation panics due to missing attribute. The middleware recovers and denies all subsequent requests.
failure_modes.cause: The policy engine does not handle missing attributes gracefully.
failure_modes.fix: Define default attribute values and nil-safe accessors in the policy engine.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain when ABAC is preferable to RBAC.
Learner must write an ABAC policy using Casbin or Open Policy Agent (Rego).
Learner must implement attribute extraction middleware that feeds context into the policy engine.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go services use Casbin (github.com/casbin/casbin) or Open Policy Agent (github.com/open-policy-agent/opa) for ABAC. These engines evaluate policies written in a declarative language against request attributes extracted from the HTTP context.
zero_magic.mental_model: ABAC is attribute-based authorization: access decisions are made by evaluating policies against user, resource, and environment attributes. Unlike RBAC (which role does the user have?), ABAC asks: does the full context permit this action?
performance_implications: ABAC policy evaluation takes 1–50ms depending on policy complexity and attribute count.
OPA evaluation is typically 1–10ms for moderate policy sizes.
Caching evaluation results per attribute combination reduces latency to <1ms.
Attribute extraction adds negligible overhead (<10µs per attribute).
zero_magic.problem_solved: RBAC becomes unwieldy when access depends on contextual attributes like resource ownership, geography, time, or clearance level. ABAC models these as attribute-based policies, keeping authorization declarative and auditable without hardcoding logic.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement ABAC for a document management API: define attributes (user.department, resource.owner, resource.classification), write Casbin or OPA policies, and verify correct allow/deny decisions in tests.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: ABAC is used in AWS IAM (condition statements), Google Cloud IAM, and Kubernetes (admission webhooks). Go microservices use OPA as a sidecar for fine-grained authorization decisions.
step_by_step_execution: Identify user attributes: department, role, region, clearance_level.
Identify resource attributes: resource_type, owner, classification, project_id.
Identify environment attributes: request_method, time_of_day, ip_range.
Write Casbin model: [request_definition], [policy_definition], [policy_effect], [matchers].
Evaluate: enforcer, _ := casbin.NewEnforcer("model.conf", "policy.csv"); allowed, _ := enforcer.Enforce(user, resource, action).
zero_magic.under_the_hood: ABAC policies are sets of rules that evaluate boolean expressions over attributes. A typical policy says: allow if user.department == resource.department AND resource.classification <= user.clearance_level. The policy engine evaluates all matching rules and applies the first applicable effect (allow/deny).
zero_magic.why_it_exists: RBAC cannot express fine-grained contextual access control (e.g., 'managers can view their direct reports' salary data during business hours'). ABAC generalizes authorization to arbitrary attribute conditions.

items.title: Tenant isolation basics
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Tenant isolation basics in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using SELECT * from shared tables without a tenant WHERE clause — one missing filter leaks all tenants' data.
Implementing tenant isolation in application code without database-level enforcement.
Storing tenant_id as a string without validation — path traversal-like attacks on tenant context.
Sharing connection pools across tenants without context propagation.
execution_timeline: Request arrives with tenant context (subdomain, header, or JWT claim).
Middleware extracts tenant_id and validates it exists and is active.
Middleware injects tenant_id into the request context.
Repository layer extracts tenant_id from context and adds it to every query.
Database enforces row-level security or schema isolation.
Response is returned with tenant-scoped data only.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer forgets to add WHERE tenant_id = $1 to a query. The query returns all tenants' data to the requesting tenant.
failure_modes.cause: Manual tenant filtering in SQL is error-prone — one missing clause causes cross-tenant data leak.
failure_modes.fix: Use database row-level security (RLS) or enforce tenant_id in a repository middleware that wraps every query.

failure_modes.scenario: Tenant A can access Tenant B's files because the file storage path uses only the file ID without the tenant prefix.
failure_modes.cause: The file storage path is /files/{fileID} instead of /files/{tenantID}/{fileID}.
failure_modes.fix: Always prefix storage paths with tenant ID: /{tenantID}/{resourceType}/{resourceID}.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must implement tenant context extraction middleware.
Learner must write a repository that automatically scopes queries by tenant_id.
Learner must identify the cross-tenant data leak in a vulnerable code example.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go multitenant services use context.Context to propagate tenant_id through the call chain. Middleware extracts tenant info from the request and sets it on the context. Repository layers read tenant_id from context and append it to every query, preventing accidental cross-tenant access.
zero_magic.mental_model: Tenant isolation is the guarantee that one tenant cannot access another tenant's data. The database is the locked filing cabinet inside that room.
performance_implications: Adding tenant_id to every query adds negligible (<1µs) overhead when indexed.
Row-level security adds 1–5µs per query for policy evaluation.
Schema-per-tenant isolation requires separate connection pools, increasing memory usage.
Caching must include tenant_id in the cache key to avoid cross-tenant cache hits.
zero_magic.problem_solved: SaaS applications serve multiple customers (tenants) from a single deployment. Without tenant isolation, one customer's users could access another customer's data. Tenant isolation ensures each tenant sees only their own data, either through shared tables with tenant_id filtering or separate database schemas.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement tenant isolation in a Go API: middleware that extracts tenant_id from the request, a repository that scopes queries by tenant_id, and tests that verify cross-tenant isolation.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every SaaS platform implements tenant isolation. GitHub (organization-scoped data), Slack (workspace isolation), and AWS (account isolation) all use tenant-scoped access patterns.
step_by_step_execution: Middleware: extract tenant_id from request (subdomain/X-Tenant-ID header/JWT claim).
Validate tenant exists and is active: query database or cache.
Inject tenant_id into context: ctx = context.WithValue(r.Context(), tenantKey, tenantID).
Repository: func (r *Repo) GetPosts(ctx context.Context) ([]Post, error) { tenantID := ctx.Value(tenantKey).(string); query with WHERE tenant_id = $1 }.
Test isolation: create two tenants, insert data for each, verify each tenant's API call returns only their data.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Shared-table isolation uses a tenant_id column on every table with a database index. Row-level security (RLS) in PostgreSQL enforces tenant filtering at the database level, preventing application bugs from leaking data. Schema-per-tenant isolation creates separate database schemas for each tenant.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Multi-tenant SaaS cannot exist without data isolation. Regulatory requirements (GDPR, SOC 2, HIPAA) mandate that one customer's data is inaccessible to another. Tenant isolation is the architectural foundation for compliance and trust.

items.title: SQL injection prevention
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply SQL injection prevention in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Concatenating user input into SQL strings: db.Query(fmt.Sprintf("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = '%s'", input)).
Using string interpolation for IN clauses: strings.Join(userIDs, ",") instead of parameterized placeholders.
Believing that input validation (e.g., stripping quotes) prevents SQL injection — it does not.
Only sanitizing GET parameters but not POST/PUT body fields or headers.
execution_timeline: User input arrives via query parameter, body, or header.
Server validates input type and format (not a security measure, but reduces attack surface).
Server constructs the SQL query using parameterized placeholders ($1, $2, etc.).
Server sends the query and the parameters separately to the database driver.
Database driver treats parameters as data, not SQL, preventing injection.
Query executes safely even if the parameter contains malicious SQL.

failure_modes.scenario: An attacker submits username = '; DROP TABLE users;--. The application concatenates this into a SQL string and the database drops the users table.
failure_modes.cause: The query was built with fmt.Sprintf or string concatenation instead of parameterized queries.
failure_modes.fix: Use parameterized queries: db.Query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = $1", userInput).

failure_modes.scenario: A dynamic ORDER BY clause uses user input without validation: db.Query("SELECT * FROM products ORDER BY "+sortCol).
failure_modes.cause: ORDER BY columns cannot be parameterized, so the input is concatenated directly.
failure_modes.fix: Whitelist allowed sort columns: allowed := map[string]bool{"price": true, "name": true}; if !allowed[sortCol] { sortCol = "name" }.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must write a vulnerable query and then fix it using parameterized placeholders.
Learner must explain why parameterized queries prevent SQL injection while input escaping does not.
Learner must implement safe dynamic sorting without exposing the database to injection.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's database/sql package uses parameterized queries exclusively. db.Query, db.QueryRow, and db.Exec all accept placeholders ($1, $2, etc. for PostgreSQL; ? for MySQL) with separate parameter arguments. The driver handles escaping and parameter binding server-side.
zero_magic.mental_model: SQL injection is a code injection attack where malicious SQL is smuggled inside a data value. Parameterized queries separate SQL code from data: the query template is parsed first (definition phase), then the parameters are substituted as data only, never as executable SQL.
performance_implications: Parameterized queries have negligible overhead vs. string concatenation.
Prepared statements can be reused across calls, improving performance.
ORM query builders (GORM, sqlx) use parameterized queries internally — no performance penalty for safety.
Input validation overhead is typically <1µs per field.
zero_magic.problem_solved: SQL injection is one of the most critical web vulnerabilities (consistently in the OWASP Top 10). It allows attackers to read, modify, or delete database contents by injecting malicious SQL through unsanitized user input. Parameterized queries eliminate the attack vector entirely.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must audit a Go codebase with SQL injection vulnerabilities, fix each one using parameterized queries, and write a test that confirms the fix prevents injection attempts.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every Go database application uses parameterized queries. sqlx, pgx, GORM, and ent all internally use parameterized queries. The Go standard library database/sql enforces the parameterized pattern.
step_by_step_execution: Identify all database operations in the codebase: grep for Query, QueryRow, Exec.
Check if any use fmt.Sprintf or + for SQL construction.
Replace with parameterized: db.Query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1", userID).
For dynamic IN clauses, use a helper: query, args := sqlx.In("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id IN (?)", ids).
For dynamic ORDER BY, use a whitelist: if !slices.Contains(allowedSorts, sortCol) { sortCol = "id" }.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: When a parameterized query is sent, the database receives the SQL template and the parameters as separate messages. The database parses the SQL template, builds a query plan, and then binds the parameters as data values. Since parameters are bound after parsing, they cannot alter the query structure.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Web applications must accept user input. Without parameterized queries, every user input that reaches a SQL string is a potential injection point. Parameterized queries exist to make the safe path the default path.

items.title: XSS
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply XSS in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Rendering user-generated HTML without escaping: template.HTML(userInput) creates a direct XSS vector.
Sanitizing input but not output — input sanitization is fragile and bypassable.
Using html/template for user-generated content without understanding context-sensitive escaping.
Disabling CSP headers because they block legitimate scripts.
execution_timeline: Attacker submits malicious JavaScript as a comment, profile field, or message.
Server stores the input in the database (properly parameterized to prevent SQL injection).
Server renders a page that includes the stored comment.
If the server outputs the comment as raw HTML, the attacker's script executes in every viewer's browser.
The script steals session cookies, redirects to phishing pages, or defaces the site.

failure_modes.scenario: A user submits a comment containing <script>document.cookie</script>. The server stores it and renders it as raw HTML on the page.
failure_modes.cause: The template uses {{.Comment}} in an unsafe context, or uses template.HTML to mark the content as safe.
failure_modes.fix: Go's html/template automatically escapes HTML in {{.Comment}}. Never use template.HTML with user input. Always use text/template only for non-HTML output.

failure_modes.scenario: A server returns JSON with user input that a frontend inserts into the DOM via innerHTML.
failure_modes.cause: Go correctly escapes the input, but the frontend uses .innerHTML instead of .textContent or React's JSX auto-escaping.
failure_modes.fix: Never use innerHTML with user-controlled data. Use textContent or a frontend framework that auto-escapes.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain the difference between stored, reflected, and DOM-based XSS.
Learner must identify an XSS vulnerability in a Go html/template rendering path.
Learner must implement Content-Security-Policy headers to mitigate XSS.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's html/template package automatically contextually escapes output based on the template context (HTML, attribute, CSS, JavaScript, URL). This prevents XSS by default when rendering templates. For API servers, Go returns JSON that the frontend must insert safely.
zero_magic.mental_model: XSS is an injection attack where malicious scripts are injected into web pages viewed by other users. html/template acts as an automatic output sanitizer: it encodes special characters (<>"'&) based on the HTML context, ensuring user data is treated as text, not code.
performance_implications: html/template escaping adds ~1–10µs per template execution.
CSP headers add ~200–500 bytes to response headers.
Input sanitization libraries (bluemonday) add 10–100µs per input.
CSP evaluation is done by the browser, not the server.
zero_magic.problem_solved: XSS allows attackers to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of a trusted website, stealing session cookies, credentials, and sensitive data. XSS prevention ensures user-supplied content is rendered as safe text, not executable code, in the browser.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must audit a Go web application that serves user-generated content, identify XSS vulnerabilities in the template rendering, fix them using html/template's context-sensitive escaping, and add CSP headers.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: XSS consistently ranks in the OWASP Top 10. Go's html/template is the standard defense for server-rendered HTML. For JSON APIs, the frontend framework is responsible for safe rendering.
step_by_step_execution: Identify all template rendering points: locate template.ParseFS, template.Execute calls.
Replace text/template with html/template for user-facing HTML output.
Review template variables: ensure no {{.UserInput}} is wrapped with template.HTML.
Add Content-Security-Policy header: w.Header().Set("Content-Security-Policy", "default-src 'self'").
Test: submit <script>alert(1)</script> and verify it is rendered as escaped text, not executed.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: html/template parses a template into a tree of nodes. When executing, it applies context-sensitive escaping rules. In an HTML body context, it escapes <>&"'. In a JavaScript string context, it uses JavaScript string escaping. The package prevents most XSS, but template.HTML (raw) bypasses escaping entirely.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Web applications need to display user-generated content (comments, profiles, reviews). Without XSS prevention, every such feature is an attack vector. html/template exists to make safe output the default.

items.title: CSRF
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply CSRF in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Building APIs without CSRF protection, assuming JSON requests are not vulnerable — CORS misconfigurations enable cross-origin JSON requests.
Using the wrong token pattern: storing CSRF tokens in cookies without the SameSite attribute.
Implementing CSRF tokens manually instead of using a proven library.
Disabling CSRF protection for authenticated endpoints.
execution_timeline: User logs into the target application and receives a session cookie.
User visits an attacker-controlled website in another tab.
Attacker's page submits a form or AJAX request to the target application.
The browser automatically includes the session cookie with the request (same-origin policy does not block cookie attachment).
The target application receives a valid session but no CSRF token — rejects the request.
The legitimate site includes the CSRF token in every state-changing request.

failure_modes.scenario: A bank's transfer API is vulnerable to CSRF. An attacker creates a page that auto-submits a form: <form action='https://bank.com/transfer' method='POST'><input name='toAccount' value='attacker'></form>.
failure_modes.cause: The bank's API accepts session cookies without requiring a CSRF token.
failure_modes.fix: Require a CSRF token in every state-changing request. Use SameSite=Strict or SameSite=Lax cookies.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer stores the CSRF token in a cookie and accepts it from the same cookie in the POST body, but does not validate that they match.
failure_modes.cause: The CSRF token cookie can be set by the attacker's subdomain or MITM if not using Secure+HttpOnly.
failure_modes.fix: Use the double-submit cookie pattern correctly: generate a random token, set it as a cookie, and require the same token in a custom header. Validate server-side.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain how CSRF attacks work and why cookies are insufficient alone.
Learner must implement CSRF protection using gorilla/csrf or a custom middleware.
Learner must write a test that verifies a CSRF attack is blocked.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go services use gorilla/csrf or chi's middleware for CSRF protection. These libraries generate per-session tokens, embed them in templates or responses, and validate them on state-changing requests. The token is compared server-side, not by the client.
zero_magic.mental_model: CSRF is a confused deputy attack: the browser is tricked into making an authenticated request on behalf of the attacker. CSRF tokens act as a secret handshake: the server embeds a unique token in each form, and only requests carrying this token are accepted.
performance_implications: CSRF token generation adds <1µs using crypto/rand.
Token validation is a constant-time comparison (O(1)).
Per-session token storage adds a small memory overhead (~100 bytes per session).
Stateless double-submit cookie pattern avoids server-side storage entirely.
zero_magic.problem_solved: CSRF exploits the browser's automatic cookie attachment: an attacker tricks an authenticated user's browser into sending a forged request to the target application. Without CSRF protection, any state-changing endpoint (transfer money, change email, delete account) can be attacked.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement CSRF protection in a Go web application, demonstrate that a forged cross-origin request is rejected, and explain the double-submit cookie pattern.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: CSRF protection is mandatory for all browser-based applications that use cookie authentication. Major Go frameworks (Chi, Gin, Echo) include CSRF middleware. Modern browsers also support SameSite cookies as a defense-in-depth measure.
step_by_step_execution: Install gorilla/csrf: go get github.com/gorilla/csrf.
Wrap the router with CSRF protection: r.Use(csrf.Protect([]byte("32-byte-auth-key"), csrf.Secure(true))).
In templates, include the token: <input type="hidden" name="gorilla.csrf.Token" value="{{.csrfToken}}">.
For API clients, read the token from the X-CSRF-Token cookie and set it as the X-CSRF-Token header.
Test: send a POST request without the CSRF token — should return 403.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The double-submit cookie pattern: the server generates a random token, sets it as a cookie, and requires the same token in a custom request header. Since the attacker's page cannot read the cookie (same-origin policy) or the custom header (CORS), they cannot forge a valid request.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: The web's authentication model (cookies sent automatically by the browser) creates a fundamental vulnerability. CSRF protection exists to close this gap by requiring an explicit, unforgeable token for every state-changing mutation.

items.title: CORS
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply CORS in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Setting Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * with credentials: true — this is invalid and causes the browser to reject the response.
Reflecting the Origin header in Access-Control-Allow-Origin without validation — allows any site to read the response.
Not handling preflight OPTIONS requests — real requests fail silently in the browser.
Using CORS as a security mechanism — CORS is a browser policy, not server-side security.
execution_timeline: Browser detects a cross-origin request (different scheme, host, or port).
For non-simple requests, browser sends a preflight OPTIONS request.
Server responds with Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Allow-Methods, Allow-Headers.
Browser checks the preflight response. If allowed, it sends the actual request.
Server processes the actual request and includes Access-Control-Allow-Origin in the response.
Browser enforces CORS: it blocks the JavaScript from reading the response if the origin is not allowed.

failure_modes.scenario: A Go API returns Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *. The frontend needs to include credentials, but the browser rejects the response because * with credentials is invalid.
failure_modes.cause: The wildcard origin * is not allowed when the request includes credentials (cookies, Authorization headers).
failure_modes.fix: Set Access-Control-Allow-Origin to the specific origin: w.Header().Set("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", "https://myapp.com").

failure_modes.scenario: A developer configures CORS to reflect the Origin header: w.Header().Set("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", r.Header.Get("Origin")). An attacker's site sends a request and the browser allows reading the response.
failure_modes.cause: Reflecting the origin without validation allows any site to bypass CORS.
failure_modes.fix: Validate the origin against a whitelist: if !allowedOrigins[origin] { w.WriteHeader(http.StatusForbidden); return }.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain the CORS preflight flow and when it is triggered.
Learner must implement CORS middleware with origin validation and proper headers.
Learner must identify the security flaw in an unrestricted CORS configuration.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go services use rs/cors (github.com/rs/cors) or Chi's CORS middleware. These libraries wrap the HTTP handler and set the appropriate CORS headers. Go's net/http allows fine-grained control: developers can inspect the Origin header and set per-origin policies.
zero_magic.mental_model: CORS is a browser-enforced access control policy for cross-origin requests. It is not a server-side security measure — a malicious client (curl, Postman) ignores CORS entirely. CORS protects the browser's same-origin policy from being bypassed by JavaScript.
performance_implications: CORS header injection adds negligible overhead (<1µs).
Preflight requests add one additional HTTP round trip for non-simple requests.
Caching preflight responses with Access-Control-Max-Age reduces redundant OPTIONS requests.
Complex origin validation (regex/database lookup) adds 1–10µs per request.
zero_magic.problem_solved: The browser's same-origin policy blocks JavaScript from reading cross-origin responses. CORS provides a controlled mechanism to relax this policy for trusted origins, enabling legitimate cross-origin API calls while maintaining security.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement CORS middleware in Go, handle preflight OPTIONS requests, validate origins against a whitelist, and explain why CORS does not protect against server-side attacks.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every public API that supports browser clients configures CORS. Cloud providers (AWS, Google Cloud), social APIs (Twitter, GitHub), and payment gateways (Stripe) all use CORS to control cross-origin access.
step_by_step_execution: Define allowed origins: allowedOrigins := map[string]bool{"https://app.example.com": true}.
Write CORS middleware: func CORSMiddleware(next http.Handler) http.Handler { return http.HandlerFunc(func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) { ... }) }.
Handle preflight: if r.Method == http.MethodOptions { set CORS headers and return 200 }.
Validate origin: origin := r.Header.Get("Origin"); if !allowedOrigins[origin] { origin = "" }.
Set response headers: w.Header().Set("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", origin); w.Header().Set("Access-Control-Allow-Credentials", "true").
zero_magic.under_the_hood: CORS headers are: Access-Control-Allow-Origin (which origins can read the response), Access-Control-Allow-Methods (allowed HTTP methods), Access-Control-Allow-Headers (allowed custom headers), and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials (whether cookies/auth headers are allowed). The browser enforces these headers; the server just sets them.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: The same-origin policy prevents malicious websites from reading data from other origins. CORS provides a standards-based escape hatch for legitimate cross-origin integrations, enabling the modern API ecosystem.
