items.title: Caching basics
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Caching basics in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using sync.Map for read-heavy workloads with simple keys — sync.Map is optimized for two patterns: (1) append-only writes with many readers, (2) write-once/read-many. For a standard read-heavy workload (90% reads, single writer), a regular map with sync.RWMutex is 2-5x faster because sync.Map's atomic Load overhead outweighs mutex contention at low concurrency.

Adding a cache without a TTL or eviction policy — an unbounded cache grows until the process OOMs. Every cache must have: max size (item count or byte limit), eviction policy (LRU, LFU, TTL), and a plan for stale data. A cache with no eviction policy is a memory leak waiting to happen.

Caching at the wrong layer — caching a computed value instead of the expensive input-to-output mapping. If the expensive work is json.Unmarshal of the same response body, cache the parsed result (Go struct), not the raw HTTP response. Caching at the right layer maximizes the cost saved per cache hit.
execution_timeline: Initial state: cache map is empty, maxSize=1000, TTL=5m, heap baseline = 100MB
→ Request 1: cache.Set("user:42", userStruct{...}) — allocates key+value in map, ~500 bytes added to heap
→ Request 2: cache.Set("user:7", userStruct{...}) — another ~500 bytes, heap grows to 100.001MB
→ ... after 1000 unique keys: heap includes 1000 entries × ~500 bytes = ~500KB of cached data
→ Key 1001 triggers eviction: LRU entry is deleted from map, its value becomes GC-eligible
→ GC runs: freed memory from evicted entry, heap returns to ~100.5MB (1000 entries remain)
→ After 1 hour with TTL=5m: entries expire lazily — on each get, expired entries are deleted
→ Steady state: ~1000 active entries, ~500KB cache heap, no growth — eviction balances writes
failure_modes: Cache stampede (thundering herd): 100 concurrent requests for the same uncached key all miss the cache and all hit the DB simultaneously — the DB sees 100x load instead of 1. Fix: request coalescing — only one goroutine fetches the value; others wait on a shared channel (singleflight.Group).

Stale cache serving deleted data: a user deletes their account, but the cache entry with TTL=30m continues serving the stale profile for 30 minutes. Fix: active invalidation — delete the cache entry in the same transaction as the DB delete, or use write-through caching that updates cache and DB atomically.

Cache poisoning from partial writes: a function that computes a cached value panics halfway through, leaving a partial/zero value in the cache. On the next hit, the zero value is treated as valid. Fix: only store in cache after the value is fully computed — write to a local variable first, then atomic.Store to the cache.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain why a cache with 1,000 entries and 100,000 requests/second still has a 50% miss rate if the request key distribution is uniform random across 10,000 keys — LRU evicts entries before they are reused.
Learner must implement a cache with TTL and LRU eviction using only the standard library (map + container/list), handle concurrent access, and benchmark it against sync.Map for a read-heavy workload.
Learner must identify the cache stampede window in a given code path and explain how singleflight.Group would prevent it.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go provides sync.Map for concurrent map access with amortized read optimization, but most production Go caching uses external libraries (hashicorp/golang-lru, dgraph-io/ristretto) or a simple map + sync.RWMutex + TTL goroutine. The standard library uses caching internally: DNS results (net.ResolveIPAddr), TLS session tickets, and HTTP connection pooling all use in-memory caches with TTL. For distributed caching, Go services use Redis or Memcached clients.
zero_magic.mental_model: A cache is a fast temporary storage that sits between the application and a slow data source. The drawer has limited space — when it fills up, the least-used tool goes back to the toolbox (LRU eviction). The key insight: caching only helps if the access pattern has locality — the same keys are requested repeatedly within the eviction window.
performance_implications: Cache hit vs miss cost ratio determines the performance gain. A DB query at 5ms vs cache lookup at 50µs = 100x faster on hit. At 90% hit rate, the effective latency = 0.9 × 50µs + 0.1 × 5ms = 545µs — 9x faster than always hitting the DB.

Cache overhead: each cached entry adds ~200 bytes for the map entry + key + value. For 1M entries, that is ~200MB of heap. The GC must scan this memory on every cycle. Use a cache with a byte-size limit, not just an item-count limit.

TTL-based caches add goroutine overhead: if each entry has its own time.Timer, 1M entries = 1M active timers = 1M goroutines on the timer bucket. Use a single cleanup goroutine that scans for expired entries every N seconds instead.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers add caches without understanding the mechanics: they use sync.Map because 'it is concurrent' without checking the workload pattern, or they use an unbounded map and wonder why the process OOMs. This lesson teaches the concrete mechanics of caching: hit/miss ratio, TTL, eviction policies, concurrency, and memory cost. After this lesson, learners can reason about caching tradeoffs instead of blindly adding a map.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement a thread-safe in-memory cache with: (1) Set(key, value, ttl), (2) Get(key) (value, bool) with TTL expiration, (3) LRU eviction when the cache exceeds maxSize, (4) a background goroutine that evicts expired entries every 30 seconds. Then benchmark: populate the cache with 10K entries, measure Get latency at 90% hit rate vs 10% hit rate, and report the latency ratio.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: In-memory caching is the most common optimization technique in Go production services. Standard patterns: (1) per-request cache (sync.Once or map inside a request context), (2) per-process cache (sync.Map or golang-lru shared by all requests), (3) distributed cache (Redis with go-redis client). Kubernetes uses in-memory caching for API object deserialization. The Go playground caches compiled binaries. Every Go HTTP service with a database should have some form of in-memory cache.
step_by_step_execution: Write: type Cache struct { mu sync.RWMutex; items map[string]*entry; maxSize int }
Write: type entry struct { value interface{}; expiresAt time.Time }
Write: Get(key) — mu.RLock, lookup item in map, check time.Now().After(item.expiresAt), if expired delete and return nil, else return item.value
Write: Set(key, value, ttl) — mu.Lock, if len(items) >= maxSize, remove LRU entry (track access order with container/list), insert new entry, mu.Unlock
Write: evictExpired() goroutine — for range time.Tick(30 * time.Second) { mu.Lock; iterate map, delete expired entries; mu.Unlock }
Benchmark: cache 10K entries, run 100K Gets with workload A (90% hits) and workload B (10% hits)
Result: workload A: avg 50µs per Get; workload B: avg 5ms (cache miss → DB) — 100x difference
zero_magic.under_the_hood: A Go map used as a cache is a hash table with O(1) average lookup, but it is not goroutine-safe without external synchronization. sync.Map uses a combination of atomic loads for read-mostly workloads and a mutex-protected 'dirty' map for writes. hashicorp/golang-lru implements LRU with a doubly-linked list + map: on access, the entry is moved to the front of the list; on eviction, the entry at the back is removed. TTL expiration in most implementations uses lazy eviction (check on Get) plus a periodic cleanup goroutine. The tradeoff is: lazy eviction is cheaper per-op but can leave stale entries in memory longer; periodic cleanup removes stale entries in batches but adds a mutex contention spike every N seconds. For very large caches, use a sharded design: divide the key space across N independent caches, each with its own lock, reducing lock contention by Nx.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Caching exists because accessing data from the original source (database, API, disk) is orders of magnitude slower than reading it from memory. A PostgreSQL query takes 1-10ms; an in-memory map lookup takes 50-500ns — 10,000x faster. Caching is the single highest-impact optimization technique because it converts slow operations into fast ones without changing the operation itself. Every production Go service needs caching to meet latency SLAs, and every developer needs to understand the mechanics to avoid the common failure modes.

items.title: Cache invalidation
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Cache invalidation in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Setting a long TTL as the only invalidation mechanism — a 30-minute TTL means updates are invisible for up to 30 minutes. Users see stale data and report bugs that are 'already fixed.' TTL-only caches are acceptable only for data that changes rarely or where staleness is acceptable (e.g., 'top 10 articles' updated hourly).

Deleting cache entries before writing the new value to the database — if the cache entry is deleted and the DB write fails, the next request sees a cache miss and the old data is never served again until a new write succeeds. The cache is now empty for this key, and the DB may be hit harder. Fix: delete after the DB write succeeds, not before.

Invalidating a cache entry that was never cached — calling cache.Delete(key) for every DB write is wasteful if the key was never cached. Track whether a key has been cached (e.g., a bloom filter or a set of cached keys) before sending invalidation events. Unnecessary invalidation is harmless for correctness but creates write amplification on the cache.

Using cache-aside without a version check — in cache-aside (lazy population), the cache stores whatever the DB returns. If two concurrent requests race: request A updates the DB, request B reads old data and writes the old value to cache — the old value overwrites the new one. Fix: use a version number or timestamp and only write to cache if the version matches.
execution_timeline: Initial state: cache has user:42 → {name: "Alice", version: 5}, expires in 30m
→ Write request: PATCH /users/42 — DB updates name to "Bob", version becomes 6
→ Cache invalidation: cache.Delete("user:42") — entry removed from map, ~500 bytes freed
→ Read request: GET /users/42 — cache miss, reads DB: {name: "Bob", version: 6}
→ Cache populated: cache.Set("user:42", {name: "Bob", version: 6}) — ~500 bytes allocated on heap
→ If invalidation is lost: cache still has {name: "Alice", version: 5} — stale data served
→ After TTL expires (30m): entry evicted, next read gets fresh data from DB
→ Race scenario: read gets miss, DB returns version 5 (old), write updates DB to version 6, read writes version 5 to cache — version 6 is invisible until TTL expires
failure_modes: Invalidation event lost due to network partition: a service deletes a user account but the cache invalidation message is lost in transit (Redis connection dropped, message queue backlog). The cache continues serving the deleted account's data indefinitely. Fix: add a short TTL as backup — even with active invalidation, every cached entry has a maximum lifetime so stale data is eventually purged.

Cascading invalidation storm: updating one entity requires invalidating N related cache keys (e.g., updating a product price invalidates the product page, the category page, the search index, and the user's cart). If the update propagates to 100 concurrent updates, 100 × N = 4000 invalidation events hit the cache simultaneously — causing a load spike on the cache server and temporary unavailability. Fix: batch invalidation events or use a version-based cache key (product:v42 instead of product) so old keys become unreferenced naturally.

Time-of-check-to-time-of-use (TOCTOU) race: request A checks the cache, gets a miss, and starts fetching from DB. Request B updates the DB. Request A finishes the DB fetch (with old data) and writes it to the cache — overwriting the now-stale data with even staler data. Fix: use a compare-and-swap on cache write — only write if the version in cache is older than the version from DB.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain why a 5-minute TTL on a cache of 'user profile' data can cause a user to see a deleted account for up to 5 minutes, even though the code deletes the cache entry when the account is deleted — the invalidation event may be lost and TTL is the only backup.

Learner must design an invalidation strategy for a cache that stores the output of a function combining data from three DB tables — when any of the three tables changes, the cached output may be stale.
Learner must identify the TOCTOU race in a given cache-aside implementation and fix it using version numbers.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go production services typically use cache-aside (lazy population) with active invalidation: on read, check cache → miss → read DB → write cache; on write, update DB → delete or update cache. write-through caching updates cache and DB atomically, but this requires the cache to support transactions. write-behind caching writes to cache immediately and asynchronously updates DB — this is fast but risks data loss if the process crashes between cache write and DB write. Go's standard library does not include a caching framework; production services use Redis (go-redis) for distributed caches or hashicorp/golang-lru for in-process caches.
zero_magic.mental_model: Cache invalidation is the problem of keeping a copy (cache) consistent with the source of truth (DB). If the erasure is forgotten, the whiteboard shows old data. If the erasure happens before the ledger update, the whiteboard is empty even though the update failed. If two people erase and write simultaneously, the final whiteboard state may match neither intended update. The three mechanisms are: TTL (the whiteboard self-erases after a timeout), active invalidation (someone erases when the ledger changes), and write-through (someone updates the whiteboard as part of the ledger update). Each has tradeoffs.
performance_implications: Active invalidation creates write amplification: every DB write triggers N cache operations (delete or update). For a service with 1000 writes/second and 5 related cache keys per write, that is 5000 additional cache operations/second — adding ~2-5% load to the cache server.

TTL-only invalidation avoids write amplification but creates read amplification on expiration: when a TTL expires, the next read hits the DB. If a popular key expires under load (all instances' TTLs align), the stampede effect multiplies DB load by the number of instances.

Version-based cache keys (cache key includes data version) eliminate invalidation entirely — old versions become unreferenced and are GC'd. But this increases key cardinality, requiring more memory and making LRU eviction less effective (old versions occupy cache slots until evicted).
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers add caching and discover that stale data is worse than no cache — users see incorrect information and report bugs. They set a TTL and pray. This lesson teaches that caching without an invalidation strategy is a correctness bug waiting to happen. The lesson covers the three invalidation strategies (TTL, active invalidation, write-through) and their tradeoffs, plus the TOCTOU race that plagues cache-aside implementations.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement a cache layer for a user profile service with: (1) cache-aside read pattern with version checking, (2) active invalidation on profile update (delete cache entry after DB write succeeds), (3) a TTL fallback of 5 minutes, (4) a test that demonstrates the TOCTOU race and the fix. The test must: start two goroutines — one reading the profile in a loop (10 reads/s), one writing the profile every 100ms — and verify that no read ever returns a version older than the last write minus one (allowing for eventual consistency).
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Cache invalidation strategies are a core architectural decision in every production Go service with caching. Kubernetes uses watch-based invalidation: clients watch for resource changes and invalidate local caches. Uber's caching layer uses a write-through pattern with Redis and a version matrix. The Go playground uses TTL-only (10 minutes) because the data changes rarely and staleness is acceptable. In distributed systems, cache invalidation is typically solved with: (1) Redis pub/sub for invalidation events, (2) database triggers that emit NOTIFY on row changes (LISTEN/NOTIFY), or (3) a version vector attached to each cache entry.
step_by_step_execution: Write: type CachedUser struct { User User; Version int; ExpiresAt time.Time }
Write: GetUser(id) — cache.Get(key) returns (CachedUser, ok); if ok and version >= expectedVersion, return user
Write: GetUser(id) — on cache miss: db.QueryRow("SELECT ...", id) returns (User, version); cache.Set(key, CachedUser{user, version, time.Now().Add(TTL)})
Write: UpdateUser(id, newData) — db.Exec("UPDATE users SET ...", newData, id) returns updated version
Write: After DB update succeeds: cache.Delete(key) — this is the active invalidation
Write: If DB update fails: do NOT delete cache — preserve the old data rather than lose it
Test: start reader goroutine (10 reads/s) and writer goroutine (10 writes/s), run for 10 seconds
Verify: no read returns a version < max_written_version - 1 (TOCTOU window allows at most one stale read)
Conclusion: cache-aside + active invalidation + TTL fallback provides eventual consistency with bounded staleness
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Cache invalidation in Go typically uses one of three patterns. (1) Cache-aside with delete: the application reads from cache first; on cache miss, reads from DB and populates the cache. On writes, it updates the DB and deletes the cache entry (or updates it). This is the most common pattern because it handles concurrent writes safely — deleting is idempotent, and the next read fetches fresh data. (2) Write-through: the application updates both cache and DB atomically. Go has no built-in distributed transaction support, so this requires coordinating a two-phase commit between cache (Redis) and DB (PostgreSQL) — typically implemented with the outbox pattern: write to a DB table, a separate process reads the outbox and updates the cache. (3) Write-behind: the application writes to cache immediately and queues the DB write. If the process crashes between cache write and DB write, the data is lost. Go's channels and worker pools implement this pattern naturally. The key invariant: in cache-aside, the cache is always a snapshot of the DB at some point in time; the invalidation strategy determines the staleness window, not the correctness.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Cache invalidation is the hardest problem in caching because it exposes the fundamental tension between performance and consistency. A cache with 100% hit rate and instant consistency is a distributed transaction, which defeats the purpose of caching (speed). Every cache invalidation strategy is a business decision about how much staleness is acceptable. Without understanding the mechanics, developers pick random TTLs and hope — the result is either stale data (TTL too long) or no caching benefit (TTL too short, high miss rate). Cache invalidation mechanics turn 'praying' into 'engineering with a bounded staleness guarantee.'

items.title: Alerting mindset
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Alerting mindset in the context of professional Go software engineering.
required_prior_knowledge: Understanding of module-12 items up to core-12-16.

beginner_mistakes: Alerting on every symptom — creating an alert for every metric that can go wrong (high CPU, high memory, slow query, error count > 0) produces thousands of alerts that operators ignore. An alert should represent a user-impacting condition that requires human action within a specific time window. If the team ignores an alert, it should be deleted or downgraded to a warning.

Setting static thresholds without historical context — alerting when p99 latency > 500ms may fire constantly for a service that normally runs at 480ms p99. The threshold must be based on the service's actual performance distribution, not an arbitrary number. Use dynamic thresholds (baseline + 3σ) or set the threshold at the SLO boundary plus a safety margin.

Not defining a runbook before creating the alert — an alert fires at 3am and the on-call engineer has no idea what to do. The alert email says 'error rate high' with no link to a runbook, dashboard, or mitigation steps. Every alert must include a link to a runbook that specifies: (1) what to check, (2) how to diagnose, (3) how to mitigate, (4) who to escalate to.
execution_timeline: Service deploys a new version with a query optimization bug
→ p99 latency rises from 200ms to 800ms over 10 minutes
→ Prometheus scrape captures the increase: http_request_duration_seconds_p99 rising
→ Alert rule evaluates: histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(...)[5m]) > 500ms for 5 minutes → pending state
→ After 5 minutes of sustained >500ms: alert fires → Alertmanager sends PagerDuty notification
→ On-call engineer receives notification on phone: 'p99 latency > 500ms for service A'
→ Engineer opens the linked Grafana dashboard: sees p99 latency spike starting 15 minutes ago
→ Engineer opens the linked runbook: 'Step 1: Check recent deployments — rollback if p99 > 500ms for >10min'
→ Engineer identifies the recent deploy, rolls back
→ p99 latency returns to 200ms, alert resolves
→ Post-incident: team adds a latency regression test to CI to prevent future deployments that degrade p99 by >50%
failure_modes: Alert fatigue from poorly-tuned thresholds: too many alerts fire without requiring action — operators stop checking notifications. When a real incident occurs, the alert is buried in noise. Fix: review all alerts quarterly, mute or delete any alert that has not triggered a human action in 3 months.

Alert that fires during known maintenance windows: a weekly DB migration script causes a 5-minute latency spike that triggers the p99 alert every Wednesday at 2am. Operators learn to ignore Wednesday alerts — and miss the real incident that happens during maintenance. Fix: use Alertmanager's silence rules for scheduled maintenance, or use time-based routing for different notification targets.

Dead man alert not configured: Prometheus itself goes down — no alerts fire. The team has no monitoring on their monitoring. Fix: configure a 'dead man's switch' alert that must fire periodically (e.g., every hour) to prove the alerting pipeline is working. If it does not fire, the team knows the monitoring system is down.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain the difference between a warning and an alert: a warning fires when a metric crosses a threshold but does not require immediate action; an alert requires action within a defined time window. Warnings go to a dashboard or email; alerts go to PagerDuty.

Learner must define an SLO for a service (99.9% availability over 30 days), compute the error budget (0.1% of total requests), set a burn-rate alert that fires when the error budget is consumed at 10x the target rate in 1 hour, and explain what the on-call engineer should do.

Learner must explain why an alert on CPU > 80% for 5 minutes may be noise for a service that batch-processes data every hour — CPU spikes during batch jobs are expected; the alert should only fire when CPU is high OUTSIDE the batch window.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Alerting in Go production services uses Prometheus Alertmanager or Grafana Alerting. The pattern is: instrument metrics → define recording rules for aggregations → define alert rules with expressions → route alerts to notification targets (PagerDuty, Slack, email). Alertmanager handles deduplication, grouping, silencing, and routing. The alert lifecycle: inactive → pending (threshold breached for evaluation window) → firing (sustained breach) → resolved (threshold cleared). Each alert must have a severity (critical, warning, info) and a link to a runbook.
zero_magic.mental_model: Alerting is a smoke detector, not a thermostat. A thermostat constantly adjusts (low-level monitoring). A smoke detector only makes noise when there is a fire (user-impacting problem). If the smoke detector goes off every time someone cooks (every deployment causes a brief latency spike), the occupants disable it (alert fatigue). The correct design: the smoke detector only goes off for actual fires — situations that require immediate human action. Everything else is a dashboard or log.
performance_implications: Alert evaluation CPU cost is proportional to the number of alert rules and the cardinality of the queried metrics. 100 rules evaluating every 30s on 10K series each = 1M queries/30s = 33K queries/s. Prometheus can handle this, but the rule evaluation adds ~5% CPU overhead.
Alertmanager notification routing is O(1) per alert. Alertmanager groups alerts by configured labels and sends one notification per group. At 100 alerts/second, Alertmanager CPU is ~1% — negligible.
Runbook links and alert metadata add ~200 bytes per alert payload. At 100 alerts/second, this is ~20KB/s of network traffic for notifications — negligible.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers set up monitoring and then create alerts for every metric they can think of. The result: 500 alerts that fire constantly, all of which are ignored. This lesson teaches the alerting mindset: an alert must represent a user-impacting condition that requires human action. Alerts that do not require action are noise. The lesson covers SLO-based alerting, burn rate, runbooks, and avoiding alert fatigue.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must define an SLO for a Go HTTP service (99.9% availability over 30 days), compute the monthly error budget, configure a Prometheus alert that fires when the error budget burn rate exceeds 10x the target for 1 hour, write a runbook for the alert with diagnosis and mitigation steps, and then simulate an error spike to verify the alert fires and the runbook steps are correct.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Alerting mindset is the foundation of SRE practice. Google's SRE book defines the standard: every alert must be: (1) urgent (requires immediate action), (2) actionable (the responder knows what to do), and (3) effective (the action mitigates the problem). Major organizations (Google, Netflix, GitHub) use burn-rate alerting based on SLOs. The 'you are not Google' principle: small teams should have <10 critical alerts; each alert should fire <1x per quarter.
step_by_step_execution: Define SLO: 99.9% of requests complete in <500ms over 30 days
Compute error budget: 0.1% of total requests over 30 days = 0.1% × 1M requests/day × 30 days = 30,000 requests
Define burn rate: 10x means consuming error budget 10x faster than target — if budget lasts 30 days at target, at 10x burn it lasts 3 days
Set alert: burn_rate = error_ratio / (1 - SLO_target) > 10 for 1 hour → critical alert
Write runbook: title 'High latency on service A', symptoms, diagnosis steps, mitigation (rollback, scale up), escalation path
Test: simulate 20% error rate for 10 minutes — burn rate > 10 → alert fires
Verify: on-call receives PagerDuty notification with runbook link → follows steps → rollback → latency recovers
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Alertmanager receives alerts from Prometheus via the Alertmanager API. Each alert has: labels (severity, service, team), annotations (summary, description, runbook_url), and a status (firing or resolved). Alertmanager applies grouping: alerts with the same group_by labels are sent as a single notification (e.g., group all alerts for service='api' into one Slack message). Inhibitions suppress low-severity alerts when a high-severity alert is firing (e.g., if the database is down, suppress all application-level alerts that depend on it). Silences mute alerts for a specified duration (used for maintenance). The notification template (default or custom Go template) formats the alert into a message for the target (Slack webhook, PagerDuty API, email). Prometheus alert rules are evaluated as part of PromQL query evaluation — the rule expression is executed against the TSDB at the evaluation interval, and the result (truth value of the expression) determines the alert state.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Alerting exists because no one watches dashboards 24/7. A dashboard tells you 'p99 latency is 800ms' — useful if someone is looking at it. An alert tells you 'p99 latency has been 800ms for 5 minutes and you need to wake up and fix it.' Without alerting, incidents are detected by user complaints. With alerting, incidents are detected by the monitoring system before users notice. The difference between a dashboard and an alert is the difference between a thermometer and a smoke detector.

items.title: Incident debugging
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Incident debugging in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Starting an incident investigation by guessing the root cause rather than gathering data — guessing leads to confirmation bias, wasted time on wrong subsystems, and delayed resolution.
Ignoring the alert dashboard and jumping straight into code — the dashboard shows the blast radius, affected users, and timeline; skipping it means investigating blind.
Focusing on a single log line instead of the full correlation-ID-scoped trace — one log line out of context is noise; the full trace shows the sequence of events.
Making changes to production (restarting, scaling) before understanding the root cause — the change may mask the symptom temporarily but does not prevent recurrence.
Not communicating status updates during the incident — stakeholders, teammates, and affected users are left guessing, eroding trust and causing parallel investigation.
execution_timeline: Alert fires: PagerDuty notifies on-call engineer with alert details (metric, threshold, duration)
→ Engineer acknowledges alert: opens dashboard, checks blast radius (which endpoints, which users, how many requests affected)
→ Engineer checks deployment timeline: compares alert time with recent deploys, config changes, or infrastructure events
→ Engineer searches logs: finds correlation IDs for affected requests, reads structured log lines in chronological order
→ Engineer identifies error pattern: all affected requests show 5-second timeout on downstream service X
→ Engineer checks downstream service dashboard: service X shows 100% error rate and 30-second p99 latency
→ Engineer escalates to service X team: provides correlation IDs and affected time window
→ Service X team identifies root cause: a recent deploy removed a connection pool configuration, causing connection exhaustion
→ Service X team rolls back the deploy: connections recover, error rate drops to 0
→ Engineer verifies recovery: original service shows 0 errors, p99 latency back to normal
→ Engineer writes postmortem: timeline, root cause, impact, action items to prevent recurrence
failure_modes: Alert without context: an alert fires saying "error rate > 5%" but provides no correlation IDs, affected endpoints, or user segments — the engineer starts from zero with no investigative lead.
Logs without correlation IDs: the engineer finds error log lines but cannot tell which log lines belong to the same request — the failure sequence across services is impossible to reconstruct.
Stale runbook: the runbook points to a dashboard URL that no longer exists — the engineer wastes 15 minutes finding the correct dashboard while the incident continues.
False alarm drills: every alert has been a false alarm for the last month — the engineer assumes this alert is also a false alarm and ignores it, delaying response to a real incident.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify which log lines are relevant to the failure before seeing the annotated trace.
Learner must explain why a partial failure is harder to detect than a full outage.
Learner must order the investigation steps (check dashboard, search logs, check deploys, identify pattern, escalate) correctly for a given incident scenario.

Learner must identify the root cause from a set of log lines, metrics charts, and deploy history — choosing between competing hypotheses (slow query, connection leak, memory pressure, network partition).
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The Go community's incident debugging approach follows the standard SRE practices: structured logging with slog, metrics with Prometheus, tracing with OpenTelemetry, and runbooks documented alongside the code. The pprof and trace tools are used for on-demand profiling during incidents. Go's runtime/pprof and net/http/pprof expose CPU, memory, goroutine, and mutex profiles at runtime — these are essential for debugging production issues without restarting the service.
zero_magic.mental_model: Incident debugging is a data-driven investigation, not a guessing game. The process is: observe (check dashboards and alerts) → correlate (find correlation IDs and trace spans) → isolate (identify the failing component or layer) → diagnose (find the root cause using logs, profiles, and dumps) → remediate (fix, roll back, or scale). Each step uses different observability data — never jump to diagnosis without first observing the blast radius.
performance_implications: pprof profiling adds ~5% CPU overhead when actively sampling — enable it on production with a secret endpoint or only when triggered by on-call engineers.
Full goroutine dumps at 100K goroutines produce ~100MB of text — use selective dump (SIGQUIT on a single goroutine) or the /debug/pprof/goroutine?debug=2 endpoint.
Trace sampling (1% of requests) keeps tracing overhead under 1% while still catching rare errors — a 0.1% error rate at 10K req/s appears in traces every 10 seconds on average.
Log aggregation adds 10-100ms of latency per log line — use async log shipping (buffered writers) to avoid blocking the request path.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers respond to production incidents by guessing: they restart the service, check a random log line, or make a code change without understanding the root cause. This wastes precious incident time and often makes things worse. This lesson provides a structured incident debugging framework: acknowledge → observe → correlate → isolate → diagnose → remediate. The learner practices this framework on realistic failure scenarios with actual observability data.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner is given a simulated production incident with: an alert notification, a metrics dashboard, structured log files with and without correlation IDs, and a deployment timeline. They must produce a postmortem containing: the exact timeline of events, the blast radius, the root cause, how they found it (which dashboard, which log query, which trace), and 3 action items to prevent recurrence.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every production incident at every Go shop follows the same pattern. Stripe's incident response process, Google's SRE handbook, and the Go community's postmortem culture all emphasize: gather data before acting, use correlation IDs to trace across services, and write postmortems without blame. The difference between a 10-minute and a 2-hour incident is almost always whether the engineer looked at the dashboard first or started guessing.
step_by_step_execution: Alert arrives: 'p99 latency for /api/orders > 2s for 5 minutes' — acknowledge in PagerDuty
Open dashboard: check /api/orders latency graph — confirm p99 is 3.2s, p50 is 200ms (only some requests are slow)
Check deployment timeline: a deploy to the inventory service happened 10 minutes before the alert — likely related
Search logs for correlation IDs of slow requests: find 5 correlation IDs, group the log lines for each
Trace each request: all 5 show the same pattern — fast handler code, slow HTTP call to inventory-service (3s response)
Check inventory service dashboard: its /health endpoint shows 30s response time — the health check is blocking a connection pool mutex
Diagnose: the inventory service deploy changed the health check to query a table that is locked by a long-running migration
Remediate: roll back the inventory service deploy — latency drops to 200ms within 2 minutes
Postmortem: document the timeline, root cause (migration + health check query), and action items (health check must use a separate pool with short timeout)
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's runtime provides pprof profiles at /debug/pprof/: goroutine (all goroutine stacks), heap (memory allocation), profile (CPU), mutex (contended mutexes), and block (blocking operations). When debugging an incident, the engineer fetches a goroutine profile to see if goroutines are stuck in Gwaiting or Gsyscall — a thousand goroutines waiting on a channel receive indicates a blocked producer. The mutex profile shows which mutexes are contended and which call sites hold them. The CPU profile shows which function is consuming CPU — useful for infinite loops or busy-waiting.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Incident debugging exists because production failures are inevitable — hardware fails, software has bugs, deployments have unintended effects, and traffic patterns change. Without a structured debugging process, every incident is a panic-driven guessing game. With structured debugging, the engineer follows a repeatable process: observe the blast radius, correlate the failing requests, isolate the failing component, diagnose the root cause, and remediate. The process turns a chaotic incident into a methodical investigation.

items.title: Reliability review
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Reliability review in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Treating the post-incident review as a blame exercise — writing 'engineer X made a mistake' as the root cause. The goal is to improve the system, not assign blame. A better root cause: 'the deployment pipeline did not have a canary stage to detect the regression before production.' Blame-free reviews encourage engineers to report incidents honestly.

Fixing the symptom without addressing the systemic gap — after an incident, patching the specific bug (fixing one missing validation) without addressing why the bug reached production (no code review for that path, no test for that edge case). The incident will repeat with a different bug. Fix: identify the systemic gap (testing, review, monitoring) and close it.

Not documenting action items with owners and deadlines — a review produces a list of fixes but no one is assigned to implement them, and there is no follow-up. The fixes are forgotten until the next incident. Every action item must have: owner, deadline, and a verification step.
execution_timeline: Incident occurs: p99 latency spikes to 5s, error rate jumps to 15%, users report timeouts
→ On-call engineer triages: acknowledges the alert, declares a SEV-2 incident in the incident management system
→ Engineer mitigates: rolls back the last deployment, latency returns to normal in 5 minutes
→ Incident resolved: 20 minutes from alert to mitigation
→ Within 48 hours: incident review meeting scheduled with all involved teams
→ Engineer writes the timeline: 12:00 deploy, 12:05 alert fires, 12:07 engineer acknowledges, 12:25 rollback starts, 12:30 latency normal
→ Team identifies root cause: a query optimization removed an index hint that the DB planner needed for the new query pattern
→ Team identifies systemic gaps: (1) no canary deployment, (2) staging DB has different query plans than production, (3) no query latency regression test in CI

→ Action items documented: (1) add canary stage to deploy pipeline (owner: platform team, deadline: next sprint), (2) add production-scale query plan comparison to staging (owner: DB team, deadline: 2 weeks), (3) add p99 latency regression test to CI (owner: service team, deadline: 1 week)
→ Follow-up in 2 weeks: review action item progress, verify items (1) and (3) are complete, (2) is in progress
failure_modes: Review without follow-up — the review meeting produces a document that is never read again. The same incident repeats 3 months later because none of the action items were implemented. Fix: assign a tracking owner who follows up on each action item weekly until complete.

Blaming the on-call engineer for missing the alert — the engineer was in a meeting and did not see the PagerDuty notification for 8 minutes. The root cause is not the engineer's response time; it is that only one person is on-call and there is no escalation path. Fix: configure alert escalation (if not acknowledged in 5 minutes, notify secondary).

Root cause analysis stops at 'human error' — 'the engineer typed the wrong command' is not a root cause. Why was the command typed manually? Why was there no approval step? Why was there no 'undo' mechanism? The real root cause is the process that allowed a single human action to cause an outage.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must write a post-incident review for a simulated incident (e.g., 'deploy of v2.3.1 caused 5xx errors on /api/orders for 12 minutes') that identifies: (1) root cause (new validation logic panics on nil pointer), (2) systemic gap (no nil-pointer test for that code path, no canary deployment), (3) action items with owners and deadlines.
Learner must explain the difference between a 'root cause' (the technical trigger) and a 'systemic gap' (the process failure that allowed the trigger to reach production) — both must be addressed.
Learner must review an existing system and identify 3 reliability risks (single point of failure, missing monitoring, manual deployment step) and propose fixes ranked by impact-to-effort ratio.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Reliability reviews in Go production services follow the post-incident review pattern from Google's SRE workbook. The format is: (1) incident summary, (2) timeline of events, (3) impact assessment (affected users, duration, revenue loss), (4) root cause, (5) systemic gaps, (6) action items with owners and deadlines. The review is stored in a knowledge base and contributes to the team's reliability maturity model. Go services specifically review: deployment process, dependency failures, configuration changes, and capacity planning.
zero_magic.mental_model: A reliability review is like an airplane crash investigation. The goal is not to punish the pilot (blame) but to understand why the crash happened and prevent it from happening again. The investigation looks at: the pilot's actions (the immediate trigger), the plane's design (the system), the airline's training program (the process), and the regulatory framework (the culture). Fixing the pilot (firing the engineer) does not fix the design, training, or culture. A good review identifies fixes at ALL levels: code, deployment, testing, monitoring, and process.
performance_implications: The cost of NOT doing reliability reviews is higher than the cost of doing them. Each review takes 2 hours of 5 people's time = 10 person-hours. A single preventable outage can cost 100 person-hours in debugging + revenue loss + reputation damage. Reviews are the cheapest insurance against repeat incidents.

Action item tracking adds process overhead. Using a spreadsheet to track 10 action items per review with weekly check-ins costs ~30 minutes/week. Using a dedicated tool (Jira, Linear) costs setup time but saves tracking effort in the long run.

The review frequency should be proportional to incident frequency. A team with 1 incident/month should review every incident. A team with 50 incidents/month should review only critical (SEV-1/SEV-2) incidents and sample the rest.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers fix the immediate bug after an incident and move on. The same incident repeats 3 months later because the root cause was the deployment process, not the code. Reliability reviews solve this by documenting the incident timeline, identifying systemic gaps, and creating action items that prevent recurrence. The lesson teaches how to write a blame-free, action-oriented post-incident review.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must conduct a reliability review of a simulated incident: a Go service during a black Friday sale experienced a 23-minute outage because a configuration change disabled connection pooling. The learner must: (1) write a timeline from deploy to resolution, (2) identify the root cause (config change removed MaxOpenConns setting), (3) identify 3 systemic gaps (no config review process, no connection pool monitoring, no canary deployment), (4) write 3 action items with owners and deadlines, (5) write a 1-paragraph summary suitable for executive communication.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Post-incident reviews are standard practice in every reliable software organization. Google's SRE team reviews every SEV-1/SEV-2 incident. GitHub publishes post-incident reviews publicly. Cloudflare's incident review process is documented in their blog. In Go production services, the review pattern is applied to: deployment failures, data loss incidents, security breaches, and capacity-related outages. The review document becomes part of the team's knowledge base and is referenced in future incident training.
step_by_step_execution: Create incident review document: title 'Reliability Review: Service A latency spike on 2025-01-15'

Write summary: 'On Jan 15 at 12:00 UTC, deployment v2.3.1 caused p99 latency to spike from 200ms to 5s due to a missing connection pool configuration. Service was degraded for 23 minutes. Root cause: config file omitted MaxOpenConns.'
Write timeline: '12:00: deploy started, 12:03: alert fired (p99 > 1s), 12:05: engineer acknowledged, 12:15: rollback started, 12:23: latency returned to normal'
Identify root cause: 'deploy v2.3.1 removed the MaxOpenConns setting from the DB config, defaulting to 0 (unlimited), causing connection pool exhaustion'
Identify systemic gaps: '(1) no config diff check in deploy pipeline, (2) no connection pool metric monitored, (3) no canary stage'

Action items: '(1) add config diff check to CI (owner: platform, deadline: Jan 30), (2) add db.MaxOpenConns metric to dashboard (owner: service, deadline: Jan 20), (3) implement canary deployment (owner: platform, deadline: Feb 15)'
Follow-up: schedule review in 2 weeks to verify action item progress
zero_magic.under_the_hood: A reliability review is a structured document, not a technical system. The structure follows Google's SRE post-incident review format: (1) Incident ID and severity, (2) Summary (1 paragraph), (3) Impact (users affected, duration, revenue loss), (4) Timeline (chronological events with timestamps), (5) Root cause analysis (5 Whys technique), (6) Systemic gaps (process, testing, monitoring, deployment), (7) Action items (what, who, when), (8) Blamelessness statement (explicitly stating no individual is blamed). The review is stored in a shared drive or wiki. Action items are tracked in the team's project management system with recurring follow-ups. The review process is iterative: after 3 months, the team reviews whether the action items prevented similar incidents.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Reliability reviews exist because the same incident will repeat unless the SYSTEMIC cause is fixed, not just the surface-level bug. A nil pointer dereference is the technical root cause. The systemic cause is 'no test for that code path' or 'no code review for that change' or 'no monitoring for panics.' Fixing the nil pointer prevents that specific crash but does not prevent the next nil pointer. Fixing the test gap, review process, or monitoring prevents the entire CLASS of incidents. Reliability reviews turn incidents into learning opportunities instead of repeatable failures.

items.title: Why architecture exists
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Why architecture exists in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Jumping straight to microservices — every new Go project starts with a gRPC service in its own repo, separate deployment, and a Kubernetes manifest. The overhead of service boundaries (network calls, serialization, deployment coordination, observability) overwhelms the team before they have product-market fit. Start with a modular monolith; extract services only when there is a proven scalability or team-boundary need.

Using architecture astronaut patterns — implementing hexagonal architecture with 7 layers of interfaces before writing a single business function. The abstractions solve problems that do not exist yet and make the code impossible to navigate. Architecture should be refactored toward, not predicted upfront.

Letting accidental architecture become permanent — the directory structure chosen in week 1 (pkg/, internal/, cmd/) becomes the team's mental model. When the service needs to split, the team is reluctant to restructure because 'that's how we've always done it.' Architecture must be treated as malleable: the right structure for 1K LOC is wrong for 100K LOC.
execution_timeline: Team starts a new Go service: one repo, one main.go, flat package structure
→ 1K LOC: everything is in main.go, easy to navigate, no package boundaries needed
→ 5K LOC: files are split by layer — handlers.go, service.go, store.go — still navigable
→ 20K LOC: handlers.go is 3000 lines, service.go has implicit dependencies, tests take 30s to compile
→ 50K LOC: circular import errors appear because domain types are mixed with storage types
→ Team realizes the architecture is fighting them: every new feature requires changing 5 files in 3 packages
→ Team does architecture refactor: extracts domain package, defines explicit interfaces at package boundaries, uses dependency injection
→ 100K LOC: architecture supports the team — new features add 2-3 files, tests compile in 5s, each package has a clear responsibility
→ Business grows: team splits into two squads, extracts one domain into a separate service
→ Architecture evolves again: service boundary is the package boundary that was already defined in the monolith
failure_modes: Over-engineering at the start — the team spends 3 weeks setting up the 'perfect' architecture (DDD aggregates, CQRS, event sourcing, 5 layers of interfaces) before writing any business logic. By week 4, they have no working product and the architecture assumptions are wrong. Fix: start with the simplest structure that could work; refactor when the structure fights you.

No architecture at all — the team adds files to main.go for 6 months. By month 7, the main package has 50 files with no internal boundaries. Any change breaks 3 unrelated features. Developers are afraid to touch code. Fix: pay down architecture debt incrementally — extract one package per sprint until the structure is navigable.

Architecture by fiat — a senior engineer designs the architecture in isolation and mandates it via a document. The team follows it mechanically but does not understand the tradeoffs. When a new requirement does not fit the architecture, they add hacks instead of evolving it. Fix: architecture decisions must be documented with alternatives considered and tradeoffs explained (ADR format).
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain why a flat package structure works at 1K LOC but fails at 100K LOC — the cost of navigating and understanding 100 files in a single namespace grows super-linearly with the number of files.
Learner must identify which of two given directory structures is better for a given scenario (e.g., a billing service vs. a real-time chat service) and explain the tradeoff.
Learner must take a given 5-package Go project with circular import errors and restructure it to eliminate the cycles using interface extraction or package regrouping.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go architected projects use a package structure that mirrors the domain, not the layers. The convention is: cmd/ (entry points), internal/ (private packages), pkg/ (shared library code). Within internal/, structure by domain boundary (e.g., internal/user/, internal/billing/, internal/orders/) rather than by layer (internal/handlers/, internal/services/, internal/repos/). Each domain package owns its types, business logic, and storage interface. Cross-domain communication uses explicit interfaces defined in the consuming package. The standard library's net/http, database/sql, and encoding/json are the architecture primitives — no framework is required.
zero_magic.mental_model: Architecture is not a blueprint drawn before construction. It is the set of decisions that are expensive to change. A good architecture makes the common case easy and the rare case possible. The right architecture for a startup's 3-person MVP is a single main.go file. The right architecture for a 50-engineer org is domain-aligned packages with explicit interfaces. Architecture evolves with the team size, codebase size, and business complexity. The skill is knowing WHEN to add structure, not knowing how to draw the perfect diagram.
performance_implications: Package boundary crossings in Go incur zero runtime cost — calling a function in another package is the same cost as calling one in the same package. The cost is in cognitive load: the developer must understand both packages and the contract between them.

Interface dispatch costs one pointer indirection (~1ns). This is negligible for business logic but matters in hot loops (e.g., calling a storage interface for every row in a 1M-row batch). Prefer concrete types for performance-critical paths.

Dependency injection at startup (constructing all dependencies in main.go and passing them to NewXxx constructors) adds ~10ms per 100 dependencies. This is acceptable for production but can slow test suites — use lazy initialization or build tags for test-specific wiring.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers start every project with either no structure (everything in main.go) or over-engineering (5 layers of abstraction for a CRUD service). Both fail at scale. This lesson teaches the third path: start simple, evolve the architecture as the codebase grows, and know the signs that it is time to add structure. The learner practices recognizing these signs and making incremental architecture improvements.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner is given a Go project at 3 stages of growth (1K LOC flat, 20K LOC with implicit dependencies, 100K LOC with circular imports). For each stage, they must: (1) identify 3 architecture smells, (2) propose a refactoring plan that can be done incrementally (not a rewrite), and (3) explain the tradeoff (what is easier after the refactor, what is harder).
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Go projects that neglect architecture become unmaintainable at ~50K LOC (the 'Go monolith wall'). Projects like Kubernetes (2M+ LOC Go), Docker, and CockroachDB all went through major architecture refactors as they grew. The pattern is consistent: start with a flat structure, extract domain packages when circular imports appear, extract services when team boundaries form. Architecture in production Go is a continuous evolution, not a one-time design.
step_by_step_execution: Start with one main.go file — at <1K LOC, flat structure is the fastest to navigate
When files exceed 1000 lines each: split by layer (handlers.go, service.go, store.go) — at 5K LOC, layer-based structure reduces file navigation time
When circular imports appear: extract a shared domain package with only types and interfaces — at 20K LOC, domain types must be independent of implementation
When test compilation exceeds 10s: organize packages so tests only depend on their own package — isolate test dependencies per package
When two teams work on the same repo: extract domain boundaries into separate internal packages with explicit API contracts — team A owns internal/billing, team B owns internal/orders
When a team outgrows the monorepo: extract one domain package into a separate service — the existing package boundary becomes the service boundary
Document each architecture decision as an ADR (Architecture Decision Record) — why this structure was chosen, what alternatives were considered, and when to revisit
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's package system is the architecture primitive. A package is a unit of compilation, import, and name visibility. Exported names (capitalized) form the package API. Unexported names are internal. This visibility boundary is the cheapest and most effective architecture tool — it forces a clear API surface. Imports create a directed acyclic graph of dependencies. If package A depends on package B, and B depends on A, the Go compiler rejects it. This constraint prevents the worst kind of coupling (circular) at compile time. The go tool's cycle detection runs on every build, making circular imports a compile-time error rather than a runtime surprise.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Architecture exists because code naturally decays into a Big Ball of Mud without intentional structure. Every file added to main.go increases the cost of every future change. Without architecture, the change cost grows O(n²) with codebase size. With architecture (packages with clear boundaries and explicit dependencies), the change cost grows O(n) — each change affects only one package. Architecture is the difference between a codebase that slows down forever and one that can scale to millions of lines.

items.title: Package boundaries
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Package boundaries in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Putting every type in one package — a single models/ or types/ package with 200 exported types has no internal cohesion. Every file imports models, creating coupling across the entire codebase. Any change to any type requires recompiling every dependent package.

Making packages too small — a package with one 5-line type and one 3-line function adds import overhead without any benefit. The cost of understanding a package boundary (what is exported, what is internal, what does the package name mean) must be justified by the cohesion within the package.

Circular imports between related packages — users.go imports orders.go and orders.go imports users.go. The Go compiler rejects this, forcing the developer to either merge the packages or extract shared types into a third package. The circular import is a signal that the boundary is wrong.
execution_timeline: Team creates a flat package: everything in package main with 30 files
→ File count grows: main package has 50 files, 15 exported types, no internal structure
→ Developer needs to add a feature: searches 50 files to find where user creation logic lives → 10 minutes of navigation
→ Team extracts internal/user/ package: User type, CreateUser function, validation logic in one place
→ internal/user/ package has 5 exported symbols and 12 unexported helpers → clear API surface
→ internal/user/ is imported by internal/http/ and internal/billing/ → dependency graph is visible in import statements
→ Circular import hits: internal/order/ needs User type from internal/user/, internal/user/ needs Order type for a method → compiler rejects it
→ Team extracts shared types to internal/user/types/ and internal/order/types/ → eliminates the cycle
→ Team documents package dependency diagram: each package is a box, imports are arrows → no cycles
→ New developer joins: reads package dependency diagram, understands architecture in 10 minutes
failure_modes: Utility package becomes a dumping ground — internal/util/ starts with 2 helper functions and grows to 50 unrelated functions (string utilities, date formatting, HTTP helpers, DB utilities). Every package imports util/, creating universal coupling. Fix: split util/ by domain — internal/stringutil/, internal/timeutil/, internal/httputil/, internal/db/.

Internal package exports too much — an internal/ package exports 50 types and 30 functions but only 3 are used outside the package. The exported surface creates a maintenance burden: every exported symbol is part of the API contract. Fix: make everything unexported by default; only export what is needed by other packages.

Vendored package boundary mismatch — the team copies a third-party library into internal/vendor/ and modifies it. The modified version has different behavior than the original, causing subtle bugs when team members assume standard behavior. Fix: fork the library into a separate repo with documentation of the changes.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify which package boundary is wrong given a circular import error trace (e.g., package a imports b imports c imports a) and propose the correct extraction (extract shared types, merge packages, or move the import to a lower-level package).
Learner must evaluate a given package's exported surface (15 exported types, 40 exported functions) and identify which symbols should be unexported, with justification for each.
Learner must explain why a package named internal/helpers/ is an architecture smell — the name implies no cohesion, which means the package will grow without limit and create universal coupling.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go packages are the fundamental unit of code organization. A package is a directory with Go files sharing the same package declaration. Exported names (starting with capital letter) form the public API. Unexported names are accessible within the package only. The go tool enforces acyclic imports: if package A imports B and B imports A, compilation fails. This constraint is deliberate — it forces packages to form a DAG (directed acyclic graph), which is the foundation of maintainable architecture. The internal/ convention (introduced in Go 1.4) restricts imports: packages under internal/ can only be imported by code rooted at the internal/ parent. This enforces module boundaries in mono-repos.
zero_magic.mental_model: A package boundary is a contract wall. Inside the wall, code can access everything (exported and unexported). Outside the wall, only the officially exported doorways (exported functions, types, constants) are accessible. The wall has a cost: every time a developer needs to cross it, they must use the official doorway. The benefit: the code inside the wall can be refactored, renamed, or rewritten as long as the doorways stay the same. A good package boundary is one where the cost of crossing is justified by the freedom it gives the code inside.
performance_implications: Package import overhead is paid at compile time only — importing a package adds ~1ms to compilation per 1000 packages. Runtime performance is unaffected.
Interface boundary crossing (calling a method through an interface) costs ~1ns per call. For a typical handler calling a service calling a repository, the total cost is ~3ns — negligible.
Type assertion at package boundaries (casting interface{} to concrete type) costs ~1ns. Using interface{} in exported APIs is discouraged not for performance but for type safety.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers either throw everything into one main package or create 50 tiny packages with no cohesion. Both approaches make the codebase impossible to navigate at scale. This lesson teaches the sweet spot: packages should be cohesive (all contents relate to a single domain) and have a small exported surface (the minimum needed by other packages). The learner practices identifying good vs. bad package boundaries and refactoring packages with wrong boundaries.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner is given a Go project with 5 packages and circular import errors. They must: (1) draw the package dependency graph, (2) identify the cycle, (3) propose a fix (extract shared types, merge packages, or split the interface), (4) implement the fix, and (5) verify that all imports are acyclic. They must also document the package boundaries with a short comment explaining each package's responsibility.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Package boundaries are the most important architectural decision in Go projects. Kubernetes has 300+ packages organized by domain (pkg/api/, pkg/kubelet/, pkg/scheduler/). Docker organizes by component (daemon/, builder/, cli/). CockroachDB organizes by SQL layer (sql/, sql/parser/, sql/exec/). Every major Go project uses packages as the primary architecture primitive. The pattern is consistent: one domain per package, small exported surface, acyclic imports enforced by the compiler.
step_by_step_execution: Identify the domain boundaries in your project: user management, billing, orders, notifications
Create one package per domain: internal/user/, internal/billing/, internal/orders/, internal/notify/
Move all types, functions, and constants related to each domain into its package
Export only what other packages need: functions, types, and constants that are called from outside the package
Run go build — if it succeeds, imports are acyclic. If it fails with import cycle, restructure to eliminate the cycle
Document the package dependency diagram in the project README — each package as a box, imports as arrows
Add an internal/ package for code that should not be imported from outside the project root
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's import resolution works as follows: the compiler resolves each import path to a directory, reads all .go files in that directory with the same package declaration, and builds a symbol table of exported names. Import cycles are detected during dependency graph construction: the compiler walks the import graph depth-first, marking each package as 'visiting.' If it encounters a package that is already 'visiting,' it reports an import cycle and stops. This check runs once per build and is the fastest possible cycle detection (O(V+E) where V is packages and E is imports). The internal/ convention is enforced by go/build: during import resolution, the tool checks whether the importing package's root is an ancestor of the internal/ package's directory. If not, the import is rejected with a compile error.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Package boundaries exist because code without boundaries becomes a Ball of Mud. When every file can access every symbol, there is no encapsulation, no API surface, and no refactoring safety. A developer cannot tell whether renaming a function will break 1 file or 50. Package boundaries create intentional contracts: the exported API is the 'yes, you may use this' surface; everything else is 'internal, may change without notice.' Without package boundaries, large codebases become unmaintainable because the cost of understanding any single change requires reading the entire codebase.

items.title: Service layer
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Service layer in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Putting business logic in HTTP handlers — the handler parses the request, calls the database, formats the response, and handles errors. When the same logic is needed for a gRPC endpoint or a background job, the handler code cannot be reused. Business logic must live in the service layer, not the transport layer.

Making the service layer a thin pass-through — the handler calls service.CreateUser which calls repo.InsertUser with no transformation, validation, or business logic. The service layer adds complexity without value. A service layer must justify its existence by containing business rules, not just forwarding calls.

Letting services become god objects — internal/service/user.go has 3000 lines with 50 methods covering user CRUD, billing, notifications, and reporting. The service layer should be split by bounded context, not by entity. User billing is a different service than user profile management.
execution_timeline: Handler calls service.CreateUser(ctx, req) — the handler only parses HTTP, delegates to service
→ Service.CreateUser validates business rules: email is unique, password meets strength requirements, user is not on blocklist
→ Service calls repo.InsertUser(ctx, user) — the service abstracts the storage implementation
→ Repo returns created user — the handler formats the HTTP response with the user data
→ A new requirement: send welcome email after user creation
→ Developer adds service.SendWelcomeEmail(ctx, user) call in CreateUser — one line change in the service layer
→ The handler is unchanged — it still just calls service.CreateUser and formats the response
→ A second consumer arrives: an admin bulk-import endpoint that creates 1000 users from a CSV file
→ Admin handler calls service.CreateUser in a loop — the same business logic, no code duplication
→ The service layer proves its value: business logic is written once, used by every transport
failure_modes: Service layer depends on transport types — service.CreateUser takes http.Request as a parameter. Now the same service cannot be called from a gRPC handler or a CLI command. Fix: service methods must take only domain types (context, structs, primitives). Transport-specific types (http.Request, proto.Message) must be converted in the handler.

Service layer leaks database concerns — CreateUser returns *sql.Rows or raw database errors. The caller must understand SQL to use the service. Fix: the service layer returns domain types and domain errors. Database errors are mapped to domain errors (ErrNotFound, ErrConflict, ErrInternal) inside the repository.

Service layer has no transaction boundary — CreateUser inserts a user AND sends a welcome email, but if the email fails, the user is already inserted. The operation is not atomic. Fix: the service layer should own the transaction boundary — begin a transaction, call repositories, commit or rollback.
hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify which code belongs in the handler vs. the service layer given a function that parses HTTP parameters, validates business rules, calls the database, and formats the response — the HTTP parsing and response formatting belong in the handler; validation and database calls belong in the service.

Learner must refactor a god-object service (50 methods across 3 domains) into separate services by bounded context — extracting UserService, BillingService, and NotificationService from a single UserService.

Learner must explain why an interface defined in the service package (type UserRepository interface { ... }) is better than importing the concrete repository type — the interface in the consumer package is the 'need to know' contract; the concrete implementation can be swapped without changing the consumer.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: The service layer in Go is a package (e.g., internal/service/) that contains business logic methods. Each method takes a context.Context and domain types, performs business rules, coordinates calls to repositories/external services, and returns domain types or domain errors. The service layer does NOT import HTTP or gRPC packages. It defines its own interfaces for dependencies (e.g., UserRepository interface with Insert, GetByID, Update methods). This interface-in-the-consumer pattern is idiomatic Go: the service defines what it needs, and the concrete implementation satisfies the interface.
zero_magic.mental_model: The service layer is the brain of the application. The handler is the mouth (talks to the outside world). The repository is the hands (talks to the database). The brain decides what to do (business rules), the mouth receives input and delivers output, and the hands execute the storage. The brain should not do the hands' job (SQL queries) or the mouth's job (HTTP parsing). The brain should be testable without the mouth or hands — inject mock dependencies and test the business logic in isolation.
performance_implications: Service layer interface dispatch costs ~1ns per method call — negligible for business logic. If a hot path calls a service method 100K times/second, the overhead is 100µs — still negligible.

Service method allocations (creating result structs, error wrapping) are the main cost, not the layer itself. A service method that allocates 5 intermediate values costs ~200ns in GC overhead. This is acceptable for most services but matters for latency-critical paths (<1ms).
Dependency injection in the service layer (passing interfaces in constructor) costs one allocation per service instance at startup — negligible.
Service methods that create transactions add ~100µs per transaction (db.Begin overhead) — use batch operations within a single transaction instead of per-row transactions.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers put all logic in HTTP handlers because it is the easiest place to start. When a second consumer (gRPC, CLI, background job) needs the same logic, they copy the code — creating a maintenance nightmare. The service layer centralizes business logic in one place, making it reusable across all transport layers and testable without HTTP or database dependencies.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner is given a Go HTTP handler with 200 lines of inline business logic (validation, business rules, database calls, email sending). They must: (1) extract the business logic into a service layer, (2) define interfaces for dependencies (repository, email sender), (3) write unit tests for the service with mock dependencies, (4) verify the handler still works end-to-end with the refactored code.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: The service layer pattern is universal in production Go services. Stripe's API handlers call the service layer for every operation. Kubernetes' API server has a service layer between the HTTP handler and the storage backend. Docker's engine API uses a service layer for image and container operations. The pattern is: handler → service → repository (or external API). The service layer is the only layer that contains business logic — handlers and repositories are mechanical.
step_by_step_execution: Identify business logic in the HTTP handler: validation, business rules, calculations, error handling
Create a service package: internal/service/
Define service interface: type UserService interface { CreateUser(ctx, req) error }
Implement service: type userService struct { repo UserRepository } with business logic methods
Define repository interface in the service package: type UserRepository interface { Insert(ctx, user) error }
Implement repository in a separate package: internal/repo/postgres/
Wire dependencies in main.go: repo := postgres.NewUserRepo(db); svc := service.NewUserService(repo); handler := httpapi.NewUserHandler(svc)
Handler calls svc.CreateUser — no business logic in the handler
Test the service: create a mock repository and test business rules in isolation
Add a second consumer (CLI command) — same svc.CreateUser call, zero code duplication
zero_magic.under_the_hood: The service layer in Go is enabled by two language features: interfaces and package visibility. Interfaces allow the service to define its dependencies abstractly (type UserRepository interface { Insert(ctx, User) error }). The concrete type satisfaction is implicit — any type with an Insert method with the right signature satisfies the interface. Package visibility (exported/unexported) enforces the layer boundary: the service package exports only business types and interfaces; implementation details are unexported. The `internal/` directory convention prevents external consumers from importing implementation packages directly. The service layer does not depend on any specific transport or storage — it is a pure Go package that could be used in any context.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: The service layer exists because business logic is the most valuable and most fragile part of a codebase. It contains the rules that make money, prevent fraud, and ensure compliance. It should not be mixed with mechanical code (HTTP parsing, SQL queries, email formatting) that changes for different reasons and at different speeds. The service layer isolates business logic so it can be: (1) tested thoroughly, (2) reused across transports, and (3) changed without affecting other layers.
