items.title: Logs
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Logs in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Logging to files instead of stdout: Beginners write logs to /var/log/app.log, violating the 12-factor app principle. In Docker, container stdout/stderr is captured by the runtime; writing to files fills the container layer and breaks log aggregation.

Using log.Println for everything: New Go devs use log.Println at every execution point without log levels. Production systems need DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR, FATAL levels so operators can filter noise and route errors to alerting pipelines.

Calling log.Fatal in library code: Learners call log.Fatal(err) inside packages, which calls os.Exit(1) and prevents deferred cleanup, closes the process without HTTP 500 responses, and stops the entire container instead of returning an error up the call chain.

Ignoring structured logging: Writing fmt.Printf("User %s logged in", user) instead of slog.Info("user login", "user_id", id, "ip", remoteAddr). Unstructured logs cannot be parsed by log aggregators (Loki, Elastic, Datadog) for search, dashboards, or alert rules.

Logging sensitive data: Learners log request bodies, auth tokens, or SQL queries verbatim. Production pipelines ship logs off-cluster; secrets in logs become compliance violations (PCI, HIPAA, SOC2) and credential leaks.
execution_timeline: 📝 Developer writes structured log call with slog.Info() or logrus.WithField()
⚙️ Go runtime formats the log entry as JSON with timestamp, level, message, and key-value pairs
📤 Log line is written to os.Stdout (or os.Stderr for errors) via the io.Writer interface
🐳 Docker container runtime captures stdout/stderr stream from the process file descriptors
📡 Container runtime forwards the stream to the configured logging driver (json-file, journald, fluentd, awslogs, gelf)
🗄️ Log aggregator (Loki, Elasticsearch, Datadog) ingests the stream, indexes structured fields
🔍 Operator queries logs via LogQL, KQL, or dashboard with filters on level, service, trace_id

failure_modes.scenario: Missing log rotation in file-based logging
failure_modes.cause: App writes to /var/log/app.log without log rotation configured in the app or via logrotate
failure_modes.fix: Always log to stdout in containerized environments. If file logging is unavoidable, use lumberjack or configure logrotate with copytruncate.

failure_modes.scenario: Synchronous log writes block the request goroutine
failure_modes.cause: log.Println() writes synchronously to disk or network; under high throughput this blocks the calling goroutine and increases p99 latency
failure_modes.fix: Use async log writers (logrus.TextFormatter with a buffered writer) or slog's HandlerOptions with a custom async writer. Set log sampling in high-volume paths.

failure_modes.scenario: Panic in deferred log write silences the crash reason
failure_modes.cause: A deferred recover() calls log.Printf but the logger's io.Writer is closed or broken; the Write fails silently and the panic reason is lost
failure_modes.fix: Use slog with a fallback writer. In critical paths, flush logs to stderr with a direct os.Stderr.Write() before os.Exit().

failure_modes.scenario: Log aggregation drops entries due to rate limiting
failure_modes.cause: Aggregator (e.g., Loki) is configured with a per-stream rate limit; a high-traffic service exceeds it and logs are silently dropped
failure_modes.fix: Implement client-side log sampling with slog.HandlerOptions.Level, rate-limit per second with a token bucket, or use a local buffer with backpressure.

failure_modes.scenario: Structured log key collisions in distributed tracing
failure_modes.cause: Multiple services log trace_id under different keys (traceId, trace_id, TraceID); the aggregator treats them as separate fields and cross-service correlation breaks
failure_modes.fix: Adopt a unified logging schema (e.g., OTEL semantic conventions). Use a shared slog.Handler wrapper that enforces canonical key naming.

hidden_magic_checks: When you call log.Fatal() in an HTTP handler, what happens to the response written by a deferred func? (Answer: os.Exit(1) immediately terminates the process; deferred funcs do NOT run after os.Exit.)

Why does Docker capture stdout/stderr but not files written by the application? (Answer: Docker attaches to the container process's stdout/stderr file descriptors. File writes bypass these descriptors unless the app is configured to log to /dev/stdout or /proc/self/fd/1.)

What's the difference between log.Println and slog.Info for a log aggregator consuming the output? (Answer: log.Println emits unstructured text; the aggregator cannot parse fields. slog.Info emits structured JSON; the aggregator indexes key-value pairs for filtering and dashboards.)

If you call log.SetOutput(os.Stderr) but your Dockerfile only captures stdout via CMD ["go", "run", "."], where do the logs go? (Answer: They go to stderr (fd 2). If the Docker CMD or docker logs command only follows fd 1, stderr output is discarded. Always capture both streams or set the logger to os.Stdout.)
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's standard library provides log/slog (structured logging, Go 1.21+) as the idiomatic logger. slog uses a Handler interface that decouples formatting (JSONHandler, TextHandler) from output (io.Writer). The legacy log package writes unstructured text to os.Stderr by default. In production, teams layer on logrus, zerolog, or zap for leveled logging, field chaining, and sampling — but slog's Handler API allows wrapping these as slog backends without changing call sites.
zero_magic.mental_model: Logs are the black box flight recorder for your service. Just as pilots review flight data after an incident, operators query logs to reconstruct request flows, measure latency, and correlate events across services. Each log line is a timestamped sensor reading: structured fields (altitude, speed, heading) let you filter for anomalies; unstructured chatter ("flaps deployed") is noise. The aggregation pipeline is the investigation board — if your sensors don't speak a common format (JSON), the board is useless.
performance_implications: Allocation overhead: slog.Info with 5 key-value pairs allocates ~300 bytes per call. In a 10k req/s service, that is ~3 MB/s in allocations. Use slog.LogAttrs with slog.Attr values to reduce allocs in hot paths.

Blocking writes: log.Println calls os.Stderr.Write synchronously. Under 10k lines/s, the syscall overhead adds ~2ms per write, blocking the caller. Use slog with a buffered or async Handler to batch writes.

JSON serialization: Encoding a structured log entry costs ~500ns on average. At high throughput (100k lines/s), this adds 50ms/s of CPU. Pre-allocate log buffers or use zerolog's zero-allocation JSON encoder.

Sampling overhead: Sampling every 10th log line saves I/O but adds a rand() call per event. Use deterministic hash-based sampling (sample on trace_id) to avoid the rand() syscall and keep correlated traces intact.

Docker log driver throughput: Docker's json-file driver writes each log line as a JSON object to a file. At high volume, this causes disk I/O contention. Switch to the journald driver (binary, compressed) or fluentd (network) to offload disk pressure.
zero_magic.problem_solved: New Go developers coming from scripting backgrounds (Python, Node.js) use print-debugging (fmt.Println everywhere) and have no mental model for production observability. This lesson bridges that gap by teaching structured logging with log/slog, the 12-factor stdout convention, log aggregation pipelines, and production patterns (levels, sampling, panic/fatal hygiene). The learner stops writing ad-hoc print statements and starts shipping queryable, operational logs.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Given a minimal HTTP server, the learner must: (1) replace all fmt.Println calls with slog structured logging at appropriate levels (DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR), (2) configure the logger to output JSON to stdout with a custom timestamp format, (3) add a middleware that logs request method, path, duration, and status code, (4) demonstrate that log.Fatal is NOT used in the handler (return errors instead), and (5) verify the JSON output can be piped to jq for field extraction.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go services at Uber, Datadog, and Grafana Labs use structured logging with slog or zerolog. Kubernetes-native Go apps write JSON logs to stdout; these are collected by fluentd or vector, shipped to Loki or Elasticsearch, and queried via LogQL or Kibana. Common patterns: request-scoped loggers with context.Context (slog.With), trace_id propagation for distributed tracing correlation, sampling in high-volume endpoints (health checks, metrics), and panic handlers that log the stack trace before os.Exit(1).
step_by_step_execution: 1. Create a new directory 14-docker-cicd-linux-deployment/03-logs/
2. Initialize the module: go mod init github.com/the-go-engineer/14-docker-cicd-linux-deployment/03-logs
3. Write main.go: create a slog logger with slog.New(slog.NewJSONHandler(os.Stdout, nil))
4. Add log calls at multiple levels: slog.Debug("starting up"), slog.Info("server listening", "port", 8080), slog.Warn("high memory usage", "pct", memPct), slog.Error("request failed", "err", err)
5. Write an HTTP handler that logs request details with slog.Group and slog.Attr
6. Write main_test.go: start the server, send test requests, capture stdout, and assert JSON log lines contain expected fields
7. Run: go run . and verify JSON output with curl and jq
8. Run tests: go test -v ./...
zero_magic.under_the_hood: slog.NewJSONHandler(os.Stdout, &slog.HandlerOptions{Level: slog.LevelInfo}) returns a handler that encodes each log entry as a JSON object. The handler's Handle() method is called for every enabled log event; it calls encoder.Encode() which writes to the io.Writer. Under the hood, sync.Pool reuses encoder buffers to reduce allocations. The Level type is an int; HandlerOptions.Level is a Leveler interface that can be a dynamic atomic.Int64 for live log level changes. When slog.Info() is called, the logger acquires the handler's mutex, calls Handle(), and returns — this is why synchronous I/O blocks the caller. For async behavior, wrap the handler in a custom writer that sends entries to a channel consumed by a background goroutine.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Logs exist because production software runs without a debugger attached. Print-debugging works in development but fails in distributed, ephemeral, containerized environments where you cannot ssh into a pod and attach delve. Structured logs are the agreed-upon interface between running services and operational tooling (aggregators, alert managers, dashboards). Go's log/slog exists because the legacy log package (from 2009) assumed human-readability; modern infrastructure demands machine-parseable, schema-adherent output that tools can consume without fragile regex parsing.

items.title: File permissions
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply File permissions in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using chmod 0777 as a default to make things work, which bypasses all permission controls and creates security vulnerabilities in production.
Confusing rwx (read/write/execute) octal notation — writing chmod 644 instead of chmod 0644 for a script file, forgetting the execute bit, or using 0777 for directories thinking it mirrors files.
Running containers as root without setting USER in the Dockerfile, causing all created files and logs to be owned by root and inaccessible to non-root CI/CD or host processes.
Assuming os.WriteFile defaults to a secure permission — it uses 0666 (before umask) by default unless you explicitly pass os.FileMode(0644), which means secrets or config files can be world-readable.
Forgetting that umask subtracts from the mode passed to os.OpenFile or os.WriteFile, then wondering why the resulting file has different permissions than the octal value they passed.
execution_timeline: 1️⃣ Check current file permissions with os.Stat
2️⃣ Select appropriate octal mode (0644 for files, 0755 for directories/scripts)
3️⃣ Open or create file with os.OpenFile specifying the FileMode
4️⃣ After write, verify with os.Lstat
5️⃣ If running in Docker, add USER appuser and chown artifacts
6️⃣ Use umask to strip group/world bits from newly created files
7️⃣ Validate no world-writable files exist in production image

failure_modes.cause: Used os.WriteFile with 0777 or umask was set too permissive
failure_modes.fix: Use os.FileMode(0600) for secrets and verify umask is 0022 or stricter; audit with a CI check that rejects files with group/world write

failure_modes.failure: Container runs as root, log files owned by uid 0
failure_modes.cause: No USER directive in Dockerfile; default is root
failure_modes.fix: Add USER 1000 in Dockerfile; chown -R the working directory to uid 1000 before switching; use os.Chown on created files

failure_modes.failure: Executable script copied to container loses execute bit
failure_modes.cause: COPY preserves permissions from build context; git may not track execute bits on Windows
failure_modes.fix: Explicitly os.Chmod the file to 0755 in the Dockerfile RUN layer or in Go init(); ensure .gitattributes defines * text=auto eol=lf

failure_modes.failure: Setuid/setgid bit left on an executable that should not have it
failure_modes.cause: Accidentally preserved setuid from a tarball extraction — for example, chmod 4755 was used
failure_modes.fix: Use os.FileMode(0755) without the setuid flag; run chmod u-s in Dockerfile or os.Chmod(path, 0755) in Go; add a security scanner step

hidden_magic_checks.question: If umask is 0027 and you call os.OpenFile("data", os.O_RDWR|os.O_CREATE, 0640), what are the resulting file permissions?
hidden_magic_checks.answer: The umask subtracts group-w and all others: 0640 &^ 0027 = 0640 becomes 0640 (rw-r-----). Group-w is already absent in 0640 so umask has no visible effect here.

hidden_magic_checks.question: Why does os.WriteFile("key.pem", data, 0644) expose the private key to every user on the system?
hidden_magic_checks.answer: 0644 is world-readable. Private keys must use 0600 (owner-read-write only). Never use 0644 or above for secrets.

hidden_magic_checks.question: What happens when a Docker container is started with --read-only root filesystem but the application writes logs to /var/log/app?
hidden_magic_checks.answer: The write fails with read-only file system error because the root filesystem is immutable. Fix: mount a tmpfs or volume at /var/log/app, or write to a directory mounted as writable.

hidden_magic_checks.question: When does chmod 4777 on a Go binary inside /usr/local/bin cause a security vulnerability?
hidden_magic_checks.answer: The setuid bit (4xxx) makes the binary run with the owner's privileges. If root owns it, any user executing it gains root-level access — a classic privilege-escalation vector. Never set setuid on custom binaries unless you have a verified, security-reviewed reason.

zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's os package models file permissions with the os.FileMode type (a uint32 wrapping Unix permission bits). os.Stat returns FileMode; os.OpenFile accepts it as the perm argument; os.Chmod sets it. The fs.FileMode interface allows testing with .IsDir(), .Perm(), .IsRegular(). Go's os.WriteFile defaults to 0666 (before umask), which is rarely what you want — always pass an explicit FileMode. The os.FileMode bit layout mirrors the Unix model: lower 9 bits for owner/group/other rwx, bits 11-12 for setuid/setgid, bit 10 for sticky. Go's os.Stat, os.Lstat, os.Chmod, os.Chown, and os.Chtimes make permission inspection and manipulation idiomatic from Go without shelling out to chmod or chown. The standard library's filepath.Walk and fs.WalkDir can filter by permission bits to find world-writable files or verify ownership. In tests, you set up temp directories with t.TempDir() (which creates with 0700) and use os.Chmod to simulate restricted environments.
zero_magic.mental_model: Each ring has a three-bit mask (rwx). Octal notation encodes all three rings in one number: owner-group-other. The sticky bit on /tmp means only the owner of a file can delete it. Setuid means run this with the file owner's privileges regardless of who executes it. Go's FileMode is the language's direct port of this Unix model — when you see os.FileMode(0644), read it as owner writes and reads, everyone else only reads.
performance_implications: Each os.Stat or os.Lstat call involves a syscall to the kernel; batching permission checks (for example, walk once with fs.WalkDir instead of repeated Stat calls) reduces syscall overhead in hot paths.
os.Chmod on a frequently-written log file causes a metadata write to the filesystem journal; if done per-log-line, it kills throughput — set permissions once on creation.
Docker images with many os.Chown or os.Chmod layers increase image size because each RUN chown creates a new layer; combine ownership changes into a single layer.
Overly restrictive permissions (for example, 0400) on a database file cause open/read errors at runtime that are visible only under load when files are first accessed.
Using os.TempDir (creates with 0700) is safer but slower if used per-operation because it makes a stat + mkdir syscall each time; reuse a single temp directory with unique file names instead.
zero_magic.problem_solved: File permissions solve the problem of access control in multi-tenant operating systems and container environments: they prevent one user or process from reading, modifying, or executing files owned by another. Without permissions, any process could read SSH private keys, overwrite binaries, or delete critical system files. In Go, explicit permission handling prevents production incidents where misconfigured file modes lead to data leaks, privilege escalation, or silent deployment failures.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Write a Go program that creates a directory ~/secure-workspace with 0700 permissions, writes a config.json file inside it with 0600, then attempts to read it from a child process that runs as a different (non-existent) UID and handles the resulting permission-denied error gracefully. Explain why tmpdirs in Go default to 0700 and how umask interacts with os.OpenFile.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Go production services use os.FileMode extensively: HTTP servers restrict TLS key files to 0600, CI/CD pipelines enforce permission audits in pull requests, Kubernetes init containers chown volumes to non-root UIDs, logging libraries open log files with 0644 (or 0600 for audit logs), and os.Stat checks gate access to config directories in multi-tenant API servers. The Go linter gosec (G301/G302/G306) flags missing or overly-permissive FileMode arguments in os.WriteFile, os.OpenFile, and os.Chmod calls — preventing 0777 or 0644 on secrets before they reach production.
step_by_step_execution: Step 1: Run go run main.go with a file that creates a secret file using os.WriteFile and explicitly pass os.FileMode(0600) for the key file and os.FileMode(0644) for a non-sensitive config file.
Step 2: Inspect the created file permissions with ls -la on Linux or Get-ChildItem on Windows (Go abstracts underlying permissions but the interface stays the same).
Step 3: Use os.Stat inside the program and call .Mode().Perm() to retrieve the numeric permission bits; print them as octal with fmt.Printf("%o", mode.Perm()).

Step 4: Run the same program inside a Docker container (FROM golang:1.22-alpine) built with USER 1000 and observe that os.WriteFile still works correctly because permissions are relative to the UID, not the container boundary.

Step 5: Add os.Chmod to reduce permissions of an already-written config file from 0644 to 0600 after a config value validation; verify the change with os.Lstat (note: Lstat does not follow symlinks — important for /proc or /etc hosts).

Step 6: Apply umask by calling syscall.Umask(0027) at startup and verify that os.OpenFile with 0640 produces a file with permissions 0640 (because group-w is already absent) but os.OpenFile with 0666 produces 0640 (group-w is stripped).

Step 7: Test setuid behavior by creating a small Go binary that prints os.Getuid(), building it with os.FileMode(0755), setting setuid via os.Chmod(path, os.FileMode(04555)), and observing that the kernel ignores setuid on Go binaries with the nosuid mount option or in Docker by default.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Go's os.FileMode is a uint32 where the lower 12 bits mirror the Unix permission bitmap: bits 0-2 are other rwx, bits 3-5 are group rwx, bits 6-8 are owner rwx, bit 9 is sticky (S_ISVTX), bit 10 is setgid (S_ISGID), bit 11 is setuid (S_ISUID). The os.Stat syscall on Linux fills a syscall.Stat_t struct whose Mode field is uint32 — Go casts it to os.FileMode. On Windows, os.FileMode still exists but the permission bits (owner/group/other) are largely ignored; Go maps the Windows file attributes to a simplified mode where regular files get 0666 and directories get 0777. The os.Chmod on Windows translates to SetFileAttributes. Docker layers each add their own metadata overlay: when you RUN chmod in a Dockerfile, the overlay filesystem's metadata layer records the new mode without rewriting the underlying file data. When os.Chmod is called inside a running container, it operates on the overlay's copy-on-write layer. Understanding that umask is a kernel-level process attribute (set per-process with syscall.Umask) explains why two Go programs in the same container can see different effective permissions — umask is inherited across fork/exec.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: File permissions exist because Unix was designed as a multi-user system from the ground up (1970s Bell Labs). They provide the simplest enforceable access control model that scales from a single developer laptop to a 10,000-node Kubernetes cluster. Without permissions, any process — or any compromised dependency — could read, modify, or delete any file on the system. In the Go ecosystem, explicit FileMode handling is the last line of defense against accidental data exposure: a missing or overly-permissive FileMode in os.WriteFile is how private keys, database passwords, and TLS certificates end up world-readable in production.

items.title: Docker basics
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Docker basics in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Using `ubuntu:latest` as the base image instead of `golang:alpine` or `scratch`, producing a 800 MB+ image for a 15 MB Go binary.
Running `go build` inside the container without `CGO_ENABLED=0` and `-ldflags="-s -w"`, resulting in a binary linked against glibc that crashes on `scratch` or `alpine`.
Copying the entire source tree with `COPY . .` instead of using `.dockerignore` or a multi-stage `COPY --from=build`, bloating layers with cached Go modules and `.git` history.
Using `CMD ./myapp` instead of the exec form `CMD ["./myapp"]`, causing signals (SIGTERM) to be swallowed by the shell wrapper and preventing graceful shutdown.
Forgetting `--chmod` on COPY or running as root, leaving the container with a security profile that violates the principle of least privilege.
execution_timeline: ✏️ Developer writes a Dockerfile with `FROM golang:1.22-alpine AS build` and `FROM scratch`.
⚙️ Docker daemon reads the Dockerfile and creates a build context from the directory (less anything in `.dockerignore`).
📦 The builder executes each instruction: `WORKDIR /app`, `COPY go.mod go.sum .`, `RUN go mod download` (layers cached).
🔨 `COPY . .` then `RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -ldflags="-s -w" -o /app/server .` produces a static binary.
🗃️ `FROM scratch` starts a fresh empty layer — nothing carried from the build stage except the copied binary.
📎 `COPY --from=build /app/server /server` — the only file in the final image.
🏷️ `docker build -t myapp:latest .` tags the image.
🚀 `docker run -d -p 8080:8080 --name myapp myapp:latest` starts the container.
🔍 `docker exec myapp ./healthcheck` runs an ad-hoc command in the running container.
🧹 `docker stop myapp` sends SIGTERM directly because CMD used exec form.

failure_modes.cause: Using `FROM golang:1.22` (full Debian) without multi-stage — final image is 900 MB.
failure_modes.fix: Switch to multi-stage: `FROM golang:1.22-alpine AS build` + `FROM scratch`.
failure_modes.scenario: Deploying a 900 MB image to AWS ECS causes slow pull times and higher cost.

failure_modes.cause: Building with CGO enabled when targeting scratch; binary segfaults because `libc.so.6` is missing.
failure_modes.fix: Set `CGO_ENABLED=0` and use `netgo` tags if needed: `-tags netgo -installsuffix netgo`.
failure_modes.scenario: CI pipeline builds a Go binary, pushes to Docker Hub, but the container exits immediately with 'standard_init_linux.go: exec user process caused: no such file or directory'.

failure_modes.cause: Using `CMD server` (shell form) prevents SIGTERM propagation; container stops with `docker kill` instead of `docker stop`.
failure_modes.fix: Use exec form: `CMD ["./server"]` or `ENTRYPOINT ["./server"]`.
failure_modes.scenario: Orchestrator (K8s) sends SIGTERM, container ignores it, K8s escalates to SIGKILL after 30 s, causing dirty shutdowns.

failure_modes.cause: No `.dockerignore` file; `.git`, `node_modules`, `vendor/` get sent to the Docker daemon, inflating the build context to 100 MB+.
failure_modes.fix: Add `.dockerignore` with `.git`, `*.md`, `vendor/`, `tmp/`, `coverage.out`.
failure_modes.scenario: Slow `docker build` on CI takes 3 minutes because the daemon must parse and hash 50k unnecessary files.

failure_modes.cause: COPY instead of ADD for remote URLs or tar auto-extraction when not intended; COPY is preferred for local files.
failure_modes.fix: Use `COPY` for local files, `ADD` only when you need automatic tar extraction or URL download.
failure_modes.scenario: `ADD --chmod=0755 https://example.com/plugin.tar.gz /plugins/` unexpectedly fetches at build time, making the build non-reproducible.

hidden_magic_checks.answer: No. `docker exec` starts a new process inside the container, but only the ENTRYPOINT/CMD process is the PID 1 that receives signals.
hidden_magic_checks.question: If I run `docker run --rm myapp` and the Go binary listens on :8080, can I use `docker exec -it myapp sh` to modify the binary while the server is running?

hidden_magic_checks.answer: Because Go can produce a statically-linked binary with `CGO_ENABLED=0` that runs on `scratch` — a completely empty image with zero vulnerabilities.
hidden_magic_checks.question: Why is `FROM scratch` a practical option for Go but not for Python or Node.js applications?

hidden_magic_checks.answer: Docker caches layers. If `COPY go.mod go.sum .` doesn't change, the `RUN go mod download` layer is reused from cache, saving minutes per rebuild.
hidden_magic_checks.question: Why should I COPY `go.mod` and `go.sum` before the rest of the source code in a Dockerfile?

hidden_magic_checks.answer: No — `alpine` uses musl libc. Build with `CGO_ENABLED=0` to produce a fully static binary, or install `gcompat`/build with `CGO_ENABLED=1` and `-tags musl`.
hidden_magic_checks.question: If I compile on my macOS (Darwin) and COPY the binary into a `golang:alpine` image, will it run?

zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's standard toolchain makes it uniquely suited for Docker. With `CGO_ENABLED=0`, `go build` produces a static binary with no external dependencies. The Go community standard practice is multi-stage builds: build with `golang:alpine`, deploy on `scratch` or `distroless`. Projects like Docker itself, Kubernetes, Prometheus, and Caddy all use this pattern. Go's vendoring and module cache also interact well with Docker layer caching — `go mod download` can be cached separately from the source COPY.
zero_magic.mental_model: A container is just a Linux process isolated by namespaces (PID, network, mount, UTS, IPC) and constrained by cgroups. The Dockerfile is a recipe that assembles a read-only filesystem snapshot (image) — each instruction adds a layer (an overlayfs diff). When you run a container, Docker overlays a writable layer on top and executes ENTRYPOINT/CMD as PID 1. For Go developers, the mental model simplifies: the binary + its layer = the running process. Everything else is packaging detail.
performance_implications: Image size: A `scratch`-based Go image can be as small as 5–15 MB vs 800+ MB for a full Debian base; smaller images pull faster, start faster, and have a smaller attack surface.
Layer caching: Order Dockerfile instructions from least-frequently-changing (go.mod, go.sum) to most-frequently-changing (source code) to maximise cache reuse and reduce build times by 60-80%.
Build speed: `docker build` parallelism (BuildKit) can execute independent stages and COPY operations concurrently; enable BuildKit with `DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1`.
Multi-stage elimination: Every COPY --from=build carries only needed artifacts; unused layers from the build stage are discarded, keeping the final image lean.
Memory limits: Use `docker run --memory=256m --cpus=0.5` to prevent a Go binary with a GC spike from starving other containers on the same host.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Docker solves environment reproducibility. Before Docker, every team member and CI server needed to install Go, MySQL, Redis, and system libraries at matching versions. Docker packages the Go binary, its runtime dependencies (or lack thereof with `scratch`), and the execution environment into a single immutable artifact. This eliminates 'works on my machine' and makes deployments atomic — you ship an image, not a tarball of binaries with install instructions.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Write a Dockerfile that takes a Go HTTP server (which listens on :8080 and serves `{"status":"ok"}` at `/healthz`), builds it with CGO disabled, and produces a final image under 20 MB. Then run it with `docker run -p 8080:8080 --rm my-go-server` and verify the health endpoint responds. Then explain in one paragraph why the image is so small.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every major Go project uses Docker for delivery. Docker Hub lists official images for Go itself, as well as Go-based tools like Traefik, Caddy, Hugo, and Syncthing. In production, Go services are deployed as Docker containers on ECS, GKE, AKS, or EKS — often built via CI (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI) and pushed to a registry, then pulled by the orchestrator. `docker-compose.yml` is used for local development to spin up the Go API alongside Postgres, Redis, and a message broker.
step_by_step_execution: Create a `.dockerignore` listing `.git`, `vendor/`, `*.md`, `tmp/`, `coverage.out`, `Dockerfile`.
Write a `Dockerfile` with `FROM golang:1.22-alpine AS build` and set `WORKDIR /app`.
Copy dependency files first: `COPY go.mod go.sum ./` then `RUN go mod download`.
Copy source: `COPY . .` and build: `RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -ldflags="-s -w" -o /server .`.
Start the minimal stage: `FROM scratch`. Copy the binary: `COPY --from=build /server /server`.
Set the runtime command in exec form: `CMD ["/server"]`.
Build: `docker build -t my-go-server .` and check size with `docker images my-go-server`.
Run: `docker run -d -p 8080:8080 --name my-go-server my-go-server`.
Test: `curl http://localhost:8080/healthz`.
Debug with `docker exec -it my-go-server sh` (or use `docker run --rm -it --entrypoint sh my-go-server` for scratch images where no shell exists).
Stop with `docker stop my-go-server && docker rm my-go-server`.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Docker is a client-server architecture. The `docker` CLI talks to `dockerd` (the daemon) via a REST API (by default on `/var/run/docker.sock`). When you run `docker build`, the daemon receives the build context (tarred), unpack it, and parses the Dockerfile instruction-by-instruction. Each instruction becomes a layer: a commit of the filesystem diff using overlayfs (or aufs, devicemapper, etc.). Layers are content-addressed and cached. When you `docker run` an image, the daemon creates a new mount namespace, overlays the image layers + a writable layer, sets up networking (bridge by default), then forks the ENTRYPOINT/CMD as PID 1 in that namespace. The Go binary runs as PID 1 — it handles all signals directly (a key reason Go and Docker work well together, since Go's runtime handles SIGTERM, SIGINT cleanly).
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Docker basics exist because modern software deployment requires immutable, reproducible, and portable application packaging. A Go binary is inherently static, making Docker an ideal delivery mechanism — but only when the developer understands base images, multi-stage builds, signal handling with exec vs shell form, and image size hygiene. Without this knowledge, a Go developer will produce slow-build, oversized, insecure, or non-restartable containers that defeat the purpose of containerization.

items.title: Docker images and layers
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Docker images and layers in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Putting RUN go build and COPY source in the same layer, defeating layer caching and forcing a full rebuild on every source change.
Using alpine as the final base image when a scratch image would suffice, ignoring that Go static binaries need nothing from the OS.
Not ordering Dockerfile instructions from least-frequently-changing to most-frequently-changing, causing unnecessary cache invalidation on every build.
Copying the entire build context (including .git, node_modules, local binaries) into the image, inflating both build time and final image size.
Believing distroless images are secure by default — they still need non-root users and proper capabilities.
execution_timeline: Developer writes multi-stage Dockerfile
docker build reads Dockerfile and sends build context to daemon (excluding .dockerignore)
Docker resolves base image: FROM golang:1.22 AS builder
Each instruction executes as a new read-only layer
Cached layers are reused when instruction + parent ID + file checksums match
RUN go mod download is cached independently from source code
COPY . . invalidates all subsequent layers if any source file changed
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 go build produces a statically linked binary (~10MB)
Second stage starts FROM scratch
COPY --from=builder copies only the binary
Final image is ~10MB with exactly 2 layers
docker history myapp shows each layer, its creation instruction, and its size

failure_modes.scenario: Docker build cache miss on every commit because go.mod is copied after all source files
failure_modes.cause: COPY . . comes before COPY go.mod go.sum and RUN go mod download, so any source change invalidates the dependency layer
failure_modes.fix: Reorder: COPY go.mod go.sum . first, RUN go mod download, then COPY . . to preserve the dependency cache

failure_modes.scenario: Final image is 800MB+ containing the Go toolchain and source code
failure_modes.cause: Single-stage build using golang:1.22 as the final base image instead of multi-stage with scratch
failure_modes.fix: Use multi-stage build: FROM golang:1.22 AS builder, then FROM scratch, COPY --from=builder /app/main /main

failure_modes.scenario: Go binary built in Docker fails with 'not found' or 'exec format error' on scratch
failure_modes.cause: Binary was compiled with CGO_ENABLED=1 producing a dynamic binary that needs libc, or for the wrong architecture
failure_modes.fix: Build with CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux GOARCH=amd64 go build -o /app/main . and use scratch or gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12 if cgo is required

failure_modes.scenario: Secrets like .env or SSH keys leak in the final image despite being deleted mid-build
failure_modes.cause: Docker layers are additive; deleting a file in a later layer leaves the original layer (with the secret) in the image history
failure_modes.fix: Use Docker BuildKit secrets: RUN --mount=type=secret,id=mysecret … or multi-stage build that never copies secrets into intermediate layers

failure_modes.scenario: docker build fails with 'no space left on device'
failure_modes.cause: Accumulated cached intermediate layers and dangling images consume all available disk space
failure_modes.fix: Run docker system prune -f and docker buildx prune -f regularly; use --no-cache for release builds to avoid unbounded cache growth

hidden_magic_checks: Check 1: Q: Why does removing a file in a later Docker layer not reduce the final image size? A: Docker layers are additive and immutable. The layer that originally added the file persists in the image history. The delete layer only adds a whiteout entry — the underlying data is still present in the layer below.

Check 2: Q: What is the caching difference between COPY and ADD for Go builds? A: COPY only copies local files and its cache key is the checksum of source files. ADD supports URL downloads and tar auto-extraction, which can produce unpredictable cache keys from network content. COPY is preferred for reproducible Go builds.

Check 3: Q: Your scratch image's Go binary crashes with 'exec format error'. What is the most likely cause? A: The binary was compiled for the wrong architecture/OS (e.g., darwin/arm64 instead of linux/amd64) or was dynamically linked. Verify GOOS, GOARCH, and CGO_ENABLED=0.

Check 4: Q: How does docker build determine whether to reuse a cached layer? A: It compares the instruction text, the checksum of files referenced by COPY/ADD, and the parent layer ID. If all three match identically, the cached layer is reused and the builder proceeds to the next instruction.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go developers use multi-stage Docker builds as the de facto standard for shipping production services. The canonical Go Dockerfile separates dependency resolution (go mod download) from source compilation, producing a statically linked binary that runs on scratch — the absolute smallest possible base image. This pattern is universal across the Go ecosystem: Docker CLI, Kubernetes, Prometheus, Grafana, Caddy, Traefik, and every HashiCorp tool rely on it.
zero_magic.mental_model: When a process reads a file, Docker's union filesystem (overlayfs) walks the stack top-to-bottom and returns the first version it finds. Deleting a file in a later overlay does not erase it from earlier overlays — it just places a 'hidden' marker on top. This mental model explains why a 800MB build stage can yield an 8MB final image, why deleting intermediate files mid-build does nothing to shrink the image, and why reordering instructions changes cache behavior.
performance_implications: Layer caching: Well-ordered layers (dependencies before source) reduce iterative build time by 80–95% because unchanged dependency layers skip re-execution entirely.

Image size: Multi-stage builds with scratch shrink production images from ~800MB (full Go toolchain) to ~10MB (single static binary), lowering network transfer, registry storage, and container cold-start time.

Build context size: Every file in the build context is sent to the Docker daemon and checksummed for COPY cache invalidation. Adding a .dockerignore to exclude .git, node_modules, and local binaries can cut context size by 90%+.

Cache invalidation: Changing go.mod invalidates the go mod download layer and all subsequent layers. Grouping dependency copying and downloading into dedicated early layers minimizes unnecessary rebuilds during source iteration.
Layer count and metadata: Each layer adds metadata and extraction overhead. While overlay2 has no hard layer limit, fewer layers mean faster docker pull extraction and lower image manifest size.
zero_magic.problem_solved: Docker images and layers solve the problem of shipping Go applications with reproducible, minimal, and cache-efficient builds. Before layers, every Docker build was monolithic — any source change triggered a full re-download of dependencies and recompilation from scratch. Before multi-stage, production images carried the full Go toolchain. Layers give developers fine-grained cache granularity and the ability to compose images from reusable filesystem diffs, making iterative development fast and production images as small as possible.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: Create a Dockerfile that builds a Go HTTP server in under 10MB using multi-stage build with properly ordered layers and a .dockerignore. Run docker build -t go-server ., then docker history go-server to list each layer with its size. Write a single paragraph explaining why each COPY, RUN, and FROM instruction is ordered the way it is and how the layer cache behaves when go.mod changes vs. when only a .go source file changes.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every major Go project that ships as a Docker image uses multi-stage builds with scratch or distroless. Docker CLI, Kubernetes (kubelet, kube-apiserver, etcd), Prometheus, Grafana, Caddy, Traefik, and HashiCorp tools (Consul, Vault, Nomad, Terraform) all follow this pattern. The Go Dockerfile is so universally clean that Docker's official documentation uses it as the canonical multi-stage example.
step_by_step_execution: 1. Create a .dockerignore excluding .git, node_modules, *.local, and any build artifacts.
2. Start the builder stage: FROM golang:1.22-alpine AS builder.
3. Set WORKDIR /build and copy dependency files first: COPY go.mod go.sum ./.
4. Download dependencies: RUN go mod download (this layer is cached unless go.mod or go.sum change).
5. Copy all remaining source code: COPY . . (this layer invalidates on any source change).
6. Build a static binary: RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux GOARCH=amd64 go build -o /app/main .
7. Start the runtime stage: FROM scratch.
8. Copy only the binary: COPY --from=builder /app/main /main.
9. Set the entrypoint: ENTRYPOINT ["/main"].
10. Build: docker build -t myservice . && docker history myservice to inspect layer sizes.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Docker images are stored under /var/lib/docker/overlay2 (Linux) as a set of read-only snapshots, each corresponding to one Dockerfile instruction. Each layer is a tarball of filesystem diffs. The union mount — overlayfs — combines all layers into a merged view via a lowerdir (image layers) and upperdir (container writable layer). When a container writes a file, the copy-on-write mechanism copies the file from a lower layer to the upper layer. docker history decompiles an image into its instruction list with per-layer sizes built from the diff metadata. The --squash flag merges all layers into one, trading cache granularity for a flatter, smaller image at the cost of losing the ability to share intermediate layers across images.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without layers, every Docker build would be monolithic: any source change would force refetching all dependencies, recompiling everything, and rebuilding the entire image from scratch. Layers exist to enable incremental, cacheable, and parallelizable image construction. They are the foundation of Docker's build speed and the multi-stage pattern that makes Go production images the smallest possible — a scratch-based Go image can be as small as 5–10 MB, impossible without the layer abstraction.

beginner_mistakes: Ignoring container lifecycle semantics with one deployment target — containers are ephemeral, not VMs. State must be externalized.
Assuming one deployment target configuration from the development environment works in production unchanged — environments differ in fundamental ways.
Not testing one deployment target failure scenarios — what happens when the registry is unreachable or the deploy fails mid-way.
execution_timeline: Configure one deployment target for the development environment.
Test one deployment target locally with docker-compose or a similar workflow.
Set up one deployment target in CI/CD pipeline.
Verify one deployment target behavior under failure conditions (network partition, resource exhaustion).
Document one deployment target configuration and operational procedures.

failure_modes.scenario: A team configures one deployment target incorrectly and the production deployment fails silently.
failure_modes.cause: The one deployment target configuration was not tested in a staging environment.
failure_modes.fix: Always test {title.lower()} changes in a staging environment before production. Automate verification.

failure_modes.scenario: A one deployment target misconfiguration causes a multi-hour production outage during a routine deploy.
failure_modes.cause: The one deployment target changes were made without understanding the production environment differences.
failure_modes.fix: Maintain environment-specific {title.lower()} configuration files. Use infrastructure-as-code to track changes.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must configure one deployment target for a Go service and verify it works correctly.
Learner must identify the production-specific one deployment target configuration that differs from development.
Learner must write a test that validates one deployment target behavior under failure conditions.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's single-binary deployment model integrates naturally with one deployment target. The compiled binary has no runtime dependencies, making it ideal for containerized deployment.
zero_magic.mental_model: It defines how the service is built, deployed, configured, and monitored. Getting one deployment target right means the difference between a service that operates smoothly and one that requires constant firefighting.
performance_implications: Properly configured one deployment target adds no runtime performance overhead.
Misconfigured one deployment target can cause deployment failures, rollbacks, and extended downtime.
Investing in one deployment target automation reduces mean-time-to-recover (MTTR) from hours to minutes.
zero_magic.problem_solved: This lesson explains how one deployment target works in the Go deployment ecosystem. Without understanding one deployment target, teams make operational mistakes that cause production incidents.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must configure one deployment target for a Go service, demonstrate it working in a local environment, and document the configuration decisions.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go services use one deployment target for every deployment. Understanding one deployment target is essential for any Go engineer working on production services.
step_by_step_execution: Research the one deployment target best practices for Go services.
Implement one deployment target following the configuration guide.
Test one deployment target locally with a representative environment.
Document the one deployment target setup and operational procedures.
Review and iterate based on production experience.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Under the hood, one deployment target for Go services follows the same patterns as other compiled languages but benefits from Go's small binary size, fast compilation, and zero runtime dependencies.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: One deployment target exists because software must be deployed reliably and repeatedly. Manual deployment processes are error-prone and do not scale. Understanding one deployment target is essential for professional Go engineering.

items.title: README writing
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply README writing in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Treating readme writing as an afterthought — it is as important as the code itself for career growth.
Writing readme writing that assumes the reader has the same context as the writer.
Using jargon without explanation — not every reader of readme writing is a Go expert.
execution_timeline: Define the audience and purpose for your readme writing.
Draft the readme writing content following established conventions.
Get feedback from peers on your readme writing draft.
Revise readme writing based on feedback.
Maintain readme writing as the project evolves.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer writes readme writing that is incomprehensible to the target audience, wasting everyone's time.
failure_modes.cause: The readme writing was written without considering the reader's background.
failure_modes.fix: Define the audience before writing. Use examples. Get a peer from the target audience to review.

failure_modes.scenario: A readme writing document goes stale and misleads new team members.
failure_modes.cause: The readme writing was not updated when the system changed.
failure_modes.fix: Treat {title.lower()} as code: review it in PRs, update it with changes, test it for accuracy.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must write readme writing for a Go project and get a peer review.
Learner must identify what is missing from a given readme writing document.
Learner must explain the difference between readme writing for internal vs external audiences.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's community emphasizes clear documentation and communication. The Go standard library's documentation is a model of clarity. README should explain: what the project does, how to run it, architecture overview, tech stack, and contribution guide. Bad READMEs are the #1 reason open source projects fail.
zero_magic.mental_model: The quality of this communication often matters more than the code quality for career outcomes.
performance_implications: Investing time in readme writing pays dividends in reduced onboarding time and fewer misunderstandings.
Poor readme writing causes repeated questions, misaligned expectations, and wasted engineering time.
Good readme writing is a force multiplier — it allows your work to be understood and used without your direct involvement.
zero_magic.problem_solved: This lesson explains how to approach readme writing effectively. Many engineers focus entirely on code and neglect the communication skills that differentiate senior engineers.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must produce readme writing for a Go project and receive actionable feedback from a peer.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every professional Go engineer produces readme writing as part of their job. Hiring managers evaluate candidates partly on their ability to communicate clearly about their work.
step_by_step_execution: Define the audience and purpose for readme writing.
Research conventions and examples of good readme writing.
Draft readme writing with a focus on clarity and completeness.
Get feedback from the target audience and revise.
Maintain readme writing as the project evolves.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Under the hood, readme writing is about empathy and clarity. The best technical communicators anticipate what their audience needs to know and present it in the most accessible way.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: README writing exists because code alone does not communicate intent, architecture decisions, or operational knowledge. Without readme writing, knowledge is trapped in individual engineers' heads.

beginner_mistakes: Treating writing project narratives as an afterthought — it is as important as the code itself for career growth.
Writing writing project narratives that assumes the reader has the same context as the writer.
Using jargon without explanation — not every reader of writing project narratives is a Go expert.
execution_timeline: Define the audience and purpose for your writing project narratives.
Draft the writing project narratives content following established conventions.
Get feedback from peers on your writing project narratives draft.
Revise writing project narratives based on feedback.
Maintain writing project narratives as the project evolves.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer writes writing project narratives that is incomprehensible to the target audience, wasting everyone's time.
failure_modes.cause: The writing project narratives was written without considering the reader's background.
failure_modes.fix: Define the audience before writing. Use examples. Get a peer from the target audience to review.

failure_modes.scenario: A writing project narratives document goes stale and misleads new team members.
failure_modes.cause: The writing project narratives was not updated when the system changed.
failure_modes.fix: Treat {title.lower()} as code: review it in PRs, update it with changes, test it for accuracy.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must write writing project narratives for a Go project and get a peer review.
Learner must identify what is missing from a given writing project narratives document.
Learner must explain the difference between writing project narratives for internal vs external audiences.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's community emphasizes clear documentation and communication. The Go standard library's documentation is a model of clarity. For each portfolio project, write: problem statement, architecture decisions, what you learned, and challenges overcome. Focus on engineering tradeoffs.
zero_magic.mental_model: The quality of this communication often matters more than the code quality for career outcomes.
performance_implications: Investing time in writing project narratives pays dividends in reduced onboarding time and fewer misunderstandings.
Poor writing project narratives causes repeated questions, misaligned expectations, and wasted engineering time.
Good writing project narratives is a force multiplier — it allows your work to be understood and used without your direct involvement.
zero_magic.problem_solved: This lesson explains how to approach writing project narratives effectively. Many engineers focus entirely on code and neglect the communication skills that differentiate senior engineers.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must produce writing project narratives for a Go project and receive actionable feedback from a peer.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every professional Go engineer produces writing project narratives as part of their job. Hiring managers evaluate candidates partly on their ability to communicate clearly about their work.
step_by_step_execution: Define the audience and purpose for writing project narratives.
Research conventions and examples of good writing project narratives.
Draft writing project narratives with a focus on clarity and completeness.
Get feedback from the target audience and revise.
Maintain writing project narratives as the project evolves.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Under the hood, writing project narratives is about empathy and clarity. The best technical communicators anticipate what their audience needs to know and present it in the most accessible way.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Writing project narratives exists because code alone does not communicate intent, architecture decisions, or operational knowledge. Without writing project narratives, knowledge is trapped in individual engineers' heads.

items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Capstone planning in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Treating capstone planning as an afterthought — it is as important as the code itself for career growth.
Writing capstone planning that assumes the reader has the same context as the writer.
Using jargon without explanation — not every reader of capstone planning is a Go expert.
execution_timeline: Define the audience and purpose for your capstone planning.
Draft the capstone planning content following established conventions.
Get feedback from peers on your capstone planning draft.
Revise capstone planning based on feedback.
Maintain capstone planning as the project evolves.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer writes capstone planning that is incomprehensible to the target audience, wasting everyone's time.
failure_modes.cause: The capstone planning was written without considering the reader's background.
failure_modes.fix: Define the audience before writing. Use examples. Get a peer from the target audience to review.

failure_modes.scenario: A capstone planning document goes stale and misleads new team members.
failure_modes.cause: The capstone planning was not updated when the system changed.
failure_modes.fix: Treat {title.lower()} as code: review it in PRs, update it with changes, test it for accuracy.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must write capstone planning for a Go project and get a peer review.
Learner must identify what is missing from a given capstone planning document.
Learner must explain the difference between capstone planning for internal vs external audiences.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's community emphasizes clear documentation and communication. The Go standard library's documentation is a model of clarity. Plan a capstone project that combines: REST API, database, authentication, background workers, tests, CI/CD, Docker, and deployment. Scope for 4-6 weeks.
zero_magic.mental_model: The quality of this communication often matters more than the code quality for career outcomes.
performance_implications: Investing time in capstone planning pays dividends in reduced onboarding time and fewer misunderstandings.
Poor capstone planning causes repeated questions, misaligned expectations, and wasted engineering time.
Good capstone planning is a force multiplier — it allows your work to be understood and used without your direct involvement.
zero_magic.problem_solved: This lesson explains how to approach capstone planning effectively. Many engineers focus entirely on code and neglect the communication skills that differentiate senior engineers.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must produce capstone planning for a Go project and receive actionable feedback from a peer.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every professional Go engineer produces capstone planning as part of their job. Hiring managers evaluate candidates partly on their ability to communicate clearly about their work.
step_by_step_execution: Define the audience and purpose for capstone planning.
Research conventions and examples of good capstone planning.
Draft capstone planning with a focus on clarity and completeness.
Get feedback from the target audience and revise.
Maintain capstone planning as the project evolves.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Under the hood, capstone planning is about empathy and clarity. The best technical communicators anticipate what their audience needs to know and present it in the most accessible way.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Capstone planning exists because code alone does not communicate intent, architecture decisions, or operational knowledge. Without capstone planning, knowledge is trapped in individual engineers' heads.

items.title: Opslane foundation and configuration
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Opslane foundation and configuration in the context of professional Go software engineering.
required_prior_knowledge: Complete Module 15 — Portfolio, Collaboration, Interview Readiness, and Capstone Prep.

beginner_mistakes: Skipping environment variable validation and letting the application start with missing config.
Hard-coding configuration values instead of using a structured config loading mechanism.
Not separating config loading from component initialization, making tests difficult.
execution_timeline: The application reads configuration from environment variables, config files, and CLI flags.
Configuration values are validated against expected types and ranges.
Dependencies (database, cache, logger) are initialized in dependency order.
The HTTP server and worker pools are started after all dependencies are ready.
A health check endpoint confirms the application is fully initialized.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer deploys a new version with a missing required config key.
failure_modes.cause: The config loader did not validate presence of required keys.
failure_modes.fix: Add explicit validation for required configuration keys at startup.

failure_modes.scenario: The application starts but crashes immediately due to a nil dependency.
failure_modes.cause: Dependency injection was not ordered correctly.
failure_modes.fix: Use a structured initialization function that creates dependencies in dependency order.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must identify which configuration sources take precedence when the same key exists in env, file, and flags.
Learner must add a new configuration key with validation to the existing config struct.
Learner must diagnose a startup failure caused by a missing dependency.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: In Go production services, configuration is typically loaded at startup using libraries like spf13/viper or envconfig. The Opslane application follows this pattern, with config structs, environment variable binding, and validation before any component initialization.
zero_magic.mental_model: how the Opslane application boots, loads config, initializes dependencies, and wires components together. The foundation and configuration layer provides a single, validated source of truth for all runtime parameters.
performance_implications: Config loading at startup takes <10ms and is negligible compared to initialization time.
Validation at startup prevents runtime failures from misconfiguration.
Structured config types improve maintainability and testability.
zero_magic.problem_solved: how the Opslane application boots, loads config, initializes dependencies, and wires components together. The foundation and configuration layer provides a single, validated source of truth for all runtime parameters.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must add a new configuration parameter to the existing config struct, bind it to an environment variable, and write a test that verifies the configuration loads correctly.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Every production application needs a structured, testable configuration mechanism. Opslane's foundation and configuration layer mirrors patterns used by Prometheus, Grafana, and other Go production services.
step_by_step_execution: Define a Go struct with fields for each configuration parameter.
Use environment variable tags or a config file to populate the struct at startup.
Validate required fields and value ranges before proceeding.
Pass the config struct to component initializers as a dependency.
Start the application only after all initialization succeeds.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Under the hood, the config loader uses Go's struct tags and reflection to map environment variables to typed struct fields. Validation uses a combination of zero-value checks (required fields), range checks (numeric limits), and custom validators for complex constraints.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without a structured foundation layer, every component handles its own config loading and initialization, leading to inconsistent behavior, hard-to-find bugs, and untestable startup sequences.

items.title: Opslane database and models
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Opslane database and models in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Writing raw SQL everywhere instead of using a structured model layer.
Ignoring database connection pooling and opening a new connection for each query.
Not using transactions for operations that modify multiple rows or tables.
execution_timeline: Define Go struct types that map to database tables (models).
Create migration files that define the table schema.
Apply migrations at startup to ensure the schema is current.
Execute queries through a connection pool with proper timeout and cancellation.
Use transactions for multi-step write operations.

failure_modes.scenario: A developer forgets to close a database row cursor, causing a connection leak.
failure_modes.cause: Query results were not properly iterated and closed.
failure_modes.fix: Always defer rows.Close() after executing a query that returns rows.

failure_modes.scenario: A migration fails halfway through and leaves the database in an inconsistent state.
failure_modes.cause: The migration was not wrapped in a transaction.
failure_modes.fix: Use transactional migrations that roll back on failure.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain why prepared statements are important for performance.
Learner must identify the difference between soft delete and hard delete in the model layer.
Learner must diagnose a connection pool exhaustion from leaked rows.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's database/sql package provides a generic interface for SQL databases. Opslane uses this with a driver (pgx for PostgreSQL) and follows the repository pattern — model structs for data, repository structs for queries, and service structs for business logic.
zero_magic.mental_model: The database and model layer is the application's memory — it defines what data exists, how it's structured, and how it persists. Just as human memory needs reliable storage and retrieval, so does application data.
performance_implications: Connection pooling reduces the overhead of establishing new connections (~1-5ms per connection).
Prepared statements improve query performance by caching query plans.
Indexed queries are 10-100x faster than full table scans on large tables.
zero_magic.problem_solved: how the application defines, migrates, and queries persistent data using SQL and Go structs. Opslane's database and model layer provides a consistent, type-safe, and testable data access pattern.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement a new model with its migration, repository methods (Create, FindByID, List, Update, Delete), and a test that verifies CRUD operations through the repository.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Production Go services like CockroachDB, TimescaleDB, and Mattermost use Go structs for data modeling and database/sql for querying. Opslane follows the same proven patterns.
step_by_step_execution: Define a Go struct for the model with json and db tags.
Write a migration file that creates the corresponding table with proper indexes.
Create a repository struct that holds the *sql.DB connection pool.
Implement CRUD methods using prepared statements and context cancellation.
Write integration tests against a test database.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Under the hood, the database driver converts Go types to SQL types and back. Connection pooling uses a channel-based pool of idle connections. Migrations are tracked in a schema_migrations table so each migration runs exactly once.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without a structured database layer, every query uses raw SQL strings scattered across handlers, making it impossible to test data access in isolation or change the database schema without hunting down every query.

items.title: Opslane authentication
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Opslane authentication in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Storing passwords in plain text instead of hashing them.
Using a fixed API key that never rotates.
Implementing custom authentication instead of using established standards.
execution_timeline: A user submits credentials (password, API key, or OAuth token).
The authentication middleware extracts and validates the credential.
For password auth: hash verification with bcrypt/scrypt.
For token auth: JWT or session token validation.
On success: set authenticated user context for downstream handlers.
On failure: return 401 Unauthorized.

failure_modes.scenario: A user's password hash is leaked, and the attacker can reverse it.
failure_modes.cause: The password was hashed with a fast algorithm like SHA-256 instead of bcrypt.
failure_modes.fix: Use bcrypt with a cost factor of 10-12 for password hashing.

failure_modes.scenario: A JWT token is accepted after its expiration time.
failure_modes.cause: The JWT verification did not check the exp claim.
failure_modes.fix: Always validate the exp, nbf, and iss claims during JWT verification.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain why bcrypt is preferred over SHA-256 for password hashing.
Learner must identify the security implications of a leaked JWT signing key.
Learner must implement a middleware that extracts user identity from a valid session.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go's standard library provides crypto/hmac, crypto/rand, and crypto/subtle for authentication primitives. The Opslane authentication layer uses golang-jwt/jwt for JWT, bcrypt for passwords, and standard Go HTTP middleware patterns.
zero_magic.mental_model: Authentication is the application's bouncer — it checks IDs at the door before letting anyone in. A good bouncer checks every ID, every time, and knows how to spot fakes.
performance_implications: bcrypt verification takes ~10-100ms per attempt, making brute force attacks slow.
JWT verification is fast (~1µs) because it uses symmetric or asymmetric crypto without database lookups.
Session-based auth requires database lookups on every request, adding ~1-5ms latency.
zero_magic.problem_solved: how the application verifies the identity of users and services. Opslane's authentication layer verifies identity using established cryptographic standards.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement a login endpoint that validates credentials and returns a signed JWT, then write a middleware that extracts and validates the JWT on protected routes.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: JWT-based authentication is used by Kubernetes, Docker, GitHub API, and most modern web services. Opslane implements the same industry-standard patterns with Go.
step_by_step_execution: User submits credentials to a login endpoint.
The login handler looks up the user, verifies the password hash with bcrypt.
On success, generate a JWT with user ID, role, expiration, and signing.
The auth middleware extracts the JWT from the Authorization header.
The middleware validates the signature, checks expiration, and sets user context.
Protected handlers read user identity from the request context.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Under the hood, JWT is a base64-encoded JSON payload with a cryptographic signature. The signature uses HMAC-SHA256 (symmetric) or RSA/ECDSA (asymmetric). bcrypt includes a random salt and is intentionally slow to thwart brute force attacks.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without authentication, every request is anonymous — no way to identify who is making the request, no way to enforce per-user limits, and no audit trail for security incidents.

items.title: Opslane authorization
items.learning_objective: Understand and apply Opslane authorization in the context of professional Go software engineering.

beginner_mistakes: Checking authorization only in the frontend or client-side code.
Using hard-coded role checks instead of a configurable permission system.
Forgetting to check authorization on every protected endpoint.
execution_timeline: Authenticate the request and extract the user's roles/permissions.
Determine the resource and action from the request (method + path).
Check if the user's roles/permissions allow the action on the resource.
Allow or deny the request (200 or 403).
Log the authorization decision for audit.

failure_modes.scenario: A user with a viewer role can delete resources.
failure_modes.cause: The authorization check was only implemented on the frontend, not the backend.
failure_modes.fix: Always enforce authorization server-side as the authoritative check.

failure_modes.scenario: A user can access another tenant's data by changing an ID in the URL.
failure_modes.cause: The authorization check only verified the role, not the resource ownership.
failure_modes.fix: Implement resource-level authorization that checks ownership or tenant scope.

hidden_magic_checks: Learner must explain the difference between RBAC (role-based) and ABAC (attribute-based) access control.
Learner must identify the security hole when authorization is only checked in middleware but not in individual handlers.
Learner must implement a permission check that verifies both role AND resource ownership.
zero_magic.how_go_uses_it: Go web frameworks and standard library middleware patterns handle authorization as HTTP middleware or handler wrappers. Opslane uses a middleware chain: authentication first, then authorization, then the handler.
zero_magic.mental_model: Authorization is the application's security guard — authentication checks your ID, but authorization checks whether you have the right pass to enter a specific room.
performance_implications: In-memory permission checks add <10µs per request.
Database-backed permission lookups add 1-5ms per request — cache when possible.
ABAC with complex attribute evaluation can add 10-50ms per request.
zero_magic.problem_solved: how the application enforces permissions — who can do what to which resources. Opslane's authorization layer enforces who can do what to which resources, preventing privilege escalation and data access violations.
zero_magic.proof_of_understanding: The learner must implement an authorization middleware that checks both role-based permissions and resource ownership, then write tests that verify allowed and denied requests.
zero_magic.real_world_usage: Authorization in Opslane follows the same patterns as Kubernetes RBAC, AWS IAM, and GitHub's permission model — roles, permissions, and resource scopes.
step_by_step_execution: Define roles and their associated permissions in configuration or database.
Extract user identity and roles from the authenticated request context.
Determine the required permission from the HTTP method and path.
Check if the user's roles include the required permission.
For resource-level checks, verify the user owns or has access to the target resource.
Return 403 Forbidden if authorization fails.
zero_magic.under_the_hood: Under the hood, authorization can use Casbin (external library), a custom middleware that checks against a permission map, or OPA (Open Policy Agent). Opslane implements a lightweight middleware-based approach with a permission cache for performance.
zero_magic.why_it_exists: Without authorization, any authenticated user can access any resource and perform any action — a single compromised account gives attackers full access to the entire system.
